- For **small hardware and fittings** → **BMC** (lowest cost, complex shapes).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Students frequently write clamping force in Pa·m² but forget to convert to kN or tonnes. Always express final answer in standard engineering units (kN, tonnes, MPa).
> 2. **Ignoring flash loss** — Real charge mass is **always higher** than the theoretical cavity mass; missing the flash factor loses 1 – 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing SMC with BMC** — SMC is supplied as sheets; BMC is supplied as a bulk dough. Material handling differences must be mentioned.
> 4. **Skipping the breathing cycle** — Many answers omit this critical phase, losing marks on the "process steps" question.
> 5. **Forgetting shrinkage compensation** — Mould dimensions must be larger than the final part dimensions by the shrinkage factor (~0.1 % – 0.5 %).
> 6. **No mention of charge coverage ratio** — Examiners expect students to know the 40 – 70 % range for SMC.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Matched die moulding is a CLOSED-MOULD, HIGH-PRESSURE process** that produces composite parts with finished surfaces on **both sides**.
- **Key industrial metrics:** Pressure **0.5 – 10 MPa**, Temperature **120 – 160 °C**, Cycle time **30 s – 10 min**.
- **Clamping force equation:** $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \cdot A_{cavity}$ — the fundamental sizing equation for press selection.
- **Charge coverage ratio (CCR) for SMC** is typically **40 – 70 %** of cavity plan area.
- **Charge mass = (cavity mass × shrinkage factor) / (1 − flash loss)** — never forget the flash and shrinkage corrections.
- **The breathing cycle** (1 – 3 s dwell at ~0.3 MPa) is critical for venting volatiles and avoiding porosity.
- **Mould materials** are typically P-20 tool steel (for moderate volumes) or hard-anodized aluminium (for prototypes).
- **Arrhenius kinetics** govern the cure reaction: $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}$ — higher temperature exponentially speeds up cure.
- **Marine applications** include boat hulls (small to medium), decks, consoles, hatches, and structural panels.
- **SMC = Sheet** (used as plies), **BMC = Bulk** (dough-like), **GMT = Glass Mat Thermoplastic**, **Prepregs = Pre-impregnated** fibres.
- **Advantages over hand lay-up:** higher Vf, faster cycles, closed-mould (lower emissions), two finished sides, better consistency.
- **Disadvantages:** high tooling cost, limited to smaller parts (press size limits), less design flexibility than hand lay-up.
- **Cycle time = closing + breathing + curing + opening + loading/unloading** — total ~1 – 5 minutes per part for typical boat SMC.
- **Fourier number criterion (Fo ≈ 0.5)** gives a quick estimate of cure time based on thermal diffusivity and part thickness.
- **Mould parallelism tolerance ≈ 0.05 mm per 1000 mm** — critical for uniform part thickness.
- **Always round UP the number of plies** calculated from the charge equation to ensure complete cavity fill.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forming Methods Employing Gas Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forming Methods Employing Gas Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Gas Pressure Forming Methods** in composite boat manufacturing refer to a family of closed-mold forming techniques in which a pressurized gas (typically compressed air, nitrogen, or steam at pressures ranging from **0.1 MPa to 7.0 MPa**) is applied either internally or externally to a fiber-reinforced laminate to consolidate, compact, and cure the composite against a rigid or flexible mold surface. These methods exploit the isostatic pressure principle to achieve low void content (target **< 2%**), controlled fiber-volume fraction (typically **55%–65%**), and superior mechanical performance demanded in marine structural components such as hull shells, decks, bulkheads, and superstructures.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework (PENST742), gas-pressure forming is recognized as a **primary manufacturing route** for marine composites because it produces parts with:
- High fiber-packing efficiency
- Excellent surface finish on the tool-side
- Reproducible laminate quality for class-society certification (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS)

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy (Plain English)**
> Imagine you have a layered sandwich of bread, cheese, and vegetables that is loose and full of air gaps. If you place it inside a sealed plastic pouch, attach a bicycle pump, and inflate the pouch, the air squeezes the sandwich evenly from all sides — pushing out the air gaps, compressing the layers tightly, and giving you a compact, uniform sandwich. **Gas pressure forming works exactly like this**, but instead of bread, we have layers of carbon/glass fibers soaked in resin. Instead of a bicycle pump, we use an industrial compressor or autoclave. Instead of a sandwich, we get a high-performance boat hull.

The "pouch" in marine composites is called a **vacuum bag** or **pressure bladder**, and the "pump" is an **autoclave** or **pressure vessel**.

## 1.3 Sub-Categorization of Gas Pressure Forming Methods

The KTU syllabus groups the following methods under the umbrella of gas-pressure forming:

1. **Autoclave Molding** (gold-standard marine method)
2. **Pressure Bag Molding**
3. **Bladder Molding / Internal Pressure Molding**
4. **Diaphragm Forming**
5. **Rubber-Tool (Flexible-Tool) Pressure Forming**
6. **Hydroclave / Hydro-Pressure Forming** (gas-assisted hybrid)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner's Hot Point**
> Out of the above, **Autoclave Molding** and **Pressure Bag Molding** carry the highest weightage in KTU university examinations for PENST742. Always draw a clear schematic (mold + laminate + bag + pressure medium) before writing the explanation — this fetches **1–2 bonus marks** in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

The successful application of gas pressure in composite forming is governed by three coupled physics phenomena. Each must be understood for proper KTU-level answers.

### 2.1.1 Isostatic Pressure Principle
A gas, unlike a unidirectional mechanical platen, transmits pressure **uniformly normal to every surface** (Pascal's Law). When applied to a laminate stack, this produces **equal compaction in all directions**, eliminating fiber-wash, bridging, and resin-rich pockets.

$$P_{\text{applied}} = P_{\text{consolidation}} = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{A_{\text{lay-up}}}$$

### 2.1.2 Darcy's Law for Resin Flow
During consolidation, the pressurized resin percolates through the fiber bed. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ follows Darcy's Law:

$$Q = -\frac{K \cdot A}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

Where:
- $K$ = permeability of the fiber preform ($\text{m}^2$)
- $A$ = laminate cross-sectional area ($\text{m}^2$)
- $\mu$ = resin dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure differential (Pa)
- $L$ = flow-path length (m)

### 2.1.3 Curing Kinetics
For thermoset resins (epoxy, vinylester, polyester) used in boats, the degree of cure $\alpha$ evolves as a function of temperature $T$ and time $t$. The simplified Kamal model used in KTU problems is:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$$

Where $k_1, k_2$ follow Arrhenius dependence on temperature, and $m, n$ are reaction orders.

## 2.2 Detailed Sub-Method Analysis

### 2.2.1 Autoclave Molding
**Workflow:**
1. Lay-up of prepreg plies on a rigid tool (mold)
2. Application of release film, bleeder, breather layers
3. Enclosure in a vacuum bag sealed with tacky tape
4. Initial vacuum compaction (~ 0.09 MPa atmospheric)
5. Loading into a pressure vessel (autoclave)
6. Application of simultaneous **heat** (typically **120 °C – 180 °C** for epoxy) and **gas pressure** (typically **0.5 MPa – 0.7 MPa**)
7. Hold under pressure during cure cycle (2–8 hours)
8. Cool-down and demold

> [!NOTE] **Why Autoclave Dominates Boat Hull Manufacturing**
> The combination of heat + pressure achieves: void elimination (voids collapse under isostatic load), fiber-volume fraction control ($V_f > 60\%$ easily attainable), and uniform cure. Most racing yachts, naval patrol boats, and high-performance defense vessels (including India's GRSE-built fast attack craft) use autoclave-cured composites.

### 2.2.2 Pressure Bag Molding
A flexible bag (silicone rubber or high-temperature polymer film) is placed over the laminate lay-up inside a rigid female mold. Pressurized gas (typically **0.3 MPa – 0.5 MPa**) inflates the bag, pressing the laminate against the mold wall. Used for:
- Smaller boat components (hatches, console panels)
- Concave-dished parts
- Prototype hull sections

### 2.2.3 Bladder (Internal Pressure) Molding
A reusable elastomeric bladder is inserted **inside** a hollow composite shell. Gas pressure (**0.4 MPa – 1.0 MPa**) inflates the bladder, expanding it against the inner surface of the part during cure. Commonly used for:
- Boat masts and spars
- Tubular structural members
- Closed-section stiffeners

### 2.2.4 Diaphragm Forming
A prepreg stack is sandwiched between two diaphragms (typically nylon or Upilex films). Gas pressure on one side forces the diaphragms to deform into a female mold cavity, while heat softens the resin for shaping. Used for **complex doubly-curved hull panels** in one-shot forming.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Gas pressure = Force / Area | Pa, MPa, bar |
| 2 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{composite}} - \rho_{\text{resin}}}{\rho_{\text{fiber}} - \rho_{\text{resin}}}$ | Fiber volume fraction rule of mixtures | dimensionless (–) |
| 3 | $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_r$ | Void volume fraction | dimensionless (–) |
| 4 | $Q = -\dfrac{K A \Delta P}{\mu L}$ | Darcy's resin flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $P_{\text{consol}} = P_{\text{ext}} - P_{\text{porous}}$ | Effective consolidation pressure | MPa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in bladder/pipe (for bladder design) | MPa |
| 7 | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{ramp}} + t_{\text{hold}} + t_{\text{cool}}$ | Total autoclave cycle time | minutes, hours |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{\alpha_E (T_{\text{cur}} - T_{\text{ref}})}{1 - \nu_E}$ | Residual thermal stress in cured laminate | MPa |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{applied}}}{P_{\text{design}}}$ | Pressure utilization factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_{\text{voids}}(\%) = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{th}} - \rho_{\text{meas}}}{\rho_{\text{th}}} \times 100$ | Porosity measurement | % |

> [!WARNING] **Markdown Safety Note**
> In the table above, vertical bars were replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` where absolute-value or modulus operations appear, to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. In your own KTU answer scripts, use $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value — never `|x|` inside a table cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Naval shipbuilding:** Autoclave-cured carbon/epoxy hulls for stealth corvettes (e.g., VISBY-class, TAPI-class derivative concepts).
- **Racing yachts:** America's Cup Class boats require autoclave-cured prepreg panels for minimum weight and maximum stiffness.
- **Coastal patrol boats:** Pressure-bag-molded glass/polyester for cost-effective mass production.
- **Offshore structures:** Bladder-molded tubular members for subsea risers and boat davits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Consolidation Pressure on a Laminate

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style):**
A boat-hull laminate is placed inside an autoclave. The autoclave pressurizes the laminate with nitrogen gas at $P_{\text{gas}} = 0.6 \text{ MPa}$. The vacuum bag underneath the laminate is held at $P_{\text{vac}} = 0.085 \text{ MPa}$ (absolute). Determine the **net consolidation pressure** acting on the laminate.

**Step 1: Identify the pressure on each face of the laminate.**
- Top face (under bag): experiences gas pressure $P_{\text{gas}}$
- Bottom face (against mold, sealed): experiences vacuum $P_{\text{vac}}$

**Step 2: Write the pressure balance.**
The net pressure pushing the laminate onto the mold is the **pressure differential** across its thickness:

$$P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{gas}} - P_{\text{vac}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**
The gas pressure is a **gauge** pressure relative to atmospheric. The vacuum is also referenced to atmospheric. So:

$$P_{\text{net}} = 0.600 \text{ MPa} - (-0.085) \text{ MPa}$$

Note the **sign convention**: a vacuum corresponds to a sub-atmospheric absolute pressure. When we express pressures as gauge values, vacuum is **negative**. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{net}} &= 0.600 - (-0.085) \\
&= 0.600 + 0.085 \\
&= 0.685 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Convert to other units for verification.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{net}} &= 0.685 \text{ MPa} \\
&= 0.685 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
&= 0.685 \times 10 \text{ bar} \\
&= 6.85 \text{ bar} \\
&\approx 99.4 \text{ psi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Physical interpretation.**
This net pressure of **0.685 MPa** is what actually compacts the laminate. This is why boat manufacturers specify both autoclave pressure AND vacuum level — the differential is what does the work.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Density and Void Content

**Problem Setup:**
A boat-deck laminate made of E-glass/epoxy has the following constituent properties:
- Fiber density $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Resin density $\rho_r = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Fiber weight fraction $W_f = 0.65$
- Measured composite density $\rho_c = 1.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$

Determine: (a) theoretical density $\rho_{\text{th}}$, (b) fiber volume fraction $V_f$, (c) void content $V_v$.

**Step 1: Compute theoretical density using rule of mixtures.**
The theoretical density (assuming zero voids) is the harmonic mean of component densities weighted by volume:

$$\rho_{\text{th}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{1 - W_f}{\rho_r}}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{th}} &= \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.65}{2.55} + \dfrac{0.35}{1.20}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.2549 + 0.2917} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.5466} \\
&\approx 1.829 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute fiber volume fraction.**
Using the relation $V_f = \dfrac{W_f \cdot \rho_c}{\rho_f}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \frac{0.65 \times 1.85}{2.55} \\
&= \frac{1.2025}{2.55} \\
&\approx 0.4716 \\
&\approx 47.16\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute void content.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_v &= \frac{\rho_{\text{th}} - \rho_c}{\rho_{\text{th}}} \\
&= \frac{1.829 - 1.850}{1.829} \\
&= \frac{-0.021}{1.829} \\
&\approx -0.0115
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**
A negative void content is physically impossible. The negative value here is within the experimental uncertainty of density measurement (typically $\pm 0.02 \text{ g/cm}^3$). In practice, this means the laminate has **negligible voids (effectively $< 0.5\%$)** — a hallmark of a well-executed gas-pressure-formed composite.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Autoclave Cycle Time Estimation

**Problem Setup:**
A 6 mm thick boat-hull laminate is being cured in an autoclave. The resin system requires a heat-up ramp of **2 °C/min** from room temperature (25 °C) to cure temperature (140 °C), a hold of **90 minutes** at 140 °C, and a cool-down ramp of **1.5 °C/min** to 60 °C. Compute total cycle time.

**Step 1: Time to heat up.**

$$t_{\text{ramp-up}} = \frac{T_{\text{cur}} - T_{\text{amb}}}{R_{\text{up}}} = \frac{140 - 25}{2} = 57.5 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2: Hold time at cure temperature.**

$$t_{\text{hold}} = 90 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3: Cool-down time.**

$$t_{\text{cool}} = \frac{T_{\text{cur}} - T_{\text{exit}}}{R_{\text{cool}}} = \frac{140 - 60}{1.5} = 53.33 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4: Total cycle time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{total}} &= t_{\text{ramp-up}} + t_{\text{hold}} + t_{\text{cool}} \\
&= 57.5 + 90 + 53.33 \\
&= 200.83 \text{ min} \\
&\approx 3 \text{ hours } 21 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Practical KTU addition.**
In an actual autoclave, add **15–30 minutes** for loading/unloading the boat panel, vacuum connection, and pressure stabilization. So practical cycle ≈ **3 hours 45 minutes to 4 hours**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bladder Hoop Stress in Internal Pressure Method

**Problem Setup:**
A cylindrical boat mast is formed using an internal elastomeric bladder of radius $r = 50 \text{ mm}$ and wall thickness $t_{\text{bl}} = 5 \text{ mm}$. The inflation pressure is $P = 0.8 \text{ MPa}$. Find the hoop stress in the bladder.

**Step 1: Apply thin-wall cylinder theory (Lamé's equation for thin walls).**

$$\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t_{\text{bl}}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{0.8 \times 50}{5} = 8.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Compare with bladder material allowable.**
Typical silicone bladder allowable $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 10 \text{ MPa}$. Since $8.0 \text{ MPa} < 10 \text{ MPa}$, the bladder is safe. **Safety factor** = $10 / 8 = 1.25$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Method | Typical Pressure | Heat Range | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autoclave | 0.5 – 0.7 MPa | 120 – 180 °C | Hulls, decks |
| Pressure Bag | 0.3 – 0.5 MPa | 25 – 80 °C | Panels, hatches |
| Bladder | 0.4 – 1.0 MPa | 80 – 150 °C | Masts, tubes |
| Diaphragm | 0.2 – 0.4 MPa | 100 – 200 °C | Doubly-curved hull panels |
| Hydroclave | 1.0 – 7.0 MPa | 120 – 180 °C | High-performance racing hulls |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Autoclave Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Lay up Prepreg Plies on Mold"] --> node2["Apply Release Film Bleeder Breather"]
    node2 --> node3["Enclose in Vacuum Bag Seal with Tacky Tape"]
    node3 --> node4["Connect Vacuum Pump Achieve 0.09 MPa Vacuum"]
    node4 --> node5["Load Assembly into Autoclave Vessel"]
    node5 --> node6["Close Autoclave Door Apply Safety Interlocks"]
    node6 --> node7["Ramp Temperature to Cure Set Point"]
    node7 --> node8["Ramp Pressure to Consolidation Level"]
    node8 --> node9["Hold During Cure Cycle 2 to 8 Hours"]
    node9 --> node10["Cool Down Controlled Ramp"]
    node10 --> node11["Release Pressure and Vacuum"]
    node11 --> node12["Remove Cured Composite Part from Autoclave"]
    node12 --> node13["Demold and Trim to Final Boat Component Geometry"]
    node13 --> node14["Quality Inspection Ultrasonic and Visual"]
```

## 4.2 Pressure Bag Molding — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Rigid Female Mold Cavity"] --> nodeB["Lay up Fiber Resin Stack"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Place Flexible Pressure Bag"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Seal Bag Periphery"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Connect Compressed Gas Line 0.3 to 0.5 MPa"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Inflate Bag to Compact Laminate"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Apply Heat via Oven or Heated Mold"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Cure and Demold Boat Component"]
```

## 4.3 Bladder (Internal Pressure) Molding — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX1["Prepare Prepreg Shell by Lay up on Male Mandrel"] --> nodeX2["Insert Elastomeric Bladder into Hollow Cavity"]
    nodeX2 --> nodeX3["Seal Bladder End Fittings"]
    nodeX3 --> nodeX4["Connect Gas Pressure Line 0.4 to 1.0 MPa"]
    nodeX4 --> nodeX5["Apply Heat for Resin Softening"]
    nodeX5 --> nodeX6["Inflate Bladder Expand Against Inner Shell"]
    nodeX6 --> nodeX7["Cure at Temperature and Pressure"]
    nodeX7 --> nodeX8["Deflate Bladder and Extract"]
    nodeX8 --> nodeX9["Finished Hollow Marine Component Mast Spar Tube"]
```

## 4.4 Diaphragm Forming — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeY1["Cut Prepreg Plies to Pattern"] --> nodeY2["Sandwich Between Two Diaphragm Films"]
    nodeY2 --> nodeY3["Place Stack on Female Mold"]
    nodeY3 --> nodeY4["Apply Heat to Soften Resin and Diaphragm"]
    nodeY4 --> nodeY5["Apply Gas Pressure on Top Side"]
    nodeY5 --> nodeY6["Diaphragm Drapes Prepreg into Mold Cavity"]
    nodeY6 --> nodeY7["Cure In Situ Under Continued Pressure"]
    nodeY7 --> nodeY8["Cool Release Pressure Remove Diaphragm"]
    nodeY8 --> nodeY9["Trim to Net Shape for Boat Panel"]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Pressure-Forming Selection Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Choose Gas Pressure Forming Method"] --> q1{"Part Size and Geometry?"}
    q1 -- "Large Doubly Curved Hull Shell" --> autoclave["Autoclave Molding"]
    q1 -- "Small Dished Component" --> presbag["Pressure Bag Molding"]
    q1 -- "Hollow Closed Section" --> blad["Bladder Molding"]

```

The above block diagrams can be re-drawn as physical layered schematics showing **mold → laminate → bag → pressure medium → heat source** in your answer book. The relationship between layers is what fetches full marks.
