<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define **autoclave molding** as applied to composite boat manufacturing. List any **three advantages** of the process for marine structural components.

**Model Answer:**
Autoclave molding is a gas-pressure forming technique in which a fiber-reinforced laminate enclosed in a vacuum bag is placed inside a pressure vessel (autoclave) and subjected simultaneously to elevated temperature and isostatic gas pressure (typically nitrogen at 0.5 – 0.7 MPa) to consolidate and cure the laminate into a finished composite boat component such as a hull shell or deck panel.

**Three advantages for marine structural components:**
1. **Low void content (< 1% achievable)** → superior fatigue life essential for wave-loaded hulls.
2. **High fiber-volume fraction (60–65%)** → high strength-to-weight ratio, critical for fast boats.
3. **Excellent surface finish on the tool-side** → reduced hydrodynamic drag on outer hull.

*(Examiner's note: Each advantage carries 1 mark; definition carries 1 mark.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Distinguish between **pressure bag molding** and **bladder molding** in the context of composite boat manufacturing. Mention one marine application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pressure Bag Molding | Bladder Molding |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure direction | External (gas pushes bag onto mold) | Internal (gas inflates bladder inside hollow part) |
| Typical pressure | 0.3 – 0.5 MPa | 0.4 – 1.0 MPa |
| Mold type | Rigid female mold | Male mandrel + hollow cavity |
| Application | Boat hatch covers, console panels | Boat masts, tubular spars |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model – Choice Option 1]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **step-by-step procedure** for autoclave molding of a 6 m long boat hull panel using carbon-fiber/epoxy prepreg. State the typical process parameters (pressure, temperature, dwell time).

**(b)** [7 Marks] A carbon/epoxy boat hull laminate is processed in an autoclave at a gas pressure of 0.7 MPa with vacuum drawn at 0.09 MPa below atmospheric. The prepreg has a fiber weight fraction of 0.62, fiber density of 1.80 g/cm³ and resin density of 1.25 g/cm³. The measured cured laminate density is 1.55 g/cm³. Calculate: (i) net consolidation pressure, (ii) theoretical density, (iii) fiber volume fraction, (iv) void content. Comment on the quality of the laminate.

**Model Solution to (a):**

The autoclave molding procedure for a boat hull panel comprises the following steps:

1. **Tool Preparation**: The aluminum or composite mold is cleaned, polished, and coated with release agent.
2. **Lay-up**: Carbon/epoxy prepreg plies are laid on the mold in the designed stacking sequence (e.g., [0/45/-45/90]ns) ensuring proper orientation for hull strength.
3. **Consumables Application**: Release film, peel ply, bleeder, and breather layers are placed over the lay-up to absorb excess resin and allow volatiles to escape.
4. **Vacuum Bagging**: A heat-resistant nylon or Upilex bag is sealed around the periphery using tacky tape. A vacuum line is connected.
5. **Pre-Cure Vacuum Compaction**: Vacuum of 0.09 MPa is drawn to remove entrapped air and pre-consolidate the stack.
6. **Loading into Autoclave**: The bagged assembly is placed inside the autoclave on a rigid rack.
7. **Cure Cycle**: Temperature is ramped at 1–3 °C/min to 140–180 °C; simultaneously, gas pressure is ramped to 0.5–0.7 MPa. Hold for 2–6 hours.
8. **Cool Down**: Controlled cool-down at 1–2 °C/min while maintaining pressure.
9. **Demold and Inspect**: After pressure release, the cured hull panel is demolded and subjected to NDT (ultrasonic, thermography).

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Net Consolidation Pressure**

$$P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{gas}} + P_{\text{vac}} = 0.70 + 0.09 = 0.79 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Theoretical Density**

$$\rho_{\text{th}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.62}{1.80} + \dfrac{0.38}{1.25}} = \frac{1}{0.3444 + 0.304} = \frac{1}{0.6484} \approx 1.542 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**(iii) Fiber Volume Fraction**

$$V_f = \frac{W_f \cdot \rho_c}{\rho_f} = \frac{0.62 \times 1.55}{1.80} = \frac{0.961}{1.80} \approx 0.534 \text{ or } 53.4\%$$

**(iv) Void Content**

$$V_v = \frac{\rho_{\text{th}} - \rho_c}{\rho_{\text{th}}} = \frac{1.542 - 1.55}{1.542} \approx -0.0052 \text{ (i.e., negligible)}$$

**Comment:** The negative void content indicates negligible porosity — an excellent laminate, typical of a properly executed autoclave cure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model – Choice Option 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **bladder molding process** for manufacturing a hollow composite boat mast. Discuss the design of the elastomeric bladder and the curing cycle.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An inflatable silicone bladder used for internal pressure molding of a boat mast has an inner diameter of 80 mm and a wall thickness of 6 mm. The inflation pressure is 1.0 MPa at a cure temperature of 150 °C. Calculate: (i) hoop stress in the bladder, (ii) longitudinal stress, (iii) factor of safety if the silicone tensile strength at 150 °C is 5.5 MPa.

**Model Solution to (a):**

A hollow composite boat mast is fabricated by first laying up carbon or glass prepreg plies around a male mandrel to form a hollow shell. An elastomeric silicone bladder is then inserted into the cavity. The bladder is sealed at both ends with end caps fitted with gas inlet valves. The assembly is placed in an oven or heated mold. Compressed gas (typically nitrogen) at **0.5 – 1.0 MPa** is introduced, inflating the bladder outward against the inner wall of the laminate shell. Simultaneously, the temperature is ramped to the resin cure temperature (e.g., **120 – 150 °C** for epoxy).

The bladder must be designed to withstand repeated thermal cycling, internal pressure, and chemical exposure to resin. Silicone rubber (typically 40 – 60 Shore A hardness) with reinforcement fabric is preferred. The bladder wall thickness is determined using thin-wall cylinder theory, with a safety factor of at least **2.5** to account for creep at elevated temperature.

After the cure cycle (typically 2–4 hours at peak temperature), the system is cooled below the glass transition temperature, the bladder is deflated and extracted, and the finished hollow mast is trimmed to length.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: Inner diameter $d = 80 \text{ mm}$, so $r = 40 \text{ mm}$. Wall thickness $t = 6 \text{ mm}$. Pressure $P = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$. Allowable $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 5.5 \text{ MPa}$ at 150 °C.

**(i) Hoop Stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot r}{t} = \frac{1.0 \times 40}{6} = 6.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Stress:**

$$\sigma_L = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t} = \frac{1.0 \times 40}{2 \times 6} = 3.333 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Factor of Safety:**

Critical stress is the higher hoop stress.

$$FoS = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{\sigma_h} = \frac{5.5}{6.667} \approx 0.825$$

Since $FoS < 1$, the bladder will **fail** under these conditions. The student should recommend either: (a) increasing wall thickness to **8 mm** (giving $\sigma_h = 5.0$ MPa and $FoS = 1.1$), or (b) reducing operating pressure to **0.8 MPa** (giving $\sigma_h = 5.33$ MPa and $FoS = 1.03$).

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure** — most students forget that vacuum is *negative gauge pressure* and add the values incorrectly. Always state: $P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{gas}} - P_{\text{vac (gauge)}}$.
> 2. **Sign error in void content calculation** — students often get confused when $\rho_{\text{measured}} > \rho_{\text{theoretical}}$. Interpret it as negligible voids, not negative voids.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — diameter and pressure must be in consistent units (mm and MPa work because 1 MPa = 1 N/mm²).
> 4. **Omitting the schematic** — in any 7-mark sub-question on forming methods, a labeled diagram is **mandatory** and fetches at least **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing up bladder molding with pressure bag molding** — bladder is *internal* pressure on a hollow part; pressure bag is *external* pressure on an open mold.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Gas pressure forming** consolidates composites by applying isostatic gas pressure (typically **0.1 – 7.0 MPa**) during cure.
- **Five principal methods**: autoclave, pressure bag, bladder, diaphragm, and hydroclave forming.
- **Autoclave molding** is the gold-standard for marine composites because it yields void content **< 1%** and $V_f$ up to **65%**.
- **Net consolidation pressure** = $P_{\text{gas}} - P_{\text{vac(gauge)}}$ — this is what compacts the laminate.
- **Darcy's Law** governs resin flow: $Q = -\frac{KA\Delta P}{\mu L}$.
- **Hoop stress in bladder** = $\frac{Pr}{t}$ (use thin-wall theory when $r/t \geq 10$).
- **Cycle time** = ramp-up + hold + cool-down + handling.
- **Rule of mixtures density** is used to compute theoretical density and void fraction.
- **Boat manufacturing preference**: autoclave for primary hull, pressure bag for secondary parts, bladder for tubular sections.
- **Always draw a schematic** showing mold, laminate, bag, and pressure source in your KTU answer.
- **Units discipline**: keep MPa–mm or Pa–m consistent; avoid mixing.
- **Safety factor**: minimum **1.25** for reusable bladders at elevated temperature is standard.
- **Class society rules (DNV, Lloyd's)** typically demand void content **< 2%** for primary hull structures — gas-pressure methods satisfy this routinely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Pressure Closed Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Low Pressure Closed Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Low Pressure Closed Moulding (LPCM)** is a family of advanced composite manufacturing processes in which dry fibre reinforcements are placed inside a sealed, two-sided mould cavity (rigid, semi-rigid, or flexible tool) and a catalysed liquid thermoset resin is introduced under a **pressure differential of less than 100 psi (≈ 7 bar)** — typically relying on either gravity, modest mechanical injection pressure, or atmospheric vacuum (≈ 1 atm or 14.7 psi) — to wet-out the preform, followed by an isothermal cure cycle and demoulding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> LPCM is a closed-mould composite fabrication technique where the reinforcement is enclosed between two mould surfaces and the resin is driven into the laminate by a *low energy input* (vacuum or low positive pressure), as opposed to high-pressure matched-die compression moulding or open-mould hand lay-up.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: The sponge-in-a-sealed-bag experiment.**
> Imagine sealing a dry kitchen sponge inside two transparent plastic sheets (the mould), leaving only a small inlet straw and an outlet straw. When you suck air out through the outlet (creating a vacuum), and pour coloured water (resin) through the inlet, the atmospheric pressure *pushes* the water uniformly into every pore of the sponge until it is completely soaked. Because the bag is sealed, no air bubbles are trapped, and the water distribution is consistent. That is exactly what happens in **Vacuum Infusion** for a boat hull — the dry fibreglass is the sponge, the sealed bag is the mould, and atmospheric pressure is the "pusher" that does the work.

**Why this matters in boat building:**
- A 12-metre yacht hull can be infused as **one single, monolithic, void-free laminate** — eliminating thousands of glued or fastened joints.
- Workers are **not exposed to volatile styrene fumes** because the mould is closed.
- Fibre-volume fraction (**V_f = 0.50 to 0.60**) is significantly higher than hand lay-up (0.30–0.40), giving a stiffer, lighter, stronger hull.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Resin injection pressure (VIP) | **−1 atm (vacuum) / 0 psi gauge** | bar / psi |
| Resin injection pressure (RTM) | **0.5 – 4.0 bar** | bar |
| Resin viscosity (epoxy, infusion grade) | **200 – 500 cP** | mPa·s |
| Resin viscosity (polyester, infusion grade) | **100 – 350 cP** | mPa·s |
| Fibre volume fraction (V_f) | **0.50 – 0.60** | dimensionless |
| Void content (target) | **< 1 %** | % |
| Tooling pressure differential (ΔP) | **1.0 × 10⁵ Pa** (≈ 1 atm) | Pa |
| Permeability of glass preform (k) | **1 × 10⁻⁹ to 1 × 10⁻¹¹** | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners specifically test these three numbers: **(i) ΔP = 1 atm for VIP**, **(ii) V_f > 0.50**, **(iii) Void content < 1 %** for a "good" LPCM laminate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of LPCM (Why It Works)

The entire physics of Low Pressure Closed Moulding rests on **three governing pillars**. A KTU answer that hits all three is a guaranteed full-marks answer.

### Pillar 1 — Darcy's Law of Flow Through Porous Media
The resin, once injected, flows through the **porous fibre bed** (preform). The flow is governed by **Henry Darcy's Law (1856)**. Because the preform is anisotropic, two permeabilities are used:
- **K₁** — in-plane permeability (parallel to fabric layers)
- **K₂** — transverse permeability (through fabric layers), usually K₂ ≪ K₁

### Pillar 2 — Pressure Differential (ΔP) as the Driving Force
Since this is *low-pressure* moulding, the driving force is intentionally limited. In **Vacuum Infusion**, the *only* driving force is **1 atm of atmospheric pressure** — the pump merely removes air; gravity and atmospheric load do the actual work of pushing the resin.

### Pillar 3 — Resin Rheology (Viscosity vs. Time)
A successful infusion requires the resin to **remain below ~500 cP** for the *entire duration* of the flow front travel — which on a 12 m hull can be **45 – 90 minutes**. This is achieved by:
- Using **room-temperature-cure** systems
- Formulating with **delayed-cure inhibitors** (e.g., MEKP systems with retarders)
- **Pre-heating the mould** to 30 – 40 °C to thin the resin

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary / Limiting Condition | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | Volumetric flow rate (m³/s) | k = permeability, ΔP = pressure drop, μ = viscosity | **Fill-rate prediction** |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{k \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \phi \cdot x}$ | Flow front velocity (m/s) | φ = porosity, x = flow front position | **Racing the gel time** |
| 3 | $x(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, k \, \Delta P \, t}{\mu \, \phi}}$ | 1-D flow front position (m) | Valid for constant ΔP and isothermal resin | **Hull infusion time** |
| 4 | $t_{fill} = \dfrac{\mu \, \phi \, L^{2}}{2 \, k \, \Delta P}$ | Total fill time (s) | L = flow length | **Process planning** |
| 5 | $V_{f} = \dfrac{\rho_{c} - \rho_{r}}{\rho_{f} - \rho_{r}}$ | Fibre volume fraction | ρ_c, ρ_r, ρ_f = composite, resin, fibre densities | **Quality control** |
| 6 | $\phi = 1 - V_{f}$ | Preform porosity | For unidirectional packing | **Required for (3) & (4)** |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{\mu_{0} \cdot L^{2}}{2 \, k \, \Delta P}$ | Time-to-gel constraint | μ₀ = initial viscosity | **Setting gel time** |
| 8 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, d_{f}}{\mu} < 1$ | Reynolds number check (laminar) | d_f = fibre diameter, ρ = resin density | **Validates Darcy regime** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners almost always test either **Formula (3)** or **Formula (4)** in a numerical problem. Memorise these two — they are the *high-yield* items.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility (Why Marine Industry Adopted LPCM)

1. **Naval Architects & Boat Builders** use LPCM to lay down monolithic hull-deck laminates with **predictable, repeatable mechanical properties** — critical for ISO 12215 structural scantling calculations.
2. **Defence Marine (Corvettes, Submersibles)** use SCRIMP/VARTM to fabricate stealth radomes and sonar domes because **void content < 1 %** ensures acoustic transparency.
3. **Wind-Energy & Aerospace (secondary)** use the same physics for wind-turbine blades and large aircraft fairings.
4. **Process Selection Logic:** If part area **> 25 m²** and **one-off/low-volume** → **VIP** is the lowest-cost-per-kg route. If part area **< 5 m²** and **medium volume (hundreds/year)** → **Light RTM** is faster cycle time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — 1-D Resin Flow Front Position (x(t))

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 Module 1. It is derived by combining the **continuity equation** with **Darcy's Law** for an incompressible fluid flowing through a rigid porous medium.

### Step 1: Start with Darcy's Law (Vector Form)

For an incompressible resin flowing through a porous preform:

$$\vec{v} = -\dfrac{k}{\mu} \, \nabla P$$

where $\vec{v}$ is the superficial (Darcy) velocity vector, $k$ is the permeability tensor, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity of the resin, and $\nabla P$ is the pressure gradient.

### Step 2: Apply Continuity (Conservation of Mass)

For an incompressible fluid, the divergence of velocity is zero:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

### Step 3: Combine — Laplace's Equation for Pressure

Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$\nabla \cdot \left( \dfrac{k}{\mu} \, \nabla P \right) = 0$$

Assuming **homogeneous, isotropic preform** and **constant μ** (isothermal), this simplifies to the **Laplace equation**:

$$\nabla^{2} P = 0$$

### Step 4: Reduce to 1-D Geometry (Resin flowing along the x-axis of a flat mould)

For 1-D flow, $\nabla^{2} P = \dfrac{d^{2}P}{dx^{2}} = 0$, so pressure varies **linearly** along the flow direction:

$$P(x) = P_{inlet} - \left( \dfrac{P_{inlet} - P_{outlet}}{L} \right) x$$

This gives a constant pressure gradient:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dx} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$$

### Step 5: Substitute into Darcy's Law (1-D form)

$$v = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = -\dfrac{k}{\mu} \, \dfrac{dP}{dx} = \dfrac{k \, \Delta P}{\mu \, L_{eff}}$$

where $L_{eff}$ is the current length of the wetted preform (which equals the flow-front position $x$ itself, because at $t=0$ the wetted length is 0).

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{k \, \Delta P}{\mu \, x}$$

### Step 6: Account for Porosity (φ)

Because only the *void space* of the preform accepts resin, the *actual* flow velocity through the pores is higher than the macroscopic Darcy velocity. The relation is:

$$v_{pore} = \dfrac{v_{Darcy}}{\phi} = \dfrac{1}{\phi} \, \dfrac{k \, \Delta P}{\mu \, x}$$

Thus the macroscopic flow-front advance equation becomes:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{k \, \Delta P}{\mu \, \phi \, x}$$

### Step 7: Separate Variables and Integrate

Rearranging:

$$x \, dx = \dfrac{k \, \Delta P}{\mu \, \phi} \, dt$$

Integrate from $x = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $x = x(t)$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{x} x' \, dx' = \dfrac{k \, \Delta P}{\mu \, \phi} \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

$$\dfrac{x^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{k \, \Delta P}{\mu \, \phi} \, t$$

### Step 8: Final Expression for Flow-Front Position

$$\boxed{\,x(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, k \, \Delta P \, t}{\mu \, \phi}}\,}$$

This is **Equation (3)** of the KTU Cheat Sheet. It is the equation KTU examiners quote as "the *infusion-flow front position* equation."

### Step 9: Derivation of Total Fill Time t_fill

If the mould cavity has total flow length $L$, set $x = L$ in the boxed equation and solve for $t$:

$$L^{2} = \dfrac{2 \, k \, \Delta P \, t_{fill}}{\mu \, \phi}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{fill} = \dfrac{\mu \, \phi \, L^{2}}{2 \, k \, \Delta P}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **quadratic dependence on L**: doubling the hull length *quadruples* the infusion time. This is why naval architects use **multiple resin inlet ports** along the length of large hulls to halve the effective $L$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Darcy's Law from First Principles (Capillary Bundle Model)

This derivation is sometimes asked in **Part B (14-mark) questions** to test the "Show that..." skill.

### Model Setup
Consider the preform as a bundle of $n$ parallel cylindrical capillaries, each of radius $r$, oriented along the flow direction.

### Step 1: Volumetric Flow Through a Single Capillary (Hagen-Poiseuille Equation)

For laminar, steady, incompressible flow through a circular pipe:

$$q_{1} = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

### Step 2: Total Flow Through n Capillaries

$$Q = n \, q_{1} = n \, \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

### Step 3: Porosity of the Bundle Model

The total cross-sectional area of the bundle is $A$. The void area is $n \cdot \pi r^{2}$. Therefore:

$$\phi = \dfrac{n \cdot \pi r^{2}}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \, \pi r^{2} = \phi A$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Simplify

$$Q = \dfrac{\phi A}{8 \mu L} \cdot r^{2} \, \Delta P = \dfrac{\phi A \, r^{2}}{8 \mu L} \, \Delta P$$

### Step 5: Identify Permeability

By **definition of permeability** in Darcy's Law ($Q = \dfrac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L}$), we equate coefficients:

$$k = \dfrac{\phi \, r^{2}}{8}$$

This is the **Kozeny-Carman-type** relation. It explains *physically* why:
- **Smaller fibres (smaller r)** → lower permeability → harder to infuse
- **Lower fibre volume fraction (higher φ)** → higher permeability → easier flow, but weaker laminate

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 6 m-long boat hull panel is to be infused using VIP. The preform has permeability $k = 5 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}^{2}$, porosity $\phi = 0.5$, and the resin viscosity is $\mu = 300 \ \text{cP}$. The vacuum pump provides a pressure differential of $\Delta P = 1.0 \times 10^{5} \ \text{Pa}$. Calculate the total fill time.

**Step 1: Convert Units**
- $\mu = 300 \ \text{cP} = 300 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa·s} = 0.30 \ \text{Pa·s}$
- $L = 6 \ \text{m}$, $k = 5 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}^{2}$, $\phi = 0.5$, $\Delta P = 1 \times 10^{5} \ \text{Pa}$

**Step 2: Apply the Fill-Time Equation**

$$t_{fill} = \dfrac{\mu \, \phi \, L^{2}}{2 \, k \, \Delta P}$$

$$t_{fill} = \dfrac{(0.30)(0.5)(6^{2})}{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{5})}$$

$$t_{fill} = \dfrac{(0.30)(0.5)(36)}{2 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{5.4}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{fill} = 5.4 \times 10^{4} \ \text{s} \approx 15 \ \text{hours}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A 15-hour fill time is **unacceptable for a real boat hull** — it means the resin would gel *long before* infusion completes. This is exactly why **multi-port injection** and **flow-media (net/grid)** are used in industry. KTU students are expected to *comment on the impracticality* of the calculated value and suggest a remedy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram A — Vacuum Infusion Process (VIP) — Step-by-Step Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the functional flow architecture of the **Vacuum Infusion Process** as it is practised in a boat-building yard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1 Prepare Rigid Hull Mould] --> A2[Step 2 Spray Gelcoat and Cure]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Lay-up Dry Fibreglass Plies]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Place Peel-Ply and Flow Media]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Lay Vacuum Bag and Seal with Tacky Tape]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Connect Resin Trap and Vacuum Pump]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Pull Full Vacuum and Leak Check]
    A7 --> A8{Leak Test Pass}
    A8 -- No --> A5
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Step 8 Open Resin Inlet Valve Resin Infusion Begins]
    A9 --> A10[Step 9 Monitor Resin Flow Front]
    A10 --> A11{Resin Reaches Outlet}
    A11 -- No --> A10
    A11 -- Yes --> A12[Step 10 Close Inlet Clamp Resin Line]
    A12 --> A13[Step 11 Maintain Vacuum and Cure at RT or Elevated T]
    A13 --> A14[Step 12 Demould and Trim]
    A14 --> A15[Finished Monolithic Composite Hull Panel]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Block-Level Architecture of a Closed-Mould Infusion Stack

This diagram shows the **layered cross-section** of an infusion stack — equivalent to a *peel-apart sandwich* — that goes into a boat hull mould.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[OUTER MOULD SURFACE Gelcoat Coated] --> L2[STRUCTURAL LAYER 1 Biaxial Glass 600 gsm]
    L2 --> L3[STRUCTURAL LAYER 2 Biaxial Glass 600 gsm]
    L3 --> L4[CORE LAYER PVC Foam or End-Grain Balsa]
    L4 --> L5[STRUCTURAL LAYER 3 Biaxial Glass 600 gsm]
    L5 --> L6[STRUCTURAL LAYER 4 Biaxial Glass 600 gsm]
    L6 --> L7[PEEL PLY Woven Nylon Release Fabric]
    L7 --> L8[FLOW MEDIA GreenFlow Infusion Net]
    L8 --> L9[VACUUM BAG Nylon Polyethylene 75 micron]
    L9 --> L10[SEAL TAPE Butyl Rubber Tacky Tape]
    L9 -. Resin inlet spiral tube .-> R1[RESIN SUPPLY HEADER]
    L9 -. Vacuum outlet spiral tube .-> R2[VACUUM PUMP and RESIN TRAP]
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Comparison of LPCM Variants (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Boat Component to be Built] --> Q2{Part Size}
    Q2 -- Less than 5 sqm --> Q3{Annual Volume}
    Q2 -- 5 to 25 sqm --> Q4{Annual Volume}
    Q2 -- More than 25 sqm --> Q5{Annual Volume}
    Q3 -- Less than 50 --> P1[Open Hand Lay-up]
    Q3 -- 50 to 500 --> P2[Light RTM]
    Q3 -- More than 500 --> P3[Matched-Die RTM]
    Q4 -- Less than 50 --> P4[Vacuum Infusion VIP]
    Q4 -- 50 to 500 --> P5[VARTM with Reusable Bag]
    Q4 -- More than 500 --> P6[SCRIMP]
    Q5 -- Less than 20 --> P7[VIP Single Hull Monolithic]
    Q5 -- 20 to 100 --> P8[VIP with Multi-Port Network]
    Q5 -- More than 100 --> P9[VARTM with Heated Tool]
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Physics of Resin Flow (1-D Infusion Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Resin Reservoir Open to Atmosphere 1 atm] --> R1[Spiral Inlet Tube]
    R1 --> R2[Preform Length x at Time t]
    R2 --> R3[Vacuum Outlet at -1 atm]
    R0 -- P equals 1 atm --> R2
    R3 -- P equals 0 atm --> R2
    R2 -- Driving Force deltaP = 1 atm --> R4[Flow Front Position x = sqrt of 2 k dt dP over mu phi]
    R4 --> R5[Fill Time t_fill = mu phi L squared over 2 k dP]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Low Pressure Closed Moulding (LPCM). List any four advantages of LPCM over open-mould hand lay-up in boat construction.**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

> **Definition:** Low Pressure Closed Moulding is a composite manufacturing process in which dry fibre reinforcements are sealed inside a two-sided mould cavity and impregnated with liquid thermoset resin using a pressure differential of less than 100 psi (typically vacuum of 1 atm or low mechanical pressure up to 4 bar), followed by cure and demoulding.
>
> **Four advantages over hand lay-up:**
> 1. **Higher fibre volume fraction** ($V_f$ = 0.50–0.60 vs 0.30–0.40) → stiffer, stronger, lighter hull.
> 2. **Lower void content** (< 1 % vs 3–5 %) → better fatigue life and water resistance.
> 3. **Reduced styrene emissions** → healthier workplace, compliant with IMO/OSHA limits.
> 4. **Closer dimensional tolerance** on both moulded surfaces → reduced fairing and finishing time.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) and Vacuum Infusion Process (VIP).**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | RTM | VIP (Vacuum Infusion) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mould type** | Rigid, two-part matched metal/composite mould | One rigid mould + one flexible vacuum bag |
| 2 | **Driving pressure** | Positive 0.5 – 4 bar from injection pump | Negative 1 atm from vacuum pump |
| 3 | **Resin injection** | Pressurised through inlet port | Drawn in by vacuum through inlet |
| 4 | **Cycle time** | 30 – 60 min (shorter) | 2 – 12 h (longer) |
| 5 | **Tooling cost** | High | Low |
| 6 | **Part size suitability** | Small to medium (≤ 5 m²) | Large (boat hulls > 25 m²) |

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the **Vacuum Infusion Process (VIP)** for fabricating a 10-metre boat hull. Support your answer with a labelled schematic of the infusion stack.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using Darcy's Law, derive the expression for the 1-D resin flow-front position $x(t)$ in a closed-mould infusion. A polyester resin with viscosity $\mu = 0.4 \ \text{Pa·s}$ is infused into a glass preform of permeability $k = 2 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}^{2}$ and porosity $\phi = 0.55$ under a pressure differential of $\Delta P = 0.9 \times 10^{5} \ \text{Pa}$. Find the time taken for the resin to reach a distance of 4 m.

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — VIP Procedure:**

1. **Mould preparation:** Clean the female hull mould; apply release agent and **gelcoat** (0.4 – 0.6 mm); cure gelcoat.
2. **Lay-up of dry reinforcement:** Stack structural plies of biaxial glass (e.g., 600 gsm) and core material (PVC foam) onto the gelcoat.
3. **Peel-ply and flow-media placement:** Lay **peel-ply** (perforated nylon) over the laminate, followed by a **flow-media net** that channels resin across the part.
4. **Bagging and sealing:** Place a **vacuum bag (75 µm nylon)** over the entire stack; seal perimeter with **tacky tape**.
5. **Connection of lines:** Connect a **resin-inlet spiral** to the resin reservoir; connect a **vacuum-outlet spiral** through a **resin trap** to a vacuum pump.
6. **Vacuum application and leak check:** Pull full vacuum; hold for 15 min; verify < 5 mbar/min leak rate.
7. **Resin infusion:** Open resin-inlet valve; resin is drawn by atmospheric pressure into the preform. Monitor flow front.
8. **Cure:** Once the entire part is wet, close the inlet and continue vacuum; cure at room temperature (or 40–50 °C post-cure).
9. **Demould and trim:** Remove vacuum bag, peel-ply, and trim flash.

**Schematic:** *(Refer to Diagram 4.2 in Section 4)*

**Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical:**

*Derivation is as in Section 3.1 of these notes. The final boxed equation is:*

$$x(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, k \, \Delta P \, t}{\mu \, \phi}}$$

*Solving for time t:*

$$t = \dfrac{x^{2} \, \mu \, \phi}{2 \, k \, \Delta P}$$

*Substituting values* ($x = 4$ m, $\mu = 0.4$ Pa·s, $\phi = 0.55$, $k = 2 \times 10^{-10}$ m², $\Delta P = 0.9 \times 10^{5}$ Pa):

$$t = \dfrac{(4)^{2} \cdot (0.4) \cdot (0.55)}{2 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot (0.9 \times 10^{5})}$$

$$t = \dfrac{16 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.55}{2 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.9 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{3.52}{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{\,t \approx 9.78 \times 10^{4} \ \text{s} \approx 27.2 \ \text{hours}\,}$$

*Engineering comment:* This fill time exceeds the typical 30–60 min gel time of polyester resin, indicating that **a single inlet at one end will fail**. The recommended industry solution is to **place 4 – 6 equispaced resin inlets along the hull length**, reducing effective L from 10 m to ~2 m, which would cut fill time to roughly **1.1 hours** (since $t \propto L^{2}$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Light RTM, VARTM, and SCRIMP** as low-pressure closed-mould processes for boat-building. Tabulate your answer in terms of mould type, pressure, typical boat application, fibre volume fraction, and cycle time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A boat manufacturer wishes to choose between **VIP** and **Light RTM** for a 4 m × 2 m small-craft hull. Discuss the techno-economic selection criteria and recommend the appropriate process with justification.

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Comparison Table:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Light RTM | VARTM | SCRIMP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mould type | Two rigid (composite) mould halves | One rigid mould + flexible bag | One rigid mould + flexible bag |
| 2 | Injection pressure | 1 – 4 bar (positive) | Vacuum + ≤ 1 bar positive | Vacuum (1 atm) only |
| 3 | Resin flow medium | Pressurised inlet | Channel in flow-media | Grooved rigid distribution medium |
| 4 | Typical boat application | Small dinghies, deck hatches | Medium hulls, superstructures | Large patrol-boat hulls |
| 5 | Fibre volume fraction V_f | 0.45 – 0.55 | 0.50 – 0.60 | 0.50 – 0.60 |
| 6 | Cycle time | 30 – 90 min | 2 – 6 h | 4 – 12 h |
| 7 | Tooling cost (index) | High (1.0) | Low (0.4) | Low (0.5) |

**Part (b) — Process Selection Discussion:**

*Selection criteria include:*
- **Annual production volume:** If < 30 hulls/year, VIP is preferred (low tooling cost dominates). If 30 – 200 hulls/year, Light RTM wins (faster cycle offsets higher tooling cost).
- **Capital investment:** VIP needs only one rigid mould + vacuum pump (≈ ₹ 4 Lakh). Light RTM needs two rigid moulds + injection machine (≈ ₹ 25 Lakh).
- **Surface finish requirements:** Light RTM gives **two Class-A moulded surfaces**; VIP gives one moulded surface only.
- **Labour skill:** VIP requires a *skilled laminator*; Light RTM is more *operator-friendly* but requires a trained injection-machine operator.
- **Part size:** 4 m × 2 m = 8 m² falls in the **overlap zone**.

*Recommendation:* For a **4 m × 2 m small-craft hull** produced in **low volume (< 30/year)** with **budget constraints**, **VIP is the recommended process** because:
1. Tooling cost is 60 % lower than Light RTM.
2. The 8 m² size is well within VIP's comfort range.
3. The single moulded surface is sufficient because the outer hull is later faired and painted anyway.
4. Workers are protected from styrene exposure, satisfying IMO regulations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> **(i)** In Part (a) numerical problems, **forgetting to convert viscosity from cP to Pa·s** is the #1 cause of lost marks. Always show unit conversion explicitly.
> **(ii)** In Part (b) comparisons, examiners **deduct 2 marks** if you write only the *names* of the processes without quoting the *numerical V_f* or *cycle time* — numbers sell the answer.
> **(iii)** Any answer that uses **"hand lay-up"** in a Low Pressure Moulding question will be **awarded zero**, because hand lay-up is *open-mould* by definition.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Boards-Prep Mode):**
> - **Definition:** LPCM = sealed mould + low pressure (≤ 100 psi / 7 bar) resin injection.
> - **Three governing equations to memorise verbatim:**
>   1. $Q = \dfrac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L}$
>   2. $x(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k \Delta P t}{\mu \phi}}$
>   3. $t_{fill} = \dfrac{\mu \phi L^{2}}{2 k \Delta P}$
> - **Standard numbers:** $V_f = 0.50$–$0.60$, void < 1 %, $\mu = 200$–$500$ cP, $\Delta P_{VIP} = 1$ atm.
> - **Three flagship processes:** **RTM** (rigid two-part, positive pressure), **VARTM** (one rigid + one flexible bag, low pressure), **SCRIMP** (one rigid + grooved distribution + vacuum only).
> - **Why LPCM is the *de facto* choice for boat hulls:** Monolithic, low void, high $V_f$, low styrene, low tooling cost, large-part capability.
> - **Square-law in L:** Doubling flow length quadruples fill time → use **multi-port inlets** on long hulls.
> - **Permeability anisotropy:** $K_1$ (in-plane) $\gg$ $K_2$ (transverse) → resin flows *between* plies first, then *through* them.
> - **Vacuum leak test rule:** Leak rate < **5 mbar/min** is the KTU-accepted threshold before infusion begins.
> - **Unit conversion traps:** 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s, 1 atm = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa, 1 gsm = 1 g/m².
> - **Gel-time constraint:** $t_{fill}$ *must* be **less than** the resin gel time, otherwise the resin will cure inside the mould and the part is scrapped.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pultrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pultrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pultrusion** is a continuous, automated, closed-mold manufacturing process for producing fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite profiles of constant cross-section. The term *pultrusion* is a portmanteau of **"pull"** and **"extrusion"**, signifying that the reinforcement is mechanically *pulled* (as opposed to *pushed* in extrusion) through a heated steel die where thermoset resin impregnation and curing take place simultaneously, yielding a cured, dimensionally stable structural profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PENST742):** Pultrusion is a continuous molding process in which dry reinforcing fibers are drawn (pulled) through a resin impregnation bath and then through a heated shaping die, wherein polymerization and consolidation occur to yield constant-cross-section composite profiles used in marine structural applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine making **continuous spaghetti, but with fiberglass instead of dough**. 

In a pasta machine, dough is *pushed* through a die to form long strands. Now flip the process: imagine long glass fibers (like thousands of thin, parallel fishing lines) being *pulled* through a tub of liquid plastic resin, then through a hot metal mold shaped like the desired boat part (say, an I-beam or a tube). The heat cures the plastic while the fiber bundle is still inside the mold, and what comes out the other end is a hard, lightweight, rock-solid structural shape that can run continuously for hundreds of meters before being cut into boat-frame pieces.

**Real-world marine analogy:** Every commercial GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic) boat you have seen on Kerala's backwaters or in Cochin shipyards has pultruded parts hidden inside — mast tubes, handrail stanchions, hull stringers, ladder rungs, and rudder stocks are commonly pultruded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Pultrusion Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Line speed:** typically **0.3 – 1.5 m/min** (slow, but continuous)
> - **Die temperature:** typically **120 °C – 180 °C** for polyester resins
> - **Resin systems:** Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Epoxy, Phenolic
> - **Fibers:** E-glass, S-glass, Carbon, Aramid (Kevlar)
> - **Typical fiber volume fraction (Vf):** **45 – 65 %** (much higher than hand lay-up's 25 – 35 %)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' — Step-by-Step Process Logic

Pultrusion is essentially a **7-stage linear manufacturing chain**. Each stage must be tightly controlled; any deviation compounds downstream defects.

1. **Creel / Roving Rack (Stage 1):** Continuous glass-fiber rovings (bundles of 4,000–16,000 filaments per roving) are mounted on a creel. *Why?* To feed thousands of parallel tension-controlled fibers into the line without snagging.
2. **Reinforcement Pre-Placement (Stage 2):** Continuous filament mats (CFM), woven rovings, veils, or surfacing veils are interleaved with the rovings. *Why?* Roving alone gives unidirectional strength; mats add transverse (cross-wise) strength needed in boat hulls.
3. **Resin Impregnation / Wet-Out Bath (Stage 3):** The dry fiber bundle is drawn through a tank of catalyzed thermoset resin (often heated to 25–40 °C for viscosity control). *Why?* To fully wet-out fibers and coat them with the polymer matrix.
4. **Pre-Former (Stage 4):** The wetted bundle passes through a series of forming guides that compress it toward the final die cross-section (removing excess resin and squeezing out air). *Why?* Pre-compaction prevents dry spots and voids in the cured profile.
5. **Heated Curing Die (Stage 5):** The heart of the process. The bundle enters a long, chrome-plated, electrically heated steel die (typically 0.6 – 1.5 m long). Inside, the resin gels, exotherms, and fully cures via free-radical polymerization (for polyester/vinyl ester) or addition reaction (for epoxy). *Why heated?* To trigger the cure reaction at controlled rate.
6. **Pulling Mechanism (Stage 6):** Reciprocating hydraulic clamps or caterpillar tracks grip the cured profile and pull it forward at constant line speed. *Why reciprocating?* The clamp releases, returns, and re-grips — providing continuous, non-slip traction.
7. **Cut-Off Saw (Stage 7):** An in-line abrasive or diamond saw cuts the continuous profile to specified lengths as commanded by the operator.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Pultrusion's Use in Boat Design

| Boat Design Need | How Pultrusion Solves It |
|---|---|
| High strength-to-weight ratio for masts/spars | Unidirectional rovings give **V_f up to 65 %** along load axis |
| Corrosion resistance in saltwater | FRP does not rust, ideal for hull stiffeners |
| Consistent, repeatable hull frame geometry | Closed-mold die ensures ±0.2 mm tolerance |
| Long continuous stringers without joints | Continuous process yields up to **kilometre-long** pieces |
| Fire/smoke compliance (e.g., lifeboat parts) | Phenolic or fire-retardant resin variants available |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Pultrusion & Composite Profile Design**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f}{A_c}$ | Fiber volume fraction; $A_f$ = fiber cross-section area, $A_c$ = total composite area | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f \, V_f}{\rho_f \, V_f + \rho_m \, (1-V_f)}$ | Weight fraction; $\rho_f$ fiber density, $\rho_m$ matrix density | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1-V_f)$ | **Rule of Mixtures** (longitudinal modulus); valid for pultruded UD profiles | GPa |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_{fT}} + \dfrac{(1-V_f)}{E_m}$ | Inverse Rule of Mixtures (transverse modulus) | $\text{GPa}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $t_{die} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{v_{line}}$ | Die residence / cure time; $L_{die}$ die length, $v_{line}$ pull speed | minutes |
| 6 | $Q_{cure} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + \Delta H_{rxn}$ | Heat balance in die; $m$ mass flow, $c_p$ specific heat, $\Delta H_{rxn}$ cure enthalpy | kW |
| 7 | $F_{pull} \geq \sigma_{friction} \cdot A_{wall} + F_{viscous}$ | Minimum pulling force; overcomes die-wall friction + viscous drag | N |
| 8 | $K = \dfrac{F_{pull}}{A_c \cdot v_{line}}$ | Pulling pressure index (process monitoring KPI) | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s/m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Equation 3 (Rule of Mixtures):** It is **highly accurate** for pultrusion because the process yields near-ideal unidirectional fiber alignment ($\eta_l \approx 0.95$–$1.0$), unlike hand lay-up where misalignment drops $\eta_l$ to 0.3–0.5.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Wing-spar stiffeners, helicopter rotor blade spars
- **Civil:** Bridge decks, rebar replacement, cooling tower structural members
- **Marine (your course):** Mast tubes, hull stringers, rudder posts, ladder rungs, handrails, transom stiffeners
- **Electrical:** Third-rail covers, ladder rails, cable trays
- **Sports:** Ski poles, arrow shafts, tent poles

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Longitudinal Modulus of a Pultruded Profile (Rule of Mixtures)

We start from the **iso-strain assumption**: in a unidirectional (UD) pultruded profile, both the fiber and the matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ along the pull direction because they are perfectly bonded and loaded in parallel (like resistors in parallel, but for stiffness).

**Step 1 — Load sharing in a UD composite element:**

Consider a small slice of the pultruded cross-section of total area $A_c$. The fiber occupies area $A_f = V_f \cdot A_c$ and the matrix occupies $A_m = (1 - V_f) \cdot A_c$.

The total axial load $P$ carried by the slice is the sum of the loads carried by the fiber and the matrix:

$$
P = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hooke's Law for each phase:**

Since $P = \sigma \cdot A = E \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A$, and iso-strain gives $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$:

$$
P = E_f \, \varepsilon \, A_f \; + \; E_m \, \varepsilon \, A_m
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out common strain:**

$$
P = \varepsilon \left( E_f \, A_f + E_m \, A_m \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Define effective composite modulus:**

By definition, $P = E_L \, \varepsilon \, A_c$, where $E_L$ is the longitudinal Young's modulus. Equating the two expressions for $P$:

$$
E_L \, A_c = E_f \, A_f + E_m \, A_m
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $A_c$ and substitute $V_f$:**

$$
E_L = E_f \, \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \, \dfrac{A_m}{A_c} = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)\,}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the same form as the parallel-spring analogy, because the fiber and matrix phases are mechanically *parallel* along the loading direction.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cure Time in the Heated Die (Residence Time)

The line speed and die length directly determine how long the resin is inside the heated die — this must equal or exceed the time required for the resin to fully gel and cure.

**Step 1 — Kinematic relation:**

If the line speed is $v_{line}$ (m/min) and the die length is $L_{die}$ (m), the time a given point on the profile spends inside the die is:

$$
t_{die} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{v_{line}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cure requirement:**

The resin manufacturer's data sheet specifies a **gel time** $t_{gel}$ and **demold / peak-exotherm time** $t_{cure}$ at a given die temperature $T_{die}$. For a defect-free profile, we require:

$$
t_{die} \geq t_{cure}(T_{die})
$$

**Step 3 — Solved worked example (typical KTU numerical):**

Given: Die length $L_{die} = 1.2$ m, line speed $v_{line} = 0.6$ m/min, and the resin system requires $t_{cure} = 1.8$ min at the set die temperature.

**Compute $t_{die}$:**

$$
t_{die} = \dfrac{1.2 \text{ m}}{0.6 \text{ m/min}} = 2.0 \text{ min}
$$

**Compare with requirement:**

$$
t_{die} = 2.0 \text{ min} \;\geq\; t_{cure} = 1.8 \text{ min} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The line speed is safe; the profile will be fully cured. If the line speed were raised to 0.8 m/min, then $t_{die} = 1.5$ min < 1.8 min, leading to under-cured, tacky profiles — a common practical defect.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Fiber Volume Fraction from Resin and Fiber Weights

In a pultrusion quality-control lab, the technician weighs the fiber input $W_f$ and the cured profile $W_c$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance:**

$$
W_c = W_f + W_m
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volumes using densities:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_c / \rho_c} = \dfrac{W_f \, \rho_c}{W_c \, \rho_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Composite density from rule of mixtures:**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example:**

Given: $W_f = 1.20$ kg, $W_c = 1.85$ kg, $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (E-glass), $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (polyester).

**Convert weights to volume shares (mass fraction first):**

$$
m_f = \dfrac{1.20}{1.85} = 0.6486
$$

**Convert mass fraction to volume fraction (using density ratio):**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{m_f / \rho_f}{m_f / \rho_f + (1 - m_f) / \rho_m}
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.6486 / 2.55}{0.6486 / 2.55 \; + \; 0.3514 / 1.20}
$$

**Compute numerator and denominator:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.2544}{0.2544 + 0.2928} = \dfrac{0.2544}{0.5472}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_f \approx 0.465 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 46.5\,\%\,}
$$

This falls within the typical pultrusion range of **45 – 65 %**, confirming a well-tuned resin bath.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pultrusion vs. Other Composite Processes

| Parameter | Hand Lay-Up | Filament Winding | **Pultrusion** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fiber volume fraction | 25 – 35 % | 50 – 65 % | **45 – 65 %** |
| Production rate | Slow (manual) | Medium | **Very high (continuous)** |
| Part geometry | Any complex shape | Axisymmetric only | **Constant cross-section only** |
| Fiber alignment control | Poor | Excellent (hoop + helical) | **Excellent (UD)** |
| Capital cost | Low | High | **High** |
| Typical boat application | Hull shell | Pressure hull, tanks | **Masts, stringers, stiffeners** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Pultrusion Line

The diagram below maps the functional flow of reinforcements and materials through the seven stages of a pultrusion line, including the feedback/control loop used by modern CNC-controlled systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CREEL RACK\nMulti-end roving spools] --> B[GUIDE PLATES\nFiber alignment & tensioning]
    B --> C[REINFORCEMENT FEED\nCFM mat + woven roving + veil]
    C --> D[RESIN IMPREGNATION BATH\nCatalyzed thermoset resin]
    D --> E[PRE-FORMER STATION\nCompaction & air removal]
    E --> F[HEATED CURING DIE\n120 to 180 degree C controlled]
    F --> G[PULLER UNIT\nReciprocating clamps or caterpillar]
    G --> H[CUT-OFF SAW\nIn-line abrasive saw]
    H --> I[FINISHED PROFILE\nStacking & QC station]
    F -. Temperature and cure feedback .-> J[CONTROL CONSOLE\nPLC and SCADA]
    G -. Pull force telemetry .-> J
    J -. Speed and clamp actuation .-> G
    J -. Die heater power .-> F
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Cross-Section of a Typical Pultrusion Die (Tapered)

The heated die has a **tapered entrance** to accommodate resin volume shrinkage during cure (typically 4 – 7 % volumetric shrinkage for polyester). A simplified cross-section flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DIE_BLOCK["HEATED STEEL DIE BLOCK"]
        direction TB
        ENTRY["ENTRANCE BELL\nLarger cross-section\nto allow wet fibers in"]
        TAPER["TAPERED LAND\nCompensates resin shrinkage\nAngle 1 to 3 degrees"]
        LAND["STRAIGHT LAND\nCuring section\n0.6 to 1.5 m long"]
        EXIT["EXIT LAND\nFinal dimensional control\nChrome plated"]
        ENTRY --> TAPER --> LAND --> EXIT
    end
    HEATER["Electric cartridge heaters\nembedded in die walls"] -. Heat flux .-> DIE_BLOCK
    COOLER["Cooling section\nair or water spray"] --> EXIT
```

### 4.3 Process Control Feedback Loop (Modern Pultrusion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[DIE TEMP SENSOR\nThermocouple] --> PLC
    S2[LINE SPEED SENSOR\nEncoder] --> PLC
    S3[PULL FORCE LOAD CELL] --> PLC
    PLC[PLC CONTROLLER\nPID loops active] --> A1[Die heater driver]
    PLC --> A2[Puller hydraulic servo]
    PLC --> A3[Saw trigger actuator]
    HMI[OPERATOR HMI SCREEN\nSCADA dashboard] --- PLC
    DB[QUALITY DATABASE\nPull force, temp, cure logs] --- PLC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define pultrusion. Name any two reinforcement materials and two resin systems commonly used in pultrusion for marine applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Pultrusion is a continuous manufacturing process in which dry reinforcing fibers are pulled through a resin impregnation bath and then through a heated shaping die, where the resin cures to produce a constant cross-section fiber-reinforced polymer composite profile.

**Reinforcement materials (1 mark — any two):**
1. E-glass rovings
2. Carbon fiber tows
3. Aramid (Kevlar) fibers
4. Continuous filament mat (CFM)

**Resin systems (1 mark — any two):**
1. Unsaturated polyester resin (UPR)
2. Vinyl ester resin
3. Epoxy resin
4. Phenolic resin (for fire-retardant marine applications)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*List any three advantages of pultrusion over hand lay-up in boat component manufacturing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher and more consistent fiber volume fraction (45–65 %)** versus hand lay-up (25–35 %), giving superior specific strength.
2. **Continuous, automated production** yields long, joint-free profiles (stringers, masts) with uniform mechanical properties.
3. **Lower long-run labor cost and higher repeatability** because the closed die guarantees consistent cross-sectional dimensions (±0.2 mm), unlike manual lay-up variability.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **pultrusion process** in detail, listing the function of **each station** from creel to cut-off saw. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pultrusion line has a die of length **1.0 m** and is running at a line speed of **0.5 m/min**. The polyester resin used requires a **cure time of 2.2 min** at the operating die temperature. **(i)** Calculate the die residence time. **(ii)** State whether the profile will be fully cured. **(iii)** If the manufacturer wants to increase production rate by 25 %, what is the maximum allowable line speed? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Process Explanation (7 marks — 1 mark per key point + 1 for diagram):**

A pultrusion line is a continuous, seven-station manufacturing system:

1. **Creel (1 mark):** Holds multiple spools of continuous rovings (typically 24–60 ends). Tensioners maintain uniform fiber tension.
2. **Reinforcement guide and pre-placement (1 mark):** Continuous filament mats, woven rovings, and surface veils are interleaved with rovings to give multi-directional strength.
3. **Resin bath (1 mark):** Fibers pass through a tank of catalyzed thermoset resin (polyester/vinyl ester at 25–40 °C). Resin viscosity and bath residence time are controlled.
4. **Pre-former (1 mark):** Squeezes out excess resin and compacts the bundle toward the final die shape, removing trapped air and preventing dry spots.
5. **Heated die (1 mark):** Chrome-plated steel die (0.6–1.5 m long) at 120–180 °C. The resin gels, exotherms, and fully cures. The die has a tapered entrance to compensate for cure shrinkage.
6. **Puller (1 mark):** Reciprocating hydraulic clamps or caterpillar tracks pull the cured profile at a constant line speed (0.3–1.5 m/min). A load cell monitors pull force in real time.
7. **Cut-off saw (1 mark):** Abrasive or diamond saw cuts the profile to specified lengths automatically.

**Block diagram (drawn by student; key feature):** Linear left-to-right flow of seven stages with resin bath marked separately.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L_{die} = 1.0$ m
- $v_{line} = 0.5$ m/min
- $t_{cure} = 2.2$ min

**(i) Die residence time (3 marks):**

$$
t_{die} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{v_{line}} = \dfrac{1.0 \text{ m}}{0.5 \text{ m/min}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{die} = 2.0 \text{ min}\,}
$$

**(ii) Is the profile fully cured? (2 marks):**

Compare $t_{die}$ with $t_{cure}$:

$$
t_{die} = 2.0 \text{ min} \;<\; t_{cure} = 2.2 \text{ min}
$$

**Conclusion:** The residence time is **less than** the required cure time, so the profile will be **under-cured (tacky, low mechanical strength)** at the given line speed. The line speed must be reduced, or the die temperature must be raised to accelerate cure.

**(iii) Maximum allowable line speed for 25 % production increase (2 marks):**

First, find the **maximum line speed** that still satisfies the cure constraint $t_{die} \geq 2.2$ min:

$$
v_{line,\,max} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{t_{cure}} = \dfrac{1.0 \text{ m}}{2.2 \text{ min}} \approx 0.4545 \text{ m/min}
$$

Now, **25 % increase in current production rate** means the *target* line speed is:

$$
v_{target} = 1.25 \times 0.5 = 0.625 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
v_{target} = 0.625 \text{ m/min} \;>\; v_{line,\,max} = 0.4545 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 25 % production-rate increase is **NOT feasible** at the current die length and resin system. To achieve 0.625 m/min, the manufacturer must either:
- Extend the die to at least $L_{die} = 0.625 \times 2.2 = 1.375$ m, or
- Switch to a faster-curing resin system, or
- Raise the die temperature to reduce $t_{cure}$ below 1.6 min.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** What is the **Rule of Mixtures** for a unidirectional pultruded composite? Derive the expression for the **longitudinal Young's modulus** $E_L$ in terms of fiber and matrix moduli and the fiber volume fraction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pultruded GRP (glass-reinforced polyester) boat mast has a fiber volume fraction of **0.55**. Given: $E_f = 72$ GPa (E-glass), $E_m = 3.5$ GPa (polyester). Calculate: **(i)** the longitudinal modulus $E_L$ of the mast, **(ii)** the mass fraction of fiber $W_f$ (using $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³), and **(iii)** the longitudinal tensile strength if the fiber strength is 2400 MPa and matrix strength is 55 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Derivation of $E_L$ (7 marks — 1 for statement, 1 for iso-strain, 2 for load balance, 2 for algebra, 1 for final boxed answer):**

The **Rule of Mixtures** is a weighted-average formula for composite properties based on the volumetric contribution of each constituent. For longitudinal modulus:

**Assumption — Iso-strain (1 mark):**
Both the fiber and the matrix phases experience the same axial strain $\varepsilon$ under tensile load along the fiber direction, because they are perfectly bonded and deform as a parallel mechanical system.

**Load balance (2 marks):**
For a small slice of the composite cross-section:

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

Using Hooke's law with iso-strain $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_c = \varepsilon$:

$$
E_L \, \varepsilon \, A_c = E_f \, \varepsilon \, A_f + E_m \, \varepsilon \, A_m
$$

**Algebra (2 marks):**
Divide both sides by $A_c$ and $\varepsilon$:

$$
E_L = E_f \left(\dfrac{A_f}{A_c}\right) + E_m \left(\dfrac{A_m}{A_c}\right)
$$

Substitute $A_f/A_c = V_f$ and $A_m/A_c = (1 - V_f)$:

$$
\boxed{\,E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)\,}
$$

**Final statement (1 mark):**
This is the longitudinal Rule of Mixtures, accurate for pultruded UD composites because of their near-perfect fiber alignment.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³
- $\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 55$ MPa

**(i) Longitudinal modulus (2 marks):**

$$
E_L = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(1 - 0.55)
$$

$$
E_L = 39.60 + 3.5 \times 0.45 = 39.60 + 1.575
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_L = 41.175 \text{ GPa}\,}
$$

**(ii) Mass (weight) fraction of fiber (3 marks):**

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$
\rho_f V_f = 2.55 \times 0.55 = 1.4025
$$

**Compute denominator term 2:**

$$
\rho_m (1 - V_f) = 1.20 \times 0.45 = 0.540
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
W_f = \dfrac{1.4025}{1.4025 + 0.540} = \dfrac{1.4025}{1.9425}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W_f \approx 0.7220 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 72.20\,\%\,}
$$

**(iii) Longitudinal tensile strength (2 marks):**

Using Rule of Mixtures for strength (valid for UD, brittle-fiber, brittle-matrix):

$$
\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)
$$

$$
\sigma_L = (2400)(0.55) + (55)(0.45)
$$

$$
\sigma_L = 1320 + 24.75
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_L = 1344.75 \text{ MPa}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Confusing line speed units:** Many students mix m/min and mm/s. KTU expects m/min. Convert 1 m/s = **60 m/min** before solving.
> 2. **Forgetting the iso-strain assumption** when writing the derivation. Examiners deduct 2 marks if it is not explicitly stated.
> 3. **Mixing $V_f$ and $W_f$** in formulas. A classic error is using $V_f$ in the mass-fraction equation. Always check which fraction the equation requires.
> 4. **Pultrusion ≠ Filament Winding:** Do not interchange the diagrams. Pultrusion has a die; filament winding has a rotating mandrel.
> 5. **Cure time vs. die residence time:** A common 1-mark loss is failing to state whether the profile is fully cured after the numerical comparison.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pultrusion = Pull + Extrusion** — fibers are *pulled* (not pushed) through a heated die.
- ✅ **Seven stages:** Creel → Guide → Resin Bath → Pre-former → Heated Die → Puller → Cut-off Saw.
- ✅ **Typical process parameters:** Line speed 0.3–1.5 m/min; die temperature 120–180 °C; die length 0.6–1.5 m.
- ✅ **V_f range = 45–65 %** — much higher than hand lay-up (25–35 %).
- ✅ **Common marine parts:** Mast tubes, hull stringers, transom stiffeners, handrail stanchions, rudder stocks, ladder rungs.
- ✅ **Resin systems:** Polyester (cheap, common), Vinyl ester (marine-grade, chemical-resistant), Epoxy (high strength, premium boats), Phenolic (fire-retardant).
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize cold:**
  - $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ (Rule of Mixtures)
  - $E_T$ from inverse rule of mixtures
  - $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}$
  - $t_{die} = L_{die} / v_{line}$ (must exceed $t_{cure}$)
- ✅ **Process defect signatures:** Under-cure (tacky, dark patches) → too high line speed; voids → poor pre-former setup; delamination → wet-out issue; die-stick (excessive pull force) → over-tapered die or resin starved.
- ✅ **Compared to hand lay-up:** pultrusion gives higher V_f, lower labor cost, but is limited to **constant cross-section** parts.
- ✅ **Compared to filament winding:** pultrusion is for **linear prismatic** parts; filament winding is for **axisymmetric** parts (pipes, tanks, pressure hulls).
- ✅ **Die design rule:** Tapered entrance angle of 1°–3° compensates for 4–7 % volumetric cure shrinkage of polyester.
- ✅ **KTU favourite exam question pattern:** "Calculate $t_{die}$, comment on cure, and find max line speed" — practice this style.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filament Winding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Filament Winding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Filament Winding** is an advanced, automated composite manufacturing process in which continuous reinforcing fibers (filaments, tows, or rovings) pre-impregnated with a thermosetting resin system are wound under controlled tension over a rotating mandrel in precisely defined geometric patterns to produce high-strength, hollow, axisymmetric or non-axisymmetric composite structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Pearl:** The process is defined by *three* mandatory simultaneous actions — (1) **continuous fiber delivery**, (2) **resin impregnation/wetting**, and (3) **winding over a mandrel at a controlled helical/geodesic angle α (alpha)**.

The process is governed by the standard **ASTM D2291** (mandrel preparation) and **ASTM D5421** (winding pattern specification), with typical production speeds ranging from **30–120 m/min** for marine and pressure vessel applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Candy Cane on a Spinning Rod"

Imagine you are wrapping a candy cane stripe around a slowly rotating wooden dowel (the mandrel) while slowly walking the candy parallel to the rod's length:

- The **rotating dowel** is analogous to the **mandrel rotation (ω, rad/s)**.
- Your **walking speed** is the **fiber payout/traverse velocity (v, m/s)**.
- The **angle** at which you lay the candy stripe is the **winding angle (α)**.
- The **stickiness of the candy** represents the **resin viscosity (η, Pa·s)**.

If you walk too fast relative to the rotation, the candy stripe becomes nearly straight (low α ≈ 0°, a *hoop* pattern). If you walk slowly, the candy wraps tightly at a steep angle (high α ≈ 90°, an *axial/helical* pattern). The angle is your **design freedom** — you choose the strength direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Application Note:** In composite boat hulls and masts, α ≈ 45° (helical) gives quasi-isotropic in-plane stiffness, which is critical for resisting biaxial wave-loads in marine service per **ISO 12215-5** scantling rules.

## 1.3 Classification of Filament Winding (Process Taxonomy)

| Process Variant | Resin Delivery | Cure State at Laydown | Typical Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wet Winding** | Fiber passes through a resin bath just before mandrel contact | Liquid (B-stage: uncured) | Boat hulls, large tanks |
| **Dry Winding** | Prepreg tow (B-staged resin on fiber) is wound | Partially cured (B-stage) | Aerospace, masts |
| **Polar Winding** | Fiber passes through a "polar" eye near the end of the mandrel | Wet or prepreg | Closed-end pressure bottles |
| **Helical Winding** | Multi-angle helical pattern via carriage reciprocation | Wet or prepreg | Pipes, driveshafts |
| **Hoop Winding** | Nearly circumferential (α ≈ 90°) | Wet | Reinforcement of cylindrical sections |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Winding Geometry — Step-by-Step Logic

A filament-wound surface is governed by a single kinematic relationship between the **mandrel rotation (ω)** and the **carriage/fiber-payout traverse velocity (v)** at the **mandrel radius (R)**.

### Step 1 — Establish the Reference Frame
- The mandrel rotates about its longitudinal axis (Z).
- The fiber-payout carriage translates parallel to Z at velocity **v**.
- The contact point traces a helix on the cylinder.

### Step 2 — Define the Instantaneous Winding Angle α
At any instant, the fiber touches the cylinder at angle α measured from the **generatrix (axial/hoop line)**:

- α → 0° → purely **circumferential (hoop)** reinforcement → resists hoop/burst stress.
- α → 90° → purely **axial** reinforcement → resists end-cap/bending loads.
- α → 55° (for closed pressure vessels) → optimal **netting analysis** angle for equal biaxial stress (Tsai classical result).

### Step 3 — Connect Kinematics to Geometry
The fiber path on a cylinder of radius R follows:

$$
\tan(\alpha) \;=\; \frac{v}{\omega R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A *small* numerator (slow traverse) + *large* denominator (fast rotation, large radius) yields small α — the fiber hugs the equator (hoop). Inverting this gives axial winding.

### Step 4 — Band Width and Coverage
The fiber tow has finite width **b** (typically 3–25 mm). As it winds, adjacent bands must overlap or abut to give **100% coverage** without gaps. The number of circuits **N** required to cover a single band position is:

$$
N \;=\; \frac{b}{\pi D \cos(\alpha)}
$$

where **D = 2R** is the mandrel diameter.

## 2.2 Geodesic vs. Isotensoid Winding — The Two Holy Grails

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Examiners frequently ask the *difference* between **Geodesic** and **Isotensoid** paths. Memorize the table below.

| Property | Geodesic Winding | Isotensoid Winding |
|---|---|---|
| **Fiber tension** | Variable along the path | Constant (iso-tension) |
| **Path shape** | Shortest path on the surface (Fermat principle) | Non-geodesic, controlled by carriage |
| **Mathematical form** | Clairaut's relation: $R \sin(\alpha) = \text{const}$ | Numerical/iterative (Lubin's solution) |
| **Implementation** | Open path, simpler mechanics | Closed-form non-geodesic, complex programming |
| **Use case** | Pressure vessels, pipes | Rocket motor casings, high-performance marine |

### Clairaut's Relation (Geodesic)
For geodesic winding on any surface of revolution:

$$
R(x) \cdot \sin(\alpha(x)) \;=\; R_0 \sin(\alpha_0) \;=\; k \; (\text{constant})
$$

where **x** is the axial coordinate. This is the **classical Clairaut differential relation** for geodesic curves on surfaces of revolution.

## 2.3 Netting Analysis — Classical Stress Prediction

For a closed-end filament-wound pressure vessel, classical **netting analysis** (ignoring matrix stiffness) gives the relationship between winding angle, internal pressure **p**, and fiber stress **σ_f**:

$$
\sigma_f \;=\; \frac{pR}{2 t \cos^2(\alpha)}
$$

where:
- **p** = internal gauge pressure (Pa)
- **R** = vessel inner radius (m)
- **t** = laminate total thickness (m)
- **α** = winding angle
- **σ_f** = tensile stress in the fiber

> [!WARNING]
> **Netting Analysis Limitation:** This analysis assumes the **matrix carries NO load** (pure fiber network). It is conservative and only accurate at high fiber volume fractions (V_f > 0.55). KTU questions often ask when netting fails — answer: at low V_f or when matrix cracking precedes fiber failure.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of numerical Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{v}{\omega R}$ | Kinematic winding angle | (rad or deg) |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{b}{\pi D \cos(\alpha)}$ | Band coverage circuits | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R \sin(\alpha) = k$ | Clairaut's geodesic invariant | m |
| 4 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{pR}{2t\cos^2(\alpha)}$ | Netting hoop-fiber stress | Pa |
| 5 | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{pR}{2t} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \tan^2(\alpha)}{\tan^2(\alpha) + 1}$ | Netting axial-fiber stress | Pa |
| 6 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m \, W_f}{\rho_f \, t - \rho_m \, W_f + \rho_f \, W_f}$ | Fiber volume fraction from areal weight W_f | dimensionless |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{N \cdot \rho_{\text{areal}}}{\rho_f}$ | Thickness per ply from areal density | m |
| 8 | $\Delta V_{\text{resin}} = \dfrac{\pi D L \, (1 - V_f)}{V_f}$ | Resin volume for a cylindrical laminate | m³ |

## 2.5 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

In boat-building, filament-wound components include:
- **Masts and spars** — high α for bending stiffness.
- **Propeller shafts** — α ≈ ±55° for balanced torque/bending.
- **Hull structural frames** — α near 90° (hoop) for radial hydrostatic load.
- **Sonar domes** — spherical filament winding (radial anisotropy).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kinematic Winding Angle from First Principles

**Goal:** Starting only from the geometry of a helix on a cylinder, derive $\tan(\alpha) = v/(\omega R)$.

### Step A — Parametrize the Helix
A point on the cylinder surface traces a helix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\tau) &= R \cos(\omega \tau) \\
y(\tau) &= R \sin(\omega \tau) \\
z(\tau) &= v \tau
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, **τ (tau)** is the time parameter (s), **R** the mandrel radius, **ω** the angular velocity, and **v** the axial traverse velocity.

### Step B — Compute the Tangent Vector
Differentiating each component with respect to τ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(\tau) &= -R\omega \sin(\omega \tau) \\
\dot{y}(\tau) &= R\omega \cos(\omega \tau) \\
\dot{z}(\tau) &= v
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Decompose into Hoop and Axial Components
The **hoop component** magnitude (in the xy-plane) is:

$$
v_{\text{hoop}} = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2} = \sqrt{R^2\omega^2\sin^2(\omega\tau) + R^2\omega^2\cos^2(\omega\tau)} = R\omega
$$

The **axial component** magnitude (along z) is simply:

$$
v_{\text{axial}} = \dot{z} = v
$$

### Step D — Form the Winding Angle
By definition, the winding angle α is the angle between the fiber tangent and the **hoop (circumferential) direction**:

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{axial component}}{\text{hoop component}} = \frac{v}{R\omega}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\tan(\alpha) \;=\; \frac{v}{\omega R}\;}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** To double α (e.g., from 30° to 60°), you must increase the traverse-to-rotation ratio by $\tan(60°)/\tan(30°) = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Clairaut's Relation for Geodesic Winding

**Goal:** Show that on a surface of revolution, the geodesic path obeys $R \sin(\alpha) = k$.

### Step A — Define the Surface of Revolution
The meridian curve is $R(z)$ with arc-length element $ds_m = \sqrt{1 + (dR/dz)^2}\, dz$. A general point on the surface has:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= R(z)\cos(\theta) \\
y &= R(z)\sin(\theta) \\
z &= z
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Express Geodesic Condition via Calculus of Variations
For a geodesic, the integral $\int ds = \int \sqrt{(R\, d\theta)^2 + (ds_m)^2}$ must be extremized. Using **Euler–Lagrange** with $\theta$ as cyclic coordinate (no explicit θ in the integrand):

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = \text{constant}
$$

where $\mathcal{L} = \sqrt{R^2 \dot{\theta}^2 + \dot{s}_m^{\,2}}$ and dots denote derivatives with respect to **s_m**.

### Step C — Evaluate the Conserved Quantity

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = \frac{R^2 \dot{\theta}}{\sqrt{R^2 \dot{\theta}^2 + \dot{s}_m^{\,2}}} = \text{const} = R \sin(\alpha)
$$

Note that $\sin(\alpha)$ is the geometric ratio of the circumferential component to the total path speed.

### Step D — State Clairaut's Theorem
Therefore, on any surface of revolution, along a geodesic:

$$
\boxed{\;R(z)\,\sin\!\bigl(\alpha(z)\bigr) \;=\; k \;=\; R_0 \sin(\alpha_0)\;}
$$

This means: as the geodesic moves toward a smaller radius, the winding angle **increases** (sin α grows to keep R·sin α constant). At the **equator** (maximum R), α is **minimum** — the fiber is closest to hoop direction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Netting Analysis Hoop Stress

**Goal:** Derive $\sigma_f = \dfrac{pR}{2 t \cos^2(\alpha)}$ for a closed-end filament-wound cylinder.

### Step A — Free-Body Cut
Cut the cylinder with a diametral plane. The internal pressure p acts on the projected area **2RL**, giving an axial tensile force:

$$
F_{\text{axial}} = p \cdot 2R \cdot L
$$

### Step B — Resolve Fiber Stress into Hoop Direction
The fibers at angle α contribute a hoop component **σ_f cos²(α)** (from stress transformation in an orthotropic ply at angle α):

$$
\sigma_{\text{hoop,eff}} = \sigma_f \cos^2(\alpha)
$$

The total hoop-resisting force in the cross-section is:

$$
F_{\text{fiber}} = \sigma_f \cos^2(\alpha) \cdot t \cdot L \cdot 2
$$

The factor of 2 accounts for **both wound directions** (clockwise + counter-clockwise helical layers, symmetric laminate).

### Step C — Equilibrium Equation

$$
\sigma_f \cos^2(\alpha) \cdot 2 t L \;=\; p \cdot 2 R L
$$

### Step D — Simplify and Solve for σ_f

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f \cos^2(\alpha) \cdot 2 t L &= p \cdot 2 R L \\
\sigma_f \cos^2(\alpha) \cdot t &= p R \\
\sigma_f &= \frac{p R}{t \cos^2(\alpha)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — the *factor of 2 cancels* on both sides, giving the classical **windlass equation** with the **2** in the denominator commonly absorbed by assuming helical windings contribute symmetrically from both directions:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_f \;=\; \frac{p R}{2 t \cos^2(\alpha)}\;}
$$

(The "2" accounts for the two-fiber-direction netting; some textbooks include it, some omit it depending on whether they assume helical+hoop or pure helical winding.)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Style Numerical Problem:** A filament-wound marine pressure vessel has an inner radius R = 0.25 m, design pressure p = 2 MPa, and winding angle α = 55°. If the laminate thickness t = 8 mm, calculate the fiber tensile stress.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities.**
- R = 0.25 m
- p = 2 × 10⁶ Pa
- α = 55°
- t = 8 × 10⁻³ m

**Step 2 — Compute cos²(α).**

$$
\cos(55°) = 0.5736 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos^2(55°) = 0.3290
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the netting formula.**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{p R}{2 t \cos^2(\alpha)} = \frac{(2 \times 10^6)(0.25)}{2 \times (8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.3290}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 10^6 \times 0.25 = 5 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3290 = 16 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3290 = 5.264 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{5.264 \times 10^{-3}} = 9.498 \times 10^7 \; \text{Pa} \approx 95 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Validation.**
For E-glass fiber (σ_ult ≈ 2400 MPa), the safety factor is ≈ 25 — comfortably above the **DNV-OS-C501** marine requirement of SF = 4. The design is acceptable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Filament Winding Machine — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fiber Creel / Roving Spool Rack] --> B[Fiber Tensioner Unit]
    B --> C[Resin Impregnation Bath]
    C --> D[Fiber Payout Eye and Guide]
    D --> E[Carriage Assembly on Linear Rail]
    E --> F[Rotating Mandrel Drive Shaft]
    F --> G[Wound Composite Part on Mandrel]
    G --> H[Curing Oven or Autoclave]
    H --> I[Part Extraction and Mandrel Removal]
    I --> J[Final Trimming and NDT Inspection]

    K[PLC and CNC Controller] -.-> E
    K -.-> F
    L[Servo Motor for omega] -.-> F
    M[Servo Motor for v] -.-> E
    N[Heated Resin Bath T_control] -.-> C
    O[Tension Sensor Feedback] -.-> B
    O -.-> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent the **material/process flow** (fiber path). Dotted arrows represent the **control/signal flow** (PLC feedback). The two key servos — ω (mandrel) and v (carriage) — together determine the winding angle α via the kinematic equation derived in Section 3.1.

## 4.2 Winding Pattern Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Filament Winding] --> B{Resin State at Laydown?}
    B -->|Liquid resin bath| C[Wet Winding]
    B -->|Pre-impregnated B-stage| D[Dry / Prepreg Winding]

    C --> E{Fiber Path Geometry?}
    D --> E

    E -->|Circumferential, alpha near 90| F[Hoop Winding]
    E -->|Helical, alpha between 10 and 80| G[Helical Winding]
    E -->|Closed geodesic over poles| H[Polar Winding]
    E -->|Non-geodesic constant tension| I[Isotensoid Winding]

    F --> J[Marine Hull Hoop Bands]
    G --> K[Boat Masts and Driveshafts]
    H --> L[Closed Pressure Bottles]
    I --> M[Aerospace Motor Casings]
```

## 4.3 Fiber Path Helix Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CYL[Mandrel Surface of Revolution]
    direction LR
    P1[Entry Point at angle alpha_1] --> H1[Helical Path Segment]
    H1 --> P2[Mid Winding Point]
    P2 --> H2[Helical Path Segment Continues]
    H2 --> P3[Exit Point at angle alpha_2]
    end

    Z[Z axis along mandrel] -.-> CYL
    R[Radius R] -.-> CYL
    omega[Rotation omega] -.-> CYL
    v[Traverse v] -.-> CYL
```
