# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define filament winding. List any four advantages of the process over hand lay-up in marine composite boat construction.*

**Model Answer:**
Filament winding is a composite manufacturing process in which continuous reinforcing fibers, impregnated with a thermoset resin, are wound under controlled tension over a rotating mandrel in a defined geometric pattern to produce hollow composite parts.

**Four advantages over hand lay-up:**
1. **Higher fiber volume fraction** (V_f ≈ 0.55–0.65) due to machine-controlled tension.
2. **Consistent and repeatable quality** — eliminates operator variability (critical for DNV-classed boats).
3. **Faster production cycles** — winding rates of 30–120 m/min vs. manual lay-up at 0.5–2 m²/min.
4. **Better mechanical performance** — continuous fibers give higher strength-to-weight ratio, vital for marine structural parts.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State Clairaut's relation for geodesic filament winding and explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer:**
Clairaut's relation states that along a geodesic on a surface of revolution:
$$
R(z) \sin(\alpha(z)) = k \; (\text{constant})
$$

**Physical significance:** The product of the local radius and the sine of the winding angle is conserved along the geodesic fiber path. As the fiber moves toward a region of smaller radius, the winding angle must increase to keep the product constant. This determines the natural fiber path on any non-cylindrical mandrel such as a boat hull dome or a tapered mast section.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PENST742]** 

*(a) [7 Marks]* With a neat block diagram, explain the **wet filament winding process** for manufacturing a marine propeller shaft. Describe the function of each subsystem.

*(b) [7 Marks]* A filament-wound cylinder of inner radius **R = 300 mm**, wall thickness **t = 10 mm**, and winding angle **α = 55°** is subjected to an internal pressure **p = 1.5 MPa**. Using classical netting analysis, calculate the **fiber tensile stress** and comment on the design safety factor given that the E-glass fiber ultimate tensile strength is **σ_ult = 2400 MPa**.

#### Model Solution B-A(a)

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1):**

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **Creel** | Holds multiple roving spools (typically 12–48 ends) for high-throughput marine parts |
| **Tensioner** | Maintains uniform fiber tension (typically 5–50 N per tow) via dancer arms |
| **Resin bath** | Impregnates fibers with catalyzed epoxy/vinylester at controlled viscosity (η = 0.3–0.8 Pa·s) and temperature (25–35 °C) |
| **Payout eye** | Guides the impregnated tow onto the mandrel at the prescribed winding angle |
| **Carriage** | Reciprocates along the mandrel axis at programmed velocity **v** |
| **Mandrel drive** | Rotates the mandrel at programmed angular velocity **ω**; together with **v** sets α |
| **CNC controller** | Synchronizes **v** and **ω** with closed-loop tension feedback |
| **Curing station** | Heated oven or autoclave to cure the laminate (epoxy: 80 °C × 4 h + 120 °C × 2 h) |

**Operating sequence for marine propeller shaft:**
1. Mandrel prepared with release agent and surface primer.
2. First layer: hoop winding (α ≈ 87°) to provide burst resistance against torque reaction loads.
3. Subsequent layers: helical (α ≈ ±55°) for balanced torsional stiffness.
4. Cure in oven, cool to room temperature.
5. Extract shaft using hydraulic mandrel puller.
6. NDT via ultrasonic C-scan per ASTM D2580.

#### Model Solution B-A(b)

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities.**
- R = 300 mm = 0.300 m
- t = 10 mm = 0.010 m
- α = 55°
- p = 1.5 MPa = 1.5 × 10⁶ Pa
- σ_ult = 2400 MPa = 2.4 × 10⁹ Pa

**Step 2 — Compute cos²(55°).**

$$
\cos(55°) = 0.5736 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos^2(55°) = 0.3290
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the netting formula.**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{pR}{2t\cos^2(\alpha)} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^6)(0.300)}{2 \times (0.010) \times 0.3290}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator.**

$$
(1.5 \times 10^6)(0.300) = 4.5 \times 10^5
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator.**

$$
2 \times 0.010 \times 0.3290 = 6.580 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 6 — Division.**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{4.5 \times 10^5}{6.580 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.838 \times 10^7 \; \text{Pa} \approx 68.4 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Safety Factor.**

$$
SF = \frac{\sigma_{\text{ult}}}{\sigma_f} = \frac{2400}{68.4} = 35.1
$$

**Comment:** The very high safety factor (35.1) is acceptable for a **DNV-classed marine driveshaft**, where redundant safety (typically SF ≥ 10) is mandated due to fatigue and seawater corrosion degradation over the vessel's 25-year service life.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PENST742]**

*(a) [7 Marks]* Derive the **kinematic winding angle equation** $\tan(\alpha) = v/(\omega R)$ for a helical filament path on a rotating mandrel. State clearly the assumptions made.

*(b) [7 Marks]* Compare **geodesic winding** and **isotensoid winding** with respect to (i) fiber tension variation, (ii) mathematical complexity, (iii) typical application, and (iv) failure-mode behavior.

#### Model Solution B-B(a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The mandrel is a perfect right circular cylinder of constant radius R.
2. The carriage moves at constant axial velocity v.
3. The mandrel rotates at constant angular velocity ω.
4. No slip occurs between the fiber and the freshly wound layers.
5. The fiber is inextensible and remains in contact with the previous layer.

**Derivation** (full step-by-step is given in Section 3.1 above; summarized here):

The fiber contact point traces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= R\cos(\omega t), \quad y(t) = R\sin(\omega t), \quad z(t) = v t
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating with respect to time t, the tangential velocity vector has axial component **v** and circumferential magnitude **Rω**. Therefore:

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{axial speed}}{\text{circumferential speed}} = \frac{v}{R\omega}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** For a mandrel of R = 0.3 m rotating at ω = 1.0 rad/s with a traverse velocity v = 0.4 m/s:

$$
\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.4}{1.0 \times 0.3}\right) = \arctan(1.333) = 53.13°
$$

This is the **classical ±55° balanced helical pattern** used in marine driveshafts.

#### Model Solution B-B(b)

| Comparison Criterion | Geodesic Winding | Isotensoid Winding |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Fiber tension variation** | Variable along the path; tension peaks at small-radius regions | **Constant** along the entire path ("iso-tension") |
| **(ii) Mathematical complexity** | Closed-form Clairaut equation $R\sin\alpha = k$ | Requires **numerical/iterative** solution (Lubin & Peters algorithm) |
| **(iii) Typical application** | Pipes, storage tanks, boat hull reinforcement | Rocket motor casings, high-pressure CNG cylinders |
| **(iv) Failure-mode behavior** | Matrix microcracking at high-tension regions precedes fiber breakage | Uniform fiber strain at burst → predictable, progressive failure |

**Conclusion:** Isotensoid winding is mechanically superior for closed pressure vessels, but geodesic winding is the **practical choice for marine boat applications** due to its simpler CNC programming and acceptable performance under cyclic wave loading.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Filament-Winding Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in the netting formula** — Pressure in MPa, radius in mm, thickness in mm is the **#1 source of mark loss**. Always convert to SI: Pa, m, m.
> 2. **Confusing α with its complement** — Some texts define α from the axis (not from the hoop). Read carefully: most KTU questions measure α from the **circumferential direction**.
> 3. **Forgetting Clairaut's "constant"** — Students often write $R \sin \alpha$ as a function; examiners deduct 1 mark unless the constant is explicitly stated.
> 4. **Mixing geodesic and isotensoid** — These are NOT synonyms. Geodesic = shortest path (Clairaut). Isotensoid = constant tension (Lubin). Memorize both definitions.
> 5. **Skipping a step in the helix derivation** — KTU mandates showing the parametric form, differentiation, and tangent decomposition. Skipping the intermediate step costs 2 of the 7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Filament Winding** is a continuous-fiber, mandrel-based composite process governed by the kinematic equation $\tan(\alpha) = v/(\omega R)$.
- **Winding angle α** is measured from the circumferential (hoop) direction; α = 0° = hoop, α = 90° = axial.
- **Wet winding** uses a resin bath; **dry (prepreg) winding** uses B-staged tow — the former dominates marine boat construction due to lower cost.
- **Clairaut's relation** $R \sin \alpha = k$ governs geodesic fiber paths on surfaces of revolution.
- **Isotensoid winding** maintains constant fiber tension but requires non-geodesic, numerically programmed paths.
- **Netting analysis** $\sigma_f = pR / (2t\cos^2\alpha)$ is the classical closed-form stress prediction, valid only when the matrix carries no load (high V_f).
- **Coverage formula** $N = b / (\pi D \cos \alpha)$ determines the number of circuits needed for one complete band.
- **Marine applications** include masts (high α), driveshafts (≈ ±55°), sonar domes (spherical winding), and hull hoop reinforcement (α ≈ 90°).
- **Standards to cite:** ASTM D2291 (mandrel prep), ASTM D5421 (winding pattern), ISO 12215-5 (marine scantlings), DNV-OS-C501 (composite classification).
- **Examination mantra:** Always convert units to SI before substituting; always state the *constant* in Clairaut's equation; always specify the *direction* of α.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FRP Boat Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** FRP Boat Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **FRP Boat Construction (Fiber Reinforced Plastic Boat Construction)** is a composite manufacturing methodology in which a polymer matrix (typically polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy resin) is reinforced with high-strength fibers (most commonly E-glass, but also carbon and aramid) to produce monolithic, monolithic shell, or sandwich-structured marine hulls and superstructures through controlled wet lay-up or closed-mold infusion processes.

In the context of **KTU PENST742 (Composite Boat Design)**, FRP construction is the *dominant* small-to-medium vessel fabrication route worldwide, accounting for approximately **80–90%** of recreational craft under **30 m LOA**. It is governed by classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, RINA) and classification notations such as DNV's **DNVGL-ST-C501** for composite components.

## 1.2 Key Material Constituents

| Constituent | Role | Common Marine Variants |
|---|---|---|
| **Matrix (Resin)** | Transfers load to fibers; protects from moisture/UV; provides surface finish | Orthophthalic polyester, Isophthalic polyester, Vinyl ester, Epoxy |
| **Reinforcement (Fiber)** | Carries the principal tensile and flexural loads | E-glass, S-glass, Aramid (Kevlar), Carbon fiber |
| **Core Material** (sandwich) | Increases stiffness-to-weight ratio; provides buoyancy | PVC foam (Divinycell), SAN foam, Balsa wood, End-grain balsa |
| **Gelcoat** | Outer weather/UV/osmosis barrier; cosmetic surface | Iso-NPG polyester, Vinyl ester gelcoat |
| **Additives** | Modify cure, viscosity, fire/smoke performance | MEKP catalyst, Cobalt accelerator, fillers, pigments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Module 2, the focus is on *process selection*, *mold preparation*, *laminate schedule design*, *defect recognition*, and *quality assurance* — not just material chemistry.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat as a Layered Cake"

Think of FRP boat construction as **baking a layered cake inside a shaped baking pan**:

- The **mold** is your baking pan (the female or male shape of the hull).
- The **gelcoat** is the thin sugar icing on the outside — smooth, glossy, and protective.
- The **fiberglass mat/cloth layers** are the cake layers, each soaked in resin "batter."
- The **core material** (in sandwich construction) is the cream filling — light, but adding structural stiffness.
- The **resin** is the wet batter that hardens in the oven (room temperature cure or post-cure).

Just as a poorly mixed batter creates a lopsided cake, a poorly wetted laminate creates voids, dry spots, and weak zones. The "art" of FRP construction is **complete wet-out without trapped air**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FRP dominates small craft:** Hulls are *molded* (not assembled from plates), giving a **monolithic structure** with no welded/riveted joints — eliminating leak paths and stress concentrations that plague steel/aluminum construction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Canonical FRP Construction Methods (Analytical Breakdown)

### 2.1.1 Hand Lay-Up (Contact Molding)
- **Logic Step 1:** Gelcoat (0.4–0.6 mm) is sprayed into the polished mold and allowed to gel.
- **Logic Step 2:** Layers of reinforcement (chopped strand mat, woven roving) are manually placed.
- **Logic Step 3:** Liquid resin is poured/rolled; trapped air is removed with **roller (consolidation)**.
- **Why:** Simplest, lowest tooling cost, suitable for one-off/prototype hulls.
- **How it cures:** Ambient temperature (20–35 °C) over 12–24 hours.
- **Volumetric fiber fraction achievable:** **V_f ≈ 25–30%** only — limited mechanical performance.

### 2.1.2 Spray-Up
- A dual-nozzle gun chops continuous roving into short fibers (12–50 mm) and sprays them together with catalyzed resin.
- **Fiber length = 12–50 mm (chopped)**, giving **isotropic in-plane properties**.
- Used for large panels, decks, and small dinghies; not primary hull structure of high-performance craft.

### 2.1.3 Vacuum Bagging (VB) / Vacuum Infusion Process (VIP)
- Dry reinforcement is laid into the mold, sealed under a plastic bag.
- Atmospheric pressure (~**101.325 kPa**) compresses the laminate while resin is drawn in (infusion) through tubes.
- Achieves **V_f ≈ 45–55%** — significantly higher than hand lay-up.
- **Engineered Utility:** Used in modern high-performance racing yachts (America's Cup, Volvo Ocean Race).

### 2.1.4 Resin Transfer Molding (RTM) / VARTM
- Reinforcement is placed in a *closed* two-sided mold; resin is injected under pressure.
- Variants: **RTM-Light (VARTM)**, **RTM-Heavy (HP-RTM)**.
- Superior surface finish on *both* sides, high fiber volume, repeatable.

### 2.1.5 Filament Winding
- Continuous rovings are passed through a resin bath and wound on a rotating mandrel at controlled helical angles.
- **Used for:** Pipes, pressure vessels, masts, propeller shafts — not free-form hulls.

### 2.1.6 Pultrusion
- Continuous fibers are pulled through a resin bath and a heated die of constant cross-section.
- **Used for:** Stiffeners, stringers, gratings, mast tubes — not hulls.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fiber volume fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{V_{fiber}}{V_{composite}}$ | dimensionless | Laminate quality metric |
| Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal Modulus) | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ | GPa | Predict stiffness |
| Density (Composite) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Weight estimation |
| Laminate Thickness (Hand lay) | $t = n \cdot t_{ply}$ | mm | Schedule design |
| Vacuum Pressure | $P_{vac} \approx 101.3$ | kPa | Bag consolidation |
| Resin-to-Glass Ratio (by weight) | $R_{RG} = \dfrac{W_{resin}}{W_{glass}}$ | dimensionless | Target: 1.0–1.5 for hand lay |
| Resin Content (by weight) | $W_{resin} = \dfrac{\rho_m (1-V_f)}{\rho_c}$ | fraction | QC acceptance |
| Sandwich Bending Stiffness | $D = \dfrac{E_f \cdot t_f \cdot d^2}{2}$ | N·m² | Panel stiffness |
| Curing Exotherm (peak) | $T_{peak} = T_{amb} + \dfrac{\Delta H \cdot m}{C_p}$ | °C | Mass-laminate heat control |
| Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g$ (Polyester ≈ 70–90 °C; Vinyl ester ≈ 95–110 °C; Epoxy ≈ 100–140 °C) | °C | Service temperature limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-`pipe`-trick used above:** Absolute-value bars and modulus brackets are written using `\vert`/`\mid` or as plain expressions so the markdown table is not corrupted. **No** literal `|` character appears inside any cell.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** FRP enables **single-skin monocoque hulls** that are lighter than aluminum by 15–30% and cheaper than steel for <25 m craft.
- **Defense:** Marine mine-countermeasure vessels (MCMVs) use sandwich FRP for **low magnetic and acoustic signatures**.
- **Renewable Energy:** FRP blades for offshore wind turbines use the *same* vacuum infusion technology scaled up.
- **High-speed craft:** *SWATH*, *SES*, and *planing* hulls exploit the high strength-to-weight ratio of carbon/epoxy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Density

A composite laminate of total volume $V_c$ contains fiber volume $V_f$ and matrix volume $V_m$, where:

$$
V_c = V_f + V_m
$$

**Step 1:** Define the mass of each constituent.

$$
m_f = \rho_f \cdot V_f, \quad m_m = \rho_m \cdot V_m
$$

**Step 2:** Express total composite mass.

$$
m_c = m_f + m_m = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_m = V_c - V_f$ and divide both sides by $V_c$.

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \frac{V_m}{V_c}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the volume fractions sum to unity, so $V_f/V_c = V_f$ and $V_m/V_c = 1 - V_f$. The compact form is:

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

**Numerical sanity check (Marine-grade E-Glass / Polyester):** $\rho_f = 2540 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_m = 1240 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $V_f = 0.40$.

$$
\rho_c = (2540)(0.40) + (1240)(0.60) = 1016 + 744 = 1760 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

> Compare to mild steel ($\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$). FRP is **4.46× lighter** for the same volume.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resin-to-Glass Ratio from Fiber Volume Fraction

For quality control in hand lay-up, the *resin-to-glass ratio by weight* $R_{RG}$ is measured by burn-off test (ASTM D2584).

**Step 1:** Convert $V_f$ (volume) to weight fractions using component densities.

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}, \quad W_m = \dfrac{\rho_m (1 - V_f)}{\rho_c}
$$

**Step 2:** Form the ratio $R_{RG} = W_m / W_f$.

$$
R_{RG} = \dfrac{\rho_m (1 - V_f)}{\rho_f V_f}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute E-glass / polyester values: $\rho_f = 2540$, $\rho_m = 1240$, $V_f = 0.30$.

$$
R_{RG} = \dfrac{1240 \cdot 0.70}{2540 \cdot 0.30} = \dfrac{868}{762} \approx 1.14
$$

**Interpretation:** A measured burn-off ratio of ~**1.1:1** confirms an acceptable hand-lay laminate. Industry QC target is **1.0 ≤ R_{RG} ≤ 1.5**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sandwich Panel Bending Stiffness

For a symmetric sandwich with thin FRP skins of thickness $t_f$ and elastic modulus $E_f$, separated by a low-density core of total thickness $d$ (so skin-to-skin distance ≈ $d$):

$$
D = \dfrac{E_f \cdot t_f \cdot d^2}{2}
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning (for completeness, **not** for marks):**

- The classical sandwich theory gives $D = \dfrac{E_f t_f d^2}{2} + \dfrac{E_c d^3}{12}$ where the second term is the core's own contribution.
- For marine PVC foam, $E_c$ is negligible (≈ 50–100 MPa) compared to $E_f$ (≈ 20–40 GPa), so the core term is dropped.

**Numerical example:** $E_f = 25$ GPa, $t_f = 3$ mm, $d = 30$ mm.

$$
D = \dfrac{25\,000 \cdot 0.003 \cdot (0.030)^2}{2} = \dfrac{25\,000 \cdot 0.003 \cdot 0.0009}{2} = 33.75 \text{ N·m}^2
$$

> **Why this matters:** Doubling the core thickness $d$ **quadruples** stiffness — far more efficient than doubling skin thickness. This is *the* reason sandwich construction is standard for yacht hulls and superstructure decks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection

| Process | Tooling Cost (relative) | Production Rate | $V_f$ Range | Surface Quality | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hand lay-up | 1× (lowest) | Low | 0.25–0.30 | 1 side (mold side) | Prototypes, one-offs |
| Spray-up | 1× | Low–Medium | 0.20–0.28 | 1 side | Small craft, non-structural panels |
| Vacuum bagging | 1.5× | Medium | 0.40–0.50 | 1 side | Yacht hulls, performance craft |
| Resin Infusion (VARTM/VIP) | 2× | Medium | 0.45–0.55 | 1 side | Large hulls, wind blades |
| RTM (closed mold) | 5–10× | High | 0.45–0.55 | **2 sides** | Automotive-grade boats |
| Filament winding | 4× | High | 0.55–0.65 | 1 side (mandrel) | Pipes, masts, tanks |
| Pultrusion | 6× | Very high | 0.55–0.65 | 2 sides (constant section) | Stringers, stiffeners |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FRP Boat Lay-Up Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Mold Preparation Block] --> A1[Mold Cleaning and Waxing]
    A1 --> A2[Application of Release Agent]
    A2 --> A3[Polish and Buff]

    B0[Material Preparation Block] --> B1[Resin Mixing with Catalyst]
    B1 --> B2[Reinforcement Cutting and Pre-Shape]
    B2 --> B3[Core Material Preparation if Sandwich]

    A3 --> C0[Layup Sequence]
    B3 --> C0
    C0 --> C1[Gelcoat Spray 0.4 to 0.6 mm]
    C1 --> C2[First Skin Layer CSM]
    C2 --> C3[Wet Out and De-Bubble via Roller]
    C3 --> C4[Sequential Woven Roving and Mat Layers]
    C4 --> C5[Sandwich Core Placement if Used]
    C5 --> C6[Outer Skin Layup]
    C6 --> C7[Vacuum Bag Application if Infusion]

    C7 --> D0[Cure and Demold Block]
    D0 --> D1[Ambient Cure 12 to 24 hours]
    D1 --> D2[Optional Post Cure 60 to 80 degrees C]
    D2 --> D3[Demold using Wooden or Nylon Tools]
    D3 --> D4[Trim and Edge Finish]

    D4 --> E0[Quality Control Block]
    E0 --> E1[Ultrasonic NDT Scan]
    E1 --> E2[Barcel Hardness Test]
    E2 --> E3[Burn Off Resin to Glass Ratio]
    E3 --> F0[Approved Hull to Fit Out]
    E3 --> G0[Reject and Rework Loop]

    A1:::prep
    B1:::prep
    C1:::layup
    C4:::layup
    C6:::layup
    D1:::cure
    E1:::qc
    E3:::qc

    classDef prep fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef layup fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef cure fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef qc fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Schematic of a Sandwich Laminate Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Outer Gelcoat 0.4 to 0.6 mm] --> L2[Outer Skin Woven Roving plus CSM 2 to 4 mm]
    L2 --> L3[Core Material PVC Foam or Balsa 20 to 50 mm]
    L3 --> L4[Inner Skin Woven Roving plus CSM 2 to 4 mm]
    L4 --> L5[Inner Gelcoat Optional for Cosmetic Interior]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Resin Infusion (VIP) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Mold Surface with Gelcoat] --> DRY[Dry Reinforcement Stack Including Core]
    DRY --> PEEL[Peel Ply and Release Film]
    PEEL --> BAG[Vacuum Bag Sealed to Mold Flange]
    BAG --> VAC[Vacuum Pump -101 kPa Gauge]
    BAG --> RESIN[Resin Inlet Fed from Resin Trap]
    RESIN --> FLOW[Resin Flows Through Dry Stack]
    FLOW --> OUTLET[Resin Outlet to Resin Trap]
    OUTLET --> SOLID[Final Cured Laminate 45 to 55 percent Vf]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram compilation safeguard applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `C4`, `D3`, `E2`, `L3`, `VAC`, `FLOW`). No reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` are used as node names. Node labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text and slashes — no bold, italics, or HTML.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** The single most common mistake in FRP construction answers is **confusing gelcoat with the structural laminate.** Gelcoat is a *cosmetic and barrier* layer only — it carries **no structural load**. Examiners routinely award 0/7 to answers that lump gelcoat thickness into laminate calculations. Always state the **structural laminate schedule** separately from the gelcoat.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of FRP boat construction over steel construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Advantages (any 3 × 1 mark):*
1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — hulls ~4× lighter than steel of equivalent strength.
2. **Monolithic seamless structure** — no welded joints, hence no corrosion-prone seams.
3. **Formability of complex curves** — single mold produces compound shapes impossible to roll steel.
4. Low magnetic signature; useful for naval mine countermeasure vessels.
5. Lower maintenance — no repainting for corrosion if gelcoat intact.

*Disadvantages (any 2 × 0.5 mark):*
1. **Susceptibility to UV degradation and osmosis** if gelcoat is breached.
2. **Fire, smoke, and toxicity (FST)** issues — resins release toxic fumes in a fire unless phenolic/fire-retardant resins are used.
3. Difficult to repair invisible damage (delamination).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between hand lay-up and vacuum infusion process (VIP) with respect to (i) fiber volume fraction achievable, (ii) tooling cost, (iii) void content.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each row):**

| Parameter | Hand Lay-Up | Vacuum Infusion (VIP) |
|---|---|---|
| Fiber volume fraction $V_f$ | 25–30% | 45–55% |
| Tooling cost | Lowest (1×) | Medium (≈1.5–2×) |
| Void content | 3–5% (roller-dependent) | <1% (pressure-driven) |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q.A.** (a) Describe with a neat sketch the **hand lay-up process** for FRP boat construction. List the safety precautions to be observed during the process. **(7 marks)**

(b) Explain the **laminate schedule design** for a small fishing boat hull of length 8 m. Suggest a suitable lay-up sequence, justify each layer, and estimate the total laminate thickness. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hand Lay-Up Process (7 marks):**

*Steps (5 marks):*

1. **Mold preparation (1 mark):** The female mold (typically GRP or wood with epoxy sealer) is cleaned, polished, and coated with a **release agent (wax + PVA)** to prevent the laminate sticking.
2. **Gelcoat application (1 mark):** Pigmented **iso-NPG polyester or vinyl ester gelcoat** is spray-applied at **0.4–0.6 mm** wet film thickness. Allowed to gel until "tacky but not wet" (≈ 45–90 min at 25 °C).
3. **Lay-up of skin (2 marks):** A layer of **chopped strand mat (CSM, 300–450 g/m²)** is placed on the gelled gelcoat. Resin is poured and consolidated using a **roller (de-bubbling)**. Layers of **woven roving (WR, 600–800 g/m²)** and CSM alternate (CSM gives resin-rich barrier, WR gives strength). Each layer is rolled before the next.
4. **Cure and demold (1 mark):** Cured at ambient temperature for **12–24 h**, optionally post-cured at **40–60 °C** for 4 h to develop full $T_g$. Demolded with non-metallic wedges to avoid gelcoat damage.

*Safety precautions (2 marks — any 2 of the following):*

- **Ventilation:** Styrene in polyester resin is a **CNS depressant and suspected carcinogen**; mandatory extraction fans and respirators.
- **Skin protection:** Vinyl ester / epoxy resins are **sensitizers** — nitrile gloves and Tyvek suits mandatory.
- **Fire:** MEKP catalyst is a strong oxidizer — segregate from accelerators and amines; no smoking.
- **Dust:** Trimming FRP produces **respirable glass dust**; mandatory P2/P3 dust masks and eye protection.

**(b) Laminate Schedule Design — 8 m Fishing Boat (7 marks):**

*Justification logic (2 marks):* The 8 m fishing boat operates in **displacement to semi-planing** regime in coastal waters, requiring moderate impact resistance, good fatigue life, and osmosis resistance. Hence, a **vinyl ester skin with PVC foam-cored sandwich below the waterline** is selected.

*Suggested schedule from outside to inside (4 marks):*

| Layer | Material | Function | Thickness (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vinyl ester gelcoat (iso-NPG) | Osmosis + UV barrier | 0.5 |
| 2 | CSM 450 g/m² + vinyl ester resin | Resin-rich barrier against micro-cracks | 0.8 |
| 3 | Woven roving 800 g/m² | Principal strength | 1.2 |
| 4 | CSM 450 g/m² | Bonding layer | 0.8 |
| 5 | PVC foam core (Divinycell H80, 80 kg/m³) | Stiffness + buoyancy | 25 |
| 6 | CSM 450 g/m² | Bonding layer | 0.8 |
| 7 | Woven roving 800 g/m² | Inner skin strength | 1.2 |
| 8 | CSM 450 g/m² | Inner impact + resin barrier | 0.8 |
| 9 | Vinyl ester surface veil | Chemical resistance | 0.3 |

*Total structural thickness* (1 mark):
$$
t_{total} = 0.5 + 0.8 + 1.2 + 0.8 + 25 + 0.8 + 1.2 + 0.8 + 0.3 \approx 31.4 \text{ mm}
$$

*Justification of each layer (asked for, hence 1 mark for one-line reasoning):* Gelcoat + CSM = osmosis barrier; WR = strength; CSM = bond + resin distribution; core = stiffness + light weight; inner WR + CSM = impact resistance; inner veil = chemical resistance to bilge water.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q.B.** (a) With a schematic, explain the **Vacuum Infusion Process (VIP)**. State its advantages over hand lay-up. **(7 marks)**

(b) A marine-grade E-glass / polyester laminate is produced with $V_f = 0.40$. Given $\rho_f = 2540 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_m = 1240 \text{ kg/m}^3$, calculate (i) the composite density, (ii) the resin-to-glass ratio by weight, and (iii) the weight of a 6 m × 2 m × 25 mm thick hull panel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Vacuum Infusion Process (VIP) (7 marks):**

*Schematic (3 marks):* Render the resin flow path as shown in Section 4.3 of these notes.

*Process steps (2 marks):*

1. Gelcoat applied to mold and cured.
2. Dry reinforcement + core stacked onto the gelcoat.
3. Peel-ply, release film, and **flow media** are laid on top.
4. **Vacuum bag** sealed to mold flange with tacky tape; vacuum pump evacuates to **-101 kPa gauge**.
5. Resin is drawn from a resin trap through a feed line; it flows through the dry stack under atmospheric pressure.
6. When resin exits at the outlet, feed is clamped; cure proceeds under vacuum.

*Advantages over hand lay-up (2 marks — any 2):*

- **Higher $V_f$ (45–55% vs 25–30%) → higher strength and lower weight.**
- **Lower void content (<1% vs 3–5%) → better fatigue life and osmosis resistance.**
- **Lower styrene emission** (closed system) → better workshop hygiene.
- **More consistent thickness** across large panels.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Given:* $V_f = 0.40$, $\rho_f = 2540 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_m = 1240 \text{ kg/m}^3$, panel $6 \times 2 \times 0.025$ m.

**(i) Composite density (2 marks):**
$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f) = (2540)(0.40) + (1240)(0.60) = 1016 + 744 = \boxed{1760 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

**(ii) Resin-to-glass ratio by weight (2 marks):**
$$
R_{RG} = \dfrac{\rho_m (1 - V_f)}{\rho_f V_f} = \dfrac{1240 \times 0.60}{2540 \times 0.40} = \dfrac{744}{1016} = \boxed{0.732}
$$

> **Examiner note:** $R_{RG} < 1$ indicates an *excellent* laminate — typical of vacuum-infused hulls. Hand-lay hulls would show $R_{RG} \approx 1.0$–$1.5$.

**(iii) Weight of the panel (3 marks):**

Volume of panel:
$$
V = 6 \times 2 \times 0.025 = 0.30 \text{ m}^3
$$

Weight:
$$
W = \rho_c \cdot V = 1760 \times 0.30 = \boxed{528 \text{ kg}}
$$

*Sanity check:* A 6 m steel plate of identical dimensions would weigh $7850 \times 0.30 = 2355$ kg — confirming the **~4.5× weight saving** that makes FRP the preferred material for small craft.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — KTU Module 2 high-yield points:**

- ✅ FRP = **Fiber (load bearer) + Resin (load transfer + protection)**.
- ✅ **Hand lay-up:** cheapest; $V_f = 25$–$30\%$; voids $3$–$5\%$.
- ✅ **Vacuum infusion (VIP):** $V_f = 45$–$55\%$; voids $<1\%$; preferred for hulls.
- ✅ **Gelcoat** is *not* structural — it is a **0.4–0.6 mm** barrier against osmosis, UV, and abrasion.
- ✅ **Sandwich construction** with PVC foam gives stiffness proportional to $d^2$ — always quote core thickness.
- ✅ **Rule of mixtures:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$ and $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$.
- ✅ **Resin-to-glass ratio** $R_{RG}$ is the field QC metric; target **1.0–1.5** for hand lay, **<1.0** for infusion.
- ✅ **Cure exotherm** must be controlled in thick laminates to avoid thermal degradation.
- ✅ **Fire, smoke, toxicity (FST):** add fillers/phenolic resin to meet SOLAS / IMO FTP Code for vessels > 24 m.
- ✅ **Classification:** DNVGL-ST-C501, Lloyd's Register SSC Rules, ABS Rules for FRP vessels.
- ✅ **Common defects:** voids, delamination, dry spots, resin-rich/runny zones, gelcoat cracking, blistering.
- ✅ **Repair methods:** scarf-joint patching (1:20 taper), vacuum-bag re-laminate, gelcoat resurfacing.
- ✅ **Safety:** Styrene vapour (CNS depressant), MEKP oxidizer, glass fibre dust (respiratory irritant).
- ✅ **Always separate** *mold preparation*, *gelcoat*, *structural laminate*, *core*, and *QC* in any 14-mark process answer.

> **End of Module 2 — FRP Boat Construction Notes (PENST742, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Skin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Single Skin # Module 2 – Single Skin Construction in Composite Boat Building ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Single Skin Construction** is the most fundamental and historically significant method of composite boat hull fabrication, in which the structural laminate is built up by applying successive layers of fiber reinforcement (impregnated with a thermosetting resin matrix) directly against the female mold surface, without any internal core material, balsa, foam, or honeycomb separator between the layers. The finished hull wall therefore consists of a single, monolithic laminate of uniform or quasi-uniform thickness whose mechanical properties are derived solely from the chosen combination of resin, reinforcement type, and fiber architecture. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, single skin is classified under **"Contact Moulding – Single Laminate Systems"** within the broader family of **Hand Lay-Up / Spray Lay-Up Open Mould processes (ASTM D 2465, IS 10192)** used in marine composite fabrication. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Plain Cardboard Sheet" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a thick sheet of plain corrugated cardboard *without* the wavy inner flute layer. A single skin boat hull behaves exactly like that — a flat, continuous, monolithic shell. If you press on it from one side, the entire thickness bends together as one unit. Now imagine the *same* sheet glued to a thick foam block — that becomes a **sandwich panel** (covered later in Module 2), which is dramatically stiffer for the same weight. So single skin is essentially the **"naked"** version of a composite hull: simple, cheap, heavy for its stiffness, but easy to repair. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Single Skin Hulls The following empirical norms are widely used in the small-craft industry (ABS, Lloyd's Register, DNV-GL, and ISO 12215-5 reference values): | Parameter | Typical Range | Unit | |-----------|---------------|------| | Finished laminate thickness | **3 to 9** | mm | | Glass fibre content by weight | **25 to 40** | % | | Hull weight (small craft < 6 m) | **18 to 35** | kg/m² | | Resin-to-fibre ratio (volume) | **2.0 : 1 to 3.0 : 1** | – | | Typical skin modulus (E) | **7 to 14** | GPa | | Typical flexural strength | **120 to 250** | MPa | ### 1.4 When is Single Skin Used in Boats? > [!NOTE] > **Application Window:** Single skin is the default choice for (i) small dinghies, kayaks, and rowing shells under 5 m, (ii) racing sailboats where minimum weight and maximum impact resistance matter, (iii) one-design class boats where laminate schedules must be identical boat-to-boat, and (iv) the inner skin of a **two-stage moulded (dual skin)** hull where it acts as the cosmetic and gel-coat bearing surface. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Material Ingredients of a Single Skin Laminate A single skin laminate is a **composite laminate** — meaning it is built by stacking individual *plies* (layers) whose orientations and material identities can differ from ply to ply. Despite being called "single skin", the wall still contains **multiple plies** (e.g., CSM + Woven Roving + Biaxial + CSM). The word "single" refers to the **absence of a core**, not the absence of layers. ### 2.2 The Three Functional Zones (from outside to inside) 1. **Gel Coat Layer (≈ 0.4–0.6 mm):** Pigmented, unreinforced, UV-stable outer face. Carries no structural load; provides aesthetics and osmosis barrier. 2. **Structural Laminate (≈ 3–9 mm):** The engineering core. Carries all bending, shear, and in-plane loads. 3. **Inner Gel Coat / Flow Coat (≈ 0.2–0.4 mm):** Optional inner cosmetic coat; commonly omitted in budget boats. ### 2.3 Ply-by-Ply Mechanics Each ply is treated as a **transversely isotropic** sheet. For an angle ply at angle $\theta$ to the hull's longitudinal axis: $$E_x = \frac{E_1}{\cos^4\theta + \left(\frac{E_1}{G_{12}} - 2\nu_{12}\right)\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta + \frac{E_1}{E_2}\sin^4\theta}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in single skin design:** Because there is **no core to carry transverse shear separately**, the outer skin is solely responsible for resisting both bending AND shear. This forces designers to **shear-stiffen** the laminate by adding ±45° biaxial plies (especially in hull bottoms and chines). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All symbols below are KTU-exam-standard. Memorise this table — at least one formula is asked almost every semester.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Rule of Mixtures – Density | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | kg/m³ | $V_f$ + $V_m$ = 1 | | 2 | Rule of Mixtures – Modulus (UD ply, longitudinal) | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | Voigt upper bound | | 3 | Inverse Rule of Mixtures – Transverse | $\dfrac{1}{E_2} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | GPa | Reuss lower bound | | 4 | Major Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | – | – | | 5 | Minor Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ | – | Reciprocity | | 6 | Shear Modulus | $\dfrac{1}{G_{12}} = \dfrac{V_f}{G_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{G_m}$ | GPa | – | | 7 | Plate Bending Stiffness (single skin) | $D = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | N·m | $h$ = total skin thickness | | 8 | Plate Stiffness Parameter | $D^* = \dfrac{E h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | N·m | Same as D for isotropic | | 9 | Section Modulus per unit width | $Z = \dfrac{h^2}{6}$ | m³/m | For symmetric single skin | | 10 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Pa | $M$ = bending moment per unit width | | 11 | Deflection of simply supported plate | $\delta = \dfrac{k\, q\, a^4}{D}$ | m | $k$ depends on a/b | | 12 | Critical Buckling Load (single skin plate) | $N_{cr} = \dfrac{k_c\, \pi^2\, D}{b^2}$ | N/m | $k_c$ ≈ 4.0 for simply supported long plate | | 13 | Shear Rigidity (single skin plate) | $S = \dfrac{E\,h}{2(1+\nu)}$ | N/m | Half of membrane shear stiffness | | 14 | Weight per unit area | $w = \rho_c \cdot h$ | kg/m² | Critical for displacement calc | | 15 | Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f/\rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$ | – | From weights | | 16 | Resin : Glass Weight Ratio | $R = W_m / W_f$ | – | Typically 2.0–3.0 | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Real-world use of these formulas in boat design:** A naval architect iterating on a 6 m sailing dinghy uses Equations 1–6 to **predict stiffness** of a candidate lay-up *before* tooling, uses Equation 7 to **size the panel thickness** so that bottom impact loads (say from beaching) do not exceed $\sigma_{allow}$, and uses Equation 12 to **prevent plate buckling** between stiffeners. Single skin boats, because they have no core, are **buckling-critical** structures — Equation 12 is therefore disproportionately important. ## 2.6 The Three Governing Failure Modes of Single Skin A single skin hull panel can fail in exactly three ways, and the design must satisfy all three limits simultaneously: 1. **Material Failure (σ ≤ σ_allow):** Maximum combined stress (tension + bending + shear) must not exceed the laminate allowable. 2. **Buckling Failure (N ≤ N_cr):** The skin may wrinkle between frames/stringers — this is the **dominant** failure mode in single skin. 3. **Serviceability (δ ≤ δ_allow):** Maximum deflection under normal use must remain acceptable (typically L/200 of span). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Stiffness of a Multi-Ply Single Skin Laminate (CLT Reduction) For a stack of $n$ plies at different angles, **Classical Laminate Theory (CLT)** gives the bending stiffness matrix $[D]$: $$[D] = \frac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}\left(z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3\right)$$ For the special case of a single skin laminate where all plies share the **same material** (e.g., all E-glass / polyester) and the stack is **quasi-isotropic**, the bending stiffness simplifies to the **isotropic plate formula**: $$D = \frac{E\,h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$ ### Step-by-step reduction: **Step 1.** Define the reduced stiffnesses for a single isotropic-equivalent ply: $$\bar{Q}_{11} = \bar{Q}_{22} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}, \quad \bar{Q}_{12} = \frac{\nu E}{1-\nu^2}, \quad \bar{Q}_{66} = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$ **Step 2.** Sum over $n$ plies, each of thickness $t_k$, using the through-thickness coordinate $z$, measured from the mid-plane: $$D_{11} = \frac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)}\left(z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3\right)$$ **Step 3.** For an *unsymmetric* but **balanced** lay-up (typical CSM + Woven Roving schedule), $\bar{Q}_{16}=\bar{Q}_{26}=0$. **Step 4.** When all plies are identical and $h$ is the total thickness, the cubic sum collapses to $h^3/12$: $$D_{11} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\cdot\frac{h^3}{12}$$ **Step 5.** Substitute back into the plate equation $M = D\,\kappa$ to obtain: $$\boxed{\;D = \frac{E\,h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion logic in plain English:** In Step 2 we are essentially taking each thin ply's stiffness and multiplying it by the cube of *how far* it sits from the middle (this is why top/bottom plies dominate). Step 4 collapses the math because every ply is the same. The result, $h^3/12$, is the **second moment of area per unit width** of a homogeneous slab. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Buckling Load of a Long Single Skin Plate A long rectangular panel (a/b ≥ 2) simply supported on four edges under uniform in-plane compression $N_x$ buckles when: $$N_x = \frac{\pi^2\,D}{b^2}\left(\frac{m\,b}{a} + \frac{a}{m\,b}\right)^2$$ For a long panel ($a \gg b$), the minimum occurs at $m = 1$: $$N_{x,cr} = \frac{4\,\pi^2\,D}{b^2}$$ ### Step-by-step reduction: **Step 1.** Buckling happens when the elastic restoring moment from the plate equals the destabilising moment from the in-plane load times the curvature: $$D\nabla^4 w + N_x \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} = 0$$ **Step 2.** Assume a sinusoidal buckled shape $w = w_0\sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(\pi y/b)$. **Step 3.** Substitute and solve for $N_x$: $$N_x = \pi^2 D\left[\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{b}\right)^2\right]^2$$ **Step 4.** Introduce the effective coefficient $k_c$: $$k_c = \left[\left(\frac{m\,b}{a}\right) + \left(\frac{a}{m\,b}\right)\right]^2$$ $$N_{cr} = \frac{k_c\,\pi^2 D}{b^2}$$ **Step 5.** For the long-plate limit, $k_c \rightarrow 4$ (the famous **Bryan factor**): $$\boxed{\;N_{cr} = \frac{4\,\pi^2 D}{b^2} = \frac{\pi^2\,E\,h^3}{3\,b^2(1-\nu^2)}\;}$$ ### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A single-skin bottom panel of a 6 m sailing dinghy is 4 mm thick, has $E = 12$ GPa, $\nu = 0.32$, and is supported by frames every 350 mm. Compute the critical buckling stress. **Step 1.** Plate data: $$h = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m},\quad b = 0.35\,\text{m},\quad E = 12 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa},\quad \nu = 0.32$$ **Step 2.** D: $$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{(12\times10^9)(4\times10^{-3})^3}{12(1-0.32^2)}$$ $$= \frac{12\times10^9 \times 64\times10^{-9}}{12(1-0.1024)} = \frac{768}{10.7712} \approx 71.30\,\text{N·m}$$ **Step 3.** Critical load per unit width: $$N_{cr} = \frac{4\pi^2 D}{b^2} = \frac{4(9.8696)(71.30)}{(0.35)^2} = \frac{2814.5}{0.1225} \approx 22\,977\,\text{N/m}$$ **Step 4.** Convert to stress: $$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{N_{cr}}{h} = \frac{22\,977}{4\times10^{-3}} \approx 5.74\,\text{MPa}$$ **Step 5.** Conclusion: The panel can withstand roughly **5.74 MPa** of in-plane hull-girder compression before wrinkling. Compare with the actual design compression in bottom panels of a 6 m dinghy (≈ 8–12 MPa) — therefore, **this 4 mm skin would buckle** and either thickness or frame spacing must be increased. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Section Modulus and Bending Stress of a Single Skin Plate For a homogeneous single skin plate of thickness $h$ under pure bending moment $M$ per unit width: **Step 1.** Section modulus per unit width (symmetric slab): $$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{bh^3/12}{h/2} = \frac{bh^2}{6}$$ **Step 2.** Per unit width ($b = 1$): $$Z = \frac{h^2}{6}$$ **Step 3.** Bending stress at the outer fibre: $$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{6M}{h^2}$$ **Step 4.** Maximum deflection of a simply supported rectangular plate under uniform pressure $q$: $$\delta_{max} = \frac{\alpha\, q\, a^4}{D}$$ where $\alpha$ is the deflection coefficient depending on $a/b$ ratio. For a long plate ($a/b \to \infty$), $\alpha \to 4/\pi^5 \approx 0.01302$. For a square plate ($a = b$), $\alpha \approx 0.00406$. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cross-Section of a Typical Single Skin Hull Wall The figure below maps the *through-thickness architecture* of a single skin laminate — note that there is **no core layer** anywhere. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["Outer Gel Coat
0.4 to 0.6 mm
UV Barrier, No Load"] node2["Ply 1 CSM
Chopped Strand Mat
300 gsm"] node3["Ply 2 Woven Roving
WR 600 gsm
0 deg and 90 deg"] node4["Ply 3 Biaxial Cloth
Plus Minus 45 deg
Shear Carrier"] node5["Ply 4 Woven Roving
WR 600 gsm
0 deg and 90 deg"] node6["Ply 5 CSM
Resin Rich Inner
300 gsm"] node7["Inner Flow Coat
Optional
0.2 to 0.4 mm"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 style node1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style node7 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style node3 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style node4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style node5 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **sand-coloured** layers (node1 and node7) are unreinforced cosmetic coats. - The **blue** layers (node3 and node5) carry longitudinal and transverse in-plane loads. - The **green** layer (node4) is the dedicated shear carrier. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Single Skin Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph pre["Stage A Preparation"] A1["Mold Inspection
and Cleaning"] A2["Application of
Release Agent"] A3["Application of
Gel Coat"] end subgraph lay["Stage B Lay-up"] B1["Lay Ply 1 CSM
Wet out"] B2["Consolidate
with Roller"] B3["Lay Plies 2 to N
Woven Biaxial WR"] B4["Final
Consolidation"] end subgraph cure["Stage C Cure and Demould"] C1["Ambient or
Accelerated Cure"] C2["Demould"] C3["Trim and
Quality Check"] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 style A3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style B1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style B3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style C1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` **What this block-flow shows:** The **Stage A → Stage B → Stage C** cascade is the **mandatory process flow** for any single skin boat. Skipping consolidation (B2 or B4) leaves **air voids** that act as crack initiation sites — a common KTU exam topic. ## 4.3 Loading-Bearing Functional Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ext["External Loads on Hull"] L1["Hydrostatic
Pressure"] L2["Wave Slamming
Impact"] L3["Hull Girder
Bending"] L4["In-plane Shear
Torsion"] end subgraph sk["Single Skin Functions"] F1["Bending
Resistor"] F2["Shear
Carrier"] F3["Tension
Membrane"] F4["Buckling
Resistor"] end L1 --> F1 L2 --> F1 L3 --> F1 L3 --> F3 L4 --> F2 F1 --> F4 style L1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style L2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style L3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style L4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style F1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style F2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style F3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style F4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Notice how **all four external load types** converge onto Function F1 (bending). Because single skin has no core, bending and buckling (F4) are inseparable concerns — a designer must satisfy both simultaneously. ## 4.4 Decision Tree: When to Choose Single Skin vs Sandwich ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{"Boat Length?"} Q2{"Critical Bending Stiffness
Required?"} Q3{"Weight Budget Tight?"} A1["Choose SINGLE SKIN
Simple and Cheap"] A2["Choose SANDWICH
High D for Weight"] Q1 -- less than 5 m --> A1 Q1 -- between 5 and 12 m --> Q2 Q1 -- more than 12 m --> A2 Q2 -- Low --> A1 Q2 -- High --> Q3 Q3 -- Yes --> A2 Q3 -- No --> A1 style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style A2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define single skin construction in composite boat building. List any three distinguishing features. **Model Answer:** Single skin construction is a composite hull-building method in which the structural laminate is built up directly on the mould surface using successive layers of fibre reinforcement and resin, **without any core material** between the layers. Three distinguishing features: 1. The hull wall is a **monolithic laminate** of uniform or quasi-uniform thickness. 2. All mechanical properties — stiffness, strength, and buckling resistance — are derived solely from the resin + reinforcement combination. 3. **Both bending and shear** are carried by the same single laminate, which makes the structure **buckling-critical**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Name the typical reinforcement materials used in single skin boat hulls and state one role of each. **Model Answer:** - **Chopped Strand Mat (CSM):** Resin carrier and surface smoothness; provides isotropic in-plane behaviour and prevents print-through of woven fabrics. - **Woven Roving (WR):** Primary tensile and compressive load carrier along 0° and 90° directions. - **Biaxial Cloth (±45°):** Dedicated **shear carrier**; resists torsional and in-plane shear loads. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Choice 1]** #### (a) Derive the expression for the bending stiffness **D** of a single skin laminate starting from Classical Laminate Theory. **[7 Marks]** **Step-by-step model solution:** **Step 1.** State the CLT definition of the bending stiffness matrix: $$[D] = \frac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}\left(z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3\right)$$ **Step 2.** For a single skin laminate where all plies are of the **same transversely isotropic material** (e.g., E-glass/polyester) with reduced stiffnesses $\bar{Q}_{11}, \bar{Q}_{12}, \bar{Q}_{66}$: $$\bar{Q}_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}},\quad \bar{Q}_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}},\quad \bar{Q}_{12} = \frac{\nu_{21}E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$ **Step 3.** For a balanced stack (CSM + WR + Biaxial sequence), the coupling matrix $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$. **Step 4.** Using the property $\sum (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3) = h^3/12$ for $n$ identical plies summing to thickness $h$: $$D_{11} = \frac{\bar{Q}_{11}\, h^3}{12} = \frac{E\, h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$ **Step 5.** By symmetry $D_{22} = D_{11}$ and $D_{12} = \nu D_{11}$: $$\boxed{\;D = \frac{E\,h^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}\;}$$ **Conversion logic in plain words:** Each ply contributes to the overall bending stiffness proportional to (a) its own stiffness $\bar{Q}$, and (b) the cube of its distance from the mid-plane. Because all plies here are identical, the distance-cube sum collapses neatly to $h^3/12$, giving the classic **isotropic plate formula**. --- #### (b) A single-skin bottom panel of a 6 m sailing dinghy is 5 mm thick, simply supported on four edges with $a = 1000$ mm, $b = 400$ mm. The material has $E = 12$ GPa, $\nu = 0.32$. The panel carries uniform pressure $q = 15$ kPa. Compute (i) the bending stiffness D, (ii) the maximum deflection, (iii) the critical buckling load $N_{cr}$. **[7 Marks]** **Step-by-step model solution:** **Step 1. Given data:** $$h = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m},\quad a = 1.0\,\text{m},\quad b = 0.4\,\text{m},\quad E = 12\times10^9\,\text{Pa}$$ $$\nu = 0.32,\quad q = 15\times10^3\,\text{Pa}$$ **Step 2. Bending stiffness D:** $$D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{(12\times10^9)(125\times10^{-9})}{12(1-0.1024)}$$ $$= \frac{1500}{10.7712} \approx 139.26\,\text{N·m}$$ **Step 3. Maximum deflection:** For $a/b = 2.5$, the deflection coefficient $\alpha \approx 0.00589$ (Timoshenko table): $$\delta_{max} = \frac{\alpha\, q\, a^4}{D} = \frac{(0.00589)(15\times10^3)(1.0)^4}{139.26}$$ $$= \frac{88.35}{139.26} \approx 0.634 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 0.634\,\text{mm}$$ This is well below $a/200 = 5$ mm, so serviceability is satisfied. **Step 4. Critical buckling load:** Since $a/b = 2.5 > 2$, treat as a long plate, $k_c = 4$: $$N_{cr} = \frac{k_c\,\pi^2 D}{b^2} = \frac{4(9.8696)(139.26)}{(0.4)^2}$$ $$= \frac{5497.4}{0.16} \approx 34\,358\,\text{N/m}$$ **Step 5. Critical buckling stress:** $$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{N_{cr}}{h} = \frac{34\,358}{5\times10^{-3}} \approx 6.87\,\text{MPa}$$ **Step 6. Comment:** The panel safely resists a 15 kPa slamming load without exceeding serviceability or buckling limits. Buckling resistance is the **marginal** criterion. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Choice 2]** #### (a) Explain the **laminate stacking sequence** recommended for a typical single skin boat hull and justify the placement of each ply. **[7 Marks]** **Step-by-step model answer:** The canonical lay-up sequence from the **mould (outer) face** to the **inner face** is: | Ply # | Material | Function | |-------|----------|----------| | 1 | Gel coat | Cosmetic, UV & osmosis barrier | | 2 | CSM (300 gsm) | Resin-rich print blocker; isolates gel coat from structural cracks | | 3 | Woven Roving (600 gsm) | Major **longitudinal** tensile/compressive carrier | | 4 | Biaxial ±45° (600 gsm) | **Shear** carrier; resists torsion and slamming | | 5 | Woven Roving (600 gsm) | Major **transverse** and secondary longitudinal carrier | | 6 | CSM (300 gsm) | Resin-rich inner surface for sealing & secondary crack arrest | | 7 | Flow coat (optional) | Inner cosmetic surface | **Justification logic (Step-by-step):** 1. **Gel coat first** so that the cosmetic surface contacts the mould — blemish-free. 2. **CSM next** because it conforms easily to mould curvature, is cheap, and "hides" the print-through of woven fabrics. 3. **Woven Roving** forms the **outer load-bearing skin** at a distance $h/2$ from mid-plane, giving **maximum bending leverage**. 4. **Biaxial ±45°** placed at mid-thickness contributes nothing to bending but contributes **maximum shear stiffness**. 5. **Inner WR** mirrors the outer WR for symmetry (avoids warp, lift, and bending-twist coupling). 6. **Inner CSM** gives a resin-rich surface that resists micro-cracking and moisture ingress. 7. The whole stack is **symmetric** about the mid-plane, which is the **golden rule** of single skin lay-up. **Conversion logic in plain words:** Think of the laminate as a sandwich-without-the-core — the outer and inner woven rovings are like the "bread" resisting bending, while the ±45° in the middle is like the "filling" resisting shear. CSM is the jam — it does not load-bear but protects everything around it. --- #### (b) Compare single skin and sandwich construction in composite boat hulls under the following heads: (i) stiffness-to-weight ratio, (ii) impact resistance, (iii) repair complexity, (iv) cost. **[7 Marks]** **Step-by-step model answer (use a comparison table):** | Parameter | Single Skin | Sandwich (for comparison) | |-----------|-------------|---------------------------| | **Stiffness / Weight (D/ρ)** | **Low** — D scales with $h^3$ but ρ with $h$, so $D/\rho \propto h^2$ | **Very high** — D scales with $h^3$ of total thickness but ρ stays low because core is light | | **Impact Resistance** | **High** — solid laminate absorbs energy through local plastic deformation | **Low** — core can be crushed easily (low-energy failure mode) | | **Repair Complexity** | **Simple** — patch lay-up follows identical process as original build | **Complex** — core must be re-bonded, moisture removed from delamination | | **Cost** | **Low** — single tool, simple materials, no core-cutting step | **High** — core material, vacuum bagging, secondary bonding | | **Best Application** | Racing dinghies, canoes, kayak shells | Yacht hulls > 9 m, superstructures, deck-cores | **Synthesis line for the answer:** Single skin is the **workhorse** of small-craft composite construction — heavier and less stiff for a given weight, but rugged, cheap, and easy to repair. Sandwich dominates when **bending stiffness per kilogram** is the dominant design driver (large yachts, performance multihulls). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in Single Skin questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the $(1-\nu^2)$ term** in the denominator of the bending stiffness formula D. Marks lost: 1–2 per question. > 2. **Mixing up section modulus** ($h^2/6$) with second moment of area ($h^3/12$). The latter is per unit width; the former is per unit width for bending stress. > 3. **Treating CSM as a structural ply** — CSM is primarily a cosmetic/resin carrier. Examiners expect students to identify WR and Biaxial as the load-bearing plies. > 4. **In buckling questions, students often forget** that $k_c = 4$ applies *only* to a long simply-supported plate ($a/b \geq 2$). For square plates, $k_c = 4\pi^2/(\text{appropriate factor})$. > 5. **Never write "fibreglass" alone** in KTU exams — always specify **"E-glass / isophthalic polyester"** or equivalent, as it shows depth of understanding. > 6. **Stacking sequence must be symmetric** about the mid-plane. Asymmetric lay-ups warp — examiners will deduct for this oversight. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Single Skin Construction** > > - [x] Single skin = monolithic laminate, **no core material** between plies. > - [x] Typical thickness range: **3–9 mm**. > - [x] Stacking order (outer → inner): **Gel coat → CSM → WR → ±45° Biaxial → WR → CSM → (Flow coat)**. > - [x] Symmetric stacking is **mandatory** to avoid warp. > - [x] Bending stiffness: $D = E h^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$. > - [x] Section modulus per unit width: $Z = h^2/6$. > - [x] Critical buckling load for long simply-supported plate: $N_{cr} = 4\pi^2 D / b^2$. > - [x] Failure modes to check: **Material yielding → Plate buckling → Serviceability deflection**. > - [x] Single skin is **buckling-critical** — no core means no separate shear-resisting element. > - [x] WR carries **axial loads**; ±45° biaxial carries **shear**; CSM is **cosmetic/resin carrier**. > - [x] Fibre volume fraction target: **25–40%** by weight. > - [x] Typical resin:glass weight ratio: **2.0:1 to 3.0:1**. > - [x] Typical $E$ for E-glass/polyester single skin: **7–14 GPa**. > - [x] Used for boats **under 5 m**, racing dinghies, canoes, kayaks, and as **inner cosmetic skin** of two-stage moulded hulls. > - [x] Compared to sandwich: lower stiffness-to-weight, higher impact resistance, simpler repair, lower cost. ---

topics.title: Sandwich Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sandwich Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sandwich Construction** is a structural composite configuration consisting of two thin, strong, and stiff outer skins (also called face sheets or laminates) bonded to a thick, lightweight central core material using an adhesive film or through co-curing in a single laminate lay-up. The skins carry the bending and in-plane loads (membrane stresses), while the core stabilizes the skins against buckling, carries transverse shear loads, and maintains the spacing between the skins to maximize the second moment of area.

In the context of KTU Module 2 of *PENST742 – Composite Boat Design*, sandwich construction is studied as the dominant lightweight hull, deck, bulkhead, and superstructure fabrication philosophy for modern GRP, CFRP, and aramid-reinforced marine vessels (per ISO 12215 and DNV GL standards).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** A sandwich panel is a stressed-skin structure in which two relatively thin, high-strength facing laminates are adhesively bonded to a low-density, shear-resisting core, producing a structural element with a flexural rigidity many times greater than that of the solid laminate of equivalent mass per unit area.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "I-Beam" of the Sea

Imagine holding a flat steel plate horizontally and trying to bend it — it deflects easily because all the material is clustered near the neutral axis where bending stresses are minimal. To make it stiffer without adding much weight, you weld a vertical web at the center (the web carries shear) and keep most of the steel far from the neutral axis (the flanges carry bending). That is exactly an **I-beam**.

Now think of a boat hull panel:
- The **top and bottom skins** of the sandwich act like the **flanges** of the I-beam.
- The **foam / honeycomb / balsa core** acts like the **vertical web**.
- The result is a panel that is **8 to 20 times stiffer** than a single-skin laminate of the **same weight**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for Students:**
> Think of a sandwich you eat — two slices of bread (strong, thin skin) with butter/cheese filling (soft, thick core). You can press a single slice of bread and it folds easily, but the assembled "sandwich" resists bending remarkably well, despite being light. The bread carries tension/compression; the filling maintains separation and resists shear collapse.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics & Constants

The following reference constants and design metrics are accepted by KTU valuation, ISO 12215-5, and DNV GL rules:

- **Effective panel thickness:** $t_{eq} = t_f + t_c + t_f$ (mm)
- **Minimum skin thickness (KTU/ISO rule):** typically **$t_f \geq 0.7$ mm** for hand lay-up; **$t_f \geq 1.0$ mm** for structural hull bottoms.
- **Core density range:** $\rho_c$ between **60 kg/m³** (PVC foam) and **400 kg/m³** (end-grain balsa).
- **Design shear strain limit (core):** $\gamma_c \leq 0.05$ for PVC/Airex foams; **$\gamma_c \leq 0.04$** for balsa cores.
- **Service temperature limit (PVC foam):** **$-30^\circ$C to $+80^\circ$C**; **PET foam** up to **$+150^\circ$C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Whenever a question asks *why sandwich construction is preferred in boat building*, your answer MUST mention all four: **(i) high specific stiffness, (ii) high specific strength, (iii) thermal & acoustic insulation, (iv) buckling resistance of the skins.**

---

## 1.4 Primary Components of a Marine Sandwich Panel

| Component | Typical Materials | Primary Function |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Inner Skin** | Woven roving, biaxial E-glass, vinylester laminate | Tension/compression face; moisture barrier |
| **Core** | PVC foam (Divinycell H-grade), SAN foam, balsa wood, aluminium honeycomb | Shear transfer; maintains skin spacing; insulation |
| **Outer Skin** | Biaxial glass + CSM, carbon/epoxy | Compression/tension face; impact & abrasion resistance |
| **Adhesive Interface** | Epoxy/polyester film adhesive, co-cured resin bond | Shear transfer zone; moisture seal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why foam cores dominate boat building:** They are closed-cell (no water absorption), machinable, have good fatigue life, and can be thermoformed into curved hull shapes. Balsa is used where high localized compression and stiffness are needed (e.g., transoms), and honeycomb is reserved for high-end racing yachts and military craft.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory – How a Sandwich Panel Carries Load

A sandwich panel subjected to a transverse bending load (such as hydrostatic pressure on a hull bottom) experiences the following internal stress distribution:

1. **The two skins are widely separated** — therefore the second moment of area ($I$) of the cross-section is dramatically increased.
2. **The upper skin (in a positive bending case)** is loaded in **compression**.
3. **The lower skin** is loaded in **tension**.
4. **The thin skins would individually buckle under these compressive/shear loads**, so the core provides **lateral support** (continuous elastic foundation) — this is the **core's "stabilizing" role**.
5. **The core itself carries the transverse shear force** ($V$) acting vertically through the panel — this is the **core's "shear" role**.
6. **The adhesive layer** must transfer shear stress between skin and core; if it fails, the skins peel off — known as **delamination** or **skin-core debond**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Logic for KTU Answers:**
> - Why thin skins? → They resist in-plane axial loads (membrane action) very efficiently.
> - Why thick core? → It moves the skins far apart, boosting $I$ (and hence $D$, the flexural rigidity).
> - Why not solid laminate? → A solid laminate of equal stiffness would weigh 5×–10× more, defeating the lightweight design intent.

---

## 2.2 Failure Modes of Sandwich Panels (KTU Must-Know)

A KTU answer that lists failure modes is considered complete only if it covers the **five classical modes**:

1. **Skin failure by yielding or rupture** (tension/compression of face sheets).
2. **Core shear failure** (shear crimping of the core under transverse load).
3. **Skin wrinkling** (local buckling of the compression skin into the core).
4. **Core indentation / dimpling** (local compression failure beneath a point load).
5. **Skin-core debonding / delamination** (failure at the adhesive interface).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *which is the most common failure in marine sandwich decks?*, the answer is **core shear failure under hull slamming loads**, because deck panels experience high transverse shear and low bending moments compared to hull bottoms.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

All formulas below are board-acceptable and align with ISO 12215-5 / Allen (1969) sandwich theory:

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2}$ | Flexural rigidity per unit width (faces only), where $d = t_c + t_f$ approx | N·m²/m |
| 2 | $D_{total} = \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, (t_c + t_f)^2}{2} + \dfrac{E_f \, t_f^{3}}{6}$ | Total panel bending stiffness per unit width | N·m²/m |
| 3 | $\tau_c = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | Average transverse shear stress in core | Pa |
| 4 | $\gamma_c = \dfrac{\tau_c}{G_c}$ | Shear strain in core | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{M}{d \, t_f}$ | Bending stress in face sheets | Pa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{wr} = 0.5 \, (E_f \, E_c \, G_c)^{1/3}$ | Wrinkling stress of face sheet (Allen, 1969) | Pa |
| 7 | $w_{max} = \dfrac{q \, L^{4}}{D_{total}} \cdot k_1$ | Maximum deflection of simply-supported panel under UDL | m |
| 8 | $F_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D}{L^{2}}$ | Critical buckling load per unit width | N/m |
| 9 | $EI_{eq} = E_f \, \dfrac{b \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2}$ | Equivalent flexural rigidity of sandwich beam (Navier) | N·m² |
| 10 | $\rho_{eq} = \dfrac{2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c}{t_{total}}$ | Equivalent panel density (for weight calc) | kg/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:** $E_f$ = skin Young's modulus; $E_c$ = core Young's modulus (compression); $G_c$ = core shear modulus; $d$ = distance between skin centroids; $V$ = transverse shear force per unit width; $q$ = distributed load; $L$ = span.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sandwich construction is the **dominant philosophy** in:
- **Hull bottoms & sides** of pleasure craft (Bayliner, Sunseeker, Jeanneau series).
- **Decks and superstructures** of high-speed patrol boats (Royal Navy RHIBs).
- **Racing yacht hulls** (America's Cup AC75 foils — 100% carbon/Nomex honeycomb sandwich).
- **Wind turbine blades** (DFIG 80 m blades are entirely sandwich).
- **Aerospace floor panels** (Boeing 787 floor grid is carbon/PMI foam).

In **boat design specifically**, sandwich construction reduces displacement → increases speed → reduces fuel burn → improves stability (lower VCG). It also gives a quieter ride (damping from the viscoelastic core).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU One-Liner to Memorize:** *"Sandwich panels deliver the highest flexural rigidity per unit mass of any monolithic or laminated plate form used in marine engineering."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Sandwich Panel Flexural Rigidity ($D_{sandwich}$)

We derive the flexural rigidity per unit width of a sandwich panel using classical laminated plate theory, treating the two skins as thin membranes located at $\pm d/2$ from the neutral axis, and the core as carrying only shear.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Let the panel have:
- Skin thickness: $t_f$ (each, top and bottom — assumed equal for symmetric sandwich)
- Core thickness: $t_c$
- Total thickness: $t = t_c + 2t_f$
- Distance between skin centroids: $d = t_c + t_f$

The neutral axis (NA) coincides with the geometric mid-plane for a symmetric sandwich, because the skin stiffness contributions are equal and opposite.

**Step 2 — Write the second moment of area per unit width about the NA.**

Using the parallel axis theorem for each face sheet (treating each as a thin rectangular strip of width $b = 1$):

$$
I_{skin} = \frac{1 \cdot t_f^{3}}{12} + (1 \cdot t_f)\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two skins (top and bottom are identical).**

$$
I_{panel} = 2 \, I_{skin} = \frac{t_f^{3}}{6} + t_f \left(\frac{d^{2}}{2}\right)
$$

Simplifying the second term:

$$
I_{panel} = \frac{t_f^{3}}{6} + \frac{t_f \, d^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the skin modulus to get flexural rigidity.**
The core is **neglected** in the bending-stiffness calculation because $E_c \ll E_f$ (typically $E_c / E_f \approx 1/100$). This is the standard sandwich approximation:

$$
D = E_f \, I_{panel} = \frac{E_f \, t_f^{3}}{6} + \frac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the thin-skin simplification.**
Since the skin is thin ($t_f \ll d$), the cubic term $t_f^{3}/6$ is much smaller than the $d^{2}$ term (often $< 2\%$), so:

$$
\boxed{\,D \;\approx\; \frac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2}\,}
$$

This is the **single most important equation** for KTU answers on sandwich construction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Core Shear Stress Under Transverse Load

A sandwich beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $q$ (e.g., hydrostatic pressure $p = \rho g h$ converted to N/m²). The reactions at supports are $R = qL/2$. The maximum transverse shear force is:

$$
V_{max} = \frac{q L}{2}
$$

The shear force per unit width carried by the core is:

$$
V_{core} = \tau_c \cdot d
$$

Equating and solving for $\tau_c$:

$$
\tau_c = \frac{V_{max}}{d} = \frac{q L}{2 d}
$$

The shear strain in the core:

$$
\gamma_c = \frac{\tau_c}{G_c} = \frac{q L}{2 d \, G_c}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Check:** The calculated $\gamma_c$ MUST be $\leq$ the allowable shear strain ($\gamma_{allow}$) of the core material. For Divinycell H-grade PVC foam, $\gamma_{allow} \approx 0.05$ (5%). Exceeding this causes **core shear crimping failure**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Skin Wrinkling Stress (Allen, 1969)

When the compression skin is too thin or the core too compliant, the skin buckles into a half-wave pattern *into* the core — called **wrinkling**. Allen derived the critical wrinkling stress by treating the skin as a plate on an elastic (Winkler) foundation of stiffness $G_c / t_c$:

$$
\sigma_{wr} = 0.5 \left( E_f \, E_c \, G_c \right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation of the result:**

1. The skin wants to buckle at wavelength $\lambda$.
2. The core resists this by shear stiffness $G_c / t_c$ per unit area.
3. Equating buckling load to foundation resistance gives the optimum $\lambda$ and the peak $\sigma_{wr}$.
4. The 0.5 factor is a numerical correction for boundary conditions and skin-core interaction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sandwich vs. Single-Skin Laminate

For the same panel weight per unit area ($w = \rho_f \cdot t_f + \rho_c \cdot t_c / 2$ approx), compare:

$$
\frac{D_{sandwich}}{D_{single}} = \frac{E_f t_f d^2 / 2}{E_f t_{mono}^{3}/12}
$$

For typical values $t_f = 1$ mm, $t_c = 25$ mm, $t_{mono} = 5$ mm, $E_f = 25$ GPa (glass/polyester):

$$
\frac{D_{sandwich}}{D_{single}} = \frac{6 \, d^{2}}{t_{mono}^{2}} = \frac{6 \times (0.0255)^{2}}{(0.005)^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 6.50 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 156
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sandwich panel is **~150 times stiffer** than the single-skin laminate of equal weight, while weighing almost the same. This is the engineering rationale for sandwich construction in every high-performance boat.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A marine sandwich deck panel has the following properties: $t_f = 1.5$ mm (each face, E-glass/polyester, $E_f = 22$ GPa); $t_c = 30$ mm (PVC foam, $G_c = 35$ MPa). Span $L = 1.2$ m, simply supported. Uniform hydrostatic equivalent load $q = 4$ kN/m². Calculate (a) panel flexural rigidity, (b) maximum core shear stress, (c) verify whether core shear failure occurs.

**Solution:**

**(a) Flexural rigidity:**

$$
d = t_c + t_f = 30 + 1.5 = 31.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0315 \text{ m}
$$

$$
D = \frac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2} = \frac{22 \times 10^{9} \times 0.0015 \times (0.0315)^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
D = \frac{22 \times 10^{9} \times 0.0015 \times 9.92 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 16{,}368 \text{ N·m}^{2}/\text{m}
$$

**(b) Maximum core shear stress:**

$$
V_{max} = \frac{q L}{2} = \frac{4000 \times 1.2}{2} = 2400 \text{ N/m}
$$

$$
\tau_c = \frac{V_{max}}{d} = \frac{2400}{0.0315} = 76{,}190 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.076 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(c) Core shear strain check:**

$$
\gamma_c = \frac{\tau_c}{G_c} = \frac{0.076}{35} = 0.00217 = 0.217\%
$$

Since $0.217\% \ll \gamma_{allow} = 5\%$, the **core is SAFE** in shear. The panel design is acceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Insight:** Step-by-step solutions where intermediate numerical values are shown (like $d = 0.0315$ m above) consistently score 13–14 marks out of 14. Skipping intermediate steps usually caps the answer at 9–10 marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define sandwich construction. List any four advantages of using sandwich panels in boat building.**

**Model Answer:**
Sandwich construction is a structural composite in which two thin, high-strength face sheets (skins) are adhesively bonded to a thick, lightweight core, producing a structural element with a flexural rigidity far greater than that of a solid laminate of equal mass.

**Four advantages (any four, 0.5 mark each):**
1. Very high specific stiffness (stiffness-to-weight ratio).
2. Excellent thermal and acoustic insulation (foam core dampens noise and heat).
3. High buckling resistance of the face sheets due to core stabilization.
4. Reduced overall weight → higher speed, better fuel efficiency, improved stability (lower vertical centre of gravity).
5. Improved impact resistance (core absorbs slamming energy).
6. Simplified stiffener design — panels span longer distances between frames.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the core in a marine sandwich panel. Name two core materials commonly used in boat building.**

**Model Answer:**
The core in a sandwich panel performs two principal mechanical roles:
1. **Shear transfer role** — it carries the transverse shear force between the two face sheets, acting like the web of an I-beam.
2. **Stabilization role** — it provides continuous lateral (elastic) support to the compression skin, preventing it from buckling/wrinkling under in-plane compressive loads.

Additionally, the core maintains the geometric spacing between the skins, maximizing the second moment of area, and provides thermal/acoustic insulation.

**Two core materials (any two, 0.5 mark each):**
1. **Closed-cell PVC foam** (e.g., Divinycell H-grade, Airex C70) — most common in hulls and decks.
2. **End-grain balsa wood** — used in transoms, stringers, and high-load areas.
3. SAN or PET foam (for higher temperature).
4. Aluminium honeycomb (for racing yachts and superstructures).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the constructional features of a typical marine sandwich hull panel. Identify the function of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sandwich hull bottom panel has the following specifications: each face sheet is 2.0 mm thick E-glass/polyester laminate with $E_f = 24$ GPa; the core is 40 mm thick PVC foam with $G_c = 40$ MPa. The panel is 1.5 m $\times$ 1.0 m, simply supported on all four edges, and is subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure of $q = 5$ kN/m². Compute (i) the flexural rigidity per unit width, and (ii) the maximum shear stress in the core. Verify if the core is safe, given allowable shear strain $= 0.04$.

---

### **Question A – Model Solution**

**(a) Constructional Features of a Marine Sandwich Hull Panel**

**Sketch (describe in words — students draw in exam):**
```
[Outer Skin: 1.5–2.0 mm biaxial glass + CSM, with gelcoat]
[Epoxy adhesive film ~150 gsm]
[Core: 30–50 mm PVC foam, e.g., Divinycell H100]
[Epoxy adhesive film ~150 gsm]
[Inner Skin: 0.7–1.5 mm biaxial glass with liner coating]
```

**Functions of each component (1.5 marks each):**
