1. **Outer (external) skin** — Carries in-plane compressive and tensile stresses due to bending from hydrostatic pressure; resists impact, abrasion, and UV; provides class-approved moisture barrier; carries gelcoat for aesthetic finish.
2. **Core** — Maintains spacing between the skins (increasing $I$ and hence $D$); carries the transverse shear force $V$; provides elastic lateral support to the compression skin against wrinkling; offers thermal and acoustic insulation.
3. **Adhesive bond layer** — Transfers shear stress between skin and core (must have peel strength > 2.5 N/mm per ISO 12215); seals against moisture ingress at the skin-core interface.
4. **Inner (interior) skin** — Carries the bending tensile/compressive stresses on the inner face; provides interior finish (liner coating); acts as secondary moisture barrier.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $t_f = 2.0$ mm $= 0.002$ m
- $E_f = 24 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $t_c = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m
- $G_c = 40 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $L = 1.5$ m, $b = 1.0$ m
- $q = 5 \times 10^{3}$ N/m²
- $\gamma_{allow} = 0.04$

**Step 1 — Compute distance between skin centroids $d$:**

$$
d = t_c + t_f = 0.040 + 0.002 = 0.042 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute flexural rigidity per unit width:**

$$
D = \frac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
D = \frac{24 \times 10^{9} \times 0.002 \times (0.042)^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
D = \frac{24 \times 10^{9} \times 0.002 \times 0.001764}{2} = \frac{84{,}672}{2} = 42{,}336 \text{ N·m}^{2}/\text{m}
$$

**(i) Flexural rigidity $\;D = 42{,}336$ N·m²/m $\;\approx 42.3$ kN·m²/m**

**Step 3 — Compute maximum transverse shear force per unit width:**

For a simply-supported panel, $V_{max} \approx q L / 2$ (treating as one-way spanning between frames):

$$
V_{max} = \frac{q \, L}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{3} \times 1.5}{2} = 3750 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute core shear stress:**

$$
\tau_c = \frac{V_{max}}{d} = \frac{3750}{0.042} = 89{,}285 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.0893 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Maximum core shear stress $\;\tau_c \approx 0.089$ MPa**

**Step 5 — Compute core shear strain and verify:**

$$
\gamma_c = \frac{\tau_c}{G_c} = \frac{0.0893}{40} = 2.23 \times 10^{-3} = 0.223\%
$$

**Comparison:** $0.223\% \;<\; \gamma_{allow} = 4\%$ → **Core is SAFE in shear.** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently forget to add $t_f$ when computing $d = t_c + t_f$, leading to $d = 0.040$ m and a $D$ value that is ~9% low — this typically costs 1 mark.
> - Common error: writing $V_{max} = q L$ instead of $q L / 2$. Remember, $V_{max}$ is at the support, equal to the reaction force.
> - Never quote the answer without UNITS. Marks are deducted for missing Pa, N/m², N·m²/m.
> - Always state the design verdict explicitly: *"Core is SAFE / NOT SAFE"*. Without this verdict sentence, 0.5–1 mark is lost.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **five classical failure modes** of sandwich panels under marine service loads. For each mode, state the governing parameter and the typical repair/preventive measure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A foam-core sandwich panel is required to replace a single-skin laminate in a boat deck. The single-skin laminate is 6 mm thick E-glass/polyester with $E_f = 22$ GPa and density $\rho_f = 1500$ kg/m³. Design an equivalent sandwich panel using 1.0 mm skins and 25 mm PVC foam core (density $\rho_c = 100$ kg/m³) such that the **flexural rigidity matches or exceeds** that of the single-skin laminate. Compute the percentage weight saving achieved.

---

### **Question B – Model Solution**

**(a) Five Classical Failure Modes of Marine Sandwich Panels**

| # | Failure Mode | Governing Parameter | Typical Cause in Boats | Prevention / Repair |
|---|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Skin yielding / rupture** | $\sigma_f \leq \sigma_{y}$ (skin tensile strength) | Slamming loads, heavy point loads | Increase skin thickness; use carbon reinforcement locally. |
| 2 | **Core shear failure (crimping)** | $\gamma_c \leq \gamma_{allow}$ | High transverse shear (small span, heavy load) | Increase core thickness; use higher-density core; add intermediate frames. |
| 3 | **Skin wrinkling** | $\sigma_c \leq \sigma_{wr}$ (Allen) | Compression skin too thin or core too soft | Increase skin thickness; use higher $G_c$ core; reduce span. |
| 4 | **Core indentation / dimpling** | Local contact stress under point load | Foot traffic on deck; dropped tools; stanchion bases | Use higher-density core locally; add insert plates under fittings. |
| 5 | **Skin-core debonding** (delamination) | $\tau_{adh} \leq \tau_{allow}$ at interface | Poor surface prep; moisture ingress; impact damage | Proper peel-ply + abrade + solvent wipe before bonding; vacuum-bag cure. |

**Real-world boat examples (for higher marks):**
1. **Hull bottom slamming** → core shear crimping (failure 2).
2. **Mooring cleat base** → core indentation (failure 4).
3. **Chainplate attachment** → skin-core debonding (failure 5).
4. **Racing yacht hull at frame** → skin wrinkling (failure 3).

---

**(b) Sandwich Design for Equivalent Flexural Rigidity**

**Single-skin laminate (baseline):**

$$
D_{mono} = \frac{E_f \, t_{mono}^{3}}{12}
$$

$$
D_{mono} = \frac{22 \times 10^{9} \times (0.006)^{3}}{12} = \frac{22 \times 10^{9} \times 2.16 \times 10^{-7}}{12}
$$

$$
D_{mono} = 396 \text{ N·m}^{2}/\text{m}
$$

**Sandwich panel design:**

$$
d = t_c + t_f = 0.025 + 0.001 = 0.026 \text{ m}
$$

$$
D_{sand} = \frac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2} = \frac{22 \times 10^{9} \times 0.001 \times (0.026)^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
D_{sand} = \frac{22 \times 10^{9} \times 0.001 \times 6.76 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 7436 \text{ N·m}^{2}/\text{m}
$$

**Comparison:** $D_{sand} / D_{mono} = 7436 / 396 = 18.78$. The sandwich is **~19× stiffer** than the single-skin panel. ✓ (Massively exceeds the baseline.)

**Step — Compute weight per unit area (kg/m²):**

Single-skin: $w_{mono} = \rho_f \cdot t_{mono} = 1500 \times 0.006 = 9.0$ kg/m²

Sandwich: $w_{sand} = 2 \rho_f t_f + \rho_c t_c = 2(1500)(0.001) + (100)(0.025) = 3.0 + 2.5 = 5.5$ kg/m²

**Percentage weight saving:**

$$
\Delta w\% = \frac{w_{mono} - w_{sand}}{w_{mono}} \times 100 = \frac{9.0 - 5.5}{9.0} \times 100 = 38.9\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout for Part B (b):** Many students forget that the sandwich has **two** skins, so the skin mass is $2 \rho_f t_f$ (not just $\rho_f t_f$). This single oversight can wipe out 2 marks in a 14-mark question. Also, density of foam in some textbooks is quoted in kg/m³, NOT g/cm³ — unit conversion is critical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 30 Bullet Points Before KTU Exam**

1. **Definition:** Two thin high-strength skins + thick low-density core bonded together.
2. **Skin role:** Carry in-plane bending stresses (tension/compression).
3. **Core role:** Carry transverse shear + stabilize skin against buckling.
4. **I-beam analogy:** Skins = flanges; Core = web.
5. **Key formula:** $D \approx \dfrac{E_f \, t_f \, d^{2}}{2}$ — memorize this FIRST.
6. **Distance $d$:** Always $d = t_c + t_f$ (NOT just $t_c$).
7. **Core shear stress:** $\tau_c = \dfrac{V}{d}$ — important for failure check.
8. **Core shear strain:** $\gamma_c = \dfrac{\tau_c}{G_c}$ — must be $\leq \gamma_{allow}$.
9. **Skin wrinkling stress (Allen):** $\sigma_{wr} = 0.5 (E_f E_c G_c)^{1/3}$.
10. **Symmetric sandwich:** NA at geometric mid-plane.
11. **Five failure modes:** Skin rupture, core shear, wrinkling, indentation, debonding.
12. **Most common boat failure:** Core shear crimping under slamming.
13. **Common core materials:** PVC foam (most), balsa, SAN, aluminium honeycomb.
14. **PVC foam density range:** **60–200 kg/m³** for marine applications.
15. **Balsa density:** ~150–400 kg/m³, end-grain only.
16. **Honeycomb advantage:** Highest $G_c$/mass ratio.
17. **Skin materials:** E-glass (most boats), carbon (racing), aramid (impact).
18. **Adhesive:** Epoxy film (preferred for marine); polyester for low-end boats.
19. **Manufacturing routes:** Hand lay-up, vacuum infusion, resin transfer moulding (RTM), prepreg autoclave.
20. **Vacuum infusion advantage:** Higher fibre volume fraction, lower void content, better skin-core bond.
21. **Weight saving:** Sandwich reduces panel weight by **30–60%** at equal stiffness.
22. **Stiffness gain:** Sandwich is **5–25×** stiffer than solid laminate of equal weight.
23. **Insulation bonus:** Foam core reduces noise by 5–15 dB; thermal conductivity ~0.03 W/m·K.
24. **Class society rules:** ISO 12215-5, DNV GL, Lloyd's Register all have explicit sandwich design clauses.
25. **Minimum skin thickness rule:** **$t_f \geq 0.7$ mm** (ISO 12215 for hand lay-up).
26. **Knock-down factor:** Marine rules apply 0.5–0.6 factor to ultimate strength for service load design.
27. **Skarf joints:** Core sheets must be joined with diagonal (skarf) joints, NOT butt joints.
28. **Edge treatment:** Sandwich panel edges must be sealed with fiberglass angle to prevent moisture ingress.
29. **Quality test methods:** Ultrasonic C-scan, thermography, ASTM C393 three-point bend test.
30. **Most-asked KTU question:** *"Compare sandwich construction with single-skin laminate"* — memorize the four-point comparison: stiffness, weight, cost, manufacturability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the sandwich cross-section first in any sandwich-related answer, label all components with their thicknesses, then proceed to formulas. This single habit typically adds **1.5–2 marks** to any Part B answer through the "presentation/imagination" mark component.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Frames (Top Hat, With or Without Core, Bulkhead)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Frames (Top Hat, With or Without Core, Bulkhead)

# Types of Frames in Composite Boat Construction

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Are Frames in Boat Construction?

In composite boat building, **frames** are the transverse (athwartships) structural members that span the hull from port to starboard, giving the hull its shape and resisting local loads such as hydrostatic pressure, slamming loads, point loads from keel/equipment, and cargo forces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Frames are primary load-bearing transverse members in a composite hull, designed to maintain hull form, distribute local bending loads to the hull shell, and provide attachment points for stringers, bulkheads, and internal fittings.

### 1.2 Classification of Frames (As per KTU Module 2)

| S.No | Frame Type | Construction |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Top Hat Frame** | Solid laminate shaped like an inverted "T" with a brim (hat profile) |
| 2 | **Cored Frame** | Sandwich construction with foam/honeycomb core |
| 3 | **Un-cored (Solid) Frame** | Solid laminate without any core material |
| 4 | **Bulkhead Frame** | Full-depth transverse watertight partition |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Block Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The "Spine & Ribs" Model):**
> Imagine a boat hull as a **human ribcage wrapped in a leather jacket**:
> - The **leather jacket** = outer fiberglass skin (the hull shell)
> - The **ribs** = the **frames** that give shape and hold the leather jacket out
> - The **breastbone / spine** = the longitudinal stringers/keel
> - A **swimmer's kickboard (foam-cored)** = a cored frame; a **solid wooden plank** = an un-cored frame; a **closed door inside the cabin** = a bulkhead frame.

A **Top Hat frame** is named for its cross-section that looks exactly like an old-fashioned gentleman's top hat — a flat brim at the foot, a vertical web (the "crown"), and a small flange at the top.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of marine plywood:** **~640 kg/m³**
- **Density of end-grain balsa core:** **~150 kg/m³**
- **Density of PVC foam (AIREX):** **~60–200 kg/m³**
- **Standard frame spacing in small craft:** **250–400 mm**
- **Typical top hat web height:** **75–200 mm**
- **Typical top hat brim width:** **60–100 mm**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Top Hat Frames

A **top hat frame** is a stiffened shell profile consisting of:
- A **vertical web** (laminate, height *h*)
- A **horizontal bottom flange (the brim)** of width *b_f*
- An optional **top return flange** of width *b_t*

It is essentially an **inverted "T" or "L" stiffener** laid against the hull shell.

### 2.1.1 Why a Top Hat and Not Just a Flat Strip?

A flat strip of the same laminate thickness has poor resistance to bending about its weak axis. The top-hat profile **moves material away from the neutral axis**, dramatically increasing the **second moment of area (*I*)** and hence the **section modulus (*Z*)**.

$$I_{\text{flat strip}} \;\ll\; I_{\text{top hat}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffness Ratio Principle:** Since flexural rigidity $D = EI$, even a modest increase in *I* (say 5×) gives 5× more bending stiffness **without adding weight**.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle (How It Works)

1. Hydrostatic pressure pushes **inward** on the hull panel.
2. The hull panel (skin) tries to deflect between frames.
3. The frame acts as a **simply supported beam** carrying this reaction.
4. The **flange (brim)** resists the bending moment.
5. The **web** resists the shear force.
6. Load is transferred back to the stringers, keel, and sheer line.

---

## 2.2 Frames With Core (Cored Frames)

These are **sandwich frames** built by laminating two GRP/carbon skins around a low-density core (balsa, PVC foam, SAN foam, or honeycomb).

### 2.2.1 Why Add a Core?

| Benefit | Mechanism |
|---------|-----------|
| ↑ Stiffness | Distance between skin layers increases *I* cubically |
| ↓ Weight | Core density ≈ 1/5 to 1/10 of solid laminate |
| ↑ Insulation | Closed-cell foams provide thermal/acoustic damping |
| ↑ Buckling resistance | Outer skins are supported against local buckling |

### 2.2.2 Construction Sequence

1. Lay-up inner skin on mold.
2. Apply core (pre-shaped) with bonding putty/adhesive.
3. Vacuum-bag or infusion the outer skin.
4. Trim and seal edges to prevent **core ingress of water**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If core edges are not properly sealed, water travels through the laminate via **wicking** and **osmosis**, causing delamination and core rot (balsa).

---

## 2.3 Frames Without Core (Un-cored / Solid Frames)

These are **monolithic laminates** — multiple plies of glass/carbon mat and cloth bonded by resin, no core.

### 2.3.1 When Are They Used?

- **High impact zones** (forward sections exposed to slamming).
- **Small craft** where manufacturing simplicity outweighs weight penalties.
- **Areas needing local reinforcement** (engine bearers, mast step, chainplates).
- **Repair sections** where core cannot be guaranteed.

### 2.3.2 Comparison Table: Cored vs. Un-cored

| Parameter | Un-cored (Solid) | Cored (Sandwich) |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Weight | Higher | **40–60% lighter** |
| Stiffness | Low | Very high |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Impact tolerance | **High** | Moderate (core crush) |
| Water resistance | Excellent | Depends on sealing |
| Inspection | Easy | Difficult (hidden defects) |

---

## 2.4 Bulkhead Frames

A **bulkhead** is a **full or partial depth transverse vertical partition** dividing the hull interior into compartments.

### 2.4.1 Functions of a Bulkhead

1. **Watertight subdivision** — limits flooding in case of hull breach (SOLAS requirement).
2. **Transverse strength** — acts as a deep girder resisting hull bending.
3. **Shape retention** — maintains hull form against racking loads.
4. **Mounting platform** — for engines, tanks, furniture, electrical panels.

### 2.4.2 Types of Bulkheads

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Collision bulkhead** | Forward-most, usually at ≤ 5% LWL from stem |
| **Engine room bulkhead** | Fire-rated, may include sound insulation |
| **Aft peak bulkhead** | Closes stern for access to steering gear |
| **Partial / partial-height** | For stiffness, not full watertight |
| **Full-depth structural** | Spans bilge to deck, integrated with frames |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examination (Module 2)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}$ | 2nd moment of area, rectangular section | m⁴ |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c}$ | Section modulus, *c* = dist. to extreme fibre | m³ |
| 3 | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress | Pa (N/m²) |
| 4 | $\delta = \dfrac{5 \, w \, L^{4}}{384 \, E \, I}$ | Mid-span deflection (simply supported, UDL) | m |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{E \, h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | Flexural rigidity, isotropic plate | N·m |
| 6 | $D_{eq} = D_{f} + D_{c} + \dfrac{E_{f} \, h_{f} \, (h_{f}+h_{c})^{2}}{2(1-\nu_{f}^{2})}$ | Equivalent sandwich panel flexural rigidity | N·m |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{V \, Q}{I \, b}$ | Shear stress (frame web) | Pa |
| 8 | $I_{\text{top hat}} = \dfrac{b_{f} \, t_{f}^{3}}{12} + b_{f} \, t_{f} \, y_{1}^{2} + \dfrac{t_{w} \, h^{3}}{12} + t_{w} \, h \, y_{2}^{2}$ | Composite 2nd moment, parallel-axis theorem | m⁴ |
| 9 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{k \, \pi^{2} \, D}{L^{2}}$ | Plate buckling load (composite skin) | N |
| 10 | $F_{s} = \rho \, g \, d$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth *d* | Pa |

> **Where:**
> - $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa)
> - $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.3 for GRP)
> - $h_f, h_c$ = face & core thickness (m)
> - $\rho$ = water density = **1025 kg/m³** (sea), **1000 kg/m³** (fresh)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Second Moment of Area of a Top Hat Frame Section

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **KTU high-priority question** — frequently asked for 7 marks in Part B.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A top hat frame has the following cross-section:
- Bottom flange (brim): width $b_f = 80$ mm, thickness $t_f = 6$ mm
- Vertical web: height $h = 120$ mm, thickness $t_w = 5$ mm
- Top return flange: width $b_t = 30$ mm, thickness $t_t = 5$ mm

Calculate the **second moment of area (*I*)** of the cross-section about its **centroidal axis parallel to the brim**.

### 3.1.2 Analytical Approach

We use the **parallel-axis theorem**:
$$I = \sum_{i} \left( I_{o,i} + A_i \, \bar{y_i}^{2} \right)$$

where $I_{o,i} = \dfrac{b_i \, t_i^{3}}{12}$ is the moment of inertia of each part about its own centroidal axis, $A_i$ is its area, and $\bar{y_i}$ is the distance from the part's centroid to the global centroid.

---

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define coordinates (origin at bottom of brim):**

Let us place the origin at the **bottom outer surface of the brim**, with *y* measured upward.

**Step 2 — Compute areas and individual centroids:**

| Part | Area $A_i$ (mm²) | Centroid $\bar{y_i}$ from origin (mm) |
|------|------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Brim (rectangle 80 × 6) | $A_1 = 80 \times 6 = 480$ | $y_1 = 3$ |
| Web (rectangle 5 × 120) | $A_2 = 5 \times 120 = 600$ | $y_2 = 6 + 60 = 66$ |
| Top flange (rectangle 30 × 5) | $A_3 = 30 \times 5 = 150$ | $y_3 = 6 + 120 + 2.5 = 128.5$ |

**Step 3 — Locate global centroid $\bar{y}$:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum A_i \, \bar{y_i}}{\sum A_i}$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{(480)(3) + (600)(66) + (150)(128.5)}{480 + 600 + 150}
$$

Numerator:

$$
(480)(3) = 1440
$$

$$
(600)(66) = 39600
$$

$$
(150)(128.5) = 19275
$$

$$
\sum A_i \, \bar{y_i} = 1440 + 39600 + 19275 = 60315
$$

Denominator:

$$
\sum A_i = 480 + 600 + 150 = 1230
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{60315}{1230} = 49.04 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute individual moments of inertia about own centroidal axis:**

$$
I_{o,1} = \frac{b_f \, t_f^{3}}{12} = \frac{80 \times 6^{3}}{12} = \frac{80 \times 216}{12} = 1440 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
I_{o,2} = \frac{t_w \, h^{3}}{12} = \frac{5 \times 120^{3}}{12} = \frac{5 \times 1728000}{12} = 720000 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
I_{o,3} = \frac{b_t \, t_t^{3}}{12} = \frac{30 \times 5^{3}}{12} = \frac{30 \times 125}{12} = 312.5 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply parallel-axis theorem (each part shifted by $d_i = \bar{y_i} - \bar{y}$):**

Distance of each part from global centroid:

$$
d_1 = 3 - 49.04 = -46.04 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_1^{2} = 2119.7
$$

$$
d_2 = 66 - 49.04 = 16.96 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_2^{2} = 287.6
$$

$$
d_3 = 128.5 - 49.04 = 79.46 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_3^{2} = 6313.9
$$

**Step 6 — Sum all contributions:**

$$
I = (I_{o,1} + A_1 \, d_1^{2}) + (I_{o,2} + A_2 \, d_2^{2}) + (I_{o,3} + A_3 \, d_3^{2})
$$

Part 1 contribution:

$$
I_1 = 1440 + (480)(2119.7) = 1440 + 1017456 = 1018896 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

Part 2 contribution:

$$
I_2 = 720000 + (600)(287.6) = 720000 + 172560 = 892560 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

Part 3 contribution:

$$
I_3 = 312.5 + (150)(6313.9) = 312.5 + 947085 = 947397.5 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

Total:

$$
\boxed{I = 1018896 + 892560 + 947397.5 = 2858853.5 \text{ mm}^{4} \approx 2.86 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Hydrostatic Load on a Frame

A small craft with frame spacing **L = 350 mm** is loaded to a draft such that hydrostatic pressure on the bottom panel is **p = 12 kPa**.

**Question:** Find the equivalent UDL on the frame and the maximum bending moment.

### Step 1: Equivalent UDL on the frame

The frame carries the load from the panel between two adjacent frames. The pressure *p* acts on the panel area of $b \times s$ (where $b$ is panel width and $s$ is frame spacing).

Assuming unit panel width (1 m):

$$
w = p \times s = 12000 \, \text{N/m}^{2} \times 0.350 \, \text{m} = 4200 \, \text{N/m}
$$

### Step 2: Maximum bending moment (simply supported)

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w \, s^{2}}{8} = \frac{4200 \times (0.350)^{2}}{8} = \frac{4200 \times 0.1225}{8}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{514.5}{8} = 64.3 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 3: Required section modulus (allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 60$ MPa)

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{64.3}{60 \times 10^{6}} = 1.07 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{3} = 1.07 \, \text{cm}^{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** A top hat section from §3.1 has $Z = I/c = 2.86 \times 10^{-6} / (128.5 - 49.04)/1000 = 2.86 \times 10^{-6} / 0.0795 = 3.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m³, which is **34× higher** than required — safe.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Frame Type

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix to decide which frame type fits a given location on the boat.**

| Hull Zone | Recommended Frame Type | Reason |
|-----------|------------------------|--------|
| Bow (impact zone) | **Un-cored solid laminate** | Slamming loads, debris impact |
| Mid-bottom (bilge) | **Cored top hat** | High bending, low weight |
| Sides above WL | **Cored top hat** | Moderate pressure, weight saving |
| Engine room | **Reinforced solid + bulkhead** | Heat, vibration, mounting loads |
| Forward collision zone | **Full bulkhead + cored frames** | SOLAS subdivision rule |
| Accommodation area | **Cored frames + partial bulkheads** | Noise, vibration, insulation |
| Stern | **Cored top hat + partial bulkhead** | Shape retention |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cross-Section Taxonomy of Frames

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FRAMES in Composite Boat Hull]:::root --> B[Solid Un-cored Frames]:::solid
    A --> C[Sandwich Cored Frames]:::cored
    A --> D[Bulkhead Frames]:::bulk

    B --> B1[Top Hat Solid]
    B --> B2[Flat Strip Frame]
    B --> B3[Laminated Girder]

    C --> C1[Cored Top Hat]
    C --> C2[Cored Box Frame]
    C --> C3[Balsa Core]
    C --> C4[PVC Foam Core]
    C --> C5[Honeycomb Core]

    D --> D1[Collision Bulkhead]
    D --> D2[Engine Room Bulkhead]
    D --> D3[Aft Peak Bulkhead]
    D --> D4[Watertight Subdivision]
    D --> D5[Partial Depth Structural]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef solid fill:#b8860b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    classDef cored fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    classDef bulk fill:#8b0000,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Top Hat Frame Cross-Section (Labelled)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TH[Top Hat Frame Cross Section]
        direction LR
        BRIM[BOTTOM FLANGE BRIM\nWidth 80mm Thickness 6mm]:::flange
        WEB[VERTICAL WEB\nHeight 120mm Thickness 5mm]:::web
        TOP[TOP RETURN FLANGE\nWidth 30mm Thickness 5mm]:::flange
        NEUT[NEUTRAL AXIS\nDistance ybar 49.04mm]:::axis
    end

    BRIM --- WEB
    WEB --- TOP
    WEB -.-> NEUT

    classDef flange fill:#d2691e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef web fill:#4682b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef axis fill:#000,stroke:#ff0,color:#ff0
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Load Path Through a Frame System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Hydrostatic Pressure p]:::load --> SKIN[GRP Hull Skin Panel]:::skin
    SKIN --> SPAN[Span Between Frames L]
    SPAN --> FRM[Frame as SS Beam]:::frame
    FRM --> BM[Bending Moment M and Shear V]
    BM --> FLG[Bottom Flange Tension Compression]:::flange2
    BM --> WEB2[Web Carries Shear Tau]:::web2
    FLG --> STR[Stringer Longl Member]:::str
    WEB2 --> STR
    STR --> KEEL[Keel Longl Girder]:::keel
    STR --> SHEER[Sheer Longl Gunwale]:::keel
    KEEL --> REACT[Support Reactions at Stem Transom]:::r

    classDef load fill:#ff6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef skin fill:#daa520,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef frame fill:#4682b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef flange2 fill:#cd853f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef web2 fill:#5f9ea0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef str fill:#2f4f4f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef keel fill:#000080,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef r fill:#556b2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sandwich Cored Frame Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAND[Sandwich Cored Frame Lamination Stack]
        GEL[Gelcoat Outer Skin 0.5mm]:::gel
        L1[CSM Chopped Strand Mat 1.0mm]:::lam
        L2[Woven Roving 0.8mm]:::lam
        L3[Main Laminate 2.5mm]:::lam
        CORE[Balsa or PVC Core 20 to 40mm]:::core
        L4[Inner Laminate 2.5mm]:::lam
        L5[Woven Roving 0.8mm]:::lam
        L6[CSM 1.0mm]:::lam
        GEL2[Inner Skin Gelcoat 0.5mm]:::gel
        GEL --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> CORE --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> GEL2
    end

    classDef gel fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef lam fill:#d2b48c,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Mermaid: Bulkhead Integration with Hull Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HULL[Hull Shell GRP]:::hull --> FRAMES[Transverse Frames at 300mm]:::fr
    FRAMES --> BULK[Bulkhead Full Depth at Station 4]:::bk
    FRAMES --> BULK2[Bulkhead Engine Room at Station 6]:::bk
    BULK --> WS1[Watertight Compartment A Bow]:::cpt
    BULK2 --> WS2[Watertight Compartment B Mid]:::cpt
    BULK --> BOND[Bonding Flange 50mm overlap]:::bond
    BULK2 --> BOND
    BULK --> STIFF[Stiffeners Vertical and Horizontal]:::stiff
    BULK2 --> STIFF
    BULK --> CUT[Cutouts with Doubling Plates]:::cut
    BULK2 --> CUT

    classDef hull fill:#4682b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fr fill:#daa520,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef bk fill:#8b0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef cpt fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef bond fill:#cd853f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef stiff fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef cut fill:#a0522d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each `flowchart` block represents a **structural or logical architecture** of boat-frame systems. Cross-sectional geometry (curves, actual hat profiles) is best drawn in CAD — the diagrams above capture the *function, hierarchy, and load-path relationships* required at the KTU board level.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a cored frame and an un-cored frame in composite boat construction.**

**Model Answer:**
A **cored frame** is a sandwich construction with two laminate skins bonded to a low-density core (balsa, PVC foam, or honeycomb), offering **high stiffness-to-weight ratio**. An **un-cored frame** is a **monolithic solid laminate** without any core, providing **high impact resistance and easier repair** but at a higher weight penalty. Cored frames are preferred in mid-hull sections for weight savings; un-cored frames are used in slamming zones and highly stressed local areas.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a "Top Hat Frame" and state two advantages of its profile.**

**Model Answer:**
A **top hat frame** is a transverse stiffener whose cross-section resembles a top hat, comprising a vertical web with a wide bottom flange (brim) and a small return flange at the top. **Two advantages:**
1. The profile maximises the **second moment of area (I)** for a given weight, increasing flexural rigidity.
2. The brim provides a wide bonding surface with the hull shell, improving **load transfer** and reducing stress concentrations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of frames used in composite boat construction, with neat cross-sectional sketches. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A top hat frame has a brim of 100 mm × 8 mm, a web of 150 mm × 6 mm, and a top return flange of 40 mm × 6 mm. Calculate:
(i) The position of the centroid from the base. **(3 Marks)**
(ii) The second moment of area about the centroidal axis. **(4 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Frame Types:**
1. **Un-cored (solid) frame** — Monolithic laminate; high impact resistance; used in slamming zones.
2. **Cored (sandwich) frame** — Core (balsa/PVC) sandwiched between laminate skins; high stiffness/weight ratio.
3. **Top hat frame** — Hat-profile stiffener; efficient bending resistance.
4. **Bulkhead frame** — Full-depth transverse partition; provides watertight subdivision and transverse stiffness.

(Neat sketches expected — refer to §4.2 above.)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Brim: $b_f = 100$ mm, $t_f = 8$ mm
- Web: $h = 150$ mm, $t_w = 6$ mm
- Top flange: $b_t = 40$ mm, $t_t = 6$ mm

**Areas:**
$$
A_1 = 100 \times 8 = 800 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
A_2 = 6 \times 150 = 900 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
A_3 = 40 \times 6 = 240 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Individual centroids from base:**
$$
y_1 = 4 \text{ mm}, \quad y_2 = 8 + 75 = 83 \text{ mm}, \quad y_3 = 8 + 150 + 3 = 161 \text{ mm}
$$

**(i) Centroid:**

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{800(4) + 900(83) + 240(161)}{800 + 900 + 240}
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{3200 + 74700 + 38640}{1940} = \frac{116540}{1940} = 60.07 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Second moment of area:**

$$
I = \sum \left(\frac{b_i t_i^{3}}{12} + A_i d_i^{2}\right)
$$

Distances from centroid:
$$
d_1 = 4 - 60.07 = -56.07 \text{ mm}, \quad d_1^{2} = 3143.8
$$

$$
d_2 = 83 - 60.07 = 22.93 \text{ mm}, \quad d_2^{2} = 525.8
$$

$$
d_3 = 161 - 60.07 = 100.93 \text{ mm}, \quad d_3^{2} = 10186.9
$$

Part 1:

$$
I_1 = \frac{100 \times 8^{3}}{12} + 800(3143.8) = 4267 + 2515040 = 2519307 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

Part 2:

$$
I_2 = \frac{6 \times 150^{3}}{12} + 900(525.8) = 1687500 + 473220 = 2160720 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

Part 3:

$$
I_3 = \frac{40 \times 6^{3}}{12} + 240(10186.9) = 720 + 2444856 = 2445576 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Total:**

$$
\boxed{I = 2519307 + 2160720 + 2445576 = 7125603 \text{ mm}^{4} \approx 7.13 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **load path** in a typical composite hull from hydrostatic pressure on the skin to support reactions at the keel and sheer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A boat's bottom panel has a hydrostatic pressure of **15 kPa**, with frames spaced at **400 mm**. The frame is a simply supported beam of length equal to the frame spacing. The frame section is a top hat with:
- Brim: 90 mm × 7 mm
- Web: 130 mm × 5 mm
- Top return: 25 mm × 5 mm

Calculate:
(i) Equivalent UDL on the frame. **(2 Marks)**
(ii) Maximum bending moment. **(3 Marks)**
(iii) Maximum bending stress. **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Load Path:**
The load transfers in the following sequence (refer to §4.3):
**Pressure → Hull Skin → Span Between Frames → Frame (bending) → Stringers / Keel / Sheer → Support Reactions.**
The frame acts as a **simply supported beam** carrying the reaction from the skin panel. Bending moment is resisted primarily by the **brim flange**, shear by the **web**, and the reactions are transferred to **longitudinal girders** (keelson and gunwale).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $p = 15$ kPa, frame spacing $s = L = 0.4$ m.

**(i) Equivalent UDL (per metre panel width):**

$$
w = p \times L = 15000 \times 0.4 = 6000 \text{ N/m}
$$

**(ii) Maximum bending moment:**

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w L^{2}}{8} = \frac{6000 \times (0.4)^{2}}{8} = \frac{6000 \times 0.16}{8} = 120 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iii) Maximum bending stress:**

First compute *I* of the section:

Areas:
$$
A_1 = 90 \times 7 = 630 \text{ mm}^{2}, \quad A_2 = 5 \times 130 = 650 \text{ mm}^{2}, \quad A_3 = 25 \times 5 = 125 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Centroids from base:
$$
y_1 = 3.5, \quad y_2 = 7 + 65 = 72, \quad y_3 = 7 + 130 + 2.5 = 139.5
$$

Centroid:

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{630(3.5) + 650(72) + 125(139.5)}{630 + 650 + 125} = \frac{2205 + 46800 + 17437.5}{1405} = \frac{66442.5}{1405} = 47.29 \text{ mm}
$$

Distance to extreme fibre: $c = 139.5 - 47.29 = 92.21$ mm

*I* calculation (similar to §3.1):

- $d_1 = 3.5 - 47.29 = -43.79$, $d_1^{2} = 1917.6$
- $d_2 = 72 - 47.29 = 24.71$, $d_2^{2} = 610.6$
- $d_3 = 139.5 - 47.29 = 92.21$, $d_3^{2} = 8502.7$

$$
I_1 = \frac{90 \times 7^{3}}{12} + 630(1917.6) = 2572.5 + 1208088 = 1210660.5
$$

$$
I_2 = \frac{5 \times 130^{3}}{12} + 650(610.6) = 915833.3 + 396890 = 1312723.3
$$

$$
I_3 = \frac{25 \times 5^{3}}{12} + 125(8502.7) = 260.4 + 1062837.5 = 1063097.9
$$

$$
I_{total} = 1210660.5 + 1312723.3 + 1063097.9 = 3586481.7 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

Section modulus:

$$
Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{3586481.7}{92.21} = 38894 \text{ mm}^{3} = 3.89 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{120}{3.89 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.08 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 3.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** Most students confuse the units of *I* (mm⁴) with metres. Always convert *Z* to **m³** before dividing *M* (in N·m) to get Pa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation is the **#1 mark-deduction reason** in Module 2 calculations.
> 2. **Centroid mistakes:** Forgetting to shift *y* from the part centroid to the **global centroid** gives wrong *I* values (and the part marks vanish).
> 3. **Confusing *I* with *Z*:** Section modulus *Z = I/c* is required for stress, not *I* itself.
> 4. **Skipping sketches:** In Part B questions (a), **labelled cross-section sketches are mandatory** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks purely for diagram clarity.
> 5. **Direction of fibres in plies:** When asked about laminates, mention **ply orientation** (0°/45°/-45°/90°) — a common follow-up question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Types of Frames**

- ☐ **Frame** = transverse structural member holding hull shape and resisting local loads.
- ☐ **Top Hat Frame** = hat-profile stiffener (brim + web + optional top flange).
- ☐ **Cored Frame** = sandwich construction with foam/balsa/honeycomb core.
- ☐ **Un-cored Frame** = solid laminate without core (slamming zones, repair areas).
- ☐ **Bulkhead** = full-depth transverse partition — watertight subdivision + structural.
- ☐ **Top hat profile** maximises *I* by placing material far from neutral axis.
- ☐ **Hydrostatic pressure:** $p = \rho g d$ → Equivalent UDL $w = p \times s$ on frame.
- ☐ **Bending stress formula:** $\sigma = M/Z$ where $Z = I/c$.
- ☐ **Parallel-axis theorem** is essential for composite section *I*.
- ☐ **Standard frame spacing:** 250–400 mm in small craft.
- ☐ **Cored frames** offer 40–60% weight savings; **un-cored** offer better impact tolerance.
- ☐ **Bulkheads** must be properly **bonded with overlap flanges** to hull and **doubled around cutouts**.
- ☐ **SOLAS rule:** collision bulkhead within 5% LWL of stem.
- ☐ **Always convert units** consistently (mm → m when *M* is in N·m).
- ☐ **Sketch labels** (flange, web, brim, neutral axis) earn easy marks.

> **End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Types of Frames (Top Hat, With or Without Core, Bulkhead)**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcome-based pedagogy.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Midship Section of FRP Boat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Typical Midship Section of FRP Boat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Midship Section (KTU Definition):** The *midship section* (or *amidships cross-section*) of an FRP boat is the transverse cross-sectional profile taken at the longitudinal station located at **0.5 L** from the Aft Perpendicular (AP) — i.e., exactly at mid-length of the vessel — illustrating the complete structural arrangement of the hull laminate, core material (if any), stiffeners, frames, stringers, bulkheads, decks, keel, and sheer line. It is the **primary reference section** for evaluating hull-girder strength, section modulus, and longitudinal bending stiffness in composite (FRP) boat design.

In the **2024 KTU Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, the midship section is treated as the controlling cross-section under **Rule-based classification rules** (e.g., DNV, Lloyd's, ISO 12215-5) for the global hull girder. Every element drawn at this station must reflect a valid laminate schedule and must satisfy the local + global load-bearing criteria.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Spine of the Boat":**
> Imagine the FRP boat as a human body lying horizontally. The **midship section** is the cross-sectional "CT-scan slice" taken right through the chest. Just as a doctor studies this slice to evaluate ribs (frames), backbone (keel), sternum (transom), and skin thickness, a naval architect studies the midship section to evaluate the **structural spine** of the boat. Every ply of fiberglass, every stiffener, every bulkhead must be visible in this slice — it is the **blueprint of structural integrity**.

If you were building a fiberglass canoe, for instance, the midship section would show you exactly **how thick the hull is**, **where the ribs are placed**, **what core material sits sandwiched inside**, and **how the deck connects to the hull** — all in one neat cross-sectional view.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following **standard reference metrics** are universally adopted in KTU-aligned composite boat design:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (FRP) |
|---|---|---|
| Hull-girder bending section modulus | $Z$ | $0.40 L^2 B \cdot CM$ cm³ |
| Hull-girder moment of inertia | $I$ | computed from lamination |
| Midship length coefficient | $CM$ | $0.90$ – $0.98$ |
| Neutral axis offset from baseline | $y_{NA}$ | depends on laminate symmetry |
| Hull-girder standard flexural strength | $\sigma_{allow}$ | $\mathbf{70}$ to $\mathbf{125}$ MPa |
| FRP typical density | $\rho_{FRP}$ | $\mathbf{1500}$ – $\mathbf{1800}$ kg/m³ |
| PVC foam core density | $\rho_{core}$ | $\mathbf{60}$ – $\mathbf{200}$ kg/m³ |
| End-grain balsa density | $\rho_{balsa}$ | $\mathbf{150}$ – $\mathbf{220}$ kg/m³ |
| Young's modulus — E-glass / polyester | $E$ | $\mathbf{15}$ – $\mathbf{25}$ GPa |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Typical FRP Midship Section

A typical midship section of an FRP boat comprises the following structural subsystems, drawn in correct relative proportions:

### A. Hull Skin (Outer Laminate)
- **Outer skin (below waterline)** — exposed to hydrodynamic pressure, abrasion, osmosis.
- **Inner skin (interior face)** — exposed to cargo, passengers, machinery vibration.

### B. Core (in Sandwich Construction)
The sandwich concept drastically increases bending stiffness **without adding weight** — the same principle used in I-beams.

- **End-grain balsa** — high compressive strength, used below waterline.
- **PVC / SAN / PET foam** — used above waterline (lightweight, water-resistant).
- **Honeycomb** (Nomex / polypropylene) — used in high-performance craft.

### C. Framing System (Transverse)
- **Main frames** (e.g., at every 400–600 mm spacing): laminated angle or T-sections, sometimes preformed and bonded.
- **Web frames / floors** at heavy-load zones.

### D. Longitudinal Stiffeners (Stringers)
- **Side stringers**, **deck stringers**, **bottom stringers** — typically pultruded or hand-laid FRP angles, T-sections, or hat profiles.

### E. Decks, Superstructure & Transom
- **Deck laminate** (often sandwich with foam core).
- **Sheer clamp / deck-to-hull joint** (often the most critical joint — bolted, bonded, or both).
- **Transom** (rear closure — solid laminate for outboards, sandwich for inboards).

### F. Keel & Chine
- **Keel**: thickened laminate along the centreline for grounding impact resistance.
- **Chine**: the knuckle line at the hull-deck or hull-flat intersection.

## 2.2 Logical Reasoning — *Why each component exists*

| Element | Why it exists (the "Why") |
|---|---|
| **Outer skin** | Resists hydrostatic pressure, abrasion, impact, osmosis barrier |
| **Inner skin** | Resists inboard pressure, fatigue, distributes point loads |
| **Sandwich core** | Increases $I$ (moment of inertia) → increases $Z$ → increases hull-girder strength for **same weight** |
| **Frames** | Maintain shape against **local** panel buckling & hydrostatic pressure |
| **Stringers** | Carry **longitudinal** hull-girder bending loads; reduce panel span |
| **Bulkheads** | Watertight subdivision; crash barrier; load transfer |
| **Keel** | Resists grounding; stiffens centreline |
| **Sheer clamp** | Resists global bending via deck-hull coupling; key shear joint |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas constitute the KTU high-yield sheet for midship section calculations.** All quantities are in standard SI units unless otherwise noted.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Section modulus (m³), governing hull-girder bending strength |
| 2 | $I = \sum_i \left[ \dfrac{b_i t_i^3}{12} + b_i t_i \cdot d_i^2 \right]$ | Parallel-axis (Steiner) theorem for composite built-up section |
| 3 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{M_{hog}}{Z_{deck}} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | Hull-girder bending stress check (sagging / hogging) |
| 4 | $M_{hog} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{32}$ (still water) | Hogging moment for uniformly distributed load (rule-based) |
| 5 | $M_{sag} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{32}$ | Sagging moment at amidships for UDL |
| 6 | $M_{wave} = C_w \cdot W \cdot L$ | Wave-induced bending moment, $C_w \approx 0.125 L$ for seagoing |
| 7 | $y_{NA} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \cdot y_i}{\sum A_i}$ | Neutral axis location from baseline |
| 8 | $EI_{hull-girder} = \sum E_i I_i$ | Effective hull-girder stiffness (transformed section) |
| 9 | $t_{eff} = t_f + \dfrac{E_c}{E_f} \cdot t_c$ | Equivalent all-skin thickness (transformed sandwich face) |
| 10 | $I_{sandwich} = \dfrac{B \cdot t_f \cdot d^2}{2} + \dfrac{B \cdot t_f^3}{6} + \dfrac{B \cdot t_c^3}{12}$ | Sandwich panel moment of inertia (per unit width) |
| 11 | $\sigma_{core} = \dfrac{P \cdot d}{2 t_c}$ | Sandwich core compressive stress |
| 12 | $\tau_{core} = \dfrac{Q}{d \cdot B}$ | Sandwich core shear stress |

Where:
- $W$ = displacement weight (N)
- $L$ = Length Between Perpendiculars (m)
- $B$ = panel width (m)
- $t_f$ = face thickness, $t_c$ = core thickness
- $d$ = distance between face centroids
- $C_w$ = wave coefficient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show **units** in the formula sheet. Marks are deducted when units are missing in derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The midship section analysis drives:
1. **Hull-girder strength certification** under DNV / Lloyd's / RINA rules.
2. **Laminate schedule specification** — the exact stacking sequence (e.g., `CSM / WRM / CSM / core / CSM / WRM / CSM`).
3. **Weight estimation** — accurate to within **±5%** for FRP vessels.
4. **Cost estimation** — laminate cost is dominated by resin content; midship data drives the bill of materials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Neutral Axis of a Composite Midship Section

**Problem:** Find the neutral axis $y_{NA}$ of a typical single-chine FRP boat midship section consisting of: bottom skin (laminate, $A_1$), side skin ($A_2$), sheer region ($A_3$), and deck ($A_4$).

**Given:**
- $A_1 = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$, $y_1 = 150 \text{ mm}$
- $A_2 = 9{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$, $y_2 = 750 \text{ mm}$
- $A_3 = 6{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$, $y_3 = 1{,}200 \text{ mm}$
- $A_4 = 18{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$, $y_4 = 1{,}450 \text{ mm}$
- All of the same E-glass/polyester laminate ($E$ uniform, so no transformation needed).

**Step 1 — State the governing formula:**

$$
y_{NA} = \frac{\sum_i A_i \, y_i}{\sum_i A_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator (first moment of area):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum A_i \, y_i &= (12{,}000)(150) + (9{,}000)(750) + (6{,}000)(1{,}200) + (18{,}000)(1{,}450) \\
&= 1{,}800{,}000 + 6{,}750{,}000 + 7{,}200{,}000 + 26{,}100{,}000 \\
&= 41{,}850{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator (total area):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum A_i &= 12{,}000 + 9{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 18{,}000 \\
&= 45{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide to get $y_{NA}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{NA} &= \frac{41{,}850{,}000}{45{,}000} \\
&= 930 \text{ mm above baseline}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The neutral axis sits at **930 mm** above the keel baseline, i.e., approximately **64% of the depth** from the baseline — typical for an FRP boat with relatively heavy deck (e.g., due to deckhouse or sandwich deck).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Section Modulus for a Sandwich Panel Midship Region

**Problem:** Compute the section modulus $Z$ per metre of width for the bottom shell of an FRP boat whose bottom laminate is sandwich-constructed with the following schedule:

- Outer face: $t_f = 4 \text{ mm}$ E-glass / polyester
- Core: $t_c = 25 \text{ mm}$ PVC foam ($E_c / E_f \approx 0.05$)
- Inner face: $t_f = 4 \text{ mm}$ E-glass / polyester
- Panel width considered: $B = 1000 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Equivalent all-skin thickness using transformed section:**

$$
t_{eff} = t_{f,outer} + t_{f,inner} + \left(\frac{E_c}{E_f}\right) t_c
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{eff} &= 4 + 4 + (0.05)(25) \\
&= 8 + 1.25 \\
&= 9.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance between face centroids:**

$$
d = t_c + t_f = 25 + 4 = 29 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Sandwich moment of inertia per metre width:**

$$
I_{sandwich} = \frac{B \, t_f \, d^2}{2} + \frac{B \, t_f^3}{6} + \frac{B \, t_c^3}{12}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{sandwich} &= \frac{(1000)(4)(29)^2}{2} + \frac{(1000)(4)^3}{6} + \frac{(1000)(25)^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{(1000)(4)(841)}{2} + \frac{(1000)(64)}{6} + \frac{(1000)(15{,}625)}{12} \\
&= 1{,}682{,}000 + 10{,}667 + 1{,}302{,}083 \\
&= 2{,}994{,}750 \text{ mm}^4 \text{ per metre}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Section modulus (assuming NA at panel mid-depth, $y_{max} = d/2 + t_f/2$):**

$$
y_{max} = \frac{29}{2} + \frac{4}{2} = 14.5 + 2.0 = 16.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{I_{sandwich}}{y_{max}} \\
&= \frac{2{,}994{,}750}{16.5} \\
&\approx 181{,}500 \text{ mm}^3 \text{ per metre width}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with equivalent solid laminate (without core):**

If the same $9.25$ mm were solid FRP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{solid} &= \frac{B \, t_{eff}^3}{12} = \frac{(1000)(9.25)^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{(1000)(791.45)}{12} \\
&= 65{,}954 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
Z_{solid} = \frac{65{,}954}{9.25/2} = 14{,}263 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Conclusion:** The sandwich construction provides **~12.7× higher section modulus** than the equivalent solid laminate at the **same weight**. This is the **central design philosophy** of FRP boatbuilding — and a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Single Skin vs Sandwich

| Criterion | Single Skin | Sandwich |
|---|---|---|
| Hull length < 6 m | ✅ Preferred | Overkill |
| Hull length 6–12 m | Optional | ✅ Preferred |
| Hull length > 12 m | ❌ Not recommended | ✅ Mandatory |
| High-speed planing craft | ✅ | With caution (slamming) |
| Displacement cruising boat | With stiffeners | ✅ Better |
| Production cost | Lower | Higher (controlled) |
| Repair complexity | Easy | Moderate |
| Bending stiffness required | Low–Moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Midship Section Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Midship Section at 0.5 L] --> B1[Outer Hull Skin]
    A0 --> B2[Inner Hull Skin]
    A0 --> B3[Sandwich Core Layer]
    A0 --> B4[Transverse Frames]
    A0 --> B5[Longitudinal Stringers]
    A0 --> B6[Watertight Bulkheads]
    A0 --> B7[Deck Laminate]
    A0 --> B8[Keel Reinforcement]
    A0 --> B9[Sheer Clamp Joint]
    A0 --> B10[Chine and Knuckle Lines]

    B1 --> C1[Hydrostatic Load Resistance]
    B1 --> C2[Osmosis Barrier Gelcoat]

    B3 --> C3[Increased Moment of Inertia]
    B3 --> C4[Thermal Insulation]

    B4 --> C5[Maintain Cross-Section Shape]
    B4 --> C6[Local Panel Buckling Prevention]

    B5 --> C7[Longitudinal Bending Load Path]
    B5 --> C8[Reduce Panel Span]

    B6 --> C9[Watertight Subdivision]
    B6 --> C10[Crash Barrier Function]

    B7 --> C11[Deck Plate Flexural Strength]
    B7 --> C12[Weather Deck Protection]

    B8 --> C13[Grounding Impact Resistance]
    B8 --> C14[Centreline Stiffness]

    B9 --> C15[Hull-Deck Shear Transfer]
    B9 --> C16[Global Bending Coupling]

    B10 --> C17[Spray Deflection]
    B10 --> C18[Structural Knuckle]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Laminate Schedule at the Bottom Shell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Bottom Skin Laminate Schedule from Outer to Inner] --> L1[Gelcoat 0.5 mm]
    L1 --> L2[CSM 450 gsm Mat]
    L2 --> L3[Woven Roving 800 gsm]
    L3 --> L4[CSM 450 gsm Mat]
    L4 --> L5[Woven Roving 800 gsm]
    L5 --> L6[CSM 450 gsm Mat]
    L6 --> L7[PVC Foam Core 25 mm]
    L7 --> L8[CSM 450 gsm Mat]
    L8 --> L9[Woven Roving 800 gsm]
    L9 --> L10[CSM 450 gsm Mat]
    L10 --> L11[Topcoat or Gelcoat]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Midship Section Load Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hydrostatic Pressure] --> Q1[Outer Skin]
    P2[Wave Slamming] --> Q1
    P3[Cargo Load] --> Q2[Inner Skin]
    P4[Global Bending Moment] --> Q3[Hull Girder Assembly]

    Q1 --> R1[Sandwich Core]
    R1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> R2[Frames and Stringers]
    Q3 --> R2
    R2 --> S1[Bulkheads]
    R2 --> S2[Keel and Chine]
    S1 --> T1[Distributed Reaction at Ends]
    S2 --> T1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid renders structural schematics as functional flow blocks. For physical line-drawing midship sections, students should reproduce the actual *sketch* in the examination hall — typically a half-breadth view showing: keel → bottom → chine → side → sheer → deck → bulkheads → frames → stringers → core.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` 
**Define the term "midship section" as applied to FRP boat design. Why is it considered the most critical cross-section?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The midship section is the transverse cross-section of the boat taken at the longitudinal station located at **0.5 L** from the Aft Perpendicular (AP), where L is the Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP). It is considered the most critical cross-section because:
1. The hull-girder **bending moment is maximum** at this station under both still-water and wave-loading conditions.
2. It serves as the **reference profile for classification society approval** (DNV, Lloyd's, ISO 12215-5).
3. The **section modulus, neutral axis location, and laminate schedule** are all defined based on this section, governing global strength.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six components typically shown in the midship section of an FRP boat.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. Outer hull laminate (with gelcoat)
2. Inner hull laminate
3. Sandwich core (PVC foam / end-grain balsa)
4. Transverse frames (T-section or angle)
5. Longitudinal stringers
6. Watertight bulkheads
7. Deck laminate (often sandwich)
8. Sheer clamp (hull-deck joint)
9. Keel reinforcement
10. Chine / knuckle line

*(Any six correct components fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Draw a neat labelled sketch of a typical midship section of a **single-chine FRP boat (12 m length, displacement hull)** built using sandwich construction. Show clearly the laminate schedule, core, frames, stringers, keel, and sheer clamp. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the bottom shell of the boat in part (a), calculate the **section modulus per metre width** using the following data, and comment on the strength adequacy:
- Outer face thickness: 4 mm (E-glass/polyester, $E_f = 18$ GPa)
- Core: 30 mm PVC foam ($E_c = 90$ MPa)
- Inner face thickness: 4 mm
- Allowable bending stress: 70 MPa
- Maximum hull-girder bending moment per metre width at midship: $M = 12.5$ kNm/m **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Sandwich geometry:**
- $t_f = 4$ mm, $t_c = 30$ mm
- Distance between face centroids: $d = t_c + t_f = 30 + 4 = 34$ mm
- $y_{max} = d/2 + t_f/2 = 17 + 2 = 19$ mm

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia (per metre width):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{B t_f d^2}{2} + \frac{B t_f^3}{6} + \frac{B t_c^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{(1000)(4)(34)^2}{2} + \frac{(1000)(4)^3}{6} + \frac{(1000)(30)^3}{12} \\
&= 2{,}312{,}000 + 10{,}667 + 2{,}250{,}000 \\
&= 4{,}572{,}667 \text{ mm}^4 \text{/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Section modulus:**

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{4{,}572{,}667}{19} = 240{,}667 \text{ mm}^3 \text{/m} = 240.67 \text{ cm}^3 \text{/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual bending stress:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{12.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Nmm/m}}{240{,}667 \text{ mm}^3/\text{m}} = 51.94 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Strength check:**

$$
\sigma = 51.94 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{allow} = 70 \text{ MPa}
$$

Factor of safety: $FOS = 70 / 51.94 = 1.35$ — **Adequate**. ✅

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between **single-skin** and **sandwich** construction in FRP boats. With neat sketches, show how each would appear in the midship section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 14 m FRP patrol boat has a midship section with the following longitudinal stiffness contributors. Calculate the **neutral axis location** and the **equivalent hull-girder section modulus** about the neutral axis, given the bending moment at midship is $M = 1.2 \times 10^8$ Nmm:
- **Bottom plate:** area $A_1 = 24{,}000$ mm² at $y_1 = 200$ mm; $I_{1,self} = 6 \times 10^5$ mm⁴
- **Side shell (both sides):** area $A_2 = 18{,}000$ mm² at $y_2 = 850$ mm; $I_{2,self} = 4.5 \times 10^5$ mm⁴
- **Deck plate:** area $A_3 = 30{,}000$ mm² at $y_3 = 1{,}500$ mm; $I_{3,self} = 7.5 \times 10^5$ mm⁴
- **Side stringers (combined):** area $A_4 = 4{,}000$ mm² at $y_4 = 1{,}200$ mm; $I_{4,self} = 1.0 \times 10^5$ mm⁴ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute total area and first moment of area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum A_i &= 24{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 76{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum A_i y_i &= (24{,}000)(200) + (18{,}000)(850) + (30{,}000)(1{,}500) + (4{,}000)(1{,}200) \\
&= 4{,}800{,}000 + 15{,}300{,}000 + 45{,}000{,}000 + 4{,}800{,}000 \\
&= 69{,}900{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Neutral axis:**

$$
y_{NA} = \frac{69{,}900{,}000}{76{,}000} = 919.74 \text{ mm} \approx 920 \text{ mm from baseline}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $I$ using parallel axis theorem:**

Distances from NA:
- $d_1 = 920 - 200 = 720$ mm
- $d_2 = 920 - 850 = 70$ mm
- $d_3 = 1500 - 920 = 580$ mm
- $d_4 = 1200 - 920 = 280$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \sum \left( I_{i,self} + A_i d_i^2 \right) \\
&= (6.0\times 10^5 + 24{,}000 \cdot 720^2) \\
&\quad + (4.5\times 10^5 + 18{,}000 \cdot 70^2) \\
&\quad + (7.5\times 10^5 + 30{,}000 \cdot 580^2) \\
&\quad + (1.0\times 10^5 + 4{,}000 \cdot 280^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= (600{,}000 + 1.244 \times 10^{10}) + (450{,}000 + 8.82 \times 10^7) \\
&\quad + (750{,}000 + 1.009 \times 10^{10}) + (100{,}000 + 3.136 \times 10^8) \\
&= 1.250 \times 10^{10} + 9.32 \times 10^7 + 1.017 \times 10^{10} + 3.236 \times 10^8 \\
&\approx 2.41 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Section modulus (use maximum $y_{max} = \max(920, 1500-920) = \max(920, 580) = 920$ mm):**

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{2.41 \times 10^{10}}{920} = 2.62 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Bending stress check:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^8}{2.62 \times 10^7} = 4.58 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is well within the FRP allowable stress of **70–125 MPa**, indicating the section is **highly adequate** for the given bending moment. ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the parallel-axis theorem** when computing $I$ of a built-up section — most common deduction (~3 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up deck vs bottom in $y_{max}$** — always take the larger distance from NA (whichever is farther from NA).
> 3. **Not labelling units** at intermediate steps — KTU valuators deduct ½ mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Drawing midship section without laminate schedule** — examiners expect at least the gelcoat, mat, woven roving, and core layers.
> 5. **Using solid-section formula** instead of sandwich formula when a core is present.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Typical Midship Section of FRP Boat:**

- ✅ Midship section is taken at **0.5 L** from AP — the location of **maximum bending moment**.
- ✅ A typical FRP midship section contains: **outer skin, inner skin, core (if sandwich), frames, stringers, bulkheads, deck, keel, sheer clamp, chine**.
- ✅ **Single-skin construction** is used for small boats (< 6 m); **sandwich construction** is mandatory for larger FRP vessels.
- ✅ **Sandwich core materials**: end-grain balsa (below waterline), PVC/SAN/PET foam (above waterline).
- ✅ Laminate schedule follows the order: **Gelcoat → CSM → Woven Roving → CSM → Core → CSM → Woven Roving → CSM → Gelcoat**.
- ✅ **Sandwich provides ~12× more section modulus** than solid laminate at the same weight.
- ✅ **Hull-girder section modulus** $Z = I / y_{max}$ is the key governing parameter.
- ✅ **Neutral axis** for FRP boats is typically at **50–65% of depth from keel baseline**.
- ✅ **Parallel-axis theorem** must always be used for $I$ of built-up sections.
- ✅ Classification rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ISO 12215-5) certify the boat based on midship section strength.
- ✅ **Bending stress check**: $\sigma = M / Z \leq \sigma_{allow}$ (typically 70–125 MPa).
- ✅ **Hull-girder stiffness** $EI$ is computed using transformed-section method for sandwich panels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — CONNECTIONS IN COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Composite Boat Design (PENST742)** as per the KTU 2024 Scheme, **connections** refer to the engineered joints used to transfer mechanical loads (tension, compression, shear, peel, and moment) between discrete composite structural members—such as hull shells, bulkheads, stiffeners, deck panels, frames, stringers, keel-to-hull junctions, mast steps, and rudder attachments—while maintaining the **in-service integrity** of the laminate under marine loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Connections in composite marine structures are classified into three principal families: (1) **Mechanical Fastened Joints** (bolted, riveted, pinned), (2) **Adhesive Bonded Joints** (structural adhesives), and (3) **Hybrid Joints** (combination of mechanical and adhesive). Each category is governed by distinct failure mechanics rooted in **anisotropic laminate theory**.

Connections are the **structural continuity points** that govern the load path from one laminate component to another. Unlike steel or aluminium boat construction where welding provides a continuous metallurgical joint, composites are inherently **non-weldable**, making the design of joints a critical determinant of overall hull strength, stiffness, fatigue life, and watertight integrity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Plywood Boat vs. The Steel Ship"**
>
> Imagine you are building a model boat. With a steel ship, you can fuse two plates by welding—the metal atoms literally merge, and the joint is as strong as the parent metal. Now try doing that with fiberglass (FRP) or carbon fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP)—the plastic matrix will simply burn and char. So how do we join composite panels in a boat?
>
> 1. **Mechanical (Bolts/Rivets)** — Like stitching two pieces of leather with a needle and thread: you drill a hole, insert a metal pin, and rely on **clamping and bearing** to transfer load. The hole is a *stress concentrator*, but the joint is inspectable and demountable.
> 2. **Adhesive Bonding** — Like gluing two pieces of wood: a thin layer of high-strength epoxy carries shear stress across the joint. The bond is **continuous, lightweight, and corrosion-free**, but you cannot easily inspect it or undo it.
> 3. **Hybrid** — Stitch *and* glue. This gives you both **fail-safe redundancy** and **continuous load transfer**, which is exactly how modern racing yachts and naval composite craft are joined.

### 1.3 Why Connections are the *Weakest Link* in Composite Boats

In any composite marine structure, the **joint is statistically the most likely location of failure**. The reason is rooted in three fundamental facts:

| Phenomenon | Effect on Joint |
|------------|-----------------|
| **Stress Concentration** | Holes, bond-line edge discontinuities, and ply-drops amplify local stresses by factors of **3–6×** nominal |
| **Anisotropy** | Composite strength is direction-dependent; a joint designed for one load direction may fail under transverse load |
| **Hygrothermal Sensitivity** | Marine environment (salt water, UV, temperature cycling) degrades both adhesive interfaces and laminate around fastener holes |

### 1.4 Standard Marine Connection Metrics

The following metrics are universally referenced in composite boat joint design and are weighted heavily in KTU examinations:

- **Bearing Strength (σᵦ)** ≈ **300–700 MPa** for typical CFRP laminates
- **Lap Shear Strength (τ)** ≈ **15–35 MPa** for structural marine epoxies
- **Joint Efficiency (η)** = (Joint Strength / Laminate Strength) × 100%
- **Pitch (p)** and **Edge Distance (e)** ratios: typically **p/d ≥ 3**, **e/d ≥ 2**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for Students:** A composite connection is *not* merely a fastening point — it is a **load-transfer mechanism** whose design dictates whether the boat survives impact, slamming, bending, and fatigue over its **25–30 year** service life.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Composite Boat Connections

Composite boat connections can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**: load transfer mechanism, fabrication methodology, and structural role.

### 2.1.1 By Load Transfer Mechanism

1. **Bearing-Type (Mechanical)** — Load transferred through fastener shank pressing on the laminate hole wall.
2. **Shear-Type (Adhesive)** — Load transferred through in-plane shear of the adhesive layer.
3. **Hybrid (Combination)** — Co-transfer through both mechanisms; provides fail-safe redundancy.

### 2.1.2 By Structural Role

- **Single-Lap Joint** — Simplest; induces secondary bending moment due to eccentricity.
- **Double-Lap Joint** — Symmetric; cancels bending; preferred in primary hull connections.
- **Stepped / Scarf Joint** — Used for thick laminates (e.g., keel-hull scarf); nearly uniform stress distribution.
- **Tapered Joint** — Adhesive thickness varies linearly to minimize peel stress.
- **Bolted Single/Double Shear** — Standard mechanical joint for bulkhead-shell interfaces.

## 2.2 Failure Modes in Mechanically Fastened Composite Joints

The four classical failure modes (per **Hart-Smith** and **Collings**) for bolted/riveted composite joints are:

| Mode | Description | Controlling Variable |
|------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Bearing Failure** | Local crushing of laminate around the hole | **σᵦ = P / (d·t)** |
| **Net Tension Failure** | Tensile rupture across the net section (through the hole) | **σ_NT = P / [(w − d)·t]** |
| **Shear-Out Failure** | Shear failure of laminate ahead of the hole toward the free edge | **τ_SO = P / (2·e·t)** |
| **Cleavage / Pull-Through Failure** | Out-of-plane tearing due to thin laminates bending | Geometry + bending stiffness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** KTU examiners frequently test *which failure mode governs* for a given (w/d, e/d, material) combination. The answer depends on **stress ratios**, not absolute strength.

## 2.3 Failure Modes in Adhesive Bonded Joints

For single-lap adhesive joints, the three classical failure modes are:

1. **Cohesive Failure** — Within the adhesive layer (preferred, indicates proper surface prep).
2. **Adhesive Failure** — At the interface (indicates poor surface treatment).
3. **Substrate (Laminate) Failure** — The composite fails before the bond; indicates an *over-designed* joint.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{d \cdot t}$ | Bearing stress on hole wall | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_{NT} = \dfrac{P}{(w - d) \cdot t}$ | Net tension stress | MPa |
| 3 | $\tau_{SO} = \dfrac{P}{2 e t}$ | Shear-out stress | MPa |
| 4 | $\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot \ell}$ | Average lap shear in adhesive joint | MPa |
| 5 | $K = 1 + 3 \dfrac{t}{\ell} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}}$ | Stress concentration factor (single-lap) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_J = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{ult,laminate}} \times 100\%$ | Joint efficiency | % |
| 7 | $\dfrac{p}{d} \geq 3, \quad \dfrac{e}{d} \geq 2$ | Standard pitch & edge-distance ratios | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_b \cdot d \cdot t}{FS}$ | Allowable bearing load per fastener | N |
| 9 | $\sigma_{max} = K \cdot \tau_{avg}$ | Peak shear at lap ends (single-lap) | MPa |
| 10 | $\tau_{peel} = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{t}{L^2} \cdot \dfrac{E_1}{G}$ | Peel stress at adhesive joint edge | MPa |

> **Notation Key:**
> $P$ = applied load (N), $d$ = fastener diameter (mm), $t$ = laminate thickness (mm), $w$ = laminate width (mm), $e$ = edge distance (mm), $\ell$ = lap length (mm), $E_1$ = laminate axial modulus (GPa), $G$ = adhesive shear modulus (MPa), $FS$ = factor of safety.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These connection principles are used in:

- **Naval frigate hull-shell to frame connections** (typically bolted + bonded hybrid).
- **America's Cup AC75 foiling yachts** (fully bonded CFRP with secondary titanium fasteners).
- **Submarine pressure hull ring-frame interfaces** (mechanical, with high e/d ratios).
- **Wind-turbine blade root joints** (technically similar to boat keel joints — high-load T-bolt connections).
- **Recreational FRP fishing boats** (riveted single-lap joins for shell-bulkhead).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bearing Strength and Failure Load for a Single Bolted Composite Joint

### Problem Setup
A single bolted connection transfers an axial tensile load **P** through a laminate of width **w**, thickness **t**, with a fastener of diameter **d** at edge distance **e**. We wish to derive the **failure load under each mode**.

### Step 1 — Bearing Failure Load
Bearing stress is the contact pressure between the fastener shank and the laminate hole wall. The projected bearing area is the **diameter × thickness** cylinder face.

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_b}{d \cdot t}$$

Solving for the failure load:

$$P_b = \sigma_{b,ult} \cdot d \cdot t$$

**Logic:** The bearing stress is force divided by projected bearing area. Setting $\sigma_b = \sigma_{b,ult}$ gives the maximum load before crushing begins.

### Step 2 — Net Tension Failure Load
The critical cross-section is the row through the hole center, where the resisting material is reduced from $w$ to $(w - d)$.

$$\sigma_{NT} = \frac{P_{NT}}{(w - d) \cdot t}$$

Setting $\sigma_{NT} = \sigma_{NT,ult}$:

$$P_{NT} = \sigma_{NT,ult} \cdot (w - d) \cdot t$$

**Logic:** The hole removes material equal to $d \cdot t$ in cross-section. The remaining net area must carry the full load.

### Step 3 — Shear-Out Failure Load
Shear-out occurs along **two parallel shear planes** of length $e$ ahead of the hole to the free edge, each of thickness $t$.

$$\tau_{SO} = \frac{P_{SO}}{2 e t}$$

Solving for the failure load:

$$P_{SO} = \tau_{SO,ult} \cdot 2 e t$$

**Logic:** Two shear planes share the load equally because of symmetry about the hole centerline.

### Step 4 — Failure Mode Identification (KTU Examiner's Method)
The *governing* failure mode is the one with the **lowest failure load**:

$$P_{fail} = \min \left\{ P_b,\ P_{NT},\ P_{SO},\ P_{cleavage} \right\}$$

This is the KTU Board's standard approach: **enumerate every mode, compute each load, select the minimum**.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Given:** Single-bolt joint, $d = 6 \text{ mm}$, $t = 4 \text{ mm}$, $w = 30 \text{ mm}$, $e = 12 \text{ mm}$.
> Material: GFRP with $\sigma_{b,ult} = 400 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_{NT,ult} = 250 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_{SO,ult} = 150 \text{ MPa}$.

**Step 5.1 — Bearing load:**

$$P_b = 400 \times 6 \times 4 = 9600 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5.2 — Net tension load:**

$$P_{NT} = 250 \times (30 - 6) \times 4 = 250 \times 24 \times 4 = 24000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5.3 — Shear-out load:**

$$P_{SO} = 150 \times 2 \times 12 \times 4 = 14400 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5.4 — Minimum:**

$$P_{fail} = \min\{9600,\ 24000,\ 14400\} = 9600 \text{ N}$$

**Conclusion:** **Bearing governs.** The joint will crush around the hole before it tears or shears out. This is the *ductile*, preferred failure mode for marine composite joints — it gives visible deformation warning before catastrophic collapse.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stress Concentration in Single-Lap Adhesive Joint (Goland-Reissner Theory — Simplified)

### Step 1 — Why Bending Matters
A single-lap joint has the load lines **offset** by the combined thickness of the two adherends. This offset creates an **eccentricity moment**:

$$M = P \cdot \frac{(t_1 + t_2)}{2}$$

This moment causes **rotation** of the adherends, generating both shear and **peel (normal) stress** at the lap ends.

### Step 2 — Average Shear Stress
The adhesive carries an average in-plane shear stress:

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{P}{w \cdot \ell}$$

### Step 3 — Peak Shear Stress (Modified for Bending)
The peak shear at the lap ends is amplified by the bending:

$$\tau_{max} = \tau_{avg} \cdot \left[ 1 + 3 \frac{t}{\ell} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}} \right]$$

Where $E_1, E_2$ are adherend axial moduli.

### Step 4 — Peak Peel Stress
The peel stress at the end of the lap is:

$$\sigma_{peel} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \cdot \frac{E_1}{G}$$

**Logic:** Peel stress grows with laminate thickness $t$ and inversely with the square of lap length $L$. Hence, **long thin laps are vastly superior to short thick laps** — a cardinal rule in composite boat joint design.

### Step 5 — Design Implication
The single-lap joint is **not recommended** for primary boat structural joints when $L/t < 50$. For primary hull-to-deck or keel-to-hull bonds, use **double-lap**, **stepped**, or **scarf** configurations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Joint Selection Guide for Boat Components

| Boat Component | Recommended Joint Type | Typical w/d or L/t | Governing Failure Mode |
|----------------|------------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hull shell to bulkhead | Adhesive (epoxy) + secondary fasteners | $L/t \geq 50$ | Peel at lap ends |
| Keel to hull | Hybrid (bolted + bonded) | $w/d = 5$, $e/d = 3$ | Bearing |
| Deck to hull flange | Bonded + riveted | $p/d = 4$ | Bearing / Net tension |
| Frame to shell (small craft) | Adhesive fillet | $L/t = 30$ | Shear in fillet |
| Mast step | Mechanical (high-strength bolts) | $w/d = 4$, $e/d = 4$ | Bearing |
| Rudder gudgeon | Bolted single-shear | $w/d = 3$, $e/d = 2$ | Shear-out |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Connection Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["COMPOSITE BOAT CONNECTIONS"] --> B["MECHANICAL JOINTS"]
    A --> C["ADHESIVE JOINTS"]
    A --> D["HYBRID JOINTS"]

    B --> B1["BOLTED"]
    B --> B2["RIVETED"]
    B --> B3["PINNED"]

    B1 --> F1["BEARING FAILURE"]
    B1 --> F2["NET TENSION FAILURE"]
    B1 --> F3["SHEAR OUT FAILURE"]
    B1 --> F4["CLEAVAGE FAILURE"]

    C --> C1["SINGLE LAP"]
    C --> C2["DOUBLE LAP"]
    C --> C3["SCARF JOINT"]
    C --> C4["TAPERED LAP"]
    C --> C5["STEPPED LAP"]

    C1 --> G1["PEEL STRESS"]
    C1 --> G2["SHEAR STRESS"]

    D --> D1["BOLTED PLUS BONDED"]
    D --> D2["RIVETED PLUS BONDED"]

    D1 --> H1["FAIL SAFE REDUNDANCY"]
    D1 --> H2["IMPROVED FATIGUE LIFE"]

    F1 --> I1["GOVERNS WHEN w/d IS SMALL"]
    F2 --> I2["GOVERNS WHEN w/d IS LARGE"]
    F3 --> I3["GOVERNS WHEN e/d IS SMALL"]

    C2 --> J1["CANCELLS BENDING MOMENT"]
    C3 --> J2["UNIFORM STRESS DISTRIBUTION"]
    C4 --> J3["MINIMIZES PEEL CONCENTRATION"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Load Path Through a Bolted Composite Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["APPLIED TENSILE LOAD P"] --> L1["LAMINATE UPPER SECTION"]
    L1 --> H1["HOLE WALL BEARING REGION"]
    H1 --> FB["FASTENER SHANK"]
    FB --> H2["HOLE WALL BEARING REGION LOWER"]
    H2 --> L2["LAMINATE LOWER SECTION"]
    L2 --> P2["REACTION FORCE"]

    H1 --> R1["BEARING STRESS SIGMA B"]
    L1 --> R2["NET TENSION AT HOLE"]
    H2 --> R3["SHEAR OUT TO FREE EDGE"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Failure Mode Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START: COMPUTE P_b, P_NT, P_SO"] --> S2["COMPARE w/d RATIO"]

    S2 -->|w/d LESS THAN 3| M1["BEARING GOVERNS"]
    S2 -->|w/d GREATER THAN 4| M2["NET TENSION GOVERNS"]

    S2 --> S3["COMPARE e/d RATIO"]

    S3 -->|e/d LESS THAN 2| M3["SHEAR OUT GOVERNS"]
    S3 -->|e/d GREATER THAN 3| M4["BEARING OR NET TENSION"]

    M1 --> OUT1["DUCTILE FAILURE PREFERRED"]
    M2 --> OUT2["BRITTLE FAILURE AVOID IF POSSIBLE"]
    M3 --> OUT3["INCREASE EDGE DISTANCE"]
    M4 --> OUT4["PROCEED TO NET TENSION CHECK"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Adhesive Joint Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ADHESIVE LAYER BETWEEN TWO LAMINATES"] --> B["PEAK SHEAR AT LAP ENDS"]
    A --> C["MINIMUM SHEAR AT LAP CENTER"]
    A --> D["PEAK PEEL AT LAP ENDS"]
    A --> E["COMPRESSIVE NORMAL AT OVERLAP MIDDLE"]

    B --> F1["CAUSES ADHESIVE COHESIVE FAILURE"]
    D --> F2["CAUSES ADHESIVE INTERFACE FAILURE"]
    E --> F3["BENEFICIAL CLAMPING STRESS"]

    F1 --> SOL1["INCREASE LAP LENGTH"]
    F2 --> SOL2["ADD TAPER OR USE SCARF"]
    F3 --> SOL3["MAINTAIN MODERATE CLAMPING PRESSURE"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Define bearing failure in a composite mechanically fastened joint. Under what geometric condition does it typically govern the design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bearing failure occurs when the compressive stress between the fastener shank and the laminate hole wall exceeds the laminate's bearing strength, causing localized crushing of the matrix and fiber micro-buckling around the hole. The governing stress is $\sigma_b = P / (d \cdot t)$.

It typically **governs** when the width-to-diameter ratio $w/d \leq 3$, i.e., when the laminate is *narrow relative to the fastener*. Bearing failure is the **preferred (ductile) failure mode** in marine composite joints because it provides visible deformation warning before ultimate collapse. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**State any three failure modes in a single-lap adhesively bonded joint. Why is single-lap configuration discouraged for primary boat structural joints?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three failure modes are:
1. **Cohesive failure** — within the adhesive layer (preferred).
2. **Adhesive (interfacial) failure** — at the adherend-adhesive interface.
3. **Substrate (laminate) failure** — within the composite adherend.

Single-lap joints are discouraged for primary structural use because the **load-line eccentricity** induces a bending moment, which generates high **peel (tensile) stress concentrations at the lap ends**. The peak shear is amplified by a factor of 3–6× the average, drastically reducing joint efficiency. For hull-to-deck or keel-to-hull primary bonds, **double-lap, stepped, or scarf** geometries are used. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

#### [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**a)** With neat sketches, explain the **four classical failure modes** of a single-bolt composite joint. For each mode, state the governing equation and the geometric parameter that controls it. **(7 marks)**
