- **Process sequence (must memorise in order):** Lines plan → Plug → Mould → Gelcoat → Layup → Cure → Demould → Trim.
- **Gelcoat thickness:** **0.4–0.6 mm**; pigmented, sprayed first on mould surface.
- **Release agent:** Carnauba wax 4–6 coats + optional PVA film; **never use metal pry bars**.
- **Layup rule of thumb:** $t_{CSM} \approx 0.31$ mm for 450 g/m²; $t_{WR} \approx 0.52$ mm for 800 g/m².
- **Resin-to-fibre ratio:** 2:1 to 3:1 by mass for hand layup; 1:1 for infusion.
- **Demoulding time:** **24 h** at 18–25 °C ambient for polyester.
- **Mould shrinkage compensation:** $L_{mould} = L_{hull} \times (1 + S)$ where $S = 0.001$ to $0.003$.
- **Fibre volume fraction target:** 30–35 % hand layup; 45–55 % VARTM; 50–60 % RTM.
- **Mould types:** Female (cavity) vs Male (plug-side); Single-piece vs Multi-piece; Open vs Closed.
- **Common defects to avoid:** Voids, dry spots, delamination, print-through, blistering, warping, mould damage.
- **Always roll** every layer with a metal/serpentine roller for consolidation and air expulsion.
- **Always assume** a default $V_f$ when not specified in numerical problems (30 % for hand layup).
- **Standard physical constants:** $\rho_{glass} = 2550$ kg/m³, $\rho_{polyester} = 1150$ kg/m³, $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa.
- **Safety:** MEKP catalyst is explosive above 60 °C — never mix directly with accelerator or in bulk.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deck Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deck Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Deck Moulding** is the controlled manufacturing process used in composite boat construction to produce the **horizontal upper shell (deck)** of a vessel using a **male (positive) mould**, where layers of fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) matrix materials are sequentially deposited over a pre-shaped plug or framework, cured under controlled ambient conditions, and subsequently demoulded to yield a finished, dimensionally-stable deck laminate.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, deck moulding is classified under **Open Mould Contact Moulding (Hand Lay-Up / Spray-Up)** when performed over a **single-sided male tool**, in contrast to the female (cavity) mould used for hull laminates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of it like making a jelly mould over a hill instead of inside a bowl.**

Imagine you want to make a chocolate shell shaped like half a coconut. Instead of pouring chocolate inside a hollow coconut-shaped bowl (the **female mould** method used for hulls), you sculpt the shape of a half-coconut in plaster, coat it with release wax, and then apply layers of chocolate over the *outside* of that hill. Once it hardens, you lift the chocolate shell off. The "hill" is your **male mould**, the chocolate layers are your **fibreglass + resin**, and the finished shell is your **deck**.

This is exactly how a boat deck is built — over a shaped male surface that mirrors the underside (interior ceiling) of the deck.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Geometric Constants

- **Standard Gelcoat Thickness:** **0.4 mm – 0.6 mm**
- **Typical Resin-to-Glass Ratio:** **2.5 : 1 by weight**
- **Ambient Cure Temperature:** **25 °C ± 3 °C**
- **Mould Release Wax Coats:** **5 – 7 buffed layers**
- **PVA (Polyvinyl Alcohol) Barrier Thickness:** **0.02 mm**
- **Demoulding Cure Time:** **24 – 48 hours** at ambient
- **Standard Male Mould Shrinkage Allowance:** **1.0 % – 1.5 %**

### 1.4 Why a Male Mould for Decks? (KTU Board Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> Hulls require **female moulds** because the boat's outer (wetted) surface must be smooth and fair for hydrodynamic efficiency. Decks, however, present the *opposite* requirement — the **inner (cabin head) surface** must be glossy and cosmetically perfect (visible to occupants), while the **outer walking surface** can tolerate a slightly textured finish.

A male mould naturally transfers its outer (fair) surface to the **inner (cosmetic) side** of the deck laminate — exactly what designers need.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence — The Six Pillars of Deck Moulding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU 2024 Scheme question on deck moulding traces back to one of these six stages. Memorize the order.

1. **Plug/Framework Fabrication** — A master shape (wood, plaster, foam, or directly the inverted hull structure) is prepared to the exact internal contour of the finished deck.
2. **Surface Preparation & Sealing** — Plug is sanded to **P400 grit**, sealed with **polyester or epoxy sealer**, and buffed.
3. **Mould Release System Application** — A semi-permanent release system is built up:
   - 5–7 coats of **mould release wax** (e.g., Meguiar's #8 or Partall Paste)
   - 1 coat of **PVA liquid release agent**
4. **Gelcoat Application** — Pigmented **isophthalic or orthophthalic polyester gelcoat** (or vinylester for marine-grade) is sprayed at **0.4 – 0.6 mm** WFT (Wet Film Thickness).
5. **Laminate Layup** — Sequential application of:
   - **First chop-strand mat (CSM) layer** — 300 g/m² for resin barrier
   - **Woven roving / biaxial fabric** — 450 g/m² to 800 g/m²
   - **Core material (optional)** — PVC foam, balsa, or end-grain balsa for stiffness
   - **Outer CSM** — for weatherability and print suppression
6. **Curing, Demoulding & Trimming** — Ambient cure 24–48 hrs, mechanical demoulding, edge trimming.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laminate Thickness (t) | $t = \dfrac{m_{glass}}{\rho_{glass}} \cdot N_{plies}$ | mm | $m$ = areal weight, $\rho$ = density, $N$ = no. of plies |
| Resin Quantity | $W_{resin} = W_{glass} \times R$ | kg | $R$ = resin-to-glass ratio (typ. **2.5**) |
| Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_r} \cdot R}$ | dimensionless | $\rho_f$ ≈ **2.55 g/cm³**, $\rho_r$ ≈ **1.12 g/cm³** |
| Plate Stiffness (D) | $D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | N·m | $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio ≈ **0.3** |
| Curing Exotherm Peak | $T_{max} = T_{amb} + \dfrac{H_r \cdot m}{c_p \cdot \rho}$ | °C | $H_r$ = heat of reaction |
| Gelcoat Coverage | $A = \dfrac{V_{gel}}{\rho_{gel} \cdot t_{gel}}$ | m² | Used for spray gun calibration |
| Deflection of Mould Plug | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^{4}}{384 E I}$ | mm | For temporary plug under self-weight |

### 2.3 Why Each Stage Matters (Engineering Utility)

- **Gelcoat** protects against **osmosis blistering** (critical for marine service life of **15 – 25 years**).
- **CSM first layer** prevents **print-through** of woven roving patterns.
- **Core material** increases **specific stiffness** ($E/\rho$) by up to **3×** without weight penalty.
- **PVA release** acts as a **safety net** — if wax fails, PVA prevents catastrophic mould damage worth lakhs of rupees.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Deck moulding underpins the construction of fishing trawlers (Kerala coast), coast guard interceptor boats, inland passenger ferries, and luxury yachts built in Sri Lanka, Kochi, and Mangalore shipyards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Gelcoat Quantity for a Boat Deck

> **Given:** A boat deck of plan area $A = 12 \,\text{m}^{2}$ is to be coated with gelcoat of target wet film thickness $t_{gel} = 0.5 \,\text{mm}$. Gelcoat density $\rho_{gel} = 1.35 \,\text{g/cm}^{3}$. Determine the volume and mass of gelcoat required, including a **15 % wastage factor**.

**Step 1 — Convert thickness to centimetres for unit consistency**

$$
t_{gel} = 0.5 \,\text{mm} = 0.05 \,\text{cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the theoretical volume of gelcoat**

$$
V_{gel} = A \cdot t_{gel}
$$

$$
V_{gel} = 12 \,\text{m}^{2} \cdot 0.005 \,\text{m} = 0.06 \,\text{m}^{3} = 60{,}000 \,\text{cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the theoretical mass**

$$
m_{gel} = V_{gel} \cdot \rho_{gel}
$$

$$
m_{gel} = 60{,}000 \,\text{cm}^{3} \cdot 1.35 \,\text{g/cm}^{3} = 81{,}000 \,\text{g} = 81 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 15 % wastage factor**

$$
m_{actual} = m_{gel} \times (1 + 0.15)
$$

$$
m_{actual} = 81 \times 1.15 = 93.15 \,\text{kg}
$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **93.15 kg** of gelcoat is required.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Laminate Weight of a Composite Deck

> **Given:** Layup schedule = **[CSM 300] / [WR 600] / [CSM 450] / [WR 600] / [CSM 300]** over a deck area of $12 \,\text{m}^{2}$, with resin-to-glass ratio $R = 2.5$.

**Step 1 — Sum total glass areal weight**

$$
m_{glass/m^{2}} = 300 + 600 + 450 + 600 + 300 = 2250 \,\text{g/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Total glass weight for the deck**

$$
W_{glass} = 2250 \,\text{g/m}^{2} \times 12 \,\text{m}^{2} = 27{,}000 \,\text{g} = 27 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute resin weight**

$$
W_{resin} = W_{glass} \times R = 27 \times 2.5 = 67.5 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Total laminate weight (excluding gelcoat)**

$$
W_{total} = W_{glass} + W_{resin} = 27 + 67.5 = 94.5 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Estimate laminate thickness (using $\rho_{glass} \approx 1.8 \,\text{g/cm}^{3}$, $\rho_{resin} \approx 1.12 \,\text{g/cm}^{3}$)**

$$
V_{glass} = \dfrac{27{,}000}{1.8} = 15{,}000 \,\text{cm}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{resin} = \dfrac{67{,}500}{1.12} \approx 60{,}268 \,\text{cm}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{lam} = 75{,}268 \,\text{cm}^{3}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{75{,}268 \,\text{cm}^{3}}{120{,}000 \,\text{cm}^{2}} \approx 0.627 \,\text{cm} \approx 6.3 \,\text{mm}
$$

> **Answer:** Total laminate weight ≈ **94.5 kg**, average thickness ≈ **6.3 mm**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Male vs Female Moulding

| Criterion | Male Mould (Deck) | Female Mould (Hull) |
|---|---|---|
| Mould surface | Outer (convex) | Inner (concave) |
| Cosmetic face transferred | Inner cabin head | Outer wetted hull |
| Demoulding direction | Pulled *up* off the tool | Pulled *out* from cavity |
| Tool cost | **Lower (≈ ₹1.5 – 3 L)** | Higher (≈ ₹4 – 12 L) |
| Demoulding force required | Lower | Higher (suction) |
| Gelcoat location | Sprayed on mould first | Sprayed on mould first |
| Geometric complexity | Easier to layup complex cabin profiles | Limited by undercutting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Deck Moulding Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Drawings] --> B[Stage 1: Plug or Framework Fabrication]
    B --> B1[Wooden ribs + stringers]
    B --> B2[Plaster or PU foam fairing]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Surface Preparation]
    C --> C1[Sanding to P400 grit]
    C --> C2[Sealer application 2 coats]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Release System]
    D --> D1[Apply 6 coats mould release wax]
    D --> D2[Buff to high gloss]
    D --> D3[Apply PVA liquid film]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Gelcoat Spray]
    E --> E1[Spray 0.5 mm isophthalic gelcoat]
    E --> E2[Initial cure 2 to 4 hours]
    E --> F[Stage 5: Laminate Layup Sequence]
    F --> F1[CSM 300 barrier layer]
    F --> F2[WR 600 structural layer]
    F --> F3[Optional PVC foam core]
    F --> F4[WR 600 + CSM 450]
    F --> F5[Final CSM 300 weather layer]
    F --> G[Stage 6: Curing and Demoulding]
    G --> G1[Ambient cure 24 to 48 hours]
    G --> G2[Demould using wedges and air jets]
    G --> G3[Edge trimming and drilling]
    G --> H[End: Finished Deck Laminate]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cross-Sectional Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Outer Walking Surface]
        O1[CSM 300 weather coat]
        O2[WR 600]
        O3[CSM 450]
        O4[WR 600]
    end
    subgraph L2[Core Zone Optional]
        K1[PVC foam 10 to 20 mm or balsa]
    end
    subgraph L3[Inner Cosmetic Cabin Surface]
        I1[CSM 300 barrier mat]
        I2[Vinylester or iso gelcoat 0.5 mm]
        I3[Male mould tool surface]
    end
    O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> O4 --> K1 --> I1 --> I2 --> I3
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Geometric Logic of Male Mould (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M[Tool Side]
        T1[Finished plug surface]
        T2[Release wax and PVA]
        T3[Gelcoat 0.5 mm]
    end
    subgraph L[Composite Laminate]
        C1[CSM barrier]
        C2[Reinforcement layers]
        C3[Core if used]
        C4[Outer skin]
    end
    subgraph F[Finished Deck]
        F1[Inner cabin surface glossy]
        F2[Outer walking surface]
    end
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    C1 -.becomes.-> F1
    C4 -.becomes.-> F2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a male mould and a female mould. Why is a male mould preferred for deck laminates?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **male mould** is a positive tool shaped as the inverse of the finished part's outer surface, while a **female mould** is a negative cavity that matches the part's outer surface. For deck laminates, the male mould is preferred because (i) it naturally produces a **glossy inner cabin-head surface**, which is cosmetically critical, (ii) it **lowers tool cost**, and (iii) it enables **easier demoulding** of complex cabin profiles without undercuts. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the function of PVA (Polyvinyl Alcohol) in the deck moulding release system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PVA acts as a **sacrificial barrier film** between the cured laminate and the waxed mould surface. It guarantees clean release even when wax application is imperfect, prevents styrene attack on the mould, and protects expensive mould tools from damage during demoulding. PVA is water-soluble and is washed off the mould after each demoulding cycle. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the complete step-by-step process of **deck moulding using a male mould** for a 12 m fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP) fishing boat. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deck of plan area **15 m²** is to be laminated using the schedule **[CSM 300] / [WR 600] / [CSM 450] / [WR 600] / [CSM 450]**. Calculate the **total glass weight, resin weight (R = 2.5), and approximate laminate thickness**. Take $\rho_{glass} = 1.8 \,\text{g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_{resin} = 1.12 \,\text{g/cm}^3$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Process Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Plug preparation:** A wooden framework of transverse frames and longitudinal stringers is erected to match the deck's internal profile. Plaster of Paris or PU foam is faired over to obtain the exact contour. Sanded to P400. (1 mark)
2. **Sealing:** Two coats of polyester sealer are applied and cured. (0.5 mark)
3. **Release system:** Six coats of paste wax are buffed; one coat of PVA liquid is sprayed. (1 mark)
4. **Gelcoat:** Isophthalic polyester gelcoat pigmented to the cabin colour is sprayed at 0.5 mm WFT and allowed to tack-cure until "just sticky". (1 mark)
5. **Laminate layup:** CSM 300 is laid first using rollers and resin (1 mark). Then WR 600 is wetted out (1 mark). Subsequent CSM 450 and WR 600 layers follow. Each layer must be thoroughly consolidated with ribbed rollers to expel air. (1 mark)
6. **Curing & demoulding:** After 24–48 hrs ambient cure, wedges and compressed air are used to release the laminate. Edges are trimmed using a jigsaw. (0.5 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total areal glass weight:**

$$
300 + 600 + 450 + 600 + 450 = 2400 \,\text{g/m}^{2}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Total glass weight:**

$$
W_{glass} = 2400 \times 15 = 36{,}000 \,\text{g} = 36 \,\text{kg}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 3 — Resin weight:**

$$
W_{resin} = 36 \times 2.5 = 90 \,\text{kg}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 4 — Volume of glass:**

$$
V_{glass} = \dfrac{36{,}000}{1.8} = 20{,}000 \,\text{cm}^{3}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Volume of resin:**

$$
V_{resin} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{1.12} \approx 80{,}357 \,\text{cm}^{3}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 6 — Total laminate thickness:**

$$
t = \dfrac{20{,}000 + 80{,}357}{15 \times 10{,}000} = \dfrac{100{,}357}{150{,}000} \approx 0.669 \,\text{cm} \approx 6.7 \,\text{mm}
$$

(1 mark)

> **Final Answer:** Glass = **36 kg**, Resin = **90 kg**, Thickness ≈ **6.7 mm**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]*

**(a)** Discuss the **role of gelcoat, CSM barrier layer, and core material** in a composite boat deck laminate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A boat yard uses **100 kg of gelcoat** to spray deck moulds. If the gelcoat density is **1.35 g/cm³** and the desired wet film thickness is **0.5 mm**, calculate the **maximum deck area** that can be coated. Assume **10 % wastage**. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Roles of Each Layer (7 marks):**

- **Gelcoat (2 marks):** Provides the cosmetic glossy finish on the cabin-head side; acts as the **primary moisture barrier** against osmosis; offers **UV resistance** through pigmented topcoat chemistry. Typically 0.4–0.6 mm.
- **CSM Barrier Layer (2.5 marks):** Placed *immediately* over the gelcoat to (i) absorb resin-rich gelcoat shrinkage stresses, (ii) hide the print-through of heavier woven fabrics, (iii) provide a resin-rich, crack-resistant interface. Typical areal weight: 300 g/m².
- **Core Material (2.5 marks):** PVC foam, SAN foam, or end-grain balsa inserted between inner and outer FRP skins creates a **sandwich construction**. It dramatically increases **flexural rigidity** (D) as $D \propto t^3$, reduces weight by 20–30 %, and provides thermal/acoustic insulation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Effective gelcoat available after wastage:**

$$
m_{eff} = 100 \times (1 - 0.10) = 90 \,\text{kg} = 90{,}000 \,\text{g}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 2 — Volume of gelcoat:**

$$
V_{gel} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{1.35} \approx 66{,}667 \,\text{cm}^{3}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 3 — Convert WFT to cm:**

$$
t = 0.5 \,\text{mm} = 0.05 \,\text{cm}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Area that can be coated:**

$$
A = \dfrac{V_{gel}}{t} = \dfrac{66{,}667}{0.05} = 1{,}333{,}333 \,\text{cm}^{2} = 133.33 \,\text{m}^{2}
$$

(3 marks)

> **Final Answer:** Maximum deck area ≈ **133.33 m²**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently mix mm, cm, and m in the same calculation. Always convert to **SI base units (m, kg)** at the very start.
> 2. **Missing Wastage Factor:** KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests for **wastage factors (10–20 %)**. Skipping this costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Male/Female Mould Terminology:** Examiners deduct marks if "male mould" is described as having an internal cavity. Remember: male = **convex (hill)**, female = **concave (bowl)**.
> 4. **Omitting Layup Order:** The order **[Gelcoat → CSM → WR → CSM → WR → CSM]** is fixed by KTU rubric. Reversed orders lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting PVA's Role:** Don't confuse PVA with wax. Wax = primary release; PVA = secondary safety barrier.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Board Pattern**

- ✔ Deck moulding uses a **MALE (positive) mould** — opposite of hull moulding.
- ✔ **Six canonical stages:** Plug → Surface Prep → Release → Gelcoat → Layup → Cure & Demould.
- ✔ **Gelcoat thickness:** **0.4–0.6 mm**; applied FIRST on the mould.
- ✔ **Standard layup order:** CSM 300 → WR 600 → CSM 450 → WR 600 → CSM 300.
- ✔ **Resin-to-Glass ratio (R):** Typically **2.5** for hand lay-up.
- ✔ **Demoulding time:** **24–48 hours** at **25 °C ambient**.
- ✔ **PVA** is the *safety net* of the release system; it is water-soluble.
- ✔ **Male mould = glossy INNER surface** of deck; female mould = glossy OUTER surface of hull.
- ✔ **Shrinkage allowance on plug:** **1.0–1.5 %**.
- ✔ **Sand plug to P400 grit** before sealer; never skip the sealer coat.
- ✔ **Core materials** (PVC foam / balsa) increase plate stiffness **cubically** per the $t^3$ relation.
- ✔ **Gelcoat quantity formula:** $m = A \cdot t \cdot \rho \cdot (1 + \text{wastage})$.
- ✔ Always convert thickness to **m** when area is in **m²** to avoid dimensional mismatch.
- ✔ KTU expects a labelled **sketch** in any 7-mark process question — full marks require both *diagram + explanation*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Environmental Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Control** in the context of composite boat design refers to the systematic regulation and management of ambient physical, chemical, and atmospheric parameters within the manufacturing facility and its associated workspaces (mould preparation rooms, lay-up halls, curing zones, and material storage areas) to ensure the consistent, defect-free production of fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) marine structures.

The principal parameters under environmental control include:

- **Temperature** (typically maintained between **18 °C and 24 °C** for most thermoset resin systems)
- **Relative Humidity (RH)** (commonly controlled between **30 % and 65 %** depending on resin chemistry)
- **Airborne Particulate Count** (governed by ISO 14644 cleanroom standards)
- **Volatile Organic Compound (VOC) Concentration**
- **Lighting Levels and Spectral Quality**
- **Air Changes per Hour (ACH)** for ventilation
- **Differential Pressure** between clean and less-clean zones

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Environmental Control is treated as a critical "controllable variable" in the design-for-manufacture (DFM) workflow of composite boat building. The quality of a marine composite laminate is highly sensitive to ambient conditions during the *entire* process chain — from raw material storage to final cure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of baking a high-end celebration cake in a commercial bakery versus your home kitchen. A professional bakery has **air-conditioned rooms**, **humidity-controlled proofing chambers**, **filtered flour storage**, and **stainless-steel dust-free work surfaces**. The same recipe, made in a dusty, hot, humid kitchen, will produce a lumpy, cracked cake. Composite boat building works on the **same principle** — the "recipe" (resin + fibre + hardener) is extremely sensitive to its surroundings. The workshop *is* part of the material.

A polyester or epoxy resin is, in chemical terms, a **living reaction** that begins the moment it is mixed. Temperature dictates its rate, humidity dictates whether side-reactions (such as amine blush in epoxies) occur, and dust dictates whether a foreign particle ends up embedded in the final hull laminate. **Environmental control is therefore not an overhead — it is a manufacturing input.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Range | Governing Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Workshop Temperature | **18 °C – 24 °C** | IMCI / Resin Manufacturer |
| Relative Humidity (Polyester/Vinylester) | **40 % – 65 %** | ISO 291, ASTM D618 |
| Relative Humidity (Epoxy) | **30 % – 50 %** | Resin TDS |
| ISO Cleanroom Class (Hull Layup) | **ISO Class 8 (≤ 3,520,000 particles/m³ ≥ 0.5 µm)** | ISO 14644-1 |
| Min. Air Changes per Hour (Spray-up) | **15 – 20 ACH** | ACGIH Industrial Ventilation |
| Curing Oven Temperature Tolerance | **± 2 °C** of setpoint | Process Specification |
| Lux Level (Inspection Area) | **≥ 1000 lux** | ISO 15406 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These values are **not arbitrary**. They are derived from the polymer chemistry of the matrix systems and the cleanliness requirements of the structural laminate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Why and How

The environmental control envelope is built around **four interdependent sub-systems**. Each sub-system has a physical driver, a control mechanism, and a defect class it prevents.

#### A. Temperature Control

**Physical driver:** Thermoset resin cure kinetics obey the **Arrhenius equation**. A change of 10 °C approximately doubles the reaction rate. Therefore, if a workshop is 30 °C instead of 20 °C, the gel time of polyester resin halves, leading to premature exotherm and possible thermal degradation of the laminate.

**Control mechanism:** HVAC units with chilled-water coils, radiant floor heating (for cold climates), and zoning to separate cold material stores from warm layup halls.

**Defects prevented:** Under-cure, over-cure, exothermic runaway, print-through, warpage, and inaccurate gel-time matching between gelcoat and laminate.

#### B. Humidity Control

**Physical driver:** Water molecules in the air can react with **isocyanate groups** (in epoxy hardeners), causing **amine blush** — a waxy, water-soluble surface layer that destroys interlaminar adhesion. With polyester, excessive humidity can suppress the MEKP-cobalt reaction.

**Control mechanism:** Desiccant dehumidifiers, refrigeration-based dehumidifiers, and steam humidifiers in dry climates.

**Defects prevented:** Amine blush, osmotic blistering in service, poor secondary bonding, voids in vacuum-bagged laminates, and surface tackiness.

#### C. Particulate (Dust) Control

**Physical driver:** A 50 µm dust particle embedded in a gelcoat film becomes a **nucleation site** for crack initiation and is visible as a "pinhole" or "fisheye" on a finished hull surface.

**Control mechanism:** HEPA filtration, positive-pressure cleanrooms, sticky mat entrances, operator gowning protocols, and air showers.

**Defects prevented:** Pinholes, voids, delamination, optical defects in clear-coat finishes, and fatigue initiation sites.

#### D. VOC and Fume Control

**Physical driver:** Styrene (in polyester and vinylester) and epoxy vapours are **hazardous air pollutants (HAPs)**. Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL) are regulated by occupational safety statutes.

**Control mechanism:** Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) at the layup station, downdraft tables, and carbon filtration.

**Defects prevented:** Operator health hazards, regulatory non-compliance, and contamination of adjacent layups.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a commercial boatyard producing a 12-metre FRP patrol craft, environmental control determines:
- Whether the hull passes an **ultrasonic NDT scan** (void content < 1 %)
- Whether the **barcol hardness** meets class rules (typically ≥ 35 for fully cured polyester)
- Whether the **glass transition temperature (T_g)** of the laminate is high enough for tropical service

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Memorise the Arrhenius expression and the gel-time doubling rule. They are the most-tested formula in this module.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $k$ = rate constant, $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | Predicting cure rate change with temperature |
| 2 | $\dfrac{t_{gel,2}}{t_{gel,1}} \approx 2^{\,(T_1 - T_2) / 10}$ | $t_{gel}$ = gel time, $T$ in °C | Quick gel-time adjustment rule (10 °C rule) |
| 3 | $\rho_{abs} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dfrac{p_{vap}}{p_{atm} - p_{vap}}$ | $\rho_{abs}$ = absolute humidity, $p_{vap}$ = partial vapour pressure | Humidity ratio calculations |
| 4 | $Q = n \cdot V$ | $Q$ = ventilation flow (m³/s), $n$ = ACH (h⁻¹), $V$ = room volume (m³) | Sizing HVAC/LEV ductwork |
| 5 | $C_n = 10^{N} \left(\dfrac{0.1}{D}\right)^{2.08}$ | ISO 14644-1 cleanroom equation: $C_n$ = max particles/m³, $N$ = class number, $D$ = particle size (µm) | Cleanroom classification check |
| 6 | $P_{diff} = \Delta h \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot g$ | $\Delta h$ = pressure-stack height difference | Positive-pressure room design |
| 7 | $Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q_{loss}$ = heat loss (W), $U$ = overall U-value (W/m²K), $A$ = area (m²) | Workshop thermal-load estimation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **absolute humidity** (mass of water per volume of air) with **relative humidity** (ratio of actual vapour pressure to saturation vapour pressure at the same temperature). The HVAC controller almost always uses RH, not absolute humidity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sizing Workshop Ventilation (ACH Method)

**Problem Context:** A hand lay-up shop for an 8 m boat has room dimensions of 30 m × 12 m × 6 m. The styrene PEL requires a minimum air-exchange rate of 15 ACH. Calculate the required supply-air flow.

**Step 1 — Calculate the room volume.**

The room is treated as a rectangular box.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= L \times W \times H \\
  &= 30 \, \text{m} \times 12 \, \text{m} \times 6 \, \text{m} \\
  &= 2160 \, \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the air-change rate from per hour to per second.**

The supply-fan specification is in m³/s, so the ACH value must be divided by 3600.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 15 \, \text{h}^{-1} \\
  &= \dfrac{15}{3600} \, \text{s}^{-1} \\
  &= 4.1667 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ventilation equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= n \cdot V \\
  &= \left(4.1667 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times 2160 \\
  &= 9.0 \, \text{m}^{3} / \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a more practical unit (m³/h) for fan selection.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 9.0 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \times 3600 \, \text{s}/\text{h} \\
  &= 32400 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The HVAC system must deliver at least **32 400 m³/h** of filtered supply air, with a matching exhaust capacity, to maintain safe styrene levels.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: ISO 14644-1 Cleanroom Particle Count Verification

**Problem Context:** A gelcoat spray booth is claimed to be ISO Class 7. Verify whether a measured count of 250 000 particles/m³ at 0.5 µm complies.

**Step 1 — Recall the ISO 14644-1 reference equation.**

$$
C_n = 10^{N} \left(\frac{0.1}{D}\right)^{2.08}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values for ISO Class 7 at D = 0.5 µm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{7,\,0.5} &= 10^{7} \left(\frac{0.1}{0.5}\right)^{2.08} \\
            &= 10^{7} \times (0.2)^{2.08}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the bracket term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.2)^{2.08} &= e^{2.08 \cdot \ln(0.2)} \\
              &= e^{2.08 \times (-1.6094)} \\
              &= e^{-3.3476} \\
              &\approx 0.0353
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the limit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{7,\,0.5} &\approx 10^{7} \times 0.0353 \\
            &\approx 353000 \, \text{particles/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to the measured value.**

The ISO Class 7 limit is 353 000 particles/m³. The measured value is 250 000 particles/m³. Since 250 000 < 353 000, the booth **passes** ISO Class 7 for 0.5 µm particles.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Workshop Temperature on Gel Time (10 °C Rule)

**Problem Context:** A polyester resin TDS specifies a gel time of 38 minutes at 25 °C. The workshop will operate at 32 °C in summer. What is the expected gel time?

**Step 1 — Apply the 10 °C rule.**

$$
\frac{t_{gel,2}}{t_{gel,1}} \approx 2^{(T_1 - T_2)/10}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 25\,^\circ\text{C} \\
T_2 &= 32\,^\circ\text{C} \\
t_{gel,1} &= 38 \,\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_1 - T_2}{10} &= \frac{25 - 32}{10} \\
                     &= -0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{-0.7} &= \frac{1}{2^{0.7}} \\
         &= \frac{1}{1.6245} \\
         &\approx 0.6156
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply to obtain the new gel time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{gel,2} &= 38 \times 0.6156 \\
          &\approx 23.4 \,\text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 7 °C rise in workshop temperature **shortens the gel time by ~38 %**. This must be compensated for in the layup schedule, or the resin will gel in the bucket before the laminator finishes the layup.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Environmental Control System for a Composite Boatyard

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[EXTERNAL AMBIENT AIR] --> node2[INTAKE PLENUM]
    node2 --> node3[PRE FILTER G4]
    node3 --> node4[COOLING COIL CHILLER]
    node4 --> node5[DEHUMIDIFIER DESICCANT WHEEL]
    node5 --> node6[HEPA FILTER H13]
    node6 --> node7[SUPPLY FAN]
    node7 --> node8[CEILING DIFFUSER GRID]
    node8 --> node9[LAYUP HALL ZONE A GELCOAT]
    node8 --> node10[LAYUP HALL ZONE B LAMINATE]
    node8 --> node11[CURING OVEN ZONE C]
    node8 --> node12[MATERIAL STORE ZONE D]
    node9 --> node13[RETURN AIR PLENUM]
    node10 --> node13
    node11 --> node13
    node12 --> node13
    node13 --> node14[EXHAUST FAN]
    node14 --> node15[CARBON FILTER VOC ABATEMENT]
    node15 --> node16[STACK DISCHARGE TO ATMOSPHERE]
    node9 --> node17[LOCAL EXHAUST VENT LEV HOOD]
    node10 --> node17
    node17 --> node15
    node18[BMS BUILDING MANAGEMENT SYSTEM] --> node4
    node18 --> node5
    node18 --> node7
    node18 --> node14
    node19[TEMP SENSOR PT100] --> node18
    node20[RH SENSOR CAPACITIVE] --> node18
    node21[PARTICLE COUNTER OPTICAL] --> node18
    node22[VOC SENSOR PID] --> node18
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Material Path Under Environmental Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[INCOMING RESIN DRUMS] --> nodeB[TEMP CONDITIONED STORE 18C]
    nodeA2[INCOMING FIBRE ROLLS] --> nodeB2[DRY STORE RH BELOW 50 PERCENT]
    nodeB --> nodeC[RESIN BATH 25C PRE WARM]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2[PRE IMPREGNATION AREA ISO 8]
    nodeC --> nodeD[WET LAYUP TABLE ISO 8]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[VACUUM BAGGING STATION]
    nodeE --> nodeF[CURING OVEN 60C 2 PERCENT TOLERANCE]
    nodeF --> nodeG[INSPECTION BAY 1000 LUX]
    nodeG --> nodeH[HULL RELEASE]
```

### 4.3 Causal Loop: Workshop Temperature Deviation and Defect Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeX[HVAC FAILURE] --> nodeY[AMBIENT TEMP RISES]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[RESIN GEL TIME SHORTENS]
    nodeZ --> nodeAA[LAYUP WINDOW MISSED]
    nodeAA --> nodeAB[UNFILLED VOIDS IN LAMINATE]
    nodeAB --> nodeAC[NDT REJECTION ULTRASONIC]
    nodeAC --> nodeAD[REWORK COST INCREASES]
    nodeY --> nodeAE[STYRENE EMISSION RATE RISES]
    nodeAE --> nodeAF[VOC ABOVE PEL]
    nodeAF --> nodeAG[OPERATOR HEALTH HAZARD]
    nodeY --> nodeAH[EXOTHERM PEAK HIGHER]
    nodeAH --> nodeAI[THERMAL DEGRADATION LAMINATE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a physical asset (filter, fan, sensor) or a process step. The arrows show air-stream or material flow. The BMS (Building Management System) is the supervisory controller that takes sensor data and modulates actuators. This is the same architecture used in a modern prepreg autoclave facility, scaled to boat-hall dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Relative Humidity" and state the recommended range for an epoxy-laminate workshop.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Relative Humidity (RH) is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour actually present in the air to the saturation vapour pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage. Formula:

$$RH = \frac{p_{vap}}{p_{sat}(T)} \times 100\,\%$$

For an **epoxy-laminate workshop**, RH should be controlled between **30 % and 50 %** to prevent amine blush on cured surfaces and to avoid moisture absorption by hygroscopic fillers and fibres. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by "Air Changes per Hour (ACH)"? Why is this metric important for a polyester spray-up booth?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
ACH is the number of times the total air volume of a room is theoretically replaced by fresh supply air in one hour, given by

$$n = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V} \quad [\text{h}^{-1}]$$

It is important for a polyester spray-up booth because **styrene monomer evaporates during atomisation**. OSHA/ACGIH permits a PEL of 50 ppm (8-h TWA). Maintaining **15–20 ACH** keeps airborne styrene below this limit, ensuring operator safety and regulatory compliance. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 (a). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice A]**
*(a)* Discuss in detail the **temperature and humidity requirements** for a composite boat manufacturing facility. Explain, with reference to the Arrhenius equation, why a 10 °C rise in workshop temperature can halve the resin gel time. (7 marks)

*(b)* A gelcoat-spray booth has dimensions 10 m × 6 m × 4 m. The air-change rate required is 18 ACH. Calculate the supply-fan capacity in m³/s. Also, determine whether the booth complies with ISO Class 8 if a particle count of 2.8 × 10⁶ particles/m³ is measured at 0.5 µm. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- Workshop temperature must be controlled between **18 °C and 24 °C** to ensure predictable gel times and uniform cure. (1 mark)
- Humidity must be controlled between **40 % and 65 %** for polyester/vinylester (or 30 %–50 % for epoxy) to prevent amine blush and styrene suppression. (1 mark)
- HVAC should provide both **filtration and humidity control**; material stores require a separate lower temperature. (1 mark)
- Arrhenius equation: $k = A \, e^{-E_a/RT}$. As $T$ increases, the exponent $-E_a/RT$ becomes less negative, so $k$ increases exponentially. (1 mark)
- For a typical polyester, $E_a \approx 60$ kJ/mol. A 10 K rise changes the rate by a factor of $\exp(60\,000/(8.314 \times 298)) - \exp(.../308) \approx 2$. (2 marks)
- Hence the "**10 °C rule**": every 10 °C doubles the cure rate, halving the gel time. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Volume of booth:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 10 \times 6 \times 4 = 240 \, \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

ACH in per second:

$$
n = 18/3600 = 0.005 \, \text{s}^{-1}
$$

Required supply flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= n \cdot V = 0.005 \times 240 = 1.2 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks for the ventilation calculation)

ISO Class 8 limit at 0.5 µm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{8,\,0.5} &= 10^{8} \times (0.1/0.5)^{2.08} \\
            &= 10^{8} \times 0.2^{2.08} \\
            &\approx 10^{8} \times 0.0353 \\
            &\approx 3.53 \times 10^{6} \, \text{particles/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Measured: $2.8 \times 10^{6}$ < $3.53 \times 10^{6}$ → booth **complies with ISO Class 8**. (4 marks for the ISO check)

---

**Q3 (b). [Alternative 14-Mark Choice B]**
*(a)* Describe the **four main sub-systems** of an environmental control envelope in a composite boatyard. (7 marks)

*(b)* A polyester resin has a gel time of 42 minutes at 20 °C. The workshop will be cooled to 14 °C during the monsoon. Estimate the new gel time using the 10 °C rule. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Temperature control** — HVAC with chilled-water coils; maintains 18–24 °C. Prevents exotherm and warpage. (2 marks)
2. **Humidity control** — desiccant or refrigeration dehumidifier; maintains 30–65 % RH. Prevents amine blush and blistering. (2 marks)
3. **Particulate control** — HEPA filtration, positive pressure, cleanroom zoning (ISO 8 for layup). Prevents pinholes and voids. (1.5 marks)
4. **VOC and fume control** — Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) at spray-up stations; 15–20 ACH general ventilation; carbon filtration of exhaust. Protects operator health. (1.5 marks)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given $t_{gel,1} = 42$ min at $T_1 = 20$ °C, find $t_{gel,2}$ at $T_2 = 14$ °C.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{t_{gel,2}}{t_{gel,1}} &\approx 2^{(T_1 - T_2)/10} \\
                           &= 2^{(20 - 14)/10} \\
                           &= 2^{0.6} \\
                           &\approx 1.5157
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t_{gel,2} = 42 \times 1.5157 \approx 63.7 \, \text{minutes}
$$

(3 marks — substitution, 2 marks — evaluation, 1 mark — unit, 1 mark — engineering interpretation)

**Engineering interpretation:** A 6 °C drop extends the gel time by ~52 %. The laminator must either pre-warm the resin or plan a longer pour window. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up RH and absolute humidity** in Part A — many students write the saturation-pressure formula incorrectly. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting to convert ACH from h⁻¹ to s⁻¹** when the answer is required in m³/s. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Using the wrong ISO class equation** — students often quote the "Class 7 = 353 000" value but apply it to a Class 8 problem. (–2 marks)
> 4. **In the gel-time rule, students forget that the exponent sign is reversed** when temperature *rises* (faster cure) versus *drops* (slower cure). (–1 mark)
> 5. **No unit on the final answer** — automatic ½ mark deduction in long-answer sub-parts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Board Exam:**

- **Environmental control is a manufacturing input**, not a luxury overhead.
- **Workshop temperature band: 18 °C – 24 °C.** Below 18 °C, epoxy cures sluggishly; above 24 °C, polyester exotherms.
- **Epoxy RH band: 30 % – 50 %; Polyester RH band: 40 % – 65 %.** Memorise the **amine blush** connection.
- **10 °C rule:** gel time halves per 10 °C rise (and doubles per 10 °C drop). Derived from Arrhenius.
- **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — know the meaning of every symbol.
- **Ventilation sizing formula:** $Q = nV$ (with $n$ in s⁻¹ if $Q$ is in m³/s).
- **ISO 14644-1 cleanroom equation:** $C_n = 10^{N} (0.1/D)^{2.08}$.
- **PEL for styrene ≈ 50 ppm (8-h TWA)** — drives the ACH requirement.
- **Barcol hardness ≥ 35** is a common proxy for full cure; it is *not* achieved if cure temperature was low.
- **Differential pressure** between layup hall and outside must be **≥ 5 Pa positive** to exclude dust.
- **Lighting ≥ 1000 lux** at the inspection table (ISO 15406).
- **Material stores** are *always* a separate conditioned zone, ideally at **15 °C – 18 °C** for pre-pregs and at **15 °C – 21 °C** for liquid resins (consult TDS).
- **Curing oven tolerance** is typically **± 2 °C** of setpoint — this is the single most tightly controlled environmental variable in composite boatbuilding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature and Humidity Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Temperature and Humidity Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Temperature and Humidity Control** in the context of composite boat design refers to the precise regulation of the thermal and moisture conditions of the air inside a boat-building workshop, mould preparation area, and curing zone, such that the resin system (polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy, phenolic) and the reinforcing fibres (glass, carbon, aramid) achieve reproducible, defect-free mechanical and chemical performance throughout the laminating, infusion, and curing stages.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic covers three governing metrics:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Dry Bulb Temperature | $T_{db}$ | °C |
| Relative Humidity | $RH$ | % |
| Absolute / Specific Humidity | $\omega$ | g water / kg dry air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly links temperature and humidity control to *process repeatability*, *void content reduction*, and *class society acceptance criteria* (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) for marine composites. You are expected to compute psychrometric quantities and predict their influence on resin gel time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery Principle"

Imagine you are baking a soufflé. If the kitchen is too cold, the egg whites will not whip to peak volume; if it is too warm, they collapse. If the air is dry, the surface cracks; if humid, it never sets. A **composite laminate is a polymer soufflé** — the resin is the egg white, the reinforcement is the flour, and the workshop atmosphere is the kitchen.

| Kitchen Variable | Composite Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Room temperature | Resin bath temperature, mould temperature |
| Humidity in air | Moisture absorbed into laminate, amine blush on epoxy |
| Oven temperature | Post-cure oven temperature |
| Recipe timing | Pot life and gel time |

Just as a baker insists on **22 °C and 60 % RH** for predictable baking, a composite boat builder insists on the same envelope for predictable laminates.

## 1.3 Why It Matters — Five Production Consequences

1. **Viscosity drift** — every 10 °C rise roughly halves resin viscosity, changing fibre wet-out behaviour.
2. **Gel time shift** — an unheated workshop in winter can extend gel time by 40–60 %, breaking production schedules.
3. **Amine blush & osmotic voids** — high humidity (>70 % RH) during epoxy cure creates a waxy surface that destroys secondary bonds.
4. **Print-through & waviness** — uncontrolled exotherm in thick laminates warps the hull skin.
5. **Classification society non-compliance** — DNVGL-ST-C501 and Lloyd's SSC require documented environmental records for hull laminates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Envelope (Industry Consensus):** Workshop **18–24 °C**, **40–65 % RH**, with resin and mould brought to **20 ± 2 °C** before lay-up. Surface temperature must be held **≥ 3 °C above the dew point** to prevent condensation on the mould.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Psychrometric Foundation

Air is a mixture of **dry air** (mostly N₂ + O₂) and **water vapour**. The maximum vapour the air can hold depends *only* on temperature — this is the **saturation vapour pressure** $P_s$.

The actual vapour pressure in the air is $P_v$. The ratio gives **relative humidity**:

$$
RH = \frac{P_v}{P_s} \times 100 \,\%
$$

Specific humidity (the mass of water carried by 1 kg of dry air):

$$
\omega = 0.622 \,\frac{P_v}{P - P_v}
$$

where $P$ is total atmospheric pressure (Pa) and $0.622$ is the ratio $M_{water}/M_{air}$.

## 2.2 The Magnus–Tetens Equation (High-Yield)

For engineering hand-calculations over water in the range 0–60 °C:

$$
P_s = 6.112 \,\exp\!\left(\frac{17.67\,T}{T + 243.5}\right) \quad [\text{hPa}]
$$

This single expression unlocks every other psychrometric quantity.

## 2.3 Dew Point — The Critical Threshold for Bonding

The **dew point temperature** $T_d$ is the temperature at which the air becomes saturated if cooled at constant pressure. Below $T_d$, water condenses on every cold surface — moulds, peel-ply, tooling.

Inverse Magnus form (from $T$ and $RH$):

$$
\gamma = \ln\!\left(\frac{RH}{100}\right) + \frac{17.67\,T}{T + 243.5}
$$

$$
T_d = \frac{243.5 \,\gamma}{17.67 - \gamma}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Trap:** A workshop at 30 °C and 70 % RH has a dew point of ≈ **24 °C**. A mould taken out of an air-conditioned lay-up room at 22 °C will *immediately* fog with condensation when moved outside. Moulds must always be conditioned to ≥ $T_d + 3$ °C.

## 2.4 Temperature Effects on Resin — Arrhenius Kinetics

Cure reaction rate $k$ obeys the Arrhenius relation:

$$
k = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)
$$

The **ratio of cure rates at two temperatures** is the workhorse of shop-floor prediction:

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

For **polyester resins**, $E_a$ is typically **60–80 kJ/mol**; for **epoxy amine systems**, **50–70 kJ/mol**.

## 2.5 Resin Viscosity vs Temperature

Resin viscosity also follows an Arrhenius (or WLF) form:

$$
\eta(T) = \eta_\infty \,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_\eta}{R\,T}\right)
$$

A practical rule of thumb taught in boat yards: **viscosity halves for every 10 °C rise**. This explains why epoxy feels "thin" on a hot summer day and "honey-thick" in winter.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relative Humidity | $RH = (P_v / P_s) \times 100$ | dimensionless, % |
| 2 | Saturation Vapour Pressure (Magnus) | $P_s = 6.112\,\exp[17.67T/(T+243.5)]$ | hPa, T in °C, 0–60 °C |
| 3 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622\,P_v/(P-P_v)$ | kg water / kg dry air |
| 4 | Dew Point Constant | $\gamma = \ln(RH/100) + 17.67T/(T+243.5)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Dew Point Temperature | $T_d = 243.5\gamma/(17.67-\gamma)$ | °C |
| 6 | Cure Rate Ratio (Arrhenius) | $k_2/k_1 = \exp[(E_a/R)(1/T_1-1/T_2)]$ | T in Kelvin |
| 7 | Resin Viscosity | $\eta = \eta_\infty\exp(E_\eta/RT)$ | Pa·s |
| 8 | Heat of Exotherm (approx.) | $Q = m_{resin} \cdot H_r$ | kJ; $H_r$ ≈ 350–500 kJ/kg |
| 9 | Laminate Cooling Time | $t_c = \rho c_p L^2 / (4 k)$ | s; L = half-thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula 6 is the master equation behind every "pot-life-vs-temperature" graph published by resin manufacturers. The KTU examiner expects you to know that a 10 °C rise typically *doubles* the cure rate of polyester — this is the *Rule of Two*.

## 2.7 Classification Society Thresholds (Real-World Hook)

| Society | Document | T (°C) | RH (%) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DNV | DNVGL-ST-C501 | 18–25 | ≤ 70 | Documented hourly log |
| Lloyd's Register | SSC Rules Ch.4 | 18–24 | 40–65 | Surface ≥ dew point + 3 °C |
| ABS | NR-546 | 18–27 | ≤ 75 | Mould log mandatory |
| ISO | ISO 9001 §7.1 | "controlled" | "controlled" | Calibration of sensors yearly |

The "**dew point + 3 °C**" rule appears in **every** class rule. Memorise it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Oriented Worked Example.** The following derivation mirrors a typical KTU ESE Module 2 Part B sub-question worth **7 marks**. Every algebraic step is explicit.

---

## 3.1 Problem Statement

A composite boat construction hall maintains air at a **dry bulb temperature of 25 °C** and **relative humidity of 60 %**. The atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**. Calculate:

1. Saturation vapour pressure $P_s$
2. Actual vapour pressure $P_v$
3. Specific humidity $\omega$
4. Dew point temperature $T_d$
5. Comment on whether a mould at 18 °C will suffer condensation.

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Saturation Vapour Pressure $P_s$

Apply the Magnus equation at $T = 25$ °C:

$$
P_s = 6.112 \times \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \times 25}{25 + 243.5}\right)
$$

Compute the argument of the exponential:

$$
\frac{17.67 \times 25}{25 + 243.5} = \frac{441.75}{268.5} = 1.6454
$$

Substitute back:

$$
P_s = 6.112 \times \exp(1.6454)
$$

Since $\exp(1.6454) = 5.183$:

$$
P_s = 6.112 \times 5.183 = 31.68 \text{ hPa}
$$

Convert to kPa for later use:

$$
P_s = 31.68 / 10 = 3.168 \text{ kPa}
$$

> **Interpretation:** At 25 °C, the air can hold a maximum of 3.168 kPa of water vapour before becoming saturated.

---

## 3.3 Step 2 — Actual Vapour Pressure $P_v$

By definition of relative humidity:

$$
P_v = \frac{RH}{100} \times P_s
$$

$$
P_v = 0.60 \times 3.168 = 1.901 \text{ kPa} = 1901 \text{ Pa}
$$

---

## 3.4 Step 3 — Specific Humidity $\omega$

Using $\omega = 0.622 \, P_v / (P - P_v)$ with $P = 101.325$ kPa:

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{1.901}{101.325 - 1.901}
$$

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{1.901}{99.424}
$$

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times 0.019118 = 0.01189 \text{ kg water / kg dry air}
$$

$$
\omega \approx 11.89 \text{ g/kg}
$$

---

## 3.5 Step 4 — Dew Point Temperature $T_d$

First compute the dimensionless constant $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \ln\!\left(\frac{RH}{100}\right) + \frac{17.67 \times T}{T + 243.5}
$$

$$
\gamma = \ln(0.60) + \frac{17.67 \times 25}{25 + 243.5}
$$

Since $\ln(0.60) = -0.5108$ and the second term is $1.6454$ (from Step 1):

$$
\gamma = -0.5108 + 1.6454 = 1.1346
$$

Now apply the inverse Magnus:

$$
T_d = \frac{243.5 \times \gamma}{17.67 - \gamma} = \frac{243.5 \times 1.1346}{17.67 - 1.1346}
$$

$$
T_d = \frac{276.28}{16.535} = 16.71 \text{ °C}
$$

---

## 3.6 Step 5 — Condensation Check

Required mould surface temperature to avoid condensation:

$$
T_{mould, min} = T_d + 3 = 16.71 + 3 = 19.71 \text{ °C}
$$

The proposed mould is at **18 °C**.

$$
T_{mould} = 18 \text{ °C} \;\; < \;\; 19.71 \text{ °C} = T_{mould, min}
$$

**Conclusion:** Condensation **will** occur on the mould surface. The mould temperature must be raised by at least **1.71 °C** before lay-up can begin.

---

## 3.7 Second Worked Problem — Arrhenius Cure Rate

A polyester resin used for hull lay-up has an activation energy $E_a = 75$ kJ/mol. The standard reference gel time is quoted at **20 °C**. Determine the ratio of cure rate at **30 °C** to that at **20 °C**, and hence the new gel time if the reference gel time is **42 minutes**.

### Solution

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:

$$
T_1 = 20 + 273.15 = 293.15 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}
$$

Apply the Arrhenius ratio:

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

Compute $E_a/R$:

$$
\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{75\,000}{8.314} = 9020.93 \text{ K}
$$

Compute the temperature bracket:

$$
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{293.15} - \frac{1}{303.15}
$$

$$
= 0.0034110 - 0.0032987 = 0.0001123 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

Combine:

$$
\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right) = 9020.93 \times 0.0001123 = 1.0131
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp(1.0131) = 2.754
$$

Since gel time is **inversely proportional** to cure rate:

$$
t_{gel,30} = \frac{t_{gel,20}}{k_2/k_1} = \frac{42}{2.754} = 15.25 \text{ minutes}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A modest 10 °C rise in workshop temperature reduces gel time from 42 min to ≈ 15 min — a 64 % reduction. This matches the empirical "Rule of Two" cited earlier, and is why summer lay-ups need different hardener dosages than winter lay-ups.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Process | Critical T (°C) | Critical RH (%) | Failure if Exceeded |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hand Lay-up (Polyester) | 18–24 | 40–65 | Tackiness, print-through |
| Vacuum Infusion (Epoxy) | 21–25 | 35–55 | Voids, dry spots |
| Prepreg Lay-up | 21–24 | 35–55 | Tack loss, FOD |
| Gelcoat Spray | 20–24 | 50–65 | Orange peel, porosity |
| Post-cure (Epoxy) | 40–80 | < 60 | Under-cure, distortion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — T&H Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Workshop Air Volume] --> B1[Temperature Sensor T_db]
    A2[Workshop Air Volume] --> B2[Humidity Sensor RH]
    B1 --> C1[Microprocessor PLC Controller]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Chilled Water Valve Signal]
    C1 --> D2[Reheat Coil Signal]
    C1 --> D3[Compressor ON OFF Signal]
    C1 --> D4[Desiccant Wheel Rotation Signal]
    D1 --> E1[Cooling Coil Heat Exchanger]
    D2 --> E2[Reheat Battery]
    D3 --> E3[Refrigeration Compressor]
    D4 --> E4[Desiccant Dehumidifier]
    E1 --> A1
    E2 --> A1
    E3 --> E1
    E4 --> A1
    F1[Dew Point Calculator Block] --> C1
    F2[Mould Surface Thermocouple] --> F1
```

**How to read this diagram:** The workshop air is sensed continuously. The PLC compares the measured $T_{db}$ and $RH$ against setpoints (e.g., 22 °C / 55 %). It then commands four actuators — chilled water valve, reheat coil, compressor, and desiccant wheel — to bring the air back into envelope. The mould surface thermocouple feeds a dew-point calculator so the system can warn the operator before condensation forms.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — T and H Influence Across the Composite Production Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Raw Material Storage] -->|Resin 20 C 60 percent RH| P2[Mould Preparation]
    P2 -->|Mould Surface T dew point plus 3| P3[Gelcoat Application]
    P3 -->|Spray Booth 22 C 55 percent RH| P4[Layup or Infusion]
    P4 -->|Workshop 22 C 55 percent RH| P5[Curing Stage]
    P5 -->|Ambient or Oven| P6[Post Cure]
    P6 -->|Controlled Cooling| P7[Demould and Trimming]
    P7 --> P8[Quality Inspection and Class Survey]
    P8 -->|Records Logged| P9[Hull Section Release]
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P3 fill:#ffe0b3
    style P4 fill:#ffe0b3
    style P5 fill:#ffe0b3
    style P6 fill:#ffcc99
    style P8 fill:#d5e8d4
```

> **Reading guide:** Blue nodes = storage / admin; orange nodes = climate-critical zones; green node = quality gate. Every orange zone must have a logged temperature and humidity record — this is what the DNV surveyor audits.

---

## 4.3 Psychrometric Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State 1 Outside Air 35 C 80 percent RH] -->|Sensible Cooling| S2[State 2 22 C Saturated]
    S2 -->|Dehumidification| S3[State 3 22 C 95 percent RH]
    S3 -->|Reheat| S4[State 4 23 C 50 percent RH Supply Air]
    S4 -->|Room Load Absorption| S5[State 5 24 C 55 percent RH Return Air]
    S5 -->|Mixed with Outside| S1
    style S1 fill:#ffcccc
    style S4 fill:#ccffcc
    style S5 fill:#cce5ff
```

**Interpretation:** Outside air (hot and humid, red) is first cooled to saturation by the cooling coil (water condenses out). It is then slightly over-cooled and finally reheated to supply conditions (green). In the workshop it absorbs heat and moisture from the process, becoming return air (blue), which mixes with fresh outside air to close the loop.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define relative humidity and absolute humidity. Why is the control of relative humidity (rather than absolute humidity) more critical in a composite boat-building workshop?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Relative humidity (RH)* is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour in air to the saturation vapour pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage — $RH = (P_v / P_s) \times 100$. *Absolute humidity* is the actual mass of water vapour per unit volume (g/m³) or per kg of dry air (g/kg).

RH is more critical because **the failure mechanisms** in composite lay-up — amine blush, gelcoat porosity, mould condensation — all depend on the *ratio* of moisture present to the moisture-holding capacity of air, which is what RH measures. Absolute humidity does not change when the temperature rises, but RH drops sharply, making condensation impossible. Hence, workshop instrumentation always logs RH.

*(Award 1 mark each: definition of RH, definition of absolute humidity, justification.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the "dew point + 3 °C rule" in composite boat construction? State its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The dew point + 3 °C rule states that the **mould surface temperature** (and any substrate about to receive resin) must always be at least **3 °C warmer** than the **dew point temperature** of the surrounding air.

**Significance (1 mark each):**

1. It **prevents surface condensation** on the mould, which would cause pinholes in the gelcoat and voids at the laminate/mould interface.
2. It is the **acceptance criterion** of Lloyd's Register, DNV and ABS for marine composite surveys.
3. It protects **secondary bonding** — contamination by moisture destroys structural adhesion in core-to-skin sandwich joints.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** A composite boat construction hall maintains air at 25 °C dry-bulb temperature and 60 % RH. Atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa. Calculate: (i) saturation vapour pressure, (ii) actual vapour pressure, (iii) specific humidity, (iv) dew point temperature. Will a mould at 18 °C suffer condensation? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A general-purpose polyester resin has activation energy $E_a = 75$ kJ/mol. At 20 °C, its gel time is 42 minutes. (i) Derive the Arrhenius expression for the ratio of cure rates at two temperatures. (ii) Compute the gel time at 30 °C. (iii) Comment on the practical implications for summer versus winter hull lay-up in Kerala. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Given:** $T = 25$ °C, $RH = 60$ %, $P = 101.325$ kPa.

**(i) Saturation vapour pressure (Magnus equation):**

$$
P_s = 6.112 \exp\!\left(\frac{17.67 \times 25}{25 + 243.5}\right)
$$

$$
P_s = 6.112 \times \exp(1.6454) = 6.112 \times 5.183 = 31.68 \text{ hPa} = 3.168 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(ii) Actual vapour pressure:**

$$
P_v = 0.60 \times 3.168 = 1.901 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(iii) Specific humidity:**

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{1.901}{101.325 - 1.901} = 0.622 \times 0.01912 = 0.01189 \text{ kg/kg} = 11.89 \text{ g/kg}
$$

**(iv) Dew point:**

$$
\gamma = \ln(0.60) + 1.6454 = -0.5108 + 1.6454 = 1.1346
$$

$$
T_d = \frac{243.5 \times 1.1346}{17.67 - 1.1346} = \frac{276.28}{16.535} = 16.71 \text{ °C}
$$

**Condensation check:**

$$
T_{mould} = 18 \text{ °C} < T_d + 3 = 19.71 \text{ °C}
$$

**Yes, condensation will form** on the mould because its temperature is 1.71 °C below the safe limit. (7 marks: 1.5 each for (i)–(iv), 1 mark for the comment.)

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Arrhenius derivation:**

For cure rate $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$, the ratio is:

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

(2 marks for derivation.)

**(ii) Numerical computation:**

$T_1 = 293.15$ K, $T_2 = 303.15$ K, $E_a/R = 75000/8.314 = 9020.93$ K.

$$
\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2} = 0.0001123 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp(9020.93 \times 0.0001123) = \exp(1.0131) = 2.754
$$

$$
t_{gel,30} = \frac{42}{2.754} = 15.25 \text{ minutes}
$$

(3 marks for calculation.)

**(iii) Practical implications for Kerala:**

Kerala's coastal climate experiences workshop temperatures of **26–34 °C** in summer and **22–28 °C** in winter. Using the same resin recipe across seasons causes:

- *Summer:* gel time drops to 12–18 min, leaving almost no working time for large hull laminates. **Mitigation:** reduce catalyst dosage, pre-cool resin and mould, or use "summer-grade" hardener.
- *Winter-monsoon:* gel time may stretch to 35–45 min; high RH (>75 %) causes amine blush on epoxy. **Mitigation:** operate HVAC at 22 °C / 55 % RH; acclimatise mould before lay-up.
- Documenting the *T–H log* is mandatory under DNVGL-ST-C501. (2 marks for comment.)

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the psychrometric processes — **sensible cooling, dehumidification, and reheat** — as applied to a composite boat-building HVAC system. Discuss why a simple air-conditioner without reheat cannot maintain stable RH. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the influence of temperature and humidity on **five** distinct composite manufacturing processes used in boat building. State the optimum envelope for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Block diagram:**

```
Outside Air (35 °C, 80 % RH)
        │
        ▼
  ┌──────────────┐
  │ Cooling Coil │  →  Sensible + latent cooling
  └──────────────┘
        │
        ▼
State A (≈ 14 °C, 95 % RH, saturated)  ← water condenses here
        │
        ▼
  ┌──────────────┐
  │ Reheat Coil  │  →  Sensible heating, no moisture change
  └──────────────┘
        │
        ▼
Supply Air (22 °C, 55 % RH)  →  Workshop
```

**Why reheat is essential (5 marks):**

A cooling coil alone over-cools the air to saturation (≈ 14 °C) to remove moisture. Supplying 14 °C air to the workshop would:

1. Cause **thermal shock** to resins and gelcoat, producing print-through and microcracks.
2. Lower the mould surface temperature *below* the dew point, **condensing water on the mould**.
3. Make workers uncomfortable and increase absenteeism.

The reheat coil restores temperature to 22 °C **without adding moisture**, dropping RH from ~95 % to ~55 %. Without reheat, RH cannot be independently controlled from temperature. (Refer to diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.)

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

| # | Process | Optimum T (°C) | Optimum RH (%) | Consequence if Exceeded |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gelcoat Spray (polyester) | 20–24 | 50–65 | Orange peel, porosity, fish-eyes |
| 2 | Hand Lay-up (polyester) | 18–24 | 40–65 | Slow wet-out, tacky cure |
| 3 | Vacuum Infusion (epoxy) | 21–25 | 35–55 | Voids, race-tracking, dry spots |
| 4 | Prepreg Lay-up (epoxy) | 21–24 | 35–55 | Tack loss, FOD attraction |
| 5 | Post-cure (oven) | 40–80 | < 60 | Under-cure, distortion, voids |

(1.4 marks per process: 0.4 for the optimum envelope + 1.0 for the failure mode discussion.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where You Will Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — Many students write $P_s$ in hPa but $P_v$ in Pa, then add them. Always convert to a common unit (kPa is safest).
> 2. **Using °C in the Arrhenius equation** — Temperatures in the Arrhenius bracket **must be Kelvin**. This is the single most common error in KTU answer scripts.
> 3. **Ignoring the dew-point check** — Even if all five quantities are computed correctly, a student who does *not* verify mould temperature against $T_d + 3$ loses the concluding mark.
> 4. **Misapplying "Rule of Two"** — The rule is empirical for polyester around 20–30 °C; do **not** extrapolate to cryogenic or very high temperatures.
> 5. **Omitting the class-society hook** — Forgetting to mention DNV/Lloyd's/ABS loses the *application* marks; KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly rewards industry linkage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition to memorise:** Relative humidity = $(P_v/P_s) \times 100$; Specific humidity = $0.622 P_v / (P - P_v)$.
- **Master equation:** Magnus–Tetens $P_s = 6.112 \exp[17.67 T / (T + 243.5)]$ for water, 0–60 °C, output in hPa.
- **Golden Rule of the workshop:** Mould surface temperature $\geq$ **dew point + 3 °C** — quoted by **every** classification society.
- **Arrhenius core:** $k_2/k_1 = \exp[(E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)]$ — Kelvin only; for polyester, $E_a \approx 75$ kJ/mol.
- **"Rule of Two":** Every 10 °C rise in workshop temperature roughly **doubles** the cure rate and halves the gel time of polyester resin.
- **Working envelope:** Workshop 18–24 °C, RH 40–65 %, surface T ≥ $T_d + 3$ °C.
- **Viscosity rule:** Resin viscosity roughly halves per 10 °C rise (Arrhenius/WLF behaviour).
- **Kerala-specific risk:** Monsoon humidity often exceeds 80 %, demanding continuous HVAC operation; summer temperatures (32 °C) cut gel times by 60–70 %.
- **Quality documentation:** T–H log is a class-society deliverable under DNVGL-ST-C501, Lloyd's SSC Ch.4, and ABS NR-546.
- **Process-by-process windows:** gelcoat (20–24 °C / 50–65 % RH), infusion (21–25 °C / 35–55 % RH), prepreg (21–24 °C / 35–55 % RH).
- **Reverse-Magnus formula** for dew point: $T_d = 243.5\gamma/(17.67-\gamma)$ where $\gamma = \ln(RH/100) + 17.67 T/(T+243.5)$.
- **Psychrometric block sequence:** Outside air → sensible cooling → dehumidification (condensation on coil) → reheat → supply air. Reheat is **non-negotiable** for stable RH.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

# FRP Boat Construction Quality Control (QC)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Fiber Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Boat Construction Quality Control (QC)* is the systematic, planned, and documented set of engineering activities — spanning **raw material verification**, **in-process manufacturing surveillance**, **non-destructive evaluation (NDE)**, **destructive coupon testing**, and **final acceptance trials** — designed to ensure that a marine composite hull conforms to its designed mechanical, physical, chemical, and geometric specifications while complying with classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS) and ISO/ASTM maritime standards.

### Key Subsystems of FRP Boat QC

1. **Material QC (MQC)** — incoming resin, fiber, core, and gel-coat certification.
2. **Process QC (PQC)** — environmental control, layup sequencing, consolidation, cure monitoring.
3. **In-Process QC (IPQC)** — interlaminar inspection between layup steps.
4. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — ultrasonic C-scan, acoustic emission, thermography.
5. **Destructive Testing (DT)** — tensile, flexural, ILSS, and Barcol hardness of witness coupons.
6. **Hull Geometry QC** — mold fidelity, hull tolerance (typically $\pm$ 3 mm/m), and waterline verification.
7. **Documentation & Traceability** — batch numbers, cure logs, weld/laminate records.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, Module 2 of PENST742 explicitly treats QC as a *process-engineering* subject — not a paperwork formality. Students must be able to draw a process flow, identify defect modes, and compute key quality metrics (void content, fiber volume fraction, Barcol hardness).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Plain-English Analogy

Think of building an FRP boat hull like **baking a multi-layered wedding cake where each layer is the strength of the entire dessert**.

- The **fiberglass mat** is like the sponge layers.
- The **resin** is like the buttercream that glues them together.
- The **mold** is the cake tin.
- The **catalyst/hardener ratio** is the oven temperature knob.
- **QC inspectors** are the bakers who check:
  - Are the ingredients fresh? (**Material QC**)
  - Is the oven at the right temperature? (**Environmental QC**)
  - Did any air bubbles form inside the cake? (**NDT — finding voids**)
  - Is the cake hard enough when tapped? (**Barcol Hardness Test**)
  - Will it hold a wedding-cake tier's weight without collapsing? (**Mechanical Coupon Testing**)

If any step is skipped — wrong resin mix, dust on the mat, under-cure, or an unbonded region — the boat may look perfect on the outside (just like a beautiful cake) but will crack, delaminate, or absorb water (like a soggy cake) once it enters service. **Quality Control is the discipline that prevents the "soggy-cake" failure.**

---

## 1.3 Why Quality Control is Non-Negotiable in Marine FRP

| Reason | Real-World Consequence if QC is Skipped |
|---|---|
| Marine environment (saltwater, UV, cyclic load) | Micro-cracks propagate into macro-delamination in 2–5 years |
| Catastrophic failure mode (no warning) | Hull suddenly opens at sea — loss of life |
| Class society rules (DNV, LR) | Vessel cannot be insured or legally registered |
| Resin-dominated properties | A 5% deviation in cure reduces ILSS by ~25% |
| Repair cost | Repair is 5–10× more expensive than prevention |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *"FRP Boat QC = MATERIAL + PROCESS + TEST + DOCUMENT"* — these four pillars form every answer in KTU.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of FRP Marine QC

The QC philosophy rests on four engineering pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Material Verification (Input Control)
Every incoming batch of resin, gel-coat, reinforcement, and core must carry a **Certificate of Conformance (CoC)** and pass an **Acceptance Quality Limit (AQL)** sampling test. Key checks:

- **Resin:** Viscosity (typically **300–500 cP** at 25 °C), gel time, acid value, and HDT (Heat Deflection Temperature).
- **Gel-coat:** Color match (ΔE ≤ 1.5 per CIE Lab), thixotropy index, Barcol 934-1 hardness ≥ 35.
- **Fiberglass:** Areal weight (gsm) tolerance ± 5%, sizing type (silane), moisture content < 0.5%.
- **Core (PVC, balsa, honeycomb):** Density, cell size, water absorption < 1%.

### Pillar 2 — Process Control (Manufacturing Surveillance)
- **Workshop Conditions:** Ambient temperature **18–28 °C**, relative humidity **< 70%**, ventilation ≥ 6 air-changes/hr (for styrene control below **TLV = 50 ppm**).
- **Catalyst Ratio:** MEKP (Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide) at **1.0–2.5% by weight of resin**.
- **Pot Life:** 15–45 minutes depending on ambient.
- **Layup Sequencing:** Lamination schedule must match the engineering drawing — wrong ply orientation changes the laminate stiffness tensor $[Q]$.
- **Consolidation:** Use of rollers and de-bulking to remove trapped air (**target void content < 2%**).

### Pillar 3 — Cure State Verification (Output Control)
Post-cure quality is judged by:
- **Barcol Hardness** (ASTM D2583) — minimum 35 for fully cured polyester.
- **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)** — residual exotherm < 5 J/g indicates full cure.
- **FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy)** — residual styrene < 2%.
- **HDT (Heat Deflection Temperature)** — meets resin class spec.

### Pillar 4 — Performance Verification (Proof Test)
Two flavors:

| Type | Method | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| Non-Destructive (NDT) | Ultrasonic C-scan, Acoustic Emission, Thermography | 100% of hull |
| Destructive (DT) | Tensile (ASTM D3039), Flexural (ASTM D790), ILSS (ASTM D2344), Barcol | Witness coupons made in parallel |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 7 formulas — they appear in nearly every numerical in KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theoretical Density** | $\rho_{th} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}}$ | g/cm³ | ASTM D2734 |
| 2 | **Actual Density** | $\rho_a = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | g/cm³ | ASTM D792 |
| 3 | **Void Content** | $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_a}{\rho_{th}} \times 100$ | % | ASTM D2734 |
| 4 | **Fiber Volume Fraction** | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | dimensionless | ASTM D3171 |
| 5 | **Resin Volume Fraction** | $V_m = 1 - V_f - V_v$ | dimensionless | ASTM D3171 |
| 6 | **Fiber Weight Fraction** | $W_f = \dfrac{m_f}{m_f + m_m} \times 100$ | % | ASTM D3171 |
| 7 | **Barcol Hardness Index** | $B = f(\text{indenter penetration depth})$ | dimensionless | ASTM D2583 |

> **Where:** $W_f$ = fiber weight, $W_m$ = matrix weight, $\rho_f$ = fiber density (**2.55 g/cm³** for E-glass), $\rho_m$ = matrix density (**1.15 g/cm³** for polyester resin).

### Boundary Conditions / Acceptance Criteria

| Test | Minimum Acceptance | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Void Content $V_v$ | ≤ 2% (marine) | DNV, Lloyd's |
| Fiber Volume Fraction $V_f$ | 35–45% for hand layup; 50–55% for infusion | Industry standard |
| Barcol Hardness | ≥ 35 (polyester), ≥ 40 (vinylester) | ASTM D2583 |
| ILSS | ≥ 25 MPa | ASTM D2344 |
| Tensile Strength | ≥ 200 MPa (CSM/polyester) | ASTM D3039 |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull weight prediction:** Composite density is critical for displacement, stability, and powering calculations.
- **Fatigue life prediction:** Void content is the single biggest driver of fatigue crack initiation — every 1% void reduces fatigue life by ~10%.
- **Class society certification:** Without a documented QC plan and the formula-derived certificate, no insurance underwriter covers a marine composite hull.
- **Repair economics:** A defect caught at QC costs **₹100–500** to fix; the same defect found at sea costs **₹5–50 lakh** to repair.
