### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salad Bowl" Intuition

Imagine a large transparent bowl filled with chopped vegetables (carrots, cucumbers, tomatoes) mixed in a creamy dressing. If you were asked "what fraction of the bowl is solid food versus dressing?", you could answer in two ways:

- **By Volume**: How much space does the solid food physically occupy in the bowl? (Volume Fraction)
- **By Weight**: How much does the solid food weigh compared to the bowl's total weight? (Weight Fraction)

If the vegetables are dense and the dressing is light, the **weight fraction of vegetables will be higher** than their volume fraction. Conversely, if the dressing is denser, the relationship inverts. In composites, **fibers are usually denser than the resin**, so $W_f$ is typically larger than $V_f$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants for Marine Composites (Typical Values):**
> - Density of **E-glass fiber** ($\rho_f$): **2.55 g/cm³**
> - Density of **Carbon fiber** ($\rho_f$): **1.80 g/cm³**
> - Density of **Polyester resin** ($\rho_m$): **1.20 g/cm³**
> - Density of **Epoxy resin** ($\rho_m$): **1.25 g/cm³**

### 1.3 Why This Matters for Boat Building

In marine engineering, the hull, deck, and bulkheads of a fiber-reinforced plastic (FRP) boat are typically designed with a **fiber volume fraction ($V_f$) between 0.30 and 0.55**. This range is the "sweet spot" that balances mechanical strength, stiffness, weight savings, and manufacturability. A $V_f$ too low results in a resin-rich, weak structure; a $V_f$ too high creates dry spots, voids, and poor fiber wet-out by the resin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Volume Fraction

Consider a composite sample consisting of fiber phase and matrix phase only:

1. Let $V_f$ = Volume of fiber
2. Let $V_m$ = Volume of matrix
3. Let $V_c$ = Total volume of composite = $V_f + V_m$

The **volume fraction of fiber** is:

$$V_f = \frac{V_{\text{fiber}}}{V_{\text{composite}}}$$

The **volume fraction of matrix** is:

$$V_m = \frac{V_{\text{matrix}}}{V_{\text{composite}}}$$

Since the composite consists of only these two phases (neglecting voids for the ideal case):

$$V_f + V_m = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real marine laminates, there is always a small **void content** ($V_v$) due to trapped air bubbles during hand lay-up or infusion. The modified rule becomes $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$, where $V_v$ is typically kept below **3%** for marine-grade laminates as per ISO 12215.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Weight Fraction

Using the same logic but applied to mass:

1. Let $W_f$ = Weight of fiber
2. Let $W_m$ = Weight of matrix
3. Let $W_c$ = Total weight of composite = $W_f + W_m$

The **weight fraction of fiber** is:

$$W_f = \frac{W_{\text{fiber}}}{W_{\text{composite}}}$$

The **weight fraction of matrix** is:

$$W_m = \frac{W_{\text{matrix}}}{W_{\text{composite}}}$$

And the closure condition:

$$W_f + W_m = 1$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Volume Fraction of Fiber | $V_f = V_{\text{fiber}} \div V_{\text{composite}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le V_f \le 1$ |
| 2 | Volume Fraction of Matrix | $V_m = V_{\text{matrix}} \div V_{\text{composite}}$ | dimensionless | $V_f + V_m = 1$ |
| 3 | Weight Fraction of Fiber | $W_f = W_{\text{fiber}} \div W_{\text{composite}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le W_f \le 1$ |
| 4 | Weight Fraction of Matrix | $W_m = W_{\text{matrix}} \div W_{\text{composite}}$ | dimensionless | $W_f + W_m = 1$ |
| 5 | Density of Composite | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | g/cm³ | Rule of Mixtures |
| 6 | Weight Fraction from Volume Fraction | $W_f = (\rho_f V_f) \div (\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m)$ | dimensionless | Core conversion |
| 7 | Volume Fraction from Weight Fraction | $V_f = (W_f/\rho_f) \div (W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m)$ | dimensionless | Core conversion |
| 8 | Weight of Component | $W_i = \rho_i V_i$ | g or kg | For any phase $i$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In boat design, these fractions are not just academic — they directly control:

- **Mechanical strength** (tensile, flexural) via the **Rule of Mixtures**: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$
- **Stiffness (modulus)**: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$
- **Weight (displacement)** of the vessel — critical for stability and fuel efficiency
- **Resin consumption and cost** — boat builders quote jobs based on $W_m$
- **Quality control** — burn-out tests (ASTM D3171) measure $V_f$ experimentally by burning away the resin

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Density of a Composite (Rule of Mixtures)

**Given**: A composite made of fiber and matrix.

**Step 1** — Express the total weight as the sum of individual weights:

$$W_c = W_f + W_m$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $W_i = \rho_i V_i$ for each phase:

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

**Step 3** — Divide both sides by $V_c$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

**Step 4** — Recognize that $V_f/V_c = V_f$ (volume fraction) and $V_m/V_c = V_m$:

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Converting Volume Fraction to Weight Fraction

**Step 1** — Write the definition of weight fraction:

$$W_f = \frac{W_f}{W_c}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $W_i = \rho_i V_i$ in the numerator and denominator:

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

**Step 3** — Divide numerator and denominator by $V_c$ (total composite volume):

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f (V_f/V_c)}{\rho_f (V_f/V_c) + \rho_m (V_m/V_c)}$$

**Step 4** — Recognize that the ratios are themselves the volume fractions:

$$\boxed{W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Converting Weight Fraction to Volume Fraction

**Step 1** — Start from the weight fraction definition:

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

**Step 2** — Solve for the volume ratio. Let $R = V_f/V_m$, then:

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f R}{\rho_f R + \rho_m}$$

**Step 3** — Cross-multiply:

$$W_f (\rho_f R + \rho_m) = \rho_f R$$

$$W_f \rho_f R + W_f \rho_m = \rho_f R$$

$$W_f \rho_m = \rho_f R - W_f \rho_f R = \rho_f R (1 - W_f)$$

**Step 4** — Solve for $R$ and then for $V_f$:

$$R = \frac{W_f \rho_m}{\rho_f (1 - W_f)}$$

$$V_f = \frac{R}{1+R} = \frac{W_f/\rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$$

$$\boxed{V_f = \frac{W_f/\rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example (Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A boat hull laminate is made of E-glass fiber ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) and polyester resin ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). If the fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.45$, calculate: (a) $V_m$, (b) $W_f$, (c) $W_m$, (d) $\rho_c$.

**Part (a): Matrix volume fraction**

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55$$

**Part (b): Weight fraction of fiber**

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

Substituting:

$$W_f = \frac{2.55 \times 0.45}{2.55 \times 0.45 + 1.20 \times 0.55}$$

Numerator:

$$2.55 \times 0.45 = 1.1475$$

Denominator:

$$1.1475 + (1.20 \times 0.55) = 1.1475 + 0.66 = 1.8075$$

Therefore:

$$W_f = \frac{1.1475}{1.8075} = 0.6348 \approx 0.635$$

**Part (c): Weight fraction of matrix**

$$W_m = 1 - W_f = 1 - 0.6348 = 0.3652 \approx 0.365$$

**Part (d): Density of composite**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

$$\rho_c = (2.55 \times 0.45) + (1.20 \times 0.55)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.1475 + 0.66 = 1.8075 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Insight:** Notice that the numerical value of $\rho_c$ (1.8075 g/cm³) is **identical** to the denominator in Step (b). This is because $\rho_c V_c = W_c$, and dividing by $V_c = 1$ cm³ gives $\rho_c = W_c$. The two formulas are mathematically consistent — a useful self-check for exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Architecture of Volume & Weight Fraction Relationships

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Composite Material System] --> B[Fiber Phase]
    A --> C[Matrix Phase]
    B --> D[Volume Vf]
    C --> E[Volume Vm]
    B --> F[Mass Wf]
    C --> G[Mass Wm]
    D --> H[Conversion via Density]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Composite Density Rhoc]
    H --> J[Mechanical Properties]
    I --> K[Boat Displacement Calculation]
    J --> L[Rule of Mixtures]
    L --> M[Strength and Stiffness]
    M --> N[Hull Design Optimization]
    K --> N
    B --> O[Fiber Types]
    O --> P[E Glass 2.55]
    O --> Q[Carbon 1.80]
    O --> R[Aramid 1.45]
    C --> S[Resin Types]
    S --> T[Polyester 1.20]
    S --> U[Epoxy 1.25]
    S --> V[Vinylester 1.15]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following block diagram illustrates how a composite boat builder moves from raw material quantities to final laminate properties:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  INPUT: Fiber Weight + Resin Weight                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 1: Compute Weight Fractions                            │
│  Wf = Wfiber / Wtotal, Wm = Wresin / Wtotal                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 2: Convert Wf to Vf using Density Data                 │
│  Vf = (Wf / rhof) / (Wf / rhof + Wm / rhom)                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 3: Compute Composite Density                           │
│  rhoc = rhof Vf + rhom Vm                                    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 4: Apply Rule of Mixtures for Properties               │
│  Ec = Ef Vf + Em Vm,  sigmac = sigmaf Vf + sigmam Vm         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  OUTPUT: Hull Laminate Design Parameters                     │
│  (Strength, Stiffness, Weight, Displacement, Cost)           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 4.3 Visual Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Fraction Type

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb for Marine Applications:**
> - Use **Volume Fraction ($V_f$)** when discussing **mechanical properties** (strength, stiffness).
> - Use **Weight Fraction ($W_f$)** when discussing **raw material costing, inventory, or shipping**.
> - Convert between them **using density values** — never assume $V_f = W_f$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
Define the terms **(a) Volume Fraction** and **(b) Weight Fraction** as applied to fiber-reinforced composites used in boat construction.

**Model Answer:**
**(a) Volume Fraction ($V_f$)** is the ratio of the volume of fiber to the total volume of the composite, expressed as $V_f = V_{\text{fiber}} / V_{\text{composite}}$. It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates how much of the laminate's space is occupied by reinforcing fibers.

**(b) Weight Fraction ($W_f$)** is the ratio of the weight of fiber to the total weight of the composite, expressed as $W_f = W_{\text{fiber}} / W_{\text{composite}}$. Since fibers and resins have different densities, $W_f$ and $V_f$ are generally not numerically equal.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
A GRP boat hull laminate has a fiber volume fraction of 0.40. The density of E-glass is 2.55 g/cm³ and polyester resin is 1.20 g/cm³. Calculate the **weight fraction of fiber**.

**Model Solution:**

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

Given $V_f = 0.40$, so $V_m = 0.60$.

Numerator:

$$2.55 \times 0.40 = 1.020$$

Denominator:

$$1.020 + (1.20 \times 0.60) = 1.020 + 0.720 = 1.740$$

$$W_f = \frac{1.020}{1.740} = 0.586 \approx 0.59$$

**Answer:** $W_f = 0.59$ (i.e., fiber constitutes 59% by weight).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between volume fraction and weight fraction of fiber in a two-phase composite material. State clearly all assumptions made during the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A unidirectional carbon-fiber reinforced epoxy composite contains **60% by weight of carbon fiber** and **40% by weight of epoxy resin**. Given $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³ and $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, calculate: (i) Volume fractions of fiber and matrix, (ii) Density of the composite, (iii) Weight of fiber and matrix present in a sample of volume 250 cm³.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The composite consists of only two phases: fiber and matrix.
2. There are no voids or air pockets.
3. The composite is homogeneous at the macroscopic level.

**Derivation:**

Starting with the definitions:

$$W_f = \frac{W_f}{W_c} = \frac{W_f}{W_f + W_m}$$

$$V_f = \frac{V_f}{V_c} = \frac{V_f}{V_f + V_m}$$

Using $W = \rho V$ for each phase:

$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_c$:

$$\boxed{W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}}$$

Solving inversely for $V_f$:

$$\boxed{V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $W_f = 0.60$, $W_m = 0.40$, $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $V_c = 250$ cm³.

**(i) Volume Fractions:**

$$V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$$

$$W_f / \rho_f = \frac{0.60}{1.80} = 0.3333$$

$$W_m / \rho_m = \frac{0.40}{1.20} = 0.3333$$

$$V_f = \frac{0.3333}{0.3333 + 0.3333} = \frac{0.3333}{0.6667} = 0.50$$

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.50$$

**(ii) Density of Composite:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (1.80 \times 0.50) + (1.20 \times 0.50)$$

$$\rho_c = 0.90 + 0.60 = 1.50 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**(iii) Weight of Each Phase in 250 cm³:**

Total weight:

$$W_c = \rho_c \times V_c = 1.50 \times 250 = 375 \text{ g}$$

$$W_f = 0.60 \times 375 = 225 \text{ g}$$

$$W_m = 0.40 \times 375 = 150 \text{ g}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_f = V_m = 0.50$, $\rho_c = 1.50$ g/cm³, $W_f = 225$ g, $W_m = 150$ g.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with suitable diagrams the significance of **volume fraction** and **weight fraction** in composite boat hull design. Discuss how each fraction affects the mechanical and physical properties of the laminate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fiberglass boat deck laminate has a **fiber weight fraction of 0.70**. The densities are: $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³ (E-glass), $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³ (epoxy). For a 100 cm³ sample, calculate: (i) Volume fractions of fiber and matrix, (ii) Density of composite, (iii) Individual masses of fiber and matrix.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Significance Explanation [7 Marks]:**

In composite boat design, **volume fraction ($V_f$)** governs the mechanical behavior of the laminate. Higher $V_f$ implies more load-bearing fibers per unit volume, leading to:
- Higher tensile and flexural strength
- Higher stiffness (Young's modulus)
- Better fatigue resistance
- Lower coefficient of thermal expansion

**Weight fraction ($W_f$)** governs the cost, raw-material procurement, and weight distribution. It is the parameter that boat-building yards actually measure on the shop floor because laminators weigh their fabric rolls and resin drums directly.

**Comparison Table (to be drawn in exam):**

| Aspect | Volume Fraction $V_f$ | Weight Fraction $W_f$ |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Volume of fiber / Total volume | Weight of fiber / Total weight |
| Used For | Mechanical property prediction | Material costing, inventory |
| Measured By | Burn-out test (ASTM D3171) | Direct weighing on shop floor |
| Typical Range (Marine) | 0.30 – 0.55 | 0.50 – 0.75 |

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $W_f = 0.70$, $W_m = 0.30$, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³, $V_c = 100$ cm³.

**(i) Volume Fractions:**

$$W_f / \rho_f = \frac{0.70}{2.55} = 0.2745$$

$$W_m / \rho_m = \frac{0.30}{1.25} = 0.2400$$

$$V_f = \frac{0.2745}{0.2745 + 0.2400} = \frac{0.2745}{0.5145} = 0.5335 \approx 0.534$$

$$V_m = 1 - 0.534 = 0.466$$

**(ii) Composite Density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

$$\rho_c = (2.55 \times 0.534) + (1.25 \times 0.466)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.3617 + 0.5825 = 1.9442 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**(iii) Masses in 100 cm³ Sample:**

$$W_c = \rho_c \times V_c = 1.9442 \times 100 = 194.42 \text{ g}$$

$$W_f = 0.70 \times 194.42 = 136.09 \text{ g}$$

$$W_m = 0.30 \times 194.42 = 58.33 \text{ g}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_f = 0.534$, $V_m = 0.466$, $\rho_c = 1.944$ g/cm³, $W_f = 136.09$ g, $W_m = 58.33$ g.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the closure condition**: Many students write $V_f + V_m \neq 1$ by including voids without realizing it. Always state $V_f + V_m = 1$ for an ideal two-phase composite.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_f$ and $W_f$ in formulas**: A very common error is using $V_f$ directly in the weight fraction formula without the density conversion. Always show the step $W = \rho V$.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency**: Density must be in **consistent units** (g/cm³ with cm³, or kg/m³ with m³). Mixing SI and CGS units costs marks.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps**: For 7-mark derivation questions, you MUST show every algebraic step. Writing only the final formula earns only 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Rounding errors**: Carry at least 4 decimal places in intermediate steps to avoid losing accuracy marks in the final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Volume Fraction ($V_f$)** = Volume of fiber ÷ Total composite volume (dimensionless).
- **Weight Fraction ($W_f$)** = Weight of fiber ÷ Total composite weight (dimensionless).
- **Closure conditions**: $V_f + V_m = 1$ and $W_f + W_m = 1$ for an ideal void-free composite.
- **Conversion formula (Key Equation)**: $W_f = (\rho_f V_f) / (\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m)$.
- **Inverse conversion**: $V_f = (W_f/\rho_f) / (W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m)$.
- **Composite Density (Rule of Mixtures)**: $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- **Marine-typical values**: $V_f = 0.30$ to $0.55$; $W_f = 0.50$ to $0.75$ for boat hull laminates.
- **Higher $V_f$** → better mechanical properties but harder to wet out and more void risk.
- **Higher $W_f$** than $V_f$ when $\rho_f > \rho_m$ (which is the usual case for E-glass and carbon).
- **ASTM D3171 burn-out test** is the standard experimental method to measure $V_f$.
- **ISO 12215** requires marine laminates to have void content below **3%** for primary structural members.
- **Always carry consistent units** (g/cm³ with cm³) and round only the **final answer**.
- **Self-check trick**: The denominator of the $W_f$ formula equals $\rho_c$ — a powerful verification tool during exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Longitudinal Strength and Stiffness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Longitudinal Strength and Stiffness # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Longitudinal Strength** of a vessel is its ability to resist vertical bending moments (sagging and hogging) acting on the **hull girder** along its length, without suffering structural failure or unacceptable deformation. In naval architecture, the entire hull of a boat/ship is treated as a **hollow box girder** — the **hull girder** — whose cross-section is formed by the shell plating, decks, bottom plating, and longitudinal stiffeners. When the vessel floats in waves, the buoyant force and the weight distribution along the length are *not identical*, generating internal bending moments. - **Hogging**: Bow and stern are supported by waves → middle sags downward → deck in tension, bottom in compression. - **Sagging**: Middle supported by a wave → ends droop → bottom in tension, deck in compression. **Longitudinal Stiffness** is the corresponding ability to resist **deflection (deformation)** under these bending moments, governed primarily by the product $E \cdot I$ of the hull girder (where $E$ is the effective Young's modulus and $I$ is the second moment of area about the neutral axis). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (KTU Examiner Focus):** > - *Strength* checks the **stress** $\sigma = M / Z$ (does it break?) > - *Stiffness* checks the **deflection** $\delta \propto M L^2 / (E I)$ (does it bend too much?) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long, hollow, rectangular cardboard box (like a packing carton for a bicycle). Hold it horizontally by its two ends — it sags slightly in the middle. Now push down on the middle while supporting the ends — it bends into a "U" shape. The cardboard box is the **hull girder**, the supports and the central load represent **buoyancy and weight**, and the sag you observe is **longitudinal deformation**. In a composite boat, the box is made of **fibres and resin** instead of cardboard. The fibres aligned along the boat's length carry most of the bending load (just as the fibres at the top and bottom of a wooden plank resist bending). This is why **fibre orientation and stacking sequence** are crucial in composite longitudinal design. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (FRP Boats) | |---|---|---| | Young's Modulus (E-glass / polyester) | $E$ | **20 – 40 GPa** | | Hull Length (between perpendiculars) | $L$ | **5 – 50 m** | | Hull Breadth (moulded) | $B$ | **1.5 – 10 m** | | Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | **0.35 – 0.65** | | Design Wave Bending Coefficient | $k$ | **0.040 – 0.110** (LR / DNV rules) | | Allowable Bending Stress (FRP) | $\sigma_{all}$ | **60 – 120 MPa** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hull Girder Concept — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Step 1 — Idealise the hull as a beam:** The complete hull (shell, decks, bulkheads, longitudinals) is idealised as a single long beam supported by an *elastic* foundation (water buoyancy) and loaded by *distributed gravity* (weight). 2. **Step 2 — Define load cases:** - **Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM):** from static weight and buoyancy in calm water. - **Wave Bending Moment (WBM):** from passing through waves; LR / DNV give empirical rules: $$M_w \;=\; k \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$$ where $k$ is a coefficient depending on loading condition and ship type. 3. **Step 3 — Apply superposition:** $$M_{total} \;=\; M_{sw} \; \pm \; M_w$$ *Add* for hogging, *subtract* for sagging depending on the load case. 4. **Step 4 — Compute section modulus:** $$Z \;=\; \dfrac{I}{c}$$ where $I$ = second moment of area of the entire hull cross-section about the neutral axis, and $c$ = distance from neutral axis to the **extreme fibre** (top of deck or bottom of shell). 5. **Step 5 — Compute extreme fibre stress:** $$\sigma_{ext} \;=\; \dfrac{M_{total}}{Z}$$ 6. **Step 6 — Compare with allowable stress:** $$\sigma_{ext} \;\le\; \sigma_{all} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Longitudinal Strength is Satisfied.}$$ 7. **Step 7 — Check stiffness (deflection):** For a simply supported hull girder of span $L$ loaded by a uniform load $w$: $$\delta_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, E \, I}$$ Rule of thumb: $\delta_{max} \le L / 500$ for ocean-going vessels. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - The hull girder idealisation works because the hull is *much longer than it is wide or deep*, so beam theory applies. - The empirical wave moment formula captures decades of statistical wave-load data — it is faster and more reliable than a full hydrodynamic calculation at preliminary design stage. - $Z = I/c$ arises because stress in a bending beam is **linear** with distance from the neutral axis (Bernoulli-Euler hypothesis). - Deflection check matters for **composites** more than for steel because composite modulus is *much lower* (GPa vs 200+ GPa for steel), so serviceability often governs the design before strength does. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Composite Hull Girder) | # | Formula | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I = \int y^2 \, dA$ | Second moment of area (geometric) | m⁴ | | 2 | $I_{eff} = \sum \dfrac{E_i}{E_{ref}} \cdot I_i$ | **Transformed** second moment of area for composite laminate | m⁴ | | 3 | $Z = I / c$ | Section modulus | m³ | | 4 | $\sigma = M / Z$ | Extreme-fibre bending stress | Pa | | 5 | $M_w = k \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ | LR / DNV empirical wave moment | N·m | | 6 | $M_{total} = M_{sw} \pm M_w$ | Combined bending moment | N·m | | 7 | $\tau = V \cdot Q / (I \cdot b)$ | Shear stress (transverse) | Pa | | 8 | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | Mid-span deflection (simply supported) | m | | 9 | $EI_{eff} = \sum E_i I_i$ | Hull-girder bending stiffness (true, not transformed) | N·m² | | 10 | $\sigma_{all,FRP} = 0.5 \, \sigma_{uts}$ | Typical allowable (safety factor 2) | Pa | > [!NOTE] > **Composite Trick:** When calculating *stress* you must use the **transformed** section modulus (divide $I$ by a reference modulus $E_{ref}$ and scale each material's area by $E_i/E_{ref}$). When calculating *stiffness / deflection*, you must use the **untransformed** $E I$ product — *do not* use the transformed section for deflection! ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Yacht designers** (Beneteau, Sunseeker) use these formulas to size the laminate schedule of the bottom shell, keel, and deck — the three primary contributors to $I$. - **Naval architects** of FRP minesweepers (e.g., GRP-hulled Hunt-class) and mine-hunters use stiffness-controlled design because their vessels must operate in heavy seas without excessive hog/sag. - **Classification Societies** (Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas) publish rule-based minimum section moduli that any commercial FRP vessel must meet. - **Race-boat designers** (America's Cup, Volvo Ocean Race) iterate $EI$ to minimise hull flex — flex wastes energy and slows the boat. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Neutral Axis of a Composite Hull Cross-Section Consider a single-skin FRP hull girder with **two laminates**: - Bottom shell: area $A_1$, modulus $E_1$, centroid at $y_1$ below waterline datum. - Deck (top): area $A_2$, modulus $E_2$, centroid at $y_2$ above waterline datum. We want to find the location $\bar{y}$ of the **neutral axis** measured from the baseline. **Step 1 — Choose a reference modulus.** Take $E_{ref} = E_1$ (the bottom shell laminate, usually the thickest structural element). **Step 2 — Transform the deck laminate.** A real area $A_2$ of modulus $E_2$ is *stiffer* by the ratio $n = E_2 / E_1$. Its equivalent area in the bottom-shell modulus is: $$A_2' \;=\; n \cdot A_2 \;=\; \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot A_2$$ **Step 3 — Apply the centroid (first moment of area) formula.** For the transformed section, the neutral axis position is: $$\bar{y} \;=\; \dfrac{A_1 \cdot y_1 \;+\; A_2' \cdot y_2}{A_1 \;+\; A_2'}$$ **Step 4 — Expand the modular ratio:** $$\bar{y} \;=\; \dfrac{A_1 \, y_1 \;+\; \left( \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \right) A_2 \, y_2}{A_1 \;+\; \left( \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \right) A_2}$$ **Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $E_1$ to clear the fraction:** $$\bar{y} \;=\; \dfrac{E_1 A_1 \, y_1 \;+\; E_2 A_2 \, y_2}{E_1 A_1 \;+\; E_2 A_2}$$ This is the **weighted (stiffness-weighted) centroid** formula — the *general* neutral axis location for any multi-material cross-section. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Second Moment of Area (Transformed Section) For the same two-laminate hull, the **transformed second moment of area** about the neutral axis is: **Step 1 — Use the parallel-axis theorem on each laminate:** $$I_{total}' \;=\; \left[ I_{1,own} \;+\; A_1 \left( \bar{y} - y_1 \right)^2 \right] \;+\; n \cdot \left[ I_{2,own} \;+\; A_2 \left( y_2 - \bar{y} \right)^2 \right]$$ where $I_{1,own}$ and $I_{2,own}$ are the *own-axis* second moments of each laminate about its own centroid. **Step 2 — Substitute $n = E_2/E_1$:** $$\begin{aligned} I_{total}' \;=\;& \left[ I_{1,own} + A_1 (\bar{y} - y_1)^2 \right] \\ & + \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \cdot \left[ I_{2,own} + A_2 (y_2 - \bar{y})^2 \right] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Re-arrange to expose the untransformed (real) stiffness-weighted $EI$:** Multiplying both sides by $E_1$: $$\begin{aligned} E_1 \cdot I_{total}' \;=\;& E_1 \left[ I_{1,own} + A_1 (\bar{y} - y_1)^2 \right] \\ & + E_2 \left[ I_{2,own} + A_2 (y_2 - \bar{y})^2 \right] \\ \;=\;& \sum_{i=1}^{2} E_i \left[ I_{i,own} + A_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2 \right] \end{aligned}$$ Therefore the **effective bending stiffness** used for deflection calculation is: $$(EI)_{eff} \;=\; \sum_{i} E_i \, I_i$$ **Step 4 — Summary table:** | Quantity | Symbol | Used For | |---|---|---| | $\displaystyle\sum_i \dfrac{E_i}{E_{ref}} I_i$ | $I_{transformed}$ | **Stress** calculation | | $\displaystyle\sum_i E_i I_i$ | $(EI)_{eff}$ | **Deflection / Stiffness** calculation | ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bending Stress in a Composite Hull Girder **Step 1 — Assume plane sections remain plane** (Euler-Bernoulli). Strain at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is: $$\varepsilon(y) \;=\; \dfrac{y}{\rho}$$ where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature. **Step 2 — Convert strain to stress via Hooke's Law in each laminate:** $$\sigma_i(y) \;=\; E_i \cdot \varepsilon(y) \;=\; E_i \cdot \dfrac{y}{\rho}$$ **Step 3 — The bending moment is the integral of stress × lever arm:** $$M \;=\; \int \sigma(y) \cdot y \, dA \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\rho} \int E(y) \, y^2 \, dA$$ The integral $\int E(y) \, y^2 \, dA$ is the **effective** second moment of area — the $(EI)_{eff}$ form we derived above: $$M \;=\; \dfrac{(EI)_{eff}}{\rho}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for curvature:** $$\dfrac{1}{\rho} \;=\; \dfrac{M}{(EI)_{eff}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute back into the stress equation** for any laminate *i* at distance $y$: $$\sigma_i(y) \;=\; \dfrac{M \cdot E_i \cdot y}{(EI)_{eff}}$$ **Step 6 — Maximum stress occurs at the extreme fibre** ($y = c$, maximum distance): $$\sigma_{i,max} \;=\; \dfrac{M \cdot E_i \cdot c_i}{(EI)_{eff}}$$ This shows that **the stiffest laminate carries the largest share of the extreme-fibre stress** — a critical consideration when laying up FRP hulls with mixed reinforcements (e.g., E-glass and carbon hybrids). ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Hull Girder Section Modulus > **Problem:** A 15 m GRP motorboat has a hull girder cross-section that can be idealised as two horizontal flanges: > - **Bottom shell laminate:** width $b_1 = 3.0$ m, thickness $t_1 = 0.020$ m, $E_1 = 25$ GPa, centroid $y_1 = 0.010$ m above keel. > - **Deck laminate:** width $b_2 = 2.6$ m, thickness $t_2 = 0.015$ m, $E_2 = 20$ GPa, centroid $y_2 = 1.50$ m above keel. > > Calculate (a) the neutral axis, (b) the transformed $I$, (c) the section modulus (referenced to $E_1$), and (d) the extreme-fibre bending stress for a hogging moment of $M = 2.5 \times 10^6$ N·m. ### (a) Neutral axis **Areas:** $$A_1 = b_1 t_1 = 3.0 \times 0.020 = 0.060 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = b_2 t_2 = 2.6 \times 0.015 = 0.039 \text{ m}^2$$ **Modular ratio:** $$n \;=\; \dfrac{E_2}{E_1} \;=\; \dfrac{20}{25} \;=\; 0.8$$ **Transformed deck area:** $$A_2' \;=\; n \cdot A_2 \;=\; 0.8 \times 0.039 \;=\; 0.0312 \text{ m}^2$$ **Neutral axis (from keel):** $$\bar{y} \;=\; \dfrac{A_1 y_1 + A_2' y_2}{A_1 + A_2'} \;=\; \dfrac{(0.060)(0.010) + (0.0312)(1.50)}{0.060 + 0.0312}$$ Numerator: $$(0.060)(0.010) = 0.00060$$ $$(0.0312)(1.50) = 0.04680$$ $$\text{Sum} = 0.04740$$ Denominator: $$0.060 + 0.0312 = 0.0912$$ Therefore: $$\bar{y} \;=\; \dfrac{0.04740}{0.0912} \;\approx\; 0.520 \text{ m above keel}$$ ### (b) Transformed second moment of area (about $\bar{y}$) **Own-axis $I$ of each flange (rectangle):** $$I_{1,own} = \dfrac{b_1 t_1^3}{12} = \dfrac{3.0 \times (0.020)^3}{12} = 2.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$ $$I_{2,own} = \dfrac{b_2 t_2^3}{12} = \dfrac{2.6 \times (0.015)^3}{12} = 7.31 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$ **Parallel-axis terms:** $$d_1 = \bar{y} - y_1 = 0.520 - 0.010 = 0.510 \text{ m}$$ $$d_2 = y_2 - \bar{y} = 1.50 - 0.520 = 0.980 \text{ m}$$ $$A_1 d_1^2 = 0.060 \times (0.510)^2 = 0.060 \times 0.2601 = 0.01561 \text{ m}^4$$ $$A_2' d_2^2 = 0.0312 \times (0.980)^2 = 0.0312 \times 0.9604 = 0.02996 \text{ m}^4$$ **Total transformed $I$:** $$I_{total}' = (2.00 \times 10^{-6} + 0.01561) + (0.731 \times 10^{-6} + 0.02996)$$ $$\;\;\; \approx 0.01561 + 0.02996 \;=\; 0.04557 \text{ m}^4$$ ### (c) Section modulus referenced to $E_1$ **Distance to extreme bottom fibre:** $$c_{bottom} = \bar{y} - (y_1 - t_1/2) = 0.520 - 0.000 = 0.520 \text{ m}$$ **Distance to extreme deck fibre:** $$c_{deck} = (y_2 + t_2/2) - \bar{y} = 1.5075 - 0.520 = 0.9875 \text{ m}$$ The smaller section modulus governs (usually the deck for a deep, narrow boat — but here the deck is further, so the bottom governs): $$Z_{bottom} = \dfrac{I_{total}'}{c_{bottom}} = \dfrac{0.04557}{0.520} \approx 0.0876 \text{ m}^3$$ $$Z_{deck} = \dfrac{I_{total}'}{c_{deck}} = \dfrac{0.04557}{0.9875} \approx 0.0461 \text{ m}^3$$ **Governing section modulus (minimum):** $$Z_{min} = 0.0461 \text{ m}^3 \quad (\text{deck})$$ ### (d) Extreme-fibre bending stress Using the reference modulus $E_1$ (since the transformed $I$ was referenced to it): $$\sigma_{deck,ext} = \dfrac{M}{Z_{deck,transformed}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^6}{0.0461}$$ $$\sigma_{deck,ext} \;\approx\; 54.2 \text{ MPa}$$ **Check:** Typical allowable bending stress for woven-roving E-glass/polyester laminate $\approx 60$ MPa → **Adequate** with $\approx 10\%$ margin. For the bottom shell, multiply by the modular ratio since it is *not* the reference material? **No** — both laminates' stresses are computed from the same transformed $I$ referenced to $E_1$. The bottom is the reference, so its stress is at the $E_1$ scale. To get the *real* stress in the deck (modulus $E_2$), scale by $n$: $$\sigma_{deck,real} = n \cdot \sigma_{deck,transformed} = 0.8 \times 54.2 = 43.4 \text{ MPa}$$ Both below the allowable. ✅ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hull Girder Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block-diagram shows how a composite boat is evaluated for **longitudinal strength and stiffness**, from raw laminates through classification. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hull Geometry Input
L B D Cb] --> B[Idealise as Hull Girder Beam] C[Laminate Schedule
E_i t_i y_i] --> D[Transformed Section Calc] B --> D D --> E[Neutral Axis ybar] D --> F[Transformed I total] E --> G[Section Modulus Z] F --> G H[Load Cases
M_sw and M_w] --> I[Total Bending Moment M] I --> J{Bending Stress sigma = M/Z} G --> J J --> K{sigma less than sigma_allow} K -- Yes --> L[Strength OK] K -- No --> M[Revise Laminate Schedule] F --> N[Bending Stiffness EI_eff] C --> N N --> O{Deflection delta less than L/500} O -- Yes --> P[Stiffness OK] O -- No --> M L --> Q[Design Approved] P --> Q M --> D ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Stress Distribution in a Composite Hull ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CS[Hull Cross-Section - Hogging Condition] direction LR DeckTop[Deck Laminate
sigma tension] NA[Neutral Axis
sigma equals 0] ShellBot[Bottom Shell
sigma compression] DeckTop --> NA --> ShellBot end subgraph Stress[Linear Stress Distribution] direction LR S1[plus sigma_max Deck] S2[zero at NA] S3[minus sigma_max Keel] S1 --> S2 --> S3 end CS --- Stress ``` ## 4.3 Composite Stiffness Contribution Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Hull Girder Stiffness EI_eff] --> K1[Bottom Shell Contribution
Largest 50 to 70 percent] Root --> K2[Deck Contribution
20 to 35 percent] Root --> K3[Side Shell Contribution
5 to 15 percent] Root --> K4[Longitudinal Stringers
5 to 10 percent] K1 --> Rule[Rule of Thumb
Bottom shell dominates longitudinal EI] K2 --> Rule K3 --> Rule K4 --> Rule ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a step in the design verification chain. The diamond blocks are *gates* — a single "No" returns the design to the laminate schedule revision stage. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "hull girder" and explain its importance in the longitudinal strength assessment of a composite boat.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **hull girder** is the engineering idealisation of the entire hull of a vessel (shell plating, decks, bulkheads, longitudinal stiffeners) as a single, long, hollow box beam spanning the length between bow and stern. It is *important* because: 1. It allows the use of **beam-bending theory** (Euler-Bernoulli) to compute stresses and deflections. (1 mark) 2. It captures the global response of the boat to bending moments caused by the difference between weight and buoyancy distributions in still water and waves. (1 mark) 3. It enables calculation of the **section modulus** $Z = I/c$ and the **bending stiffness** $EI$, which are the two governing parameters for strength and stiffness respectively. (1 mark) ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between longitudinal strength and longitudinal stiffness of a boat hull. State the governing formulas for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Longitudinal Strength | Longitudinal Stiffness | |---|---|---| | Quantity checked | Bending stress $\sigma$ | Deflection $\delta$ | | Formula | $\sigma = M/Z$ | $\delta = 5 w L^4 / (384 EI)$ | | Failure mode | Material yielding / fracture | Excessive hog / sag | | Parametric dependence | $\propto 1/Z$ | $\propto 1/(EI)$ | | Critical for | Deep, heavily loaded vessels | Long, slender, low-modulus FRP vessels | | Governing design | $Z_{min}$ per LR / DNV rules | Serviceability limit $L/500$ | (3 marks — 1 for correct definition of each + 1 for the formulas) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the location of the neutral axis of a composite hull cross-section consisting of *n* different laminates. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 20 m GRP sailing yacht has an idealised hull girder cross-section with the following properties: | Element | Area (m²) | Centroid above keel (m) | E (GPa) | |---|---|---|---| | Bottom shell | 0.080 | 0.012 | 28 | | Deck | 0.055 | 1.85 | 22 | | Side shell (port + stbd combined) | 0.042 | 0.92 | 25 | Compute the (i) neutral axis location, (ii) the transformed second moment of area about the neutral axis (referenced to the bottom-shell modulus), and (iii) the minimum section modulus. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** - Plane sections remain plane after bending (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory). (1 mark) - Full composite action exists between laminates (no slip at interfaces). (1 mark) - Linear elastic behaviour. (1 mark) - Each laminate is homogeneous within itself. (1 mark) **Derivation:** Let $E_i$ be the Young's modulus of the $i$-th laminate and $A_i$ its cross-sectional area at centroidal distance $y_i$ from a chosen reference datum (e.g., the keel). **Step 1:** Choose a reference modulus $E_{ref}$ (commonly the stiffest or the dominant structural laminate). **Step 2:** Define the modular ratio $n_i = E_i / E_{ref}$ for each laminate. **Step 3:** Transform each laminate's area to the equivalent area in the reference modulus: $$A_i' = n_i A_i$$ **Step 4:** Apply the *stiffness-weighted centroid* formula (first moment of area = 0 about the neutral axis): $$\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum_i A_i' \, y_i}{\sum_i A_i'} = \dfrac{\sum_i (E_i / E_{ref}) A_i \, y_i}{\sum_i (E_i / E_{ref}) A_i}$$ **Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $E_{ref}$: $$\boxed{\;\bar{y} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \, A_i \, y_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \, A_i}\;}$$ This is the **generalised neutral axis** for any multi-material cross-section. (3 marks — full derivation) #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Neutral axis** (using $E_{ref} = E_1 = 28$ GPa): $$\begin{aligned} n_1 &= E_1 / E_1 = 1.000 \\ n_2 &= E_2 / E_1 = 22 / 28 = 0.7857 \\ n_3 &= E_3 / E_1 = 25 / 28 = 0.8929 \end{aligned}$$ Transformed areas: $$A_1' = 1.000 \times 0.080 = 0.0800 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2' = 0.7857 \times 0.055 = 0.0432 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_3' = 0.8929 \times 0.042 = 0.0375 \text{ m}^2$$ Numerator of $\bar{y}$: $$\sum A_i' y_i = (0.0800)(0.012) + (0.0432)(1.85) + (0.0375)(0.92)$$ $$= 0.00096 + 0.07992 + 0.03450 = 0.11538$$ Denominator: $$\sum A_i' = 0.0800 + 0.0432 + 0.0375 = 0.1607$$ Neutral axis: $$\bar{y} = 0.11538 / 0.1607 \approx 0.718 \text{ m above keel}$$ **(ii) Transformed second moment of area:** Computing each contribution $\left[ I_{own,i} + A_i' (y_i - \bar{y})^2 \right]$: Assume each laminate acts as a thin plate; for simplicity take $I_{own} \approx 0$ relative to the parallel-axis term (acceptable for thin laminates). For the **bottom shell** ($i=1$): $$d_1 = 0.012 - 0.718 = -0.706 \text{ m}, \quad d_1^2 = 0.4984$$ $$A_1' d_1^2 = 0.0800 \times 0.4984 = 0.03987 \text{ m}^4$$ For the **deck** ($i=2$): $$d_2 = 1.85 - 0.718 = 1.132 \text{ m}, \quad d_2^2 = 1.2814$$ $$A_2' d_2^2 = 0.0432 \times 1.2814 = 0.05536 \text{ m}^4$$ For the **side shell** ($i=3$): $$d_3 = 0.92 - 0.718 = 0.202 \text{ m}, \quad d_3^2 = 0.0408$$ $$A_3' d_3^2 = 0.0375 \times 0.0408 = 0.00153 \text{ m}^4$$ **Total transformed $I$:** $$I_{total}' \approx 0.03987 + 0.05536 + 0.00153 = 0.09676 \text{ m}^4$$ **(iii) Minimum section modulus:** Extreme distances: - $c_{bottom} = \bar{y} - 0 \approx 0.718$ m (approximating keel-level extreme fibre) - $c_{deck} = (1.85 + t_2/2) - \bar{y} \approx 1.85 - 0.718 = 1.132$ m $$Z_{bottom} = I_{total}' / c_{bottom} = 0.09676 / 0.718 = 0.1348 \text{ m}^3$$ $$Z_{deck} = I_{total}' / c_{deck} = 0.09676 / 1.132 = 0.0855 \text{ m}^3$$ **Governing (minimum):** $Z_{min} = 0.0855$ m³ (deck). **(7 marks complete)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the difference between **hogging** and **sagging** conditions in a vessel. Show the resulting stress distributions on the hull cross-section. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the LR / DNV empirical formula $M_w = k L^2 B C_B$, calculate the wave bending moment for a 25 m GRP patrol boat with: - Length between perpendiculars $L = 25$ m - Moulded breadth $B = 5.6$ m - Block coefficient $C_B = 0.48$ - Wave coefficient $k = 0.055$ (hogging condition) If the still-water bending moment is $M_{sw} = 0.8 \times 10^6$ N·m (sagging), determine: (i) the maximum sagging moment, (ii) the maximum hogging moment, and (iii) comment on the structural implication of the result. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Hogging Condition** (bow and stern supported, midship unsupported by wave): - Bow and stern sit on successive wave crests → buoyant force high at the ends, low amidships. - Vessel bends into a "U" shape (concave upward at midship). - **Deck is in TENSION** (top fibre stretches). - **Bottom shell is in COMPRESSION** (bottom fibre shortens). - Net effect: keel tries to straighten, deck tries to elongate. **Sagging Condition** (midship supported, ends drooping): - A wave crest supports the midship; bow and stern hang in troughs. - Vessel bends into an inverted "U" (concave downward at midship). - **Deck is in COMPRESSION.** - **Bottom shell is in TENSION.** - Net effect: keel stretches, deck compresses. **Stress distribution** (linear, passing through zero at the neutral axis): ``` Hogging: Deck (+) Tension ─────── 0 ─────── (−) Compression Keel Sagging: Deck (−) Compression ─────── 0 ─────── (+) Tension Keel ``` The neutral axis (N.A.) remains at the stiffness-weighted centroid of the transformed section. (7 marks — 2 for definition of each condition, 2 for stress state of each element, 2 for diagram, 1 for neutral-axis mention) #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Wave bending moment (hogging):** $$M_w = k \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B = 0.055 \times (25)^2 \times 5.6 \times 0.48$$ $$= 0.055 \times 625 \times 5.6 \times 0.48$$ Step-wise multiplication: $$0.055 \times 625 = 34.375$$ $$34.375 \times 5.6 = 192.5$$ $$192.5 \times 0.48 = 92.4$$ **Units check:** $k$ is dimensionless × m² × m × dimensionless = m³. So $M_w = 92.4 \text{ t·m}$ if $k$ is in $t/m^3$, or $92.4 \times 9.81 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m} = 9.06 \times 10^5$ N·m. Treating the result as **N·m directly** for class purposes: $$M_w \approx 9.06 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}$$ **(ii) Maximum moments:** Still-water is sagging (positive convention here): $M_{sw} = 0.8 \times 10^6$ N·m (sagging). Wave moment magnitude $M_w = 0.906 \times 10^6$ N·m (hogging). Maximum sagging (wave adds to still-water sagging — *worst case*): $$M_{sag,max} = M_{sw} + M_w = 0.800 + 0.906 = 1.706 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}$$ Maximum hogging (wave subtracts from sagging still-water): $$M_{hog,max} = M_w - M_{sw} = 0.906 - 0.800 = 0.106 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m} = 1.06 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}$$ **(iii) Structural commentary:** - The **sagging condition is critical** ($M_{sag,max} \approx 16$ times the hogging moment in this case). The bottom shell will be in maximum tension and must be sized for the larger stress. - The **deck** experiences minimal hogging (0.106 MN·m) but full sagging compression (1.706 MN·m). - Design implication: the bottom laminate schedule (roving, mat orientation, core thickness) must be upgraded; the deck can use a more economical lay-up. - The large sagging moment also implies that the **keel region** must be carefully reinforced, especially at the keel-hull joint, because GRP is notch-sensitive in tension. **(7 marks complete)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing transformed $I$ with true $EI$:** Many students use the transformed section modulus for *deflection* calculation — this is wrong. Deflection requires the **true** $(EI)_{eff} = \sum E_i I_i$. Using transformed $I$ alone gives a fictitious, much higher stiffness. Loss: 2–3 marks per question. > > 2. **Forgetting to scale stresses by modular ratio:** When laminates of different $E$ share a transformed section, the *real* stress in the non-reference laminate must be multiplied by $n = E_i/E_{ref}$. Ignoring this under- or over-estimates the laminate stress. Loss: 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Sign convention for hogging vs sagging:** Mixing up which laminate is in tension vs compression is a classic 2-mark loss. Always state *which element* is in tension for the load case shown. > > 4. **Unit inconsistency in $M_w$ formula:** The LR / DNV coefficient $k$ is sometimes given in $t/m^3$ (tonnes) or in kN/m³. Failing to convert gives a 10³ error in $M$. Loss: 2 marks. > > 5. **Ignoring own-axis $I$ in $I_{total}$:** For very thick laminates (e.g., 50+ mm solid FRP or sandwich with thick core), the own-axis $I_{i,own} = b t^3/12$ is non-negligible. Examiners expect it included if thickness > 30 mm. Loss: 1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Longitudinal Strength and Stiffness** - **Hull girder idealisation:** entire hull = single box beam, span = LBP. - **Two governing checks:** strength ($\sigma = M/Z \le \sigma_{all}$) **and** stiffness ($\delta \le L/500$). - **Wave bending moment:** $M_w = k L^2 B C_B$ — LR / DNV empirical rule, $k$ depends on vessel type and load case. - **Total moment:** $M = M_{sw} \pm M_w$; sagging and hogging computed separately. - **Neutral axis of composite section:** *stiffness-weighted* centroid — formula $\bar{y} = \sum E_i A_i y_i / \sum E_i A_i$. - **Transformed area:** $A_i' = (E_i / E_{ref}) A_i$. - **Transformed $I$ (used for stress):** $I_{trans} = \sum (E_i/E_{ref})[I_{i,own} + A_i(y_i-\bar{y})^2]$. - **True $EI$ (used for stiffness):** $(EI)_{eff} = \sum E_i I_i$ — **never** use transformed $I$ for deflection. - **Extreme-fibre stress:** $\sigma_{i,max} = M E_i c_i / (EI)_{eff}$. - **Hogging:** deck tension, keel compression. - **Sagging:** deck compression, keel tension. - **Deflection limit:** $L/500$ (ocean-going), $L/300$ (smaller craft). - **Bottom shell contributes 50–70 %** of $EI$ in most FRP designs — never under-design it. - **Stiffness, not strength, often governs** FRP longitudinal design because $E_{FRP}$ is ~1/5 to 1/7 of steel. - **Heterogeneous laminates** (E-glass + carbon hybrids) require transformed-section analysis. - **Always specify the reference modulus** $E_{ref}$ before starting any transformed-section calculation. - **Classification rules** (LR, DNV, BV, ABS) provide minimum $Z$ requirements; always cross-check your design against the relevant rule book.

topics.title: Transverse Section Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transverse Section Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transverse Section Modulus (Z):** It is the ratio of the **Second Moment of Area** (Moment of Inertia, $I$) of a transverse cross-section of the hull girder about its **neutral axis** to the distance ($y$) from the neutral axis to the **extreme fibre** (outermost fibre) of that section.

Mathematically, the Transverse Section Modulus is expressed as:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y}$$

Where:
- $Z$ = Transverse Section Modulus, expressed in $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$ (SI units).
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia) of the transverse section about the neutral axis, expressed in $\text{mm}^4$ or $\text{m}^4$.
- $y$ = Perpendicular distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre, expressed in $\text{mm}$ or $\text{m}$.

In a **composite boat hull**, the transverse section is taken perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the vessel. The hull is treated as a **box girder (hull girder)**, and the transverse section modulus quantifies its resistance to bending caused by **still-water bending moment** and **wave-induced bending moment**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a thin wooden ruler held horizontally between your palms. If you press down on the middle, the ruler bends. The top surface of the ruler gets compressed (squeezed), and the bottom surface gets stretched (tension). Now imagine the ruler is the spine of a boat floating on water, and waves or cargo weight are pressing down on it. The boat's cross-section resists this bending — and **Transverse Section Modulus is the numerical "strength number" that tells you how well it resists this bending**.

- A **high section modulus** = the section is stiff and resists bending efficiently (like a deep I-beam).
- A **low section modulus** = the section is weak and bends easily (like a thin stick).

**Real-world Analogy:** Think of two straws — one empty and one packed with a wooden chopstick. Both have the same outer diameter, but the one with the chopstick resists bending far better. The "amount of material distributed away from the centerline" is what the section modulus captures.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units and Conventions in KTU Naval Architecture:**
> - Section Modulus $Z$ is reported in **$\text{cm}^3$** in classical shipbuilding literature, or **$\text{m}^3$** in modern SI-based naval architecture.
> - **Neutral Axis:** The axis in the transverse section where longitudinal stress is zero.
> - **Extreme Fibre:** The point in the section farthest from the neutral axis (either at the deck or at the keel).
> - **Two critical values:** $Z_{\text{deck}}$ (deck section modulus) and $Z_{\text{keel}}$ (keel/bottom section modulus). For most ships, $Z_{\text{deck}} \neq Z_{\text{keel}}$ because the hull is **asymmetric** about its horizontal neutral axis.

## 1.4 Role in Composite Boat Design (KTU 2024 Context)

In a **composite boat** (typically FRP — Fibre Reinforced Plastic — sandwich construction), the transverse section modulus is a **primary design driver** because:

1. It determines the **required structural scantlings** (laminate thickness, core thickness, stiffener spacing).
2. It directly feeds into the **longitudinal strength calculation** under the **Righting Moment (RM)** and **Wave Bending Moment (WBM)**.
3. Classification societies (e.g., **DNV, Lloyd's Register, IRS, ISO 12215**) prescribe **minimum section modulus** values that the boat's midship section must satisfy.
4. The neutral axis position depends on the **stacking sequence of composite laminates** (because each ply has a different $E$-modulus in composite construction), making $Z$ calculation more involved than in isotropic steel hulls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hull Girder Concept — Theoretical Foundation

When a boat floats on water and is subjected to gravity loads (weight) and buoyancy loads (upthrust), the hull behaves as a **long, hollow beam** — this is called the **Hull Girder** in naval architecture.

The fundamental assumptions are:

1. The hull is treated as a **prismatic beam** whose cross-section is the **midship transverse section** (assumed constant along most of the length).
2. Bending occurs about a **horizontal neutral axis** lying in the plane of the waterline.
3. Bending stresses are distributed **linearly** across the depth, following the **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory**: $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$.

## 2.2 Why Two Section Moduli? ($Z_d$ and $Z_k$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because the boat's transverse section is **asymmetric** (deck and keel are at different distances from the neutral axis, and the sectional area distribution is not symmetric), there are **two separate section moduli**:
> - $Z_d = \dfrac{I}{y_d}$ → Section modulus to the **deck** (upper extreme fibre).
> - $Z_k = \dfrac{I}{y_k}$ → Section modulus to the **keel** (lower extreme fibre).

The **bending stress** at the deck and keel respectively are:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{M}{Z_d} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_k = \frac{M}{Z_k}$$

The **larger** of $Z_d$ and $Z_k$ is called the **minor section modulus**, and the **smaller** is the **major section modulus**. Since $\sigma = M/Z$, the **smaller Z** governs design (gives the larger stress).

## 2.3 Logical Breakdown — How Transverse Section Modulus Works

- **Step 1:** Identify the transverse cross-section of the hull at midships.
- **Step 2:** Locate the **neutral axis** by ensuring $\sum (a_i \cdot y_i) = 0$ (first moment of area about NA = 0). For composite sections with different $E$-moduli, use the **transformed section method** where areas are weighted by $E_i / E_{\text{ref}}$.
- **Step 3:** Compute the **Second Moment of Area** $I = \sum \left[ \dfrac{b_i h_i^3}{12} + a_i \cdot d_i^2 \right]$ about the neutral axis, where $d_i$ is the distance from each element's centroid to the neutral axis.
- **Step 4:** Determine $y_d$ (NA to deck) and $y_k$ (NA to keel).
- **Step 5:** Compute $Z_d = I / y_d$ and $Z_k = I / y_k$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU University Exam — Transverse Section Modulus**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y}$ | General definition of section modulus | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress in the extreme fibre | $\text{N/mm}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$ |
| 3 | $I = \sum \left[ \dfrac{b_i h_i^3}{12} + a_i d_i^2 \right]$ | Second moment of area of composite section about NA | $\text{m}^4$ |
| 4 | $Z_d = \dfrac{I}{y_d}$ | Section modulus to the deck | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 5 | $Z_k = \dfrac{I}{y_k}$ | Section modulus to the keel | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 6 | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum (a_i y_i)}{\sum a_i}$ | Position of neutral axis from reference (isotropic case) | $\text{m}$ |
| 7 | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum (E_i a_i y_i)}{\sum (E_i a_i)}$ | Position of neutral axis (transformed/composite case) | $\text{m}$ |
| 8 | $Z_{\text{rect}} = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$ | Section modulus of a solid rectangle ($b$ = width, $d$ = depth) | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 9 | $Z_{\text{rect,h}} = \dfrac{B D^2}{6}$ | Approximate section modulus of a hull approximated as a rectangular box (depth $D$, breadth $B$) | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 10 | $M_{\text{sw}} = W \cdot L / g \cdot C_w$ | Still-water bending moment (rule-of-thumb) | $\text{N·m}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Avoidance Note:** In all formulas, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value — **never the bare vertical bar `|` inside tables** — to prevent markdown parsing errors.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The transverse section modulus is used in:

- **Classification society rule checks** (e.g., DNV GL, Lloyd's, ISO 12215-5 for FRP craft).
- **Longitudinal strength assessment** under hogging and sagging conditions.
- **Composite scantling optimization** to minimize weight while meeting minimum $Z$ requirements.
- **Failure analysis** of hulls that developed structural cracks along deck/keel regions.
- **Comparative benchmarking** between steel, aluminium, and FRP hulls of equivalent size.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Beam Theory to Section Modulus

Starting from the **Euler-Bernoulli bending stress equation**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

Where $M$ is the bending moment at the section, $y$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the fibre of interest, and $I$ is the second moment of area of the section about the neutral axis.

> **Step-by-step explanation:**
> - This equation tells us that stress varies **linearly** with $y$.
> - The **maximum stress** in the section occurs at the **extreme fibre** ($y = y_{\max}$).
> - Therefore, the maximum bending stress is: $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I}$.

> **Rearrangement — isolating M/I:**
> $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{I / y_{\max}}$$

> **Recognizing the group $I / y_{\max}$ as the section modulus $Z$:**
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z}}$$

> **Therefore the section modulus is rigorously defined as:**
> $$\boxed{Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}}$$

This derivation is the **foundation** of every section modulus problem in KTU examinations. Memorize the order: $\sigma = My/I \rightarrow \sigma_{\max} = M \cdot y_{\max}/I \rightarrow Z = I/y_{\max}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Section Modulus of a Solid Rectangular Section

Consider a rectangle of width $b$ and depth $d$, with the neutral axis passing horizontally through the centroid.

> **Step 1:** For a rectangle, the second moment of area about its centroidal axis is:
> $$I = \frac{b d^3}{12}$$

> **Step 2:** The distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre (top or bottom) is half the depth:
> $$y_{\max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

> **Step 3:** Substituting into the definition of section modulus:
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{b d^3 / 12}{d / 2}$$

> **Step 4:** Simplifying:
> $$Z = \frac{b d^3}{12} \times \frac{2}{d} = \frac{b d^2}{6}$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{Z = \frac{b d^2}{6}}$$

This formula is widely used in KTU problems to estimate the section modulus of a simplified hull treated as a rectangular box girder.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Neutral Axis Position for a Composite (Asymmetric) Section

Consider a hull with **two horizontal elements**: a deck plate (area $a_d$, centroid at $y_d$ from keel) and a keel/bottom plate (area $a_k$, centroid at $y_k$ from keel), both with the same material (isotropic case, $E$ = constant).

> **Step 1:** The neutral axis is defined by the condition that the **first moment of area above the NA equals the first moment of area below the NA** (sum is zero):
> $$\sum a_i (y_i - \bar{y}) = 0$$

> **Step 2:** Expanding:
> $$\sum a_i y_i - \bar{y} \sum a_i = 0$$

> **Step 3:** Solving for $\bar{y}$:
> $$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum a_i y_i}{\sum a_i}$$

> **Step 4:** For a hull with a deck at $y_d$ and bottom at $y_k$ (measured from keel baseline):
> $$\bar{y} = \frac{a_d \cdot y_d + a_k \cdot y_k}{a_d + a_k}$$

> **Step 5 (for composite materials with different $E$-moduli):** Replace areas with **transformed (stiffness-weighted) areas** $E_i a_i$:
> $$\bar{y} = \frac{\sum E_i a_i y_i}{\sum E_i a_i}$$

This **transformed-section approach** is the standard method taught in KTU for FRP composite boat sections with different laminate stacking sequences.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Second Moment of Area of a Composite Section

For a section composed of multiple rectangular elements (typical hull framing), the parallel axis theorem is used:

$$I = \sum \left[ \frac{b_i h_i^3}{12} + a_i d_i^2 \right]$$

Where:
- $b_i, h_i$ = width and depth of the $i$-th element.
- $a_i = b_i \cdot h_i$ = area of the $i$-th element.
- $d_i$ = vertical distance from the centroid of the $i$-th element to the neutral axis of the entire section.

> **Why this works:** The first term $\dfrac{b_i h_i^3}{12}$ is the moment of inertia of the element **about its own centroidal axis**, and the second term $a_i d_i^2$ is the **parallel axis correction** that shifts the reference to the NA of the whole section.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A small FRP boat hull has the following midship transverse section data (in metres):
- Deck flange: width $b_d = 1.6$ m, thickness $t_d = 0.015$ m, located at $y_d = 1.20$ m from keel.
- Side shell: two plates, each $b_s = 1.20$ m deep, $t_s = 0.010$ m thick.
- Keel/bottom: $b_k = 1.4$ m wide, $t_k = 0.020$ m thick, at $y_k = 0.010$ m from keel baseline.

> **Step 1 — Find areas:**
> - $a_d = 1.6 \times 0.015 = 0.0240 \text{ m}^2$
> - $a_s = 2 \times 1.20 \times 0.010 = 0.0240 \text{ m}^2$ (two sides)
> - $a_k = 1.4 \times 0.020 = 0.0280 \text{ m}^2$

> **Step 2 — Find centroids (measured from keel baseline):**
> - Deck centroid: $y_d = 1.20 - 0.015/2 = 1.1925$ m
> - Side centroid: $y_s = 0.020 + 1.20/2 = 0.620$ m
> - Keel centroid: $y_k = 0.020/2 = 0.010$ m

> **Step 3 — Neutral axis position (assuming single $E$ material, so isotropic):**
> $$\bar{y} = \frac{(0.0240)(1.1925) + (0.0240)(0.620) + (0.0280)(0.010)}{0.0240 + 0.0240 + 0.0280}$$

> **Computing numerator:**
> $$\text{Num} = 0.02862 + 0.01488 + 0.00028 = 0.04378 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Computing denominator:**
> $$\text{Den} = 0.0760 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Neutral axis position:**
> $$\bar{y} = \frac{0.04378}{0.0760} = 0.5761 \text{ m from keel}$$

> **Step 4 — Distances from neutral axis to extreme fibres:**
> - Distance to deck: $y_d^* = 1.20 - 0.5761 = 0.6239$ m
> - Distance to keel: $y_k^* = 0.5761$ m

> **Step 5 — Second moment of area about neutral axis:**

> For deck: $I_d = \dfrac{1.6 \times (0.015)^3}{12} + (0.0240)(1.1925 - 0.5761)^2$
> $$I_d = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} + 0.0240 \times (0.6164)^2$$
> $$I_d = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} + 0.0240 \times 0.3799 = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} + 9.118 \times 10^{-3} = 9.118 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

> For sides: $I_s = 2 \times \left[ \dfrac{0.010 \times (1.20)^3}{12} + (0.0120)(0.620 - 0.5761)^2 \right]$
> $$I_s = 2 \times \left[ 1.44 \times 10^{-3} + 0.0120 \times (0.0439)^2 \right]$$
> $$I_s = 2 \times \left[ 1.44 \times 10^{-3} + 2.314 \times 10^{-5} \right] = 2 \times 1.463 \times 10^{-3} = 2.926 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

> For keel: $I_k = \dfrac{1.4 \times (0.020)^3}{12} + (0.0280)(0.010 - 0.5761)^2$
> $$I_k = 9.33 \times 10^{-7} + 0.0280 \times (0.5661)^2$$
> $$I_k = 9.33 \times 10^{-7} + 0.0280 \times 0.3205 = 9.33 \times 10^{-7} + 8.974 \times 10^{-3} = 8.975 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

> **Total moment of inertia:**
> $$I = 9.118 \times 10^{-3} + 2.926 \times 10^{-3} + 8.975 \times 10^{-3} = 2.102 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^4$$

> **Step 6 — Section moduli:**
> $$Z_d = \frac{I}{y_d^*} = \frac{2.102 \times 10^{-2}}{0.6239} = 3.370 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3$$
> $$Z_k = \frac{I}{y_k^*} = \frac{2.102 \times 10^{-2}}{0.5761} = 3.650 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3$$

> **Conclusion:** $Z_k > Z_d$, so the **deck** has the higher stress under sagging moment, and $Z_d$ is the **governing (minor) section modulus**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transverse Section Modulus Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Transverse Section Geometry] --> B[Identify All Structural Elements]
    B --> C[Compute Area of Each Element: a_i = b_i x h_i]
    C --> D{Composite Material?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply Transformed Section: E_i x a_i]
    D -- No --> F[Use Raw Areas: a_i]
    E --> G[Compute Centroid y_i of Each Element]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Neutral Axis: y_bar = Sum E_i a_i y_i / Sum E_i a_i]
    H --> I[Compute Moment of Inertia: I = Sum b_i h_i^3 / 12 + a_i d_i^2]
    I --> J[Identify Extreme Fibre Distances: y_deck and y_keel]
    J --> K[Compute Z_deck = I / y_deck]
    J --> L[Compute Z_keel = I / y_keel]
    K --> M[Output: Section Moduli Z_d and Z_k]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compute Bending Stress: sigma = M / Z]
    N --> O[Check Against Allowable Stress]
    O --> P{Within Limit?}
    P -- No --> Q[Redesign Section: Increase Thickness or Add Stiffeners]
    P -- Yes --> R[Design Accepted]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hull Girder Bending Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Weight of Boat W_down] --> C[Hull Girder]
    B[Buoyancy Force F_up] --> C
    C --> D{Loading Type}
    D -- Hogging --> E[Bow and Stern Down, Midship Up]
    D -- Sagging --> F[Midship Down, Bow and Stern Up]
    E --> G[Tension at Deck, Compression at Keel]
    F --> H[Compression at Deck, Tension at Keel]
    G --> I[Compute sigma_deck = M / Z_deck]
    H --> J[Compute sigma_deck = M / Z_deck]
    I --> K[Compare with Allowable sigma_allow]
    J --> K
    K --> L{sigma_max less than sigma_allow?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Section Safe]
    L -- No --> N[Section Undersized - Strengthen]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Transverse Section of a Composite Boat

```
                   y_deck
        <-----------|--------->  ← Deck Plate (top flange)
       ****************************  ←--- y_d* = NA to deck
       *                          *
       *                          *  ← Side Shell (webs)
       *                          *
       *                          *
       ****************************  ←--- y_k* = NA to keel
        <----------------------->  ← Keel Plate (bottom flange)
                   y_keel
                  (NA) ← Neutral Axis
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **neutral axis (NA)** lies somewhere between the deck and the keel.
> - The distance from NA to the deck is $y_d^* = \bar{y}_{\text{deck}} - \bar{y}$.
> - The distance from NA to the keel is $y_k^* = \bar{y} - 0$.
> - Section modulus to the deck: $Z_d = I / y_d^*$.
> - Section modulus to the keel: $Z_k = I / y_k^*$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Define the term "Transverse Section Modulus" of a boat hull. State its SI unit.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The transverse section modulus ($Z$) of a boat hull is defined as the ratio of the second moment of area ($I$) of a transverse cross-section of the hull girder, taken about its horizontal neutral axis, to the perpendicular distance ($y$) from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre of the section.
>
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y}$$
>
> **SI Unit:** $\text{m}^3$ (cubic metres). In smaller craft, $\text{cm}^3$ or $\text{mm}^3$ may also be used.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for unit.)*

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Why are there two different values of section modulus ($Z_d$ and $Z_k$) for a typical ship hull, and which one is critical for design?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A typical ship hull has an **asymmetric** transverse cross-section about its horizontal neutral axis, since the distance from the NA to the deck ($y_d$) is different from the distance to the keel ($y_k$). Therefore, the section moduli to the deck ($Z_d = I/y_d$) and to the keel ($Z_k = I/y_k$) are **different**.
>
> The **smaller** of the two values is **critical for design**, because the bending stress $\sigma = M/Z$ is **inversely proportional** to $Z$. The smaller $Z$ produces the **higher stress** under the same bending moment, and hence governs the structural design.
>
> *(1 mark for asymmetry reason, 1 mark for inverse relationship, 1 mark for identifying critical Z.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, Q4(a)-(b)]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the transverse section modulus of a solid rectangular section of breadth $b$ and depth $d$. (**7 marks**)

**(b)** A small fishing boat has a midship section that can be approximated as a rectangular box girder of breadth $B = 2.0$ m and depth $D = 1.5$ m. Calculate the section modulus of this section and the maximum bending stress if the design bending moment is $M = 75$ kN·m. (**7 marks**)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> **Step 1:** State the general definition of section modulus. *(1 mark)*
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

> **Step 2:** Write the expression for second moment of area of a rectangle about its centroidal axis. *(1 mark)*
> $$I = \frac{b d^3}{12}$$

> **Step 3:** Identify the distance from the centroidal (neutral) axis to the extreme fibre. *(1 mark)*
> $$y_{\max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

> **Step 4:** Substitute $I$ and $y_{\max}$ into the section modulus formula. *(1 mark)*
> $$Z = \frac{b d^3 / 12}{d / 2}$$

> **Step 5:** Simplify the expression algebraically. *(1 mark)*
> $$Z = \frac{b d^3}{12} \times \frac{2}{d} = \frac{2 b d^2}{12}$$

> **Step 6:** Final simplified form. *(1 mark)*
> $$\boxed{Z = \frac{b d^2}{6}}$$

> **Step 7:** State the units and physical meaning. *(1 mark)*
> The section modulus has units of $\text{m}^3$ (length³), and represents the geometric stiffness of the section against bending.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

> **Step 1:** Identify the given data. *(1 mark)*
> $B = 2.0$ m, $D = 1.5$ m, $M = 75$ kN·m $= 75 \times 10^3$ N·m

> **Step 2:** Apply the rectangular section modulus formula. *(1 mark)*
> $$Z = \frac{B D^2}{6} = \frac{2.0 \times (1.5)^2}{6}$$

> **Step 3:** Compute the value. *(1 mark)*
> $$Z = \frac{2.0 \times 2.25}{6} = \frac{4.50}{6} = 0.75 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Step 4:** State the bending stress formula. *(1 mark)*
> $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

> **Step 5:** Substitute and compute. *(1 mark)*
> $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{75 \times 10^3}{0.75} = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ N/m}^2 = 100 \text{ kPa}$$

> **Step 6:** Convert to a more standard unit. *(1 mark)*
> $$\sigma_{\max} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 0.1 \text{ MPa} = 0.1 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> **Step 7:** Conclude. *(1 mark)*
> The section modulus is $0.75 \text{ m}^3$ and the maximum bending stress is $0.1$ MPa. For a typical composite (FRP) boat, this stress must be compared with the allowable laminate stress (typically 50–100 MPa for GRP), and the section is therefore well within safe limits under the given moment.

---

### Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3, Q5(a)-(b)]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the hull girder and state why the transverse section modulus is a critical design parameter. (**7 marks**)

**(b)** A composite boat hull has a midship section consisting of three horizontal elements:
- Deck plate: area $a_d = 0.030$ m², centroid at $y_d = 1.10$ m above keel.
- Side webs (combined): area $a_s = 0.020$ m², centroid at $y_s = 0.55$ m above keel.
- Keel plate: area $a_k = 0.040$ m², centroid at $y_k = 0.020$ m above keel.

Calculate the position of the neutral axis, the second moment of area, and the section modulus to the deck and keel. Assume a uniform $E$ (isotropic equivalent). (**7 marks**)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> **Step 1: Define the hull girder concept.** *(2 marks)*
> The hull girder is the conceptual model in which the entire boat hull is treated as a single, long, hollow beam. The transverse cross-section of this beam (typically taken at midships) is used to calculate the longitudinal strength of the vessel under bending. The hull is loaded vertically by self-weight (downward) and buoyancy (upward), and these create bending moments that are maximum at midships.

> **Step 2: Explain hogging and sagging.** *(2 marks)*
> - **Hogging** occurs when the bow and stern are supported by waves but the midship is in a wave trough — the deck is in **tension** and the keel is in **compression**.
> - **Sagging** occurs when the midship is supported by a wave crest but the ends are in troughs — the deck is in **compression** and the keel is in **tension**.

> **Step 3: Importance of section modulus in design.** *(2 marks)*
> The transverse section modulus $Z = I/y$ directly determines the **maximum longitudinal bending stress** $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z$. A higher $Z$ means lower stress for the same bending moment, allowing the designer to use thinner laminates (less weight) while meeting strength requirements. Classification society rules prescribe **minimum $Z$** values, and failure to meet these can lead to catastrophic structural failure (e.g., deck or keel cracking). For composite boats, $Z$ also informs the **placement and sizing of stiffeners, stringers, and core materials**.

> **Step 4: Concluding statement.** *(1 mark)*
> Therefore, the transverse section modulus is a **primary structural design parameter** that controls the longitudinal strength and stiffness of the boat hull, and must be carefully computed and verified during the design process.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

> **Step 1: Neutral axis position (isotropic case).** *(2 marks)*
> $$\bar{y} = \frac{a_d y_d + a_s y_s + a_k y_k}{a_d + a_s + a_k}$$
> $$\bar{y} = \frac{(0.030)(1.10) + (0.020)(0.55) + (0.040)(0.020)}{0.030 + 0.020 + 0.040}$$
> $$\bar{y} = \frac{0.0330 + 0.0110 + 0.0008}{0.090} = \frac{0.0448}{0.090} = 0.4978 \text{ m from keel}$$

> **Step 2: Distances from NA to extreme fibres.** *(1 mark)*
> - $y_d^* = 1.10 - 0.4978 = 0.6022$ m
> - $y_k^* = 0.4978 - 0.020 = 0.4778$ m (distance to keel centroid; for a thin keel plate, $y_k^* \approx \bar{y} \approx 0.4978$ m)

> **Step 3: Second moment of area — direct approximation.** *(1 mark)*
> Treating each element as a concentrated area at its centroid (lumped-area approximation):
> $$I = a_d (y_d - \bar{y})^2 + a_s (y_s - \bar{y})^2 + a_k (y_k - \bar{y})^2$$
> $$I = 0.030(0.6022)^2 + 0.020(0.0522)^2 + 0.040(0.4778)^2$$
> $$I = 0.030(0.3626) + 0.020(0.00272) + 0.040(0.2283)$$
> $$I = 0.01088 + 0.0000544 + 0.009132 = 0.02007 \text{ m}^4$$

> **Step 4: Section modulus to the deck.** *(1 mark)*
> $$Z_d = \frac{I}{y_d^*} = \frac{0.02007}{0.6022} = 0.0333 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Step 5: Section modulus to the keel.** *(1 mark)*
> $$Z_k = \frac{I}{y_k^*} = \frac{0.02007}{0.4778} = 0.0420 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Step 6: Identify the governing (critical) section modulus.** *(1 mark)*
> Since $Z_d < Z_k$, the **deck** has the **higher bending stress** under sagging and is the **critical** design location. $Z_d = 0.0333 \text{ m}^3$ governs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the parallel axis term** $a_i d_i^2$ in $I$ calculation — this is the most common reason for losing **3 to 4 marks** in section modulus problems. Always include both the local $I$ and the parallel axis shift.
> 2. **Confusing $Z_d$ and $Z_k$**: students often take the larger $Z$ as critical, but the **smaller** $Z$ governs. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Wrong neutral axis for composite sections**: if the problem says "composite" or "different laminates with different $E$", you **must** use the **transformed section method** with $E_i a_i$, not just $a_i$. Failure to do so loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Unit mismatches**: mixing cm and m in the same calculation is a silent killer. Convert all dimensions to a single unit (preferably SI: metres) **before** starting the calculation.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the units** in the final answer: $Z$ in $\text{m}^3$, $I$ in $\text{m}^4$, $\sigma$ in $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/mm}^2$. Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transverse Section Modulus**

- **Definition:** $Z = I / y_{\max}$ — ratio of second moment of area to extreme-fibre distance.
- **Stress formula:** $\sigma_{\max} = M / Z$ — the **governing equation** for hull girder bending.
- **Two values for asymmetric hulls:** $Z_d$ (deck) and $Z_k$ (keel), with $Z_d \neq Z_k$ in general.
- **Governing Z:** the **smaller** of $Z_d$ and $Z_k$ gives the **higher stress** and controls the design.
- **Neutral axis condition:** $\sum a_i (y_i - \bar{y}) = 0$ (isotropic) or $\sum E_i a_i (y_i - \bar{y}) = 0$ (composite).
- **Parallel axis theorem:** $I = \sum [b_i h_i^3 / 12 + a_i d_i^2]$ — **both terms are mandatory**.
- **Solid rectangle formula:** $Z = b d^2 / 6$ — a high-yield shortcut for KTU problems.
- **Units:** $Z$ in $\text{m}^3$, $I$ in $\text{m}^4$, $y$ in m, $\sigma$ in Pa or $\text{N/mm}^2$.
- **Physical meaning:** $Z$ is a **geometric stiffness index** — higher $Z$ = stiffer, more bending-resistant section.
- **Composite correction:** use $E_i a_i$ (not $a_i$ alone) when materials have different stiffnesses.
- **Hogging vs. sagging:** remember to identify which loading produces tension/compression at deck and keel, and check both cases.
- **Classification rules:** ISO 12215-5, DNV, and Lloyd's Register all prescribe minimum $Z$ values for various craft categories — these are frequently referenced in KTU viva questions.
- **Common mistake:** mixing up which fibre is in tension vs. compression during hogging and sagging — draw a free-body sketch to avoid this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shear Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Shear Modulus** (also designated as the **Modulus of Rigidity** or **Modulus in Shear**, represented by the symbol $G$ or sometimes $\mu$) is defined as the ratio of **shear stress** ($\tau$) to the corresponding **shear strain** ($\gamma$) within the elastic limit of a material. In the context of composite boat design, it quantifies a marine composite laminate's resistance to deformation when subjected to tangential (parallel-to-surface) forces that cause one plane of the material to slide relative to an adjacent plane.

Mathematically, the governing relation is expressed as:
