$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In composite boat hulls, the shear modulus is not a single value but a tensor quantity with components $G_{12}$ (in-plane shear), $G_{13}$, and $G_{23}$ (interlaminar/transverse shear components). Each plays a distinct role in hull girder stiffness and panel buckling resistance.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Deck of Cards"

Imagine a thick **deck of playing cards** resting on a table. If you place your palm flat on top of the deck and push it horizontally:

- The **top card** moves sideways relative to the **bottom card** (which stays put due to friction with the table).
- The cards in between **slide incrementally** — each one moves a tiny bit more than the one below it.
- The deck as a whole **deforms** — its originally rectangular cross-section becomes a **parallelogram**.

This sliding, layer-by-layer deformation is exactly what **shear strain** represents. Now imagine trying the same with two materials:
- A **rubber block** (low $G$): It deforms easily — even a small force produces a large parallelogram tilt.
- A **solid steel block** (high $G$): It barely deforms — you'd need enormous force to see any visible tilt.

**Shear modulus $G$ is the material property that tells you how much the material "fights back" against this kind of sliding deformation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Units:**
> - **SI Unit:** Pascal (Pa), more commonly expressed in **GPa** ($1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$)
> - **Typical Range for Marine Composites:** $G_{12}$ for E-glass/epoxy ≈ **3–5 GPa**; for carbon/epoxy ≈ **4–7 GPa**
> - **For comparison:** Steel has $G \approx 79$ GPa; aluminum $G \approx 26$ GPa
> - **Boundary Condition:** For isotropic materials, $0 < G < \infty$ and Poisson's ratio must satisfy $-1 < \nu < 0.5$

### Why Shear Modulus Matters in Boat Design

In a **composite boat hull**, the following loadings predominantly induce shear:

1. **Torsional loading** on the hull girder (e.g., when the boat rolls or twists in rough seas).
2. **Transverse shear** in hull panels (the skin trying to slide over the core in sandwich construction).
3. **Interlaminar shear** at ply interfaces in multi-layered laminates.
4. **In-plane shear** in panels under diagonal loading (sail loads, wave impact).

A hull with **low shear modulus** will deform excessively under these loads, leading to:
- Loss of hull shape (poor hydrodynamic performance)
- Local panel buckling
- Delamination between plies
- Reduced structural life

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Concept

#### Step 1: Shear Stress — The Driving Force

When a force $F$ is applied **parallel** to a surface of area $A$, the resulting shear stress is:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} \quad [\text{Units: Pa or N/m}^2]$$

In a boat hull, consider a section of the side shell: water pressure pushes the shell inward, but the hull bottom and deck resist. The resulting tangential force along the panel's plane creates $\tau$.

#### Step 2: Shear Strain — The Resulting Deformation

If a rectangular element of height $h$ has its top face displaced by a distance $\delta$ relative to its bottom (fixed) face, the shear strain is:

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{h} = \tan\theta \approx \theta \quad (\text{for small } \theta)$$

The angle $\theta$ (in radians) represents how much the originally right-angled corner has tilted.

#### Step 3: Hooke's Law in Shear — The Linear Regime

Within the **elastic limit**, shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

This is the shear analog of Hooke's law ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$).

#### Step 4: The Universal Elastic Relationship (Isotropic Materials)

For isotropic, homogeneous materials, $G$ is **not independent** of Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$. The fundamental relationship is:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This relation tells us that knowing any two of $\{E, G, \nu\}$ is enough to compute the third for isotropic materials. For **composite laminates**, however, this simple relation does **not** hold — each $G_{ij}$ must be measured or computed from micromechanics.

#### Step 5: Shear Modulus in Composite Laminates

For a unidirectional composite ply, the **in-plane shear modulus** $G_{12}$ is given by the **Rule of Mixtures inverse form** (Halpin-Tsai or direct micromechanics):

$$\frac{1}{G_{12}} = \frac{V_f}{G_{f12}} + \frac{V_m}{G_m}$$

where:
- $V_f, V_m$ = volume fractions of fiber and matrix
- $G_{f12}$ = shear modulus of the fiber
- $G_m$ = shear modulus of the matrix

For the **transverse shear modulus** $G_{23}$, a more complex Halpin-Tsai form is used.

### 2.2 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

| Application | Role of Shear Modulus |
|-------------|----------------------|
| **Hull girder stiffness** | Torsional rigidity $J \cdot G$ determines resistance to twisting |
| **Sandwich core selection** | Core shear modulus (PVC, balsa, foam) directly controls panel deflection |
| **Laminate stacking sequence** | $\pm 45°$ plies maximize in-plane shear contribution to stiffness |
| **Hull-deck joint design** | Shear modulus governs load transfer efficiency |
| **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** | $G_{12}, G_{13}, G_{23}$ are required input material properties |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Definition of shear modulus | Pa |
| 2 | $\tau = F / A$ | Shear stress | Pa |
| 3 | $\gamma = \delta / h$ | Shear strain (small angle) | rad (dimensionless) |
| 4 | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | Isotropic relation | Pa |
| 5 | $1/G_{12} = V_f/G_{f12} + V_m/G_m$ | UD composite in-plane shear (inverse ROM) | Pa |
| 6 | $G_{12} = \tau_{12}/\gamma_{12}$ | Composite ply shear modulus | Pa |
| 7 | $\theta \approx \tan\theta = \delta / h$ | Small-angle approximation for shear strain | rad |
| 8 | $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ | Bulk modulus (related, not always needed) | Pa |
| 9 | $\nu = E/[2G] - 1$ | Rearranged isotropic relation | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G_{LT} = G_{12}$ (notation) | Longitudinal-Transverse shear modulus | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize formulas 1, 2, 3, and 4 by heart. Formula 5 is a favorite for Part B questions on composite micromechanics. Always state the **assumptions** (e.g., "isotropic, linear elastic, small strains") when applying Hooke's law in shear.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Shear Modulus from Basic Stress–Strain Definition

**Starting Point:** We apply a tangential force $F$ to the top face of a cubic element of side length $L$, with the bottom face fixed.

**Step 1 — Compute the shear stress on the top face:**

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{L^2}$$

This is the force per unit area acting tangentially on the upper surface.

**Step 2 — Define the shear strain as the lateral displacement per unit height:**

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{L}$$

where $\delta$ is the horizontal displacement of the top face.

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear (linear elastic regime):**

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:**

$$\frac{F}{L^2} = G \cdot \frac{\delta}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the shear modulus:**

$$G = \frac{F \cdot L}{\delta \cdot L^2} = \frac{F}{\delta \cdot L}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{F}{\delta \cdot L}}$$

This is the **experimental** form used in torsion tests of cylindrical specimens and in Iosipescu / Arcan shear tests for composites.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ for Isotropic Materials

This derivation uses the principle of **strain energy equivalence** in pure shear versus uniaxial tension.

**Step 1 — Strain Energy Density in Uniaxial Tension:**

When a material is pulled in one direction with stress $\sigma_x$, the resulting axial strain is $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x / E$, and the lateral strains are $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \sigma_x / E$. The strain energy per unit volume is:

$$u_{tension} = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_x \varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{2E}$$

**Step 2 — Strain Energy Density in Pure Shear:**

A pure shear stress state $\tau_{xy}$ produces shear strain $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$. The strain energy per unit volume is:

$$u_{shear} = \frac{1}{2}\tau_{xy}\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}^2}{2G}$$

**Step 3 — Use Stress Transformation:**

The pure shear state $\tau_{xy}$ on the $xy$-plane is **equivalent** (via 45° Mohr's circle rotation) to a uniaxial tension of magnitude $\sigma = \tau_{xy}$ along the 45° principal direction, with a perpendicular compression of equal magnitude.

**Step 4 — Apply Equivalence of Strain Energy:**

For the same magnitude of principal stress, the strain energies must be equal:

$$u_{tension} = u_{shear}$$

$$\frac{\sigma_x^2}{2E} = \frac{\tau_{xy}^2}{2G}$$

**Step 5 — Set $\sigma_x = \tau_{xy}$ (equal magnitude from the 45° equivalence):**

$$\frac{1}{E} = \frac{1}{G}$$

This would imply $E = G$, which is **incorrect** because we have not yet accounted for the lateral contraction effects in tension.

**Step 6 — Correct Accounting: Use Full 3D Strain Energy**

In uniaxial tension, the full strain energy density (including lateral strains) is:

$$u_{tension} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\sigma_x \varepsilon_x + \sigma_y \varepsilon_y + \sigma_z \varepsilon_z\right]$$

With $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$, $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x/E$, $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \sigma_x/E$:

$$u_{tension} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\sigma_x^2}{E} - 2 \cdot \frac{\nu \sigma_x^2}{E}\right] = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{2E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

**Step 7 — Equate to Shear Strain Energy with $\sigma_x = \tau_{xy}$:**

$$\frac{\sigma_x^2}{2E}(1 - 2\nu) = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{2G}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify:**

$$1 - 2\nu = \frac{E}{G}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** For steel, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$:
> $G = 200 / [2(1.30)] = 200/2.6 \approx 76.9$ GPa — matches the standard handbook value of $\approx 79$ GPa (minor differences arise from exact $\nu$ and $E$ used). ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Shear Modulus of a Unidirectional Composite Ply (Rule of Mixtures)

**Assumption:** Fibers and matrix are **isotropic**, **perfectly bonded**, and the loading is **isostrain** in shear.

**Step 1 — Under isostrain (equal $\gamma_{12}$ in fiber and matrix):**

$$\gamma_{12}^f = \gamma_{12}^m = \gamma_{12}$$

**Step 2 — Total shear stress is the volume-weighted sum:**

$$\tau_{12} = V_f \tau_{12}^f + V_m \tau_{12}^m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\tau = G\gamma$:**

$$G_{12}\gamma_{12} = V_f G_{f12}\gamma_{12} + V_m G_m \gamma_{12}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\gamma_{12}$:**

$$\boxed{G_{12} = V_f G_{f12} + V_m G_m} \quad \text{(Upper bound, Voigt form)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Important caveat:** The **direct** (Voigt) rule of mixtures **overestimates** $G_{12}$ in practice. A more accurate estimate uses the **inverse** form (Reuss bound), which assumes isostress:
> $1/G_{12} = V_f/G_{f12} + V_m/G_m$. For boat design, the **Halpin-Tsai** semi-empirical equation is often preferred as it lies between these bounds.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A unidirectional E-glass/epoxy composite has fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.55$, $G_{f12} = 28$ GPa, $G_m = 1.2$ GPa. Compute $G_{12}$ using (a) Voigt rule, (b) Reuss inverse rule, and (c) the Halpin-Tsai equation with $\xi = 1$.

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.55 \Rightarrow V_m = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$
- $G_{f12} = 28$ GPa
- $G_m = 1.2$ GPa

#### Part (a) — Voigt Rule

$$G_{12}^{V} = V_f G_{f12} + V_m G_m$$

$$G_{12}^{V} = (0.55)(28) + (0.45)(1.2)$$

$$G_{12}^{V} = 15.4 + 0.54 = 15.94 \text{ GPa}$$

#### Part (b) — Reuss Inverse Rule

$$\frac{1}{G_{12}^{R}} = \frac{V_f}{G_{f12}} + \frac{V_m}{G_m}$$

$$\frac{1}{G_{12}^{R}} = \frac{0.55}{28} + \frac{0.45}{1.2}$$

$$\frac{1}{G_{12}^{R}} = 0.01964 + 0.375 = 0.39464 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$G_{12}^{R} = \frac{1}{0.39464} = 2.534 \text{ GPa}$$

#### Part (c) — Halpin-Tsai with $\xi = 1$

$$G_{12}^{HT} = G_m \cdot \frac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$$

where $\eta = \dfrac{G_{f12}/G_m - 1}{G_{f12}/G_m + \xi}$

**Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{G_{f12}}{G_m} = \frac{28}{1.2} = 23.33$$

**Compute $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{23.33 - 1}{23.33 + 1} = \frac{22.33}{24.33} = 0.9178$$

**Compute the numerator and denominator:**

$$1 + \xi \eta V_f = 1 + (1)(0.9178)(0.55) = 1 + 0.5048 = 1.5048$$

$$1 - \eta V_f = 1 - (0.9178)(0.55) = 1 - 0.5048 = 0.4952$$

**Compute $G_{12}^{HT}$:**

$$G_{12}^{HT} = (1.2) \cdot \frac{1.5048}{0.4952} = 1.2 \cdot 3.039 = 3.647 \text{ GPa}$$

**Result:** $G_{12}^{V} = 15.94$ GPa, $G_{12}^{R} = 2.534$ GPa, $G_{12}^{HT} = 3.65$ GPa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The Halpin-Tsai value (3.65 GPa) is realistic for a unidirectional E-glass/epoxy. The Voigt value (15.94 GPa) is too high because it assumes unrealistic isostrain. The Reuss value (2.53 GPa) is a lower bound. The truth lies in between — for design, use the **Halpin-Tsai** result.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Influence of Parameters on $G$

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on $G_{12}$ (UD composite) | Physical Reason |
|-----------|--------|------------------------------------|-----------------|
| Fiber volume fraction | $V_f \uparrow$ | $G_{12}$ increases (stiffer fibers dominate) | Fibers carry most shear load |
| Fiber shear modulus | $G_{f12} \uparrow$ | $G_{12}$ increases | Stiffer reinforcement |
| Matrix shear modulus | $G_m \uparrow$ | $G_{12}$ increases (smaller effect) | Matrix acts as load-transfer medium |
| Temperature | $T \uparrow$ | $G_{12}$ decreases | Matrix softens (resin-dominated property) |
| Moisture absorption | $M \uparrow$ | $G_{12}$ decreases | Plasticization of resin |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pure Shear Deformation — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Tangential Force F] --> B[Force Distributed over Area A]
    B --> C[Shear Stress tau equals F divided by A]
    C --> D[Deformation of Element]
    D --> E[Top Face Displaces by delta]
    E --> F[Shear Strain gamma equals delta divided by h]
    F --> G{Within Elastic Limit?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Hooke Law in Shear tau equals G times gamma]
    G -- No --> I[Plastic or Failure Region]
    H --> J[Shear Modulus G equals tau divided by gamma]
    I --> K[Nonlinear or Damage Analysis]
    J --> L[Output: Material Stiffness Property]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Relationship Between Elastic Constants (Isotropic Materials)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Young Modulus E] --> R[Elastic Constants Relation]
    NU[Poisson Ratio nu] --> R
    R --> G[Shear Modulus G]
    R --> K[Bulk Modulus K]
    G --> F1[G equals E divided by 2 times 1 plus nu]
    K --> F2[K equals E divided by 3 times 1 minus 2 nu]
    R --> A1[Any 2 of 3 determine the third]
```

### 4.3 Composite Boat Hull Loading Diagram (Schematic Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Hull Girder Torsion] --> S1[Torsional Rigidity J times G12]
    L2[Wave Impact Pressure] --> S2[Transverse Shear G13 and G23]
    L3[Sail or Rigging Loads] --> S3[In-Plane Shear G12]
    L4[Sandwich Panel Loading] --> S4[Core Shear Modulus Gc]
    S1 --> H[Hull Deflection and Stress State]
    S2 --> H
    S3 --> H
    S4 --> H
    H --> O[Structural Performance]
    O --> O1[Hull Shape Retention]
    O --> O2[Buckling Resistance]
    O --> O3[Fatigue Life]
```

### 4.4 Test Method Block Diagram (Iosipescu Shear Test for Composites)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Specimen in Iosipescu Fixture] --> F[Notched Specimen with V Grooves]
    F --> M[Apply Two Counter Forces at Notch Tips]
    M --> P[Pure Shear Region Develops Between Notches]
    P --> R[Measure Load and Strain Gauge Output]
    R --> C[Compute tau12 and gamma12]
    C --> O[G12 equals tau12 divided by gamma12]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** These block diagrams capture the **functional logic** of shear deformation, the inter-relationship of elastic constants, and the practical testing methodology used in marine composite laboratories. For physical drawings of specimen geometry and stress states, refer to ASTM D5379 (Iosipescu) and ASTM D3518 (in-plane shear) standards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define shear modulus. What are its SI units? Mention its significance in composite boat hull design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Shear Modulus** is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit of a material. It represents the material's resistance to deformation under tangential (shear) loading.

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

**SI Unit:** Pascal (Pa) or N/m²; commonly expressed in **GPa**.

**Significance in composite boat hull design:** The shear modulus determines the hull girder's resistance to torsional deformation under wave loading, governs the interlaminar shear strength at ply interfaces, and controls the transverse shear stiffness of sandwich panel construction. A higher $G$ value produces a stiffer hull with better hydrodynamic performance and resistance to panel buckling.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for units, 1 mark for significance in boat design)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the relationship between Young's modulus, shear modulus, and Poisson's ratio for an isotropic material. Mention the range of values of Poisson's ratio for stable isotropic materials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Relationship:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus, $G$ is shear modulus, and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

**Range of Poisson's ratio for stable isotropic materials:**

$$-1 < \nu < 0.5$$

For most engineering materials, $\nu$ lies between 0.2 and 0.35. Cork has $\nu \approx 0$ (a notable exception). The upper bound $\nu = 0.5$ corresponds to an incompressible material (e.g., rubber under short-term loading).

*(1.5 marks for the relationship, 1.5 marks for the range with explanation)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ for an isotropic, linear elastic material using the principle of strain energy equivalence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional carbon-fiber/epoxy composite has fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.60$, fiber shear modulus $G_{f12} = 20$ GPa, and matrix shear modulus $G_m = 1.1$ GPa. Compute the in-plane shear modulus $G_{12}$ using (i) the Voigt (isostrain) rule and (ii) the Reuss (isostress) rule. Comment on which value is more realistic and why. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$:**

**Step 1:** Consider a uniaxial tensile stress $\sigma_x$ applied along the $x$-axis. The corresponding strains are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 2:** The strain energy density (energy per unit volume) in tension, including lateral contributions:

$$u_{tension} = \frac{1}{2}(\sigma_x \varepsilon_x + \sigma_y \varepsilon_y + \sigma_z \varepsilon_z)$$

With $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$:

$$u_{tension} = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_x \varepsilon_x + \frac{1}{2}\sigma_y \varepsilon_y + \frac{1}{2}\sigma_z \varepsilon_z$$

$$u_{tension} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\sigma_x^2}{E} - 2 \cdot \frac{\nu \sigma_x^2}{E}\right] = \frac{\sigma_x^2 (1 - 2\nu)}{2E}$$

**Step 3:** Now consider a pure shear stress state $\tau_{xy}$. The shear strain is $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$. The strain energy density is:

$$u_{shear} = \frac{1}{2}\tau_{xy}\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}^2}{2G}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the 45° stress transformation: A pure shear state $\tau_{xy}$ is equivalent to a uniaxial tension of magnitude $\sigma = \tau_{xy}$ along the 45° direction, with perpendicular compression of equal magnitude.

**Step 5:** Equate the strain energies (both represent the same physical state):

$$\frac{\sigma_x^2 (1 - 2\nu)}{2E} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{2G} \quad \text{(with } \sigma_x = \tau_{xy}\text{)}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:

$$1 - 2\nu = \frac{E}{G}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

*(Full marks: 7 — 1 mark for each major step)*

---

**Part (b) — Computation for Carbon-Fiber/Epoxy:**

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.60$, $V_m = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40$
- $G_{f12} = 20$ GPa, $G_m = 1.1$ GPa

**(i) Voigt (Isostrain) Rule:**

$$G_{12}^{V} = V_f G_{f12} + V_m G_m$$

$$G_{12}^{V} = (0.60)(20) + (0.40)(1.1)$$

$$G_{12}^{V} = 12.0 + 0.44 = 12.44 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Reuss (Isostress) Rule:**

$$\frac{1}{G_{12}^{R}} = \frac{V_f}{G_{f12}} + \frac{V_m}{G_m}$$

$$\frac{1}{G_{12}^{R}} = \frac{0.60}{20} + \frac{0.40}{1.1}$$

$$\frac{1}{G_{12}^{R}} = 0.030 + 0.3636 = 0.3936 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$G_{12}^{R} = \frac{1}{0.3936} = 2.541 \text{ GPa}$$

**Comment on Realism:** The **Reuss value (2.54 GPa)** is closer to experimentally measured values for typical carbon/epoxy systems. The Voigt rule **overestimates** $G_{12}$ because it assumes both phases experience identical strain, which is unrealistic for shear loading where the soft matrix dominates the deformation. In practice, the **Halpin-Tsai equation** is preferred as it provides an interpolated estimate that closely matches experimental data for unidirectional composites.

*(2 marks for each rule computation, 1 mark for the comment, 2 marks for justification)*

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the concept of shear stress and shear strain in a composite material. Define shear modulus and explain how it differs in isotropic metals versus anisotropic composite laminates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The hull of a sailing yacht is constructed of a $[0/\pm 45/90]_s$ glass-fiber/epoxy laminate. The mid-plane shear modulus in the boat's longitudinal-transverse plane is $G_{xy}^{lam} = 8.5$ GPa. If the hull is subjected to a torsional moment $T = 12$ kN·m, and the effective shear area of the hull cross-section is $A_s = 0.85$ m², estimate the average shear stress developed. Comment on whether the hull is safe if the allowable shear stress is $\tau_{allow} = 25$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Shear Stress and Shear Strain in Composites:**

**Conceptual Explanation:**
When a tangential force $F$ is applied parallel to the surface of a material element of area $A$, it produces a **shear stress**:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

The element deforms such that one face displaces by $\delta$ relative to the opposite face over a distance $h$, producing a **shear strain**:

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{h}$$

**Diagram (in words):** A rectangular element of dimensions $L \times L$ is shown. The bottom face is fixed. A force $F$ is applied tangentially on the top face. The element deforms into a parallelogram with the originally right-angled corner tilting by angle $\theta = \gamma$.

**Definition:** Shear modulus is the ratio of shear stress to shear strain:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

**Difference between Isotropic Metals and Anisotropic Composites:**

| Property | Isotropic Metals | Anisotropic Composite Laminates |
|----------|------------------|--------------------------------|
| Number of independent shear moduli | One ($G$) | Multiple: $G_{12}, G_{13}, G_{23}$ |
| Relation to $E$ and $\nu$ | $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ | No simple relation; must measure each |
| Symmetry | Full 3D symmetry | Symmetry depends on stacking sequence |
| Typical values | Steel $\approx 79$ GPa, Al $\approx 26$ GPa | E-glass/epoxy $G_{12} \approx 3$–$5$ GPa |

*(2 marks for definition + diagram, 3 marks for differences, 2 marks for example values)*

---

**Part (b) — Hull Shear Stress Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $T = 12$ kN·m $= 12{,}000$ N·m
- $A_s = 0.85$ m²
- $\tau_{allow} = 25$ MPa

**Step 1 — Use the average shear stress formula for thin-walled closed sections:**

For a thin-walled section under torsion, the average shear stress is approximated by:

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{T}{2 \cdot A_s \cdot t_{eff}}$$

However, for this problem, we are given $A_s$ as the "effective shear area" already incorporating the wall thickness, so the simplified average shear stress formula is:

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{T}{2 \cdot A_s}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ N·m}}{2 \times 0.85 \text{ m}^2}$$

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{12{,}000}{1.70} = 7058.8 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\tau_{avg} \approx 7.06 \text{ kPa} = 0.00706 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with allowable:**

$$\tau_{avg} = 0.00706 \text{ MPa} \ll \tau_{allow} = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Safety Conclusion:** The hull is **very safe** under the given torsional loading. The induced shear stress is more than **3,500 times smaller** than the allowable limit, indicating that either the load case is light, or the section has substantial reserve capacity. In a real design scenario, other load cases (slamming, combined bending + torsion) would govern the design.

**Note on $G_{xy}^{lam}$:** The given $G_{xy}^{lam} = 8.5$ GPa is not directly used in this simplified stress calculation but would be used in a more detailed analysis to compute shear strain and twist angle:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G_{xy}^{lam}} = \frac{0.00706 \times 10^6}{8.5 \times 10^9} \approx 8.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ rad}$$

This extremely small strain confirms the hull's excellent rigidity under this load.

*(2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution and calculation, 2 marks for safety comment, 1 mark for additional $G$ usage note)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Converting kN·m to N·m correctly is critical. Mixing units between GPa, MPa, and Pa is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. **Always use SI base units (N, m, Pa) consistently.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $G_{12}$ vs. $G_{23}$:** In composites, $G_{12}$ is in-plane shear (fibers + matrix), while $G_{13}$ and $G_{23}$ are much smaller (matrix-dominated, no fibers in transverse direction). Don't use the same value for all.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Assumptions:** When applying $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$, you **must** state "isotropic, linear elastic, homogeneous." Examiners deduct marks if assumptions are not mentioned.
>
> 4. **Sign Errors in $\nu$:** Poisson's ratio is positive for most engineering materials. A negative sign in $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ would be a serious conceptual error.
>
> 5. **Voigt vs. Reuss Confusion:** Many students apply the wrong rule of mixtures. Remember: **Voigt = isostrain = upper bound**; **Reuss = isostress = lower bound**. Halpin-Tsai = realistic intermediate.
>
> 6. **Skipping Diagrams in 7-mark Questions:** A neat, labeled diagram of shear deformation is worth 1–2 marks. Don't skip it.
>
> 7. **Not Commenting on Realism:** In part (b) of numerical questions, always comment on which computed value is realistic and why. Examiners specifically look for engineering judgment, not just arithmetic.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Shear Modulus $G$** = ratio of shear stress $\tau$ to shear strain $\gamma$; SI unit: **Pa (or GPa)**.
- **Hooke's Law in Shear:** $\tau = G\gamma$ — valid only in the linear elastic regime.
- **Universal isotropic relation:** $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ — **not valid** for anisotropic composites.
- **For UD composite plies:**
  - Voigt (isostrain): $G_{12} = V_f G_{f12} + V_m G_m$ — upper bound, often **overestimates**.
  - Reuss (isostress): $1/G_{12} = V_f/G_{f12} + V_m/G_m$ — lower bound, often **underestimates**.
  - Halpin-Tsai: provides realistic interpolated value (preferred in design).
- **Composite laminates have multiple shear moduli:** $G_{12}$ (in-plane), $G_{13}$, $G_{23}$ (transverse/interlaminar).
- **Boat hull relevance:** $G$ governs torsional rigidity, panel shear stiffness, and interlaminar shear strength.
- **Range of Poisson's ratio:** $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials.
- **Small-angle approximation:** $\gamma = \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians) — valid for $\theta < 5°$ typically.
- **Test methods:** Iosipescu (ASTM D5379), $\pm 45°$ tension (ASTM D3518), torsion of cylindrical specimens.
- **Design implication:** Low $G$ in the matrix (resin) means $G_{12}$ of the composite is much lower than $G_{f12}$ of the fiber alone — matrix shear properties often govern overall composite shear behavior.
- **Temperature and moisture** reduce $G$ significantly — always account for service environment in marine applications.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For Part B questions, always structure your answer with: **(1) Given data, (2) Assumptions, (3) Governing formula, (4) Step-by-step substitution, (5) Result with units, (6) Engineering comment.** This is the exact pattern that KTU board examiners reward with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson's Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Poisson's Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Poisson's Ratio (ν or μ)** is a fundamental elastic constant that quantifies the **negative ratio of lateral (transverse) strain to longitudinal (axial) strain** experienced by a material subjected to uniaxial mechanical loading within its elastic limit. It is a dimensionless scalar property intrinsic to a homogeneous material's elastic response.

Mathematically, for a uniaxial stress state along the x-direction:

$$\nu_{xy} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{y}}{\varepsilon_{x}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{z}}{\varepsilon_{x}}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_{x}$ = longitudinal strain (direction of applied load)
- $\varepsilon_{y}, \varepsilon_{z}$ = transverse strains (perpendicular to load)
- The **negative sign** ensures ν is positive for conventional materials (which contract laterally when stretched)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For composites, Poisson's ratio is a tensor, not a scalar.** A unidirectional fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) has two independent Poisson's ratios:
> - **Major Poisson's Ratio ($\nu_{12}$)** — lateral contraction in the transverse direction (2) due to longitudinal (1) loading
> - **Minor Poisson's Ratio ($\nu_{21}$)** — lateral contraction in the longitudinal direction (1) due to transverse (2) loading

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In Composite Boat Design (PENST742), Poisson's ratio is critical for predicting **hull plate curvature, panel buckling under hydrostatic pressure, differential resin-fiber strains, and stress concentrations at hull-to-deck joints**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** between your fingers. As it elongates and becomes thinner, your fingers observe the band getting narrower. The ratio of how much narrower it gets compared to how much it stretched is Poisson's ratio.

For a **marine composite** like a fiberglass hull panel under wave-induced bending:
- The **outer skin stretches** (tensile strain, positive)
- The panel **contracts sideways** — its width reduces (transverse strain, negative)
- The ratio between these is the **effective Poisson's ratio of the laminate**

A **high Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.5)** means the material maintains its volume when deformed — like a **honeycomb sandwich core** or rubber. A **low Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.05)** means the material barely contracts sideways — like **cork** or some **carbon fiber composites**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Some modern auxetic composites (used in blast-resistant boat bulkheads) exhibit **negative Poisson's ratio** — they *expand* laterally when stretched, offering superior energy absorption and indentation resistance.

## 1.3 Standard Values for Boat-Building Materials

| Material | Typical ν | Engineering Implication |
|----------|----------|-------------------------|
| Steel (marine grade) | 0.30 | Predictable hull deformation |
| Marine Aluminum (5083) | 0.33 | Common in fast patrol boats |
| GFRP (woven roving) | 0.25 – 0.32 | Depends on fiber architecture |
| CFRP (unidirectional) | 0.20 – 0.30 | Highly anisotropic |
| Marine plywood | 0.10 – 0.20 | Low transverse coupling |
| Foam core (PVC, SAN) | 0.30 – 0.40 | Near-incompressible |
| Cork | 0.00 | Negligible lateral strain |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Design Warning:** Using an isotropic value (single ν) for a **woven composite** can lead to **20–40% error** in predicting hull deflection and buckling loads. Always use the **full orthotropic engineering constants** in FEA for boat hull analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Poisson's ratio emerges directly from the **Generalized Hooke's Law** for linear elastic materials. For a 3D stress state in an **orthotropic composite** (the most common case in boat building):

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= \frac{1}{E_1}\sigma_1 - \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\sigma_2 - \frac{\nu_{31}}{E_3}\sigma_3 \\
\varepsilon_2 &= -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_1 + \frac{1}{E_2}\sigma_2 - \frac{\nu_{32}}{E_3}\sigma_3 \\
\varepsilon_3 &= -\frac{\nu_{13}}{E_1}\sigma_1 - \frac{\nu_{23}}{E_2}\sigma_2 + \frac{1}{E_3}\sigma_3
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.1.1 Physical Meaning Decoded
1. **Sign Convention**: Tensile stress is positive, compressive is negative.
2. **Symmetry of Compliance Matrix**: The off-diagonal terms are symmetric, leading to the **Maxwell-Betti Reciprocal Theorem**:
$$\frac{\nu_{ij}}{E_i} = \frac{\nu_{ji}}{E_j}$$
3. **Conservation of Energy**: This reciprocity is enforced by thermodynamic constraints — the strain energy must be path-independent.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Reciprocity Relation

Consider applying stress $\sigma_1$ alone (all other stresses zero). The transverse strain is:
$$\varepsilon_2^{(1)} = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_1$$

Now apply $\sigma_2$ alone. The longitudinal strain is:
$$\varepsilon_1^{(2)} = -\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\sigma_2$$

By **Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem** (equal complementary strain energy):
$$\sigma_1 \cdot \varepsilon_1^{(2)} = \sigma_2 \cdot \varepsilon_2^{(1)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = \frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}$$

This is the **fundamental orthotropic relation** used universally in composite boat design.

## 2.3 Bounds on Poisson's Ratio

For **isotropic, thermodynamically stable** materials, requiring non-negative strain energy imposes strict limits:

$$-1 \lt \nu \lt 0.5$$

- **Lower bound ($\nu > -1$)**: Prevents negative shear modulus (auxetic materials approach but rarely reach $-1$).
- **Upper bound ($\nu < 0.5$)**: Corresponds to **incompressibility** ($\nu = 0.5$ gives infinite bulk modulus $K$).
- Most **engineering metals and composites** fall within $0.20 \leq \nu \leq 0.40$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Limit:** Cork has $\nu \approx 0$ (perfectly decoupled response). Rubber approaches $\nu \approx 0.5$ (volume conservation). Negative-ν materials are **engineered auxetics** with re-entrant unit cells.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these are the equations that appear in 80% of KTU board exam questions on elastic constants.**

| # | Equation | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | ν, ε | Definition |
| 2 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | E, G, ν | Isotropic stiffness link |
| 3 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | K, E, ν | Hydrostatic bulk modulus |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | λ, E, ν | Lamé's first constant |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}$ | ν, E | **Orthotropic reciprocity** |
| 6 | $\Delta V / V_0 = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3$ | ΔV, ε | Volumetric strain (3D) |
| 7 | $\Delta V / V_0 = (1-2\nu)\dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | ΔV, ν, σ, E | Volumetric strain (uniaxial) |
| 8 | $G_{12} = \dfrac{E_1 E_2}{E_1(1+\nu_{23}) + E_2(1+\nu_{13})}$ | E, ν, G | Composite G estimate |
| 9 | $e = \dfrac{1-2\nu}{E}$ | e, ν, E | Hydrostatic compliance |
| 10 | $\nu_{eff}^{laminate} = f(V_f, \nu_f, \nu_m, \theta)$ | ν, V_f, θ | Rule of mixtures for laminate |

> [!TIP]
> **Units Reminder:** All strains are dimensionless. Poisson's ratio is dimensionless. $E$, $G$, $K$ have units of **GPa or MPa**. The ratio $E/G$ is dimensionless and equals $2(1+\nu)$ — a useful sanity check during numerical work.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Boat Design

1. **Hull Plate Buckling**: The critical buckling stress depends on $\nu_{12}$ and $\nu_{21}$. A 10% error in ν can shift predicted buckling load by 15–25%.
2. **Resin Microcracking**: Differential Poisson's ratios between fiber and matrix generate **interfacial shear stresses** that cause resin cracking in marine GRP.
3. **Joint Design**: Hull-to-keel joints experience **Poisson expansion mismatch**, creating stress concentrations that must be designed out using tapered plies.
4. **Sandwich Core Selection**: Foam core ν affects face-sheet wrinkling — a low-ν core reduces wrinkling under in-plane loads.
5. **FEA Convergence**: Correctly specifying orthotropic ν's in ANSYS/Abaqus is essential for accurate **hull deflection predictions** in calm water and slamming conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ from Pure Shear

**Objective:** Show that for an isotropic material, Young's modulus $E$, shear modulus $G$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ are coupled by $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.

**Step 1 — Apply pure shear stress $\tau$ to a square element:**

The state of stress is $\sigma_x = \tau$, $\sigma_y = -\tau$, $\sigma_z = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute principal stresses:**

The Mohr's circle for pure shear gives principal stresses:
$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law for principal strains:**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau + 0)\right] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu\tau\right] = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Total shear strain from principal strains:**

For pure shear, engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ equals the difference in principal strains:
$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Use shear stress-strain relation $\tau = G\gamma$:**

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 = \frac{2G(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This equation is the cornerstone of isotropic elasticity — knowing any two of $(E, G, \nu)$ determines the third. For boat designers, it means measuring $E$ and $G$ experimentally gives $\nu$ by $\nu = E/(2G) - 1$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bulk Modulus $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$

**Step 1 — Apply hydrostatic pressure $p$:**

Stress state: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.

**Step 2 — Compute the volumetric strain:**

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$. Using generalized Hooke's law:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

Volumetric strain (sum of three normal strains):
$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Apply definition of bulk modulus:**

$$K = -\frac{p}{\Delta V / V_0} = -\frac{p}{\dfrac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As $\nu \to 0.5$, $K \to \infty$ — the material becomes incompressible.** This is why rubber and many marine sealants resist volume change under hydrostatic pressure.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Orthotropic Reciprocity $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$

**Step 1 — Apply $\sigma_1$ alone (uniaxial stress):**

From Hooke's law for orthotropic material:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_1, \quad \varepsilon_3 = -\frac{\nu_{13}}{E_1}\sigma_1$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\sigma_2$ alone:**

$$\varepsilon_1 = -\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\sigma_2, \quad \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2}, \quad \varepsilon_3 = -\frac{\nu_{23}}{E_2}\sigma_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Maxwell-Betti Reciprocal Theorem:**

For a linearly elastic material, the work done by force system 1 acting through displacements of system 2 equals vice versa:
$$\sigma_1 \cdot \varepsilon_1^{(2)} = \sigma_2 \cdot \varepsilon_2^{(1)}$$

Substituting:
$$\sigma_1 \cdot \left(-\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\sigma_2\right) = \sigma_2 \cdot \left(-\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_1\right)$$

Dividing both sides by $-\sigma_1 \sigma_2$:
$$\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = \frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Aluminum Hull Plate

**Problem:** A 1.5 m long × 0.8 m wide × 6 mm thick marine aluminum plate (E = 70 GPa, ν = 0.33) experiences a longitudinal tensile stress of 100 MPa. Calculate:
- (i) Longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_x$
- (ii) Transverse strain $\varepsilon_y$
- (iii) Change in length and width
- (iv) Volumetric strain

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Longitudinal strain:**
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{70 \times 10^9} = 1.4286 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Transverse strain:**
$$\varepsilon_y = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -0.33 \times 1.4286 \times 10^{-3} = -4.7143 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Change in dimensions:**
$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_x \cdot L = 1.4286 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 \text{ mm} = 2.143 \text{ mm (elongation)}$$

$$\Delta W = \varepsilon_y \cdot W = -4.7143 \times 10^{-4} \times 800 \text{ mm} = -0.377 \text{ mm (contraction)}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric strain (3D):**

For thin plate, $\varepsilon_z \approx 0$ (plane stress), so:
$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = (1.4286 - 0.4714 + 0) \times 10^{-3} = 9.572 \times 10^{-4}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check using formula:** $\Delta V/V_0 = (1-2\nu)\sigma/E = (1 - 0.66)(100 \times 10^6)/(70 \times 10^9) = 4.857 \times 10^{-4}$
>
> **Note the discrepancy** — the first method assumed plane stress with $\varepsilon_z = 0$, while the second formula accounts for full 3D state where $\varepsilon_z = -\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/E = -\nu \sigma_x/E$. The 3D formula is more accurate; the plane-stress simplification overestimates by neglecting through-thickness strain.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Unidirectional Carbon Fiber Composite

**Problem:** A unidirectional CFRP boat keel plate has $E_1 = 135$ GPa, $E_2 = 10$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$. Find the minor Poisson's ratio $\nu_{21}$ and the longitudinal strain under a longitudinal stress of 200 MPa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply reciprocity relation:**
$$\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = \frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.30 \times \frac{10}{135} = 0.0222$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal strain:**
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{135 \times 10^9} = 1.4815 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Transverse strain:**
$$\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_1 = -0.30 \times 1.4815 \times 10^{-3} = -4.444 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — If 1 m × 0.5 m panel, change in dimensions:**

$\Delta L_1 = 1.4815 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 1.48$ mm
$\Delta L_2 = -4.444 \times 10^{-4} \times 500 = -0.222$ mm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** The minor Poisson's ratio ($\nu_{21} = 0.022$) is **14 times smaller** than $\nu_{12}$. This is why unidirectional composites are **not interchangeable** with isotropic materials — the directional stiffness creates strong coupling asymmetry that must be modeled in FEA.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Shear Modulus from Elastic Constants

**Problem:** A marine-grade GFRP has E = 25 GPa and ν = 0.30. Calculate the shear modulus G and bulk modulus K.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Shear modulus:**
$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{25}{2(1.30)} = \frac{25}{2.60} = 9.615 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2 — Bulk modulus:**
$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{25}{3(0.40)} = \frac{25}{1.20} = 20.833 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For a stable isotropic material, the order should be $G < E < K$. Here: $9.6 < 25 < 20.8$... wait, $K < E$ violates the expected order. This indicates ν is approaching the upper bound (0.5) and the material is **near-incompressible** — typical of some polymer resins.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Load to Boat Hull Deformation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how Poisson's ratio propagates through the engineering design chain for a composite boat hull:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Hull Load] --> B[Hydrostatic Pressure]
    A --> C[Wave Slamming Force]
    A --> D[Mooring Load]
    B --> E[Multiaxial Stress State]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Longitudinal Strain epsilon1]
    E --> G[Transverse Strain epsilon2]
    E --> H[Shear Strain gamma12]
    F --> I[Poisson Coupling Effect]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Material Type}
    J --> K[Isotropic Metal]
    J --> L[Orthotropic FRP]
    J --> M[Sandwich Composite]
    K --> N[Single nu Value]
    L --> O[Major nu12 and Minor nu21]
    L --> P[Reciprocity nu12 over E1 equals nu21 over E2]
    M --> Q[Effective Laminated nu]
    N --> R[Hull Plate Deflection]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Panel Buckling Check]
    R --> T[Joint Stress Analysis]
    R --> U[Resin Microcracking Risk]
    S --> V[Design Validation]
    T --> V
    U --> V
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the path from external loads (top) through the strain coupling to the material branch (isotropic / orthotropic / sandwich), then to the three critical design outputs (right side). The branching at "Material Type" is where most design errors occur.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Poisson's Ratio in Composite Boat Design Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Material Selection] --> B[Determine E1, E2, G12]
    B --> C[Measure or Estimate nu12]
    C --> D[Calculate nu21 using Reciprocity]
    D --> E[Define Laminate Layup]
    E --> F[Compute Effective Engineering Constants]
    F --> G[Input to FEA Model]
    G --> H[Apply Hydrostatic and Slamming Loads]
    H --> I[Extract Strain Field]
    I --> J[Check against Allowable Strains]
    J --> K{Design OK?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Proceed to Fabrication]
    K -->|No| M[Revise Layup or Material]
    M --> B
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Physical Meaning of Poisson's Ratio

A schematic representation of the strain components in a uniaxial tensile test (visualization block):

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INITIAL["Initial State: Unstressed Specimen"]
        A1["Length L0"]
        A2["Width W0"]
    end
    subgraph LOADED["Loaded State: Under Uniaxial Tension sigma"]
        B1["New Length L0 plus deltaL positive"]
        B2["New Width W0 minus deltaW negative"]
    end
    subgraph RESULT["Computed Poisson Ratio"]
        C1["Longitudinal Strain epsilon equals deltaL over L0"]
        C2["Transverse Strain epsilonT equals minus deltaW over W0"]
        C3["Poisson Ratio nu equals minus epsilonT over epsilon"]
    end
    INITIAL --> LOADED
    LOADED --> RESULT
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip:** When teaching this concept, draw a rectangular specimen (e.g., 100 mm × 50 mm) and show arrows: horizontal arrows pointing outward (tension) and vertical arrows pointing inward (contraction). Label the strains with signs to reinforce the sign convention.

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Isotropic vs Orthotropic Response

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Uniaxial Stress sigma1 Applied] --> B{Isotropic Material}
    A --> C{Orthotropic Material}
    B --> D[Single nu governs all transverse responses]
    D --> E[epsilon2 equals minus nu sigma1 over E]
    D --> F[epsilon3 equals minus nu sigma1 over E]
    C --> G[Two independent Poisson ratios nu12 and nu21]
    G --> H[epsilon2 equals minus nu12 sigma1 over E1]
    G --> I[epsilon3 depends on stacking sequence]
    E --> J[Symmetric lateral contraction]
    F --> J
    H --> K[Anisotropic lateral contraction]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Simple FEA Modeling]
    K --> M[Full Orthotropic FEA Required]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Define Poisson's ratio. Why is it a critical parameter in composite boat design?**

**Model Answer:**

Poisson's ratio (ν) is defined as the **negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain** in a material subjected to uniaxial tensile loading within the elastic limit:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

It is a **dimensionless elastic constant** characterizing the material's lateral contraction response.

In composite boat design, its importance stems from:
1. **Directional coupling** — composite hull plates have different ν along fiber and transverse directions, affecting plate stiffness under hydrostatic load.
2. **Buckling prediction** — panel buckling stress depends on $\nu_{12}$ and $\nu_{21}$; errors in ν propagate to **20–30% error** in critical buckling load.
3. **Joint stress** — Poisson mismatch at hull-deck-keel interfaces creates stress concentrations that initiate fatigue cracks.
4. **FEA accuracy** — incorrect orthotropic ν values give misleading hull deflection predictions under wave slamming.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to state **why** Poisson's ratio is critical in *composites* (not just isotropic metals). Always mention the **directional/anisotropic** nature for full marks.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**State and prove the relation between Young's modulus (E), shear modulus (G), and Poisson's ratio (ν) for an isotropic material.**

**Model Answer:**

The relationship is:

$$E = 2G(1+\nu)$$

**Proof:** Consider a square element under pure shear stress $\tau$. The principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$. Using Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

The engineering shear strain is:
$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Since $\tau = G\gamma$, we have $G = \tau/\gamma = E/[2(1+\nu)]$, giving:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently **forget the negative sign** in Hooke's law (using $+\nu\sigma_2$ instead of $-\nu\sigma_2$) or **mix up the principal stresses** in pure shear. Draw a Mohr's circle to be safe.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a) Define Poisson's ratio. With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the longitudinal and lateral strains developed in a cylindrical specimen under uniaxial tension. Hence derive the relation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Poisson's ratio is the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain under uniaxial loading:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$$

**Diagram (2 marks):** A cylindrical specimen of original length L₀ and diameter d₀ is shown. Under axial tensile force F, the new length becomes L₀ + ΔL (elongation) and new diameter becomes d₀ − Δd (contraction). The longitudinal strain is $\varepsilon_{long} = \Delta L / L_0$ and lateral strain is $\varepsilon_{lat} = \Delta d / d_0$.

**Derivation (3 marks):** As shown in Section 3.1, applying pure shear to a square element and using Hooke's law for principal stresses $\sigma_1 = \tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

The shear strain $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$. Since $\tau = G\gamma$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

---

**(b) A unidirectional glass fiber composite used in a boat's mast has $E_1 = 38$ GPa (fiber direction), $E_2 = 8.5$ GPa (transverse), and major Poisson's ratio $\nu_{12} = 0.30$. Calculate (i) the minor Poisson's ratio $\nu_{21}$, (ii) the shear modulus G using isotropic approximation, and (iii) the longitudinal and transverse strains under a longitudinal stress of 150 MPa.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Minor Poisson's ratio (2 marks):**

Using orthotropic reciprocity:
$$\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = \frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}$$

$$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.30 \times \frac{8.5}{38} = 0.30 \times 0.2237 = 0.0671$$

**Part (ii) — Shear modulus approximation (2 marks):**

For an isotropic-like estimate (recognizing this is approximate for orthotropic):
$$G_{12}^{iso} = \frac{E_1}{2(1+\nu_{12})} = \frac{38}{2(1.30)} = \frac{38}{2.60} = 14.615 \text{ GPa}$$

*(Note: For a true orthotropic composite, $G_{12}$ must be measured independently. The above gives a design estimate only.)*

**Part (iii) — Strains under 150 MPa (3 marks):**

Longitudinal strain:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} = \frac{150 \times 10^6}{38 \times 10^9} = 3.947 \times 10^{-3}$$

Transverse strain:
$$\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_1 = -0.30 \times 3.947 \times 10^{-3} = -1.184 \times 10^{-3}$$

(Equivalent form: $\varepsilon_2 = -\nu_{21}\sigma_2/E_2$ when $\sigma_2 = 0$ — both give same magnitude via reciprocity.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often use $\nu_{12} = \nu_{21}$ in composites. This is **wrong** for orthotropic materials. The reciprocity relation is mandatory. Also, $G_{12} \neq E_1/[2(1+\nu_{12})]$ for true orthotropic materials — state this as a known limitation.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a) Discuss the physical significance and bounds of Poisson's ratio for isotropic materials. Why does rubber approach ν = 0.5 while cork has ν ≈ 0?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Physical Significance (3 marks):**

Poisson's ratio measures the **lateral strain response** of a material to axial loading. It quantifies the **degree of volume preservation** during deformation. Physically:
- **High ν (close to 0.5)** → material preserves its volume during deformation (incompressible behavior)
- **Low ν (close to 0)** → lateral and longitudinal deformations are decoupled
- **Negative ν** → material expands laterally when stretched (auxetic behavior)

In boat design, ν determines how the hull **distorts** under hydrostatic pressure. A hull panel under wave loading contracts laterally, and this contraction (governed by ν) affects the curvature and stress distribution.

**Bounds (2 marks):**

For a thermodynamically stable isotropic material, the strain energy density must be positive semi-definite. This requires the **elastic constants** to satisfy positivity conditions, which translate to:

$$-1 \lt \nu \lt 0.5$$

- **Lower bound**: If $\nu < -1$, the shear modulus $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ becomes negative, violating thermodynamic stability.
- **Upper bound**: If $\nu = 0.5$, the bulk modulus $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ becomes infinite, meaning the material cannot change volume.

**Rubber vs Cork (2 marks):**

- **Rubber ($\nu \approx 0.5$)**: Rubber consists of long polymer chains with weak intermolecular bonds. When stretched, the chains uncoil, but the **weak bonds prevent significant volume change** — the material behaves nearly incompressibly. As $\nu \to 0.5$, $K \to \infty$, meaning infinite resistance to volumetric compression.

- **Cork ($\nu \approx 0$)**: Cork is composed of sealed, air-filled cells with hexagonal honeycomb-like structure. When compressed axially, the cell walls **buckle sideways** without transmitting lateral forces — the cellular architecture decouples the lateral response. This is why cork stoppers are easy to insert/remove from wine bottles.

---

**(b) A 2 m × 1.2 m × 8 mm thick marine aluminum plate ($E = 70$ GPa, $\nu = 0.33$) is used in a boat deck. Under service loading, it experiences a longitudinal tensile stress of 80 MPa. Calculate: (i) longitudinal strain, (ii) transverse strain, (iii) change in length and width, (iv) volumetric strain using the 3D formula, and (v) verify using $\Delta V/V_0 = (1-2\nu)\sigma/E$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Longitudinal strain (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{70 \times 10^9} = 1.1429 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Part (ii) — Transverse strain (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon_y = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -0.33 \times 1.1429 \times 10^{-3} = -3.7714 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Part (iii) — Change in dimensions (2 marks):**
$$\Delta L_x = \varepsilon_x \cdot L_x = 1.1429 \times 10^{-3} \times 2000 \text{ mm} = 2.286 \text{ mm (elongation)}$$

$$\Delta L_y = \varepsilon_y \cdot L_y = -3.7714 \times 10^{-4} \times 1200 \text{ mm} = -0.4526 \text{ mm (contraction)}$$

**Part (iv) — 3D Volumetric strain (1.5 marks):**

For thin plate under uniaxial stress, $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$:
$$\varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) = -\frac{0.33}{70 \times 10^9}(80 \times 10^6 + 0) = -3.7714 \times 10^{-4}$$

Volumetric strain:
$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = (1.1429 - 0.3771 - 0.3771) \times 10^{-3} = 3.886 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Part (v) — Verification (1.5 marks):**

Using the uniaxial volumetric strain formula:
$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \frac{(1-2\nu)\sigma}{E} = \frac{(1 - 0.66)(80 \times 10^6)}{70 \times 10^9} = \frac{0.34 \times 80}{70{,}000} = 3.886 \times 10^{-4}$$

✓ **Both methods match** (as they must), confirming the calculation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Common mistakes include:
> 1. **Confusing** plane stress ($\sigma_z = 0$, $\varepsilon_z \neq 0$) with plane strain ($\varepsilon_z = 0$, $\sigma_z \neq 0$). For thin hull plates, plane stress is correct.
> 2. **Forgetting** to include $\varepsilon_z$ in the 3D volumetric strain.
> 3. **Unit errors** — always convert E to MPa or GPa consistently with σ.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition**: $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{long}$ — negative sign ensures positive ν for conventional materials.

- ✅ **Isotropic Relations (Must Memorize)**:
  - $E = 2G(1+\nu)$
  - $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$
  - $\lambda = E\nu/[(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$

- ✅ **Bounds**: $-1 \lt \nu \lt 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials.

- ✅ **Orthotropic Reciprocity (Composite-Specific)**: $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$ — this is the **single most tested relation** for composite boat design.

- ✅ **Composite Anisotropy**: $\nu_{12} \neq \nu_{21}$ in general; $\nu_{12} \cdot E_2 = \nu_{21} \cdot E_1$ holds instead.

- ✅ **Engineering Values for Boat Materials**:
  - Steel: ν = 0.30
  - Marine Aluminum: ν = 0.33
  - GFRP (woven): ν = 0.25–0.32
  - Cork: ν ≈ 0
  - Rubber: ν ≈ 0.5

- ✅ **Design Implications**:
  - High ν → incompressibility, large lateral strains, buckling risk
  - Low ν → dimensionally stable, low coupling
  - Negative ν → auxetic behavior (specialty composites)

- ✅ **Common Exam Traps**:
  1. Confusing $G_{12}$ for orthotropic with $E_1/[2(1+\nu_{12})]$ — only an approximation
  2. Forgetting that plane stress ≠ plane strain
  3. Assuming $\nu_{12} = \nu_{21}$ in composites
  4. Using isotropic $\nu$ for FRP without justification

- ✅ **FEA Modeling Reminder**: For boat hulls in ANSYS/Abaqus, always specify the **full orthotropic engineering constants** ($E_1, E_2, E_3, \nu_{12}, \nu_{13}, \nu_{23}, G_{12}, G_{13}, G_{23}$) — not a single ν.

- ✅ **Practical Testing Standard**: ASTM D3039 (tension) and ASTM D5379 (shear) for measuring Poisson's ratio of composite coupons.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"E equals 2G(1+nu), K equals E over 3(1-2nu)"** — these two equations cover 70% of Part A questions on elastic constants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structural Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Structural Analysis in Composite Boat Design?

**Structural Analysis** of a composite boat is the systematic engineering process of determining the **stresses, strains, deformations, and stability** of fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) hull structures when subjected to operational and environmental loads. In the context of FRP boatbuilding, it specifically involves applying **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)**, **failure criteria** (Tsai-Wu, Tsai-Hill, Hashin), and **stability checks** (panel buckling) to predict whether a proposed laminate schedule will survive the seagoing loads mandated by classification societies such as **DNV-GL, Lloyd's Register, ABS, or ISO 12215-5**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PENST742 Module 3):**
> Structural analysis of FRP boat hulls encompasses the mathematical modelling of multi-layered anisotropic composite panels under combined in-plane loads, bending moments, transverse shear, and hydrostatic pressure, leading to the determination of the laminate's effective stiffness matrix $[A]$, $[B]$, and $[D]$ (the **ABD matrix**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plywood Sandwich" Intuition

Imagine a **plywood sheet** as a **sandwich** with many thin layers of wood glued together, where each layer's grain runs in a different direction. A composite boat hull behaves identically: it is built by stacking many thin plies of **fiberglass, carbon fiber, or aramid (Kevlar)** cloth, with each ply oriented at a specific angle (0°, ±45°, 90°). 

When ocean waves push against the hull:
- Some plies resist **stretching** (longitudinal fibers take the load).
- Other plies resist **shearing** (the ±45° plies, like a basket weave, hold the panel together).
- Still others resist **buckling** (outer plies stabilize the surface).

> A **monolayer (isotropic) steel plate** uses the same material in all directions, so a designer has only **one knob to turn** (thickness). A **composite laminate** has **dozens of knobs** (ply angle, ply thickness, stacking sequence, material selection). **Structural analysis is the calculator that tells the naval architect which combination of these knobs will keep the boat afloat without cracking or buckling.**

---

## 1.3 Why Structural Analysis is the Heart of Composite Boat Design

| Aspect | Importance in Boat Design |
|---|---|
| **Weight Optimization** | FRP boats are sold by speed and fuel economy — over-design wastes displacement. |
| **Safety & Classification** | Det Norske Veritas (DNV) and ISO 12215-5 require mathematical proof of structural adequacy. |
| **Cost Control** | Fiberglass cloth and resin are expensive; analysis prevents over-stacking. |
| **Failure Prevention** | Composite failure modes (delamination, matrix cracking, fiber breakage) are invisible until catastrophic. |
| **Stability** | Thin composite panels under water pressure can buckle; analysis predicts the critical load. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - **Young's Modulus of E-glass fiber:** $E_f \approx 72 \text{ GPa}$
> - **Young's Modulus of Vinylester resin (cured):** $E_m \approx 3.5 \text{ GPa}$
> - **Density of cured FRP laminate:** $\rho \approx 1500 \text{ to } 1900 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Poisson's ratio (typical for FRP):** $\nu \approx 0.30$
> - **Sea water density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

---

## 1.4 Roadmap of Topics Covered in This Module Note

The remainder of these notes follow a logical escalation from **single-ply behaviour** → **laminated plate theory** → **failure criteria** → **hull-girder analysis**, mirroring the way a naval architect actually works through a structural design problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Composite Structural Analysis

A **complete** structural analysis of a composite boat panel/hull requires the following five steps, in order. KTU exam questions almost always test at least three of these in a single 14-mark problem.

### **Pillar 1: Ply-Level Constitutive Law (The Hooke's Law for One Ply)**

A single unidirectional ply is **orthotropic** — it has different stiffnesses along the fiber direction (the **1-direction**) and transverse to it (the **2-direction**). The relationship between in-plane stress $\{\sigma\}$ and strain $\{\varepsilon\}$ is given by the **reduced stiffness matrix** $[Q]$:

$$\{ \sigma \} = [Q] \{ \varepsilon \}$$

For a ply with off-axis fiber angle $\theta$ (measured from the laminate x-axis to the fiber direction), the **transformed reduced stiffness** $[\bar{Q}]$ in the laminate coordinate system is computed using the **transformation equations** involving $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$, $m = \cos\theta$, $n = \sin\theta$:

$$\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11} m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{22} n^4$$

$$\bar{Q}_{12} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{12}(m^4 + n^4)$$

$$\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11} n^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{22} m^4$$

$$\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) m^3 n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66}) m n^3$$

$$\bar{Q}_{26} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) m n^3 + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66}) m^3 n$$

$$\bar{Q}_{66} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{66}(m^4 + n^4)$$

These six independent $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ values feed directly into the laminate stiffness.

---

### **Pillar 2: Classical Lamination Theory (CLT) — The ABD Matrix**

CLT assumes **plane sections remain plane and normal to the mid-surface after deformation** (the **Kirchhoff-Love hypothesis**, extended to plates). For a laminate of $N$ plies, the **force resultants** $\{N\}$ (N/m) and **moment resultants** $\{M\}$ (N·m/m) are related to the **mid-plane strains** $\{\varepsilon^0\}$ and **curvatures** $\{\kappa\}$ through the **ABD matrix**:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} N_x \\ N_y \\ N_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} & A_{16} \\ A_{12} & A_{22} & A_{26} \\ A_{16} & A_{26} & A_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0_x \\ \varepsilon^0_y \\ \gamma^0_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} \\ B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} \\ B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{Bmatrix} M_x \\ M_y \\ M_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} \\ B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} \\ B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0_x \\ \varepsilon^0_y \\ \gamma^0_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} D_{11} & D_{12} & D_{16} \\ D_{12} & D_{22} & D_{26} \\ D_{16} & D_{26} & D_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

In **compact 6×6 matrix form**:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Definitions of the matrix elements** (summing over all plies $k = 1 \to N$, with $z_{k-1}$ and $z_k$ being the lower and upper z-coordinates of ply $k$):

$$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}^{(k)}_{ij} (z_k - z_{k-1})$$

$$B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}^{(k)}_{ij} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$$

$$D_{ij} = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}^{(k)}_{ij} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$$

**Physical meaning of each sub-matrix:**
- $[A]$: **Extensional stiffness** — relates in-plane forces to in-plane strains.
- $[B]$: **Coupling stiffness** — couples extension to bending. **$B = 0$ for symmetric laminates.**
- $[D]$: **Bending stiffness** — relates moments to curvatures.

---

### **Pillar 3: Compliance Inversion — Finding Strains from Loads**

The ABD matrix is inverted to get the **abd matrix** (note the lowercase, divided into $a, b, d$ sub-matrices):

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b^T & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix}$$

Once the mid-plane strains and curvatures are known, the **strain in any ply $k$** at distance $z$ from the mid-plane is:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}_k = \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0_x \\ \varepsilon^0_y \\ \gamma^0_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} + z \begin{Bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

Finally, the **stresses in ply $k$** in the laminate coordinate system are:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}_k = [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}_k$$

These are then **rotated back to the material (1-2) coordinate system** to check against the allowable strength values $X_t, X_c, Y_t, Y_c, S$.

---

### **Pillar 4: Failure Criteria — The Strength Verdict**

After stresses in each ply are known, we must check whether the ply has **failed**. Four criteria are common:

**a) Maximum Stress Criterion (simplest):**

$$\sigma_1 < X_t \quad (\text{tension}), \quad \sigma_1 > -X_c \quad (\text{compression})$$

$$\sigma_2 < Y_t \quad (\text{tension}), \quad \sigma_2 > -Y_c \quad (\text{compression})$$

$$\vert \tau_{12} \vert < S \quad (\text{shear})$$

**b) Maximum Strain Criterion:**

$$\varepsilon_1 < \varepsilon_{1t}, \quad \varepsilon_1 > -\varepsilon_{1c}, \quad \varepsilon_2 < \varepsilon_{2t}, \quad \varepsilon_2 > -\varepsilon_{2c}, \quad \vert \gamma_{12} \vert < \gamma_{12u}$$

**c) Tsai-Hill Criterion (interactive, no separate shear term):**

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X}\right)^2 - \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X^2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 \leq 1$$

(where $X = X_t$ if $\sigma_1 > 0$, $X = X_c$ if $\sigma_1 < 0$; similarly for $Y$).

**d) Tsai-Wu Criterion (most general, KTU-favoured):**

$$F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11} \sigma_1^2 + F_{22} \sigma_2^2 + F_{66} \tau_{12}^2 + 2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \leq 1$$

The strength coefficients are:

$$F_1 = \frac{1}{X_t} - \frac{1}{X_c}, \quad F_2 = \frac{1}{Y_t} - \frac{1}{Y_c}, \quad F_{11} = \frac{1}{X_t X_c}, \quad F_{22} = \frac{1}{Y_t Y_c}, \quad F_{66} = \frac{1}{S^2}$$

The interaction term $F_{12}$ is found experimentally; a common engineering estimate is $F_{12} = -\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11} F_{22}}$.

---

### **Pillar 5: Panel Buckling under Lateral Pressure**

A flat or curved composite panel under transverse pressure (e.g., a hull bottom plate under hydrostatic load) can **buckle** elastically long before the material fails. The **critical buckling load** for an orthotropic simply-supported rectangular plate under uniform in-plane compression $N_x$ is:

$$N_{x,cr} = \frac{\pi^2}{b^2} \left[ D_{11} \left(\frac{m b}{a}\right)^2 + 2(D_{12} + 2D_{66}) + D_{22} \left(\frac{a}{m b}\right)^2 \right]$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the plate dimensions and $m$ is the number of half-waves in the buckling mode. For a panel under uniform lateral pressure $p$ (relevant to a hull), the **classical critical pressure** is:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{C \pi^2}{b^4} \left[ D_{11} \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^2 + 2(D_{12} + 2D_{66}) + D_{22} \left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^2 \right]$$

where $C$ is a boundary-condition coefficient (4.0 for simply-supported all edges, 10.0 for clamped all edges). The actual collapse pressure accounts for **initial imperfections** and is typically $p_{cr}/2$ in design.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most-tested set of equations in PENST742 Module 3. Memorize the symbols and the use-cases; the numerical substitutions are shown in the worked examples below.**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **When Used** |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Ply reduced stiffness $Q_{11}$ | $Q_{11} = \dfrac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Pa | Ply-level orthotropic stiffness |
| 2 | Ply reduced stiffness $Q_{22}$ | $Q_{22} = \dfrac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Pa | Ply-level orthotropic stiffness |
| 3 | Ply reduced stiffness $Q_{12}$ | $Q_{12} = \dfrac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \dfrac{\nu_{21} E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Pa | Coupling term |
| 4 | Ply reduced stiffness $Q_{66}$ | $Q_{66} = G_{12}$ | Pa | In-plane shear |
| 5 | A-matrix element | $A_{ij} = \sum \bar{Q}_{ij} (z_k - z_{k-1})$ | N/m | Extensional stiffness |
| 6 | B-matrix element | $B_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum \bar{Q}_{ij} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$ | N | Coupling stiffness |
| 7 | D-matrix element | $D_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{3} \sum \bar{Q}_{ij} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$ | N·m | Bending stiffness |
| 8 | Ply strain at height $z$ | $\{\varepsilon\}_k = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z \{\kappa\}$ | — | Recovering ply strains |
| 9 | Ply stress (laminate axes) | $\{\sigma\}_k = [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \{\varepsilon\}_k$ | Pa | Ply stress in $x, y$ |
| 10 | Tsai-Hill index | $\left(\tfrac{\sigma_1}{X}\right)^2 - \tfrac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X^2} + \left(\tfrac{\sigma_2}{Y}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2$ | — | $\leq 1$ means safe |
| 11 | Tsai-Wu index | $F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11} \sigma_1^2 + F_{22} \sigma_2^2 + F_{66} \tau_{12}^2 + 2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | — | $\leq 1$ means safe |
| 12 | Hull-girder section modulus | $Z = I / y_{max}$ | m³ | Bending stress $\sigma = M/Z$ |
| 13 | Critical panel pressure | $p_{cr} = C \pi^2 \sqrt{D_{11}D_{22}}/b^4$ (approx) | Pa | Buckling check |
| 14 | Wave-bending moment (hogging) | $M = C_w L^3 B \rho g \alpha$ | N·m | Rule-of-thumb envelope |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ABD matrix and the failure indices are not academic curiosities — they are the **exact equations encoded inside every modern naval-architecture FEA package** (ANSYS Composite PrePost, ABAQUS, MAESTRO, Composica, FEMAP). A classification society surveyor will sign a boat's drawing only when the **designer's CLT calculation shows every ply in every panel has a Tsai-Wu index below 1.0 under the most severe Rule wave load**. Thus, the ABD matrix is, in a very real sense, the **legal contract** between a boat builder and the certifying authority.
