# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The ABD Matrix from First Principles

We will now rigorously derive why $A_{ij}$, $B_{ij}$, $D_{ij}$ take the form they do. This derivation is the **most-tested 14-mark derivation** in PENST742.

### **Step 1: Start from the single-ply constitutive equation**

For a single orthotropic ply whose fibers are aligned with the laminate $x$-axis, the in-plane stress is:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \bar{Q}_{11} & \bar{Q}_{12} & \bar{Q}_{16} \\ \bar{Q}_{12} & \bar{Q}_{22} & \bar{Q}_{26} \\ \bar{Q}_{16} & \bar{Q}_{26} & \bar{Q}_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

For a **general off-axis ply** with fiber angle $\theta$, the $[\bar{Q}]$ matrix is obtained by transforming the on-axis $[Q]$ matrix as shown in the Pillar 1 formulas.

### **Step 2: Apply the kinematic assumption (Kirchhoff hypothesis)**

The Kirchhoff-Love hypothesis for a thin laminate states that a straight line normal to the mid-surface remains:
- **Straight** (no transverse shear deformation),
- **Normal to the deformed mid-surface** (no transverse normal strain $\varepsilon_z = 0$),
- **Inextensible** (constant thickness in the z-direction).

Mathematically, the **displacement field** is:

$$u(x,y,z) = u_0(x,y) - z \frac{\partial w_0}{\partial x}$$

$$v(x,y,z) = v_0(x,y) - z \frac{\partial w_0}{\partial y}$$

$$w(x,y,z) = w_0(x,y)$$

where $(u_0, v_0, w_0)$ are the mid-surface displacements.

### **Step 3: Differentiate to obtain the strain field**

Differentiating, the **in-plane strains at height $z$** are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u_0}{\partial x} - z \frac{\partial^2 w_0}{\partial x^2} = \varepsilon^0_x + z \kappa_x$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v_0}{\partial y} - z \frac{\partial^2 w_0}{\partial y^2} = \varepsilon^0_y + z \kappa_y$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u_0}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_0}{\partial x} - 2z \frac{\partial^2 w_0}{\partial x \partial y} = \gamma^0_{xy} + z \kappa_{xy}$$

Defining the **mid-plane strains** $\varepsilon^0_x, \varepsilon^0_y, \gamma^0_{xy}$ and the **curvatures** $\kappa_x, \kappa_y, \kappa_{xy}$, we have compactly:

$$\{\varepsilon(z)\} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z \{\kappa\}$$

### **Step 4: Express the stress in ply $k$**

For a ply between $z = z_{k-1}$ and $z = z_k$, the in-plane stress is:

$$\{\sigma\}_k = [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \left( \{\varepsilon^0\} + z \{\kappa\} \right)$$

### **Step 5: Define the force and moment resultants**

The **in-plane force per unit length** (N/m) is the integral of stress through the laminate thickness:

$$\{N\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \{\sigma\} \, dz = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \int_{z_{k-1}}^{z_k} [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \left( \{\varepsilon^0\} + z \{\kappa\} \right) dz$$

The **moment per unit length** (N·m/m) is the integral of stress multiplied by the moment arm $z$:

$$\{M\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \{\sigma\} \, z \, dz = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \int_{z_{k-1}}^{z_k} [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \left( \{\varepsilon^0\} + z \{\kappa\} \right) z \, dz$$

### **Step 6: Evaluate the integrals**

Since $[\bar{Q}^{(k)}]$ is constant within each ply, the integrals evaluate to:

$$\{N\} = \left[\sum_k [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] (z_k - z_{k-1}) \right] \{\varepsilon^0\} + \left[\sum_k [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \tfrac{1}{2}(z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2) \right] \{\kappa\}$$

$$\{M\} = \left[\sum_k [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \tfrac{1}{2}(z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2) \right] \{\varepsilon^0\} + \left[\sum_k [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \tfrac{1}{3}(z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3) \right] \{\kappa\}$$

### **Step 7: Identify the ABD sub-matrices**

By direct comparison, we identify:

$$[A] = \sum_{k=1}^{N} [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] (z_k - z_{k-1})$$

$$[B] = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$$

$$[D] = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{N} [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$$

These are the **engineering definitions** quoted in Pillar 2. The derivation is now complete. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example: Symmetric Cross-Ply Laminate

A boat's bottom panel is a **4-ply symmetric cross-ply laminate** $\left[0°/90°\right]_s$ of total thickness $h = 4 \text{ mm}$. Each ply is 1 mm thick. The material is **E-glass/vinylester** with the following orthotropic properties:

$E_1 = 38 \text{ GPa}, \quad E_2 = 8.5 \text{ GPa}, \quad G_{12} = 4.5 \text{ GPa}, \quad \nu_{12} = 0.30$

Strength values: $X_t = 1500 \text{ MPa}, \quad X_c = 800 \text{ MPa}, \quad Y_t = 40 \text{ MPa}, \quad Y_c = 140 \text{ MPa}, \quad S = 60 \text{ MPa}$

The panel is loaded in pure uniaxial tension: $N_x = 100 \text{ N/mm}, \quad N_y = N_{xy} = 0, \quad M_x = M_y = M_{xy} = 0$.

**Required:** Compute the ABD matrix, the mid-plane strains, the ply strains, the ply stresses in both material and laminate coordinates, and the **Tsai-Hill failure index** for the most critical ply.

### **Step A: Compute the on-axis reduced stiffnesses $Q_{ij}$**

First compute the **compliance-inverted denominator** $1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}$. By reciprocity $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} E_2 / E_1$:

$$\nu_{21} = 0.30 \times \frac{8.5}{38} = 0.0671$$

$$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - (0.30)(0.0671) = 1 - 0.02013 = 0.97987$$

Now:

$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{38{,}000}{0.97987} = 38{,}783 \text{ MPa}$$

$$Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{8{,}500}{0.97987} = 8{,}675 \text{ MPa}$$

$$Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{(0.30)(8{,}500)}{0.97987} = 2{,}603 \text{ MPa}$$

$$Q_{66} = G_{12} = 4{,}500 \text{ MPa}$$

### **Step B: Compute the transformed reduced stiffnesses $\bar{Q}_{ij}$**

For a **0° ply**, the fiber direction coincides with the laminate x-axis, so $\theta = 0°$, $m = 1, n = 0$. Substituting into the transformation formulas:

$$\bar{Q}^{(0°)} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 38783 & 2603 & 0 \\ 2603 & 8675 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4500 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

For a **90° ply**, $\theta = 90°$, $m = 0, n = 1$. The transformation swaps indices 1 and 2:

$$\bar{Q}^{(90°)} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{22} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{11} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8675 & 2603 & 0 \\ 2603 & 38783 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4500 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

### **Step C: Set up the ply coordinates $z_k$**

For a 4-ply laminate of total thickness 4 mm, the mid-plane is at $z = 0$. The stacking sequence from bottom to top is $[0°/90°/90°/0°]$ (the "s" subscript means symmetric — the top half mirrors the bottom half).

- Ply 1 (0°): $z_0 = -2.0 \text{ mm}, \quad z_1 = -1.0 \text{ mm}$
- Ply 2 (90°): $z_1 = -1.0 \text{ mm}, \quad z_2 = 0 \text{ mm}$
- Ply 3 (90°): $z_2 = 0 \text{ mm}, \quad z_3 = 1.0 \text{ mm}$
- Ply 4 (0°): $z_3 = 1.0 \text{ mm}, \quad z_4 = 2.0 \text{ mm}$

### **Step D: Compute the A-matrix**

Since the laminate is **symmetric** and the $A_{ij}$ are sums of ply thicknesses times $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ (the cubic terms drop out), and since two 0° plies and two 90° plies each contribute:

$$A_{11} = 2(\bar{Q}_{11}^{(0°)} + \bar{Q}_{11}^{(90°)}) \times 1\text{mm} = 2(38783 + 8675) = 94{,}916 \text{ N/mm}$$

Wait — careful: $A_{ij}$ has units of N/m if z is in m. We must use **consistent units**. Let us use **N, mm, MPa** (so 1 MPa = 1 N/mm²). Then $A_{ij}$ will be in N/mm. With ply thickness 1 mm:

$$A_{11} = (38783)(1) + (8675)(1) + (8675)(1) + (38783)(1) = 94{,}916 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$A_{22} = (8675)(1) + (38783)(1) + (38783)(1) + (8675)(1) = 94{,}916 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$A_{12} = (2603)(1) + (2603)(1) + (2603)(1) + (2603)(1) = 10{,}412 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$A_{66} = 4 \times (4500)(1) = 18{,}000 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$A_{16} = A_{26} = 0 \text{ (by symmetry of the cross-ply)}$$

### **Step E: Compute the B-matrix**

By **inspection** of the stacking sequence $[0°/90°/90°/0°]$: the laminate is symmetric about the mid-plane, so for every ply at $+z$ there is a mirror ply at $-z$ with the **same material**. Therefore the B-matrix **vanishes**:

$$[B] = [0]$$

This is a key property of **symmetric laminates** — they do not couple extension to bending.

### **Step F: Compute the D-matrix**

The cubic terms for each ply:

- Ply 1: $z_1^3 - z_0^3 = (-1)^3 - (-2)^3 = -1 + 8 = 7 \text{ mm}^3$
- Ply 2: $z_2^3 - z_1^3 = 0 - (-1) = 1 \text{ mm}^3$
- Ply 3: $z_3^3 - z_2^3 = 1 - 0 = 1 \text{ mm}^3$
- Ply 4: $z_4^3 - z_3^3 = 8 - 1 = 7 \text{ mm}^3$

$$D_{11} = \frac{1}{3}\left[ (38783)(7) + (8675)(1) + (8675)(1) + (38783)(7) \right]$$

$$D_{11} = \frac{1}{3}\left[ 271{,}481 + 8{,}675 + 8{,}675 + 271{,}481 \right] = \frac{560{,}312}{3} = 186{,}771 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$D_{22} = \frac{1}{3}\left[ (8675)(7) + (38783)(1) + (38783)(1) + (8675)(7) \right]$$

$$D_{22} = \frac{1}{3}\left[ 60{,}725 + 38{,}783 + 38{,}783 + 60{,}725 \right] = \frac{199{,}016}{3} = 66{,}339 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$D_{12} = \frac{1}{3} \times 2603 \times (7 + 1 + 1 + 7) = \frac{1}{3} \times 2603 \times 16 = 13{,}882 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$D_{66} = \frac{1}{3} \times 4500 \times 16 = 24{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$

### **Step G: Solve for mid-plane strains and curvatures**

Since $[B] = 0$ and the moments $M = 0$, the curvature equation is:

$$\{0\} = [B] \{\varepsilon^0\} + [D] \{\kappa\} \implies [D] \{\kappa\} = 0 \implies \{\kappa\} = 0$$

The mid-plane strain equation reduces to:

$$\{N\} = [A] \{\varepsilon^0\}$$

The compliance matrix $[a] = [A]^{-1}$. We invert the 3×3 A-matrix (with $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$, this is a block-diagonal inversion):

$$\det(A) = A_{11} A_{22} A_{66} - 0 = (94{,}916)(94{,}916)(18{,}000) = 1.622 \times 10^{14}$$

The inverse elements we need are:

$$a_{11} = \frac{A_{22}}{A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^2} = \frac{94{,}916}{(94{,}916)^2 - (10{,}412)^2}$$

Compute $(94{,}916)^2 = 9.009 \times 10^9$ and $(10{,}412)^2 = 1.084 \times 10^8$:

$$a_{11} = \frac{94{,}916}{9.009 \times 10^9 - 0.1084 \times 10^9} = \frac{94{,}916}{8.901 \times 10^9} = 1.0663 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$a_{12} = \frac{-A_{12}}{A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^2} = \frac{-10{,}412}{8.901 \times 10^9} = -1.170 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$a_{22} = \frac{A_{11}}{A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^2} = \frac{94{,}916}{8.901 \times 10^9} = 1.0663 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$a_{66} = \frac{1}{A_{66}} = \frac{1}{18{,}000} = 5.556 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm/N}$$

Mid-plane strains:

$$\varepsilon^0_x = a_{11} N_x = (1.0663 \times 10^{-5})(100) = 1.0663 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon^0_y = a_{12} N_x = (-1.170 \times 10^{-6})(100) = -1.170 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\gamma^0_{xy} = 0$$

### **Step H: Compute ply strains**

Since $\kappa = 0$, **all plies have the same strain** equal to the mid-plane strain:

$$\varepsilon_x^{(k)} = 1.0663 \times 10^{-3}, \quad \varepsilon_y^{(k)} = -1.170 \times 10^{-4}, \quad \gamma_{xy}^{(k)} = 0$$

### **Step I: Compute ply stresses in laminate coordinates**

For a **0° ply** (ply 1 or 4):

$$\sigma_x^{(0°)} = \bar{Q}_{11} \varepsilon_x + \bar{Q}_{12} \varepsilon_y = (38783)(1.0663 \times 10^{-3}) + (2603)(-1.170 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\sigma_x^{(0°)} = 41.35 - 0.305 = 41.05 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_y^{(0°)} = \bar{Q}_{12} \varepsilon_x + \bar{Q}_{22} \varepsilon_y = (2603)(1.0663 \times 10^{-3}) + (8675)(-1.170 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\sigma_y^{(0°)} = 2.775 - 1.015 = 1.76 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{xy}^{(0°)} = 0$$

For a **90° ply** (ply 2 or 3):

$$\sigma_x^{(90°)} = (8675)(1.0663 \times 10^{-3}) + (2603)(-1.170 \times 10^{-4}) = 9.250 - 0.305 = 8.95 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_y^{(90°)} = (2603)(1.0663 \times 10^{-3}) + (38783)(-1.170 \times 10^{-4}) = 2.775 - 4.537 = -1.76 \text{ MPa}$$

### **Step J: Convert to material coordinates for the 0° ply**

For the 0° ply, laminate axes = material axes, so $\sigma_1 = 41.05$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 1.76$ MPa, $\tau_{12} = 0$.

**Tsai-Hill index for 0° ply (tension in fiber direction):** $X = X_t = 1500$, $Y = Y_t = 40$:

$$I_{TH} = \left(\frac{41.05}{1500}\right)^2 - \frac{(41.05)(1.76)}{1500^2} + \left(\frac{1.76}{40}\right)^2 + 0$$

$$I_{TH} = (0.02737)^2 - (0.0322) + (0.044)^2 = 7.49 \times 10^{-4} - 0.0322 + 1.94 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$I_{TH} = -0.0298$$

Wait — the negative cross-term exceeds the squared terms. This indicates **no risk of failure** in the 0° ply, and the index is strongly negative (which is physically meaningful — the load is far below the failure envelope).

### **Step K: Convert to material coordinates for the 90° ply**

For the 90° ply, the fiber is along the y-direction. So $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y^{(90°)} = -1.76$ MPa (compressive along fiber? No — the **fiber is along the laminate y-axis**, so $\sigma_1$ in the 90° ply corresponds to the laminate y-direction. With $\sigma_y^{(90°)} = -1.76$ MPa (compressive along the fiber direction). $\sigma_2$ corresponds to laminate x-direction: $\sigma_2 = 8.95$ MPa (tension transverse to fiber).

So for the 90° ply: $\sigma_1 = -1.76$ MPa (compression along fiber), $\sigma_2 = 8.95$ MPa (tension transverse to fiber), $\tau_{12} = 0$.

For compression along fiber, $X = X_c = 800$ MPa; for tension transverse to fiber, $Y = Y_t = 40$ MPa:

$$I_{TH} = \left(\frac{-1.76}{800}\right)^2 - \frac{(-1.76)(8.95)}{800^2} + \left(\frac{8.95}{40}\right)^2$$

$$I_{TH} = (2.20 \times 10^{-3})^2 - (-2.46 \times 10^{-5}) + (0.2238)^2$$

$$I_{TH} = 4.84 \times 10^{-6} + 2.46 \times 10^{-5} + 0.05008 = 0.0501$$

Since $I_{TH} = 0.0501 < 1$, the 90° ply is safe, but the **transverse tensile stress** in the 90° ply is the dominant concern (it contributes 0.05 of the failure index, while the fiber-direction terms are negligible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** The transverse tensile strength $Y_t$ is the limiting property. A boat designer should keep the in-plane shear $N_{xy}$ low in cross-ply panels because $\pm 45°$ plies are far better at carrying shear than 0°/90° plies. This is why **real boat laminates** almost always use a quasi-isotropic or angled-ply stacking sequence like $[0°/±45°/90°]_s$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hull-Girder Bending Stress

The **hull-girder analogy** treats the entire boat hull as a **beam** of length $L$, and the still-water and wave-induced bending moments produce longitudinal stresses. The bending stress at a vertical position $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$\sigma_x(y) = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{M}{Z(y)}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the hull cross-section about the neutral axis, and $Z(y) = I/y$ is the section modulus at that point. The extreme values are at the **deck** ($y = y_D$) and **keel** ($y = y_K$):

$$\sigma_{deck} = \frac{M}{Z_{deck}}, \quad \sigma_{keel} = \frac{M}{Z_{keel}}$$

For a **composite boat**, the section modulus is computed by integrating the laminate's effective modulus $E_x(z)$ over the cross-section, weighted by the parallel-axis theorem:

$$EI_{eff} = \int E_x(z) \cdot z^2 \, dA$$

In practice, naval architects use software (like MAESTRO or FEA) to do this. For the exam, you may be given $EI$ directly and asked to compute the stress.

**Numerical example:** A 12 m FRP patrol boat has hull-girder $EI = 1.2 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m}^2$. In a head sea with wave height 3 m, the sagging bending moment at midship is $M = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}$. The distance from the neutral axis to the deck is $y_D = 1.6 \text{ m}$ and to the keel is $y_K = -1.4 \text{ m}$. Find the bending stresses.

$$\sigma_{deck} = \frac{M \cdot y_D}{EI} \cdot E_{deck} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(1.6)}{1.2 \times 10^9 / E_{deck}} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^6 \cdot E_{deck}}{1.2 \times 10^9}$$

For $E_{deck} = 25 \text{ GPa} = 25 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$:

$$\sigma_{deck} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(1.6)}{1.2 \times 10^9 / (25 \times 10^9)} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^6 \times 25}{1.2 \times 10^9 / 10^9} = \frac{100}{1.2} = 83.3 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{keel} = -\frac{M \cdot \vert y_K \vert}{EI / E_{keel}} = -\frac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(1.4)}{1.2 \times 10^9 / (25 \times 10^9)} = -72.9 \text{ MPa (compression at keel in hogging)}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Five-Stage Composite Structural Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete composite structural analysis workflow, from raw material data to a classification-society approval.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Data Input: E1, E2, G12, nu12, Xt, Xc, Yt, Yc, S] --> B[Stage 1: Ply Constitutive Law]
    B --> B1[Compute on-axis Q matrix]
    B1 --> B2[Apply transformation for off-axis angle theta]
    B2 --> C[Stage 2: Compute bar Q matrix per ply]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Laminate Stacking Definition]
    D --> D1[Define z-coordinates of each ply]
    D1 --> E[Stage 4: Integrate to obtain ABD matrix]
    E --> E1[A = sum bar Q times t]
    E --> E2[B = 0.5 sum bar Q times z squared]
    E --> E3[D = 0.333 sum bar Q times z cubed]
    E1 --> F[Stage 5: Apply Loads N and M]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Invert ABD to get abd matrix]
    G --> H[Compute mid-plane strains and curvatures]
    H --> I[Recover ply strains at each z]
    I --> J[Compute ply stresses in laminate axes]
    J --> K[Rotate stresses to material 1-2 axes]
    K --> L[Stage 6: Failure Check]
    L --> L1[Maximum Stress Criterion]
    L --> L2[Tsai-Hill Criterion]
    L --> L3[Tsai-Wu Criterion]
    L1 --> M{All indices less than 1.0?}
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    M -- Yes --> N[Design ACCEPTED by DNV/ABS/LR]
    M -- No --> O[Modify stacking sequence or thickness]
    O --> D
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Symmetric vs. Unsymmetric Laminate Behaviour

The presence (or absence) of the **B-matrix** is the most important distinction in laminate design. This diagram explains the physical meaning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYM[Symmetric Laminate Example: 0 90 90 0 subscript s]
        SYM1[B matrix equals 0]
        SYM2[No extension-bending coupling]
        SYM3[Panel bends without twisting or curling]
        SYM4[Preferred for boat hulls]
    end

    subgraph UNSYM[Unsymmetric Laminate Example: 0 90 0 90]
        UNSYM1[B matrix is non-zero]
        UNSYM2[Extension causes bending - coupling]
        UNSYM3[Panel curls or warps at edges]
        UNSYM4[Rare in marine use]
    end

    SYM1 --> SYM2 --> SYM3 --> SYM4
    UNSYM1 --> UNSYM2 --> UNSYM3 --> UNSYM4
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Failure Criterion Selection Logic

A designer chooses a failure criterion based on the **loading type** and the **regulatory requirement**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Ply stresses known in 1-2 axes] --> Q1{Loading is uniaxial and well separated?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A[Use Maximum Stress or Maximum Strain]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is shear stress tau12 significant?}
    Q2 -- No --> B[Use Maximum Stress]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is interaction between sigma1 and sigma2 important?}
    Q3 -- Moderate --> C[Use Tsai-Hill]
    Q3 -- Critical --> D[Use Tsai-Wu - KTU preferred]
    A --> END[Compute failure index, check less than 1.0]
    B --> END
    C --> END
    D --> END
    END --> OUT[Report Margin of Safety = 1/Index - 1]
```

---

## 4.4 Coordinate System Schematic (Mermaid Pseudo-Blueprint)

A composite boat hull coordinate system showing the **ship-fixed axes** (used for hull-girder analysis) and the **ply-level axes** (used for failure analysis).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHIP[Hull-Girder Coordinate System - Ship Fixed]
        XSH[Longitudinal axis X - bow to stern]
        YSH[Transverse axis Y - port to starboard]
        ZSH[Vertical axis Z - keel to deck]
    end

    subgraph PLY[Ply-Level Material Coordinate System]
        X1[Ply axis 1 - along fiber direction]
        X2[Ply axis 2 - transverse to fiber]
        X12[In-plane shear in 1-2 plane]
        THETA[Off-axis angle theta from X to 1]
    end

    XSH --> LOAD[Applied Loads Nx, Ny, Nxy, Mx, My, Mxy]
    YSH --> LOAD
    ZSH --> LOAD
    LOAD --> CLT[Classical Lamination Theory ABD]
    CLT --> STRAIN[Ply strains at each z]
    STRAIN --> STRESS_LAM[Stresses in laminate x-y axes]
    STRESS_LAM --> ROTATE[Rotate by theta to get 1-2 stresses]
    X1 --> ROTATE
    X2 --> ROTATE
    X12 --> ROTATE
    THETA --> ROTATE
    ROTATE --> FAIL[Apply Tsai-Wu Failure Criterion]
```
