# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Define the term "ABD matrix" as used in the classical lamination theory of composite boat hulls. Briefly explain the physical significance of the A, B, and D sub-matrices.**

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ABD matrix** is the 6×6 stiffness matrix that relates the **in-plane force resultants** $\{N\}$ (N/m) and the **moment resultants** $\{M\}$ (N·m/m) of a laminated composite panel to its **mid-plane strains** $\{\varepsilon^0\}$ and **curvatures** $\{\kappa\}$, via the equation $\{N,M\}^T = [A,B;B,D]\{\varepsilon^0,\kappa\}^T$. The matrix is symmetric and has 21 independent constants.

**Physical significance of the three sub-matrices:**

- **$[A]$ — Extensional Stiffness Matrix (3×3):** Relates in-plane forces to mid-plane strains. It determines how the panel stretches under pure in-plane loading. Units: N/m.
- **$[B]$ — Coupling Stiffness Matrix (3×3):** Couples in-plane forces to curvatures and moments to mid-plane strains. It is **zero for symmetric laminates**. It governs twisting and bending-stretching coupling. Units: N.
- **$[D]$ — Bending Stiffness Matrix (3×3):** Relates moments to curvatures. It governs how the panel bends under out-of-plane loading (such as hydrostatic water pressure on a hull bottom). Units: N·m.

The matrix elements are obtained by integration: $A_{ij} = \sum \bar{Q}_{ij} \Delta z$, $B_{ij} = (1/2)\sum \bar{Q}_{ij} \Delta z^2$, $D_{ij} = (1/3)\sum \bar{Q}_{ij} \Delta z^3$.

---

### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Distinguish between the Tsai-Hill and Tsai-Wu failure criteria for composite laminates. State one advantage of each.**

#### **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Tsai-Hill Criterion** | **Tsai-Wu Criterion** |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Quadratic with no linear terms and a fixed interaction term $F_{12} = -1/(2X^2)$ (or similar). | General quadratic with **separate** linear and quadratic coefficients $F_1, F_2, F_{11}, F_{22}, F_{66}, F_{12}$. |
| **Number of strength parameters** | 5 ($X_t, X_c, Y_t, Y_c, S$) | 6 (adds explicit $F_{12}$ interaction) |
| **Tension–Compression asymmetry** | Handled by choosing $X$ and $Y$ based on sign of $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$. | Handled intrinsically via the linear $F_1 \sigma_1$ and $F_2 \sigma_2$ terms. |
| **Advantage** | Simpler, requires fewer material tests; suitable for **first-pass design**. | More general, captures **interactive failure** between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ accurately; **preferred by DNV and ABS for certification**. |
| **Disadvantage** | Cannot distinguish tension from compression in the same equation; less accurate for biaxial stress states. | Requires experimental determination of the interaction term $F_{12}$. |

**KTU examiner's expected one-liner:** Tsai-Hill is a special case of Tsai-Wu with a fixed interaction coefficient. Tsai-Wu is the more general criterion used in production design.

---

## 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **🔹 Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper, Q. Module-3-1]**

**(a)** For a **3-ply unidirectional glass/epoxy laminate** $[0°/45°/-45°]$ with each ply of thickness 1.0 mm, calculate the **A-matrix** of the laminate. Given the orthotropic ply properties: $E_1 = 38$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.5$ GPa, $G_{12} = 4.5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$.

**(b)** If the laminate is loaded by $N_x = 50$ N/mm, $N_y = 10$ N/mm, $N_{xy} = 0$, and all moments are zero, determine the **mid-plane strains** and the **stresses in each ply** in the laminate coordinate system. Comment on the **B-matrix** for this laminate and its physical implications.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute the on-axis Q matrix.**

As derived in the worked example, with $\nu_{21} = 0.0671$ and $1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 0.97987$:

$$[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} 38783 & 2603 & 0 \\ 2603 & 8675 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4500 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\bar{Q}$ for the 45° ply.**

For $\theta = 45°$, $m = n = 1/\sqrt{2}$, so $m^2 = n^2 = m^2 n^2 = 1/2$ and $m^3 n = m n^3 = 1/4$.

$$\bar{Q}_{11}^{(45°)} = Q_{11}(1/2)^2 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})(1/2)(1/2) + Q_{22}(1/2)^2$$

$$= (38783)(0.25) + 2(2603 + 9000)(0.25) + (8675)(0.25)$$

$$= 9696 + 5802 + 2169 = 17{,}667 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{12}^{(45°)} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})m^2 n^2 + Q_{12}(m^4 + n^4)$$

$$= (38783 + 8675 - 18000)(0.25) + 2603(0.5) = 7365 + 1302 = 8{,}667 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{22}^{(45°)} = \bar{Q}_{11}^{(45°)} \text{ (by symmetry of transformation for ±45°)} = 17{,}667 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{16}^{(45°)} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^3 n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})m n^3$$

$$= (38783 - 2603 - 9000)(0.25) + (2603 - 8675 + 9000)(0.25)$$

$$= (27180)(0.25) + (2928)(0.25) = 6795 + 732 = 7{,}527 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{26}^{(45°)} = \bar{Q}_{16}^{(45°)} = 7{,}527 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{66}^{(45°)} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})(0.25) + Q_{66}(0.5)$$

$$= (38783 + 8675 - 5206 - 9000)(0.25) + 4500(0.5) = 8313 + 2250 = 10{,}563 \text{ MPa}$$

For the **−45° ply**, by the symmetry property $\bar{Q}_{16}^{(-45°)} = -\bar{Q}_{16}^{(45°)}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}^{(-45°)} = -\bar{Q}_{26}^{(45°)}$, with all other entries identical.

**Step 3: A-matrix calculation.**

With all plies 1 mm thick and stacked from $z = -1.5$ to $+1.5$ mm:

$$[A] = [\bar{Q}^{(0°)}] + [\bar{Q}^{(45°)}] + [\bar{Q}^{(-45°)}]$$

The **45°/−45° pair** has $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ cancel out, so $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$.

$$A_{11} = 38783 + 17667 + 17667 = 74{,}117 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$A_{22} = 8675 + 17667 + 17667 = 44{,}009 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$A_{12} = 2603 + 8667 + 8667 = 19{,}937 \text{ N/mm}$$

$$A_{66} = 4500 + 10563 + 10563 = 25{,}626 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Final A-matrix:**

$$[A] = \begin{bmatrix} 74117 & 19937 & 0 \\ 19937 & 44009 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 25626 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/mm}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Invert [A] to get [a].**

$$\Delta = A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^2 = (74117)(44009) - (19937)^2 = 3.262 \times 10^9 - 3.975 \times 10^8 = 2.864 \times 10^9$$

$$a_{11} = A_{22}/\Delta = 44009 / 2.864 \times 10^9 = 1.537 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$a_{12} = -A_{12}/\Delta = -19937 / 2.864 \times 10^9 = -6.962 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$a_{22} = A_{11}/\Delta = 74117 / 2.864 \times 10^9 = 2.588 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm/N}$$

$$a_{66} = 1/A_{66} = 1/25626 = 3.902 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm/N}$$

**Step 2: Mid-plane strains.**

$$\varepsilon^0_x = a_{11} N_x + a_{12} N_y = (1.537 \times 10^{-5})(50) + (-6.962 \times 10^{-6})(10) = 7.685 \times 10^{-4} - 6.962 \times 10^{-5} = 6.989 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon^0_y = a_{12} N_x + a_{22} N_y = (-6.962 \times 10^{-6})(50) + (2.588 \times 10^{-5})(10) = -3.481 \times 10^{-4} + 2.588 \times 10^{-4} = -8.93 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\gamma^0_{xy} = 0$$

**Step 3: Stresses in each ply.**

For the **0° ply**:

$$\sigma_x = 38783(6.989 \times 10^{-4}) + 2603(-8.93 \times 10^{-5}) = 27.11 - 0.232 = 26.88 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_y = 2603(6.989 \times 10^{-4}) + 8675(-8.93 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.819 - 0.775 = 1.044 \text{ MPa}$$

For the **45° ply** (using $\bar{Q}$ from earlier):

$$\sigma_x^{(45°)} = 17667(6.989 \times 10^{-4}) + 8667(-8.93 \times 10^{-5}) + 7527(0) = 12.35 - 0.774 = 11.58 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_y^{(45°)} = 8667(6.989 \times 10^{-4}) + 17667(-8.93 \times 10^{-5}) = 6.057 - 1.578 = 4.48 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{xy}^{(45°)} = 7527(6.989 \times 10^{-4}) + 7527(-8.93 \times 10^{-5}) = 5.260 - 0.672 = 4.59 \text{ MPa}$$

For the **−45° ply**, $\tau_{xy}^{(-45°)} = -4.59$ MPa; $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ are the same as 45° ply.

**Step 4: Comment on B-matrix.**

The stacking sequence $[0°/45°/-45°]$ is **NOT symmetric** about its mid-plane. Ply 1 (0°) is at $z = -1.5$ to $-0.5$ mm, but the mirror image (0° at $z = +0.5$ to $+1.5$ mm) is missing. Therefore $[B] \neq 0$. Specifically, $B_{16}$ and $B_{26}$ will be **non-zero** because the 45° and −45° plies do not sum to zero through the asymmetric thickness distribution.

**Physical implication:** A non-zero B-matrix means the panel will **exhibit bending-stretching coupling**. If the panel is loaded only in tension ($N_x$), it will simultaneously **curl out-of-plane** (the well-known "bilamellar strip" or "unsymmetric laminate warping" effect). For boat hulls this is undesirable because it produces **residual stresses** and **distortion** during manufacture and service. The remedy is to use a **symmetric stacking** such as $[0°/45°/-45°/-45°/45°/0°] = [0°/±45°]_s$, which sets $[B] = 0$ identically.

---

### **🔹 Question B-B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Q. Module-3-1 (Alternative Choice)]**

**(a)** A **single unidirectional E-glass/epoxy ply** of thickness 2.0 mm is loaded in a state of **combined in-plane stress**: $\sigma_1 = 80$ MPa (tension, along the fibers), $\sigma_2 = 12$ MPa (tension, transverse to fibers), and $\tau_{12} = 6$ MPa (in-plane shear). The ply strength values are: $X_t = 1500$ MPa, $X_c = 800$ MPa, $Y_t = 40$ MPa, $Y_c = 140$ MPa, $S = 60$ MPa. Determine the **Tsai-Wu failure index** and the **margin of safety**. Comment on whether the design is acceptable.

**(b)** Explain the **hull-girder bending analogy** for a composite boat, and derive the relationship between the **vertical position $y$** from the neutral axis, the **bending moment $M$**, and the resulting **longitudinal stress $\sigma_x$**. How is the **effective bending stiffness $EI_{eff}$** of a composite hull computed, and why is the parallel-axis theorem essential?

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute the Tsai-Wu strength coefficients.**

$$F_1 = \frac{1}{X_t} - \frac{1}{X_c} = \frac{1}{1500} - \frac{1}{800} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} - 1.250 \times 10^{-3} = -5.833 \times 10^{-4} \text{ MPa}^{-1}$$

$$F_2 = \frac{1}{Y_t} - \frac{1}{Y_c} = \frac{1}{40} - \frac{1}{140} = 0.02500 - 0.00714 = 0.01786 \text{ MPa}^{-1}$$

$$F_{11} = \frac{1}{X_t X_c} = \frac{1}{(1500)(800)} = \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^6} = 8.333 \times 10^{-7} \text{ MPa}^{-2}$$

$$F_{22} = \frac{1}{Y_t Y_c} = \frac{1}{(40)(140)} = \frac{1}{5600} = 1.786 \times 10^{-4} \text{ MPa}^{-2}$$

$$F_{66} = \frac{1}{S^2} = \frac{1}{(60)^2} = \frac{1}{3600} = 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \text{ MPa}^{-2}$$

For the interaction coefficient, use the **engineering approximation** $F_{12} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11} F_{22}}$:

$$F_{12} = -\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(8.333 \times 10^{-7})(1.786 \times 10^{-4})} = -\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{1.488 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$F_{12} = -\frac{1}{2}(1.220 \times 10^{-5}) = -6.099 \times 10^{-6} \text{ MPa}^{-2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the Tsai-Wu polynomial.**

$$I_{TW} = F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11} \sigma_1^2 + F_{22} \sigma_2^2 + F_{66} \tau_{12}^2 + 2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$$

Compute each term:

- $F_1 \sigma_1 = (-5.833 \times 10^{-4})(80) = -0.04667$
- $F_2 \sigma_2 = (0.01786)(12) = 0.21429$
- $F_{11} \sigma_1^2 = (8.333 \times 10^{-7})(6400) = 5.333 \times 10^{-3}$
- $F_{22} \sigma_2^2 = (1.786 \times 10^{-4})(144) = 0.02571$
- $F_{66} \tau_{12}^2 = (2.778 \times 10^{-4})(36) = 0.01000$
- $2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2 = 2(-6.099 \times 10^{-6})(80)(12) = -0.01171$

Sum:

$$I_{TW} = -0.04667 + 0.21429 + 0.00533 + 0.02571 + 0.01000 - 0.01171 = 0.1970$$

**Step 3: Compute margin of safety.**

$$MoS = \frac{1}{I_{TW}} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.1970} - 1 = 5.076 - 1 = 4.076$$

**Step 4: Verdict.**

The Tsai-Wu index is $0.197 < 1.0$ and the margin of safety is $4.08 > 0$. The design is **acceptable** with a healthy safety factor. Note that the **transverse tension** $\sigma_2 = 12$ MPa contributes the largest single term ($0.214$), so the design is sensitive to the **transverse tensile strength** $Y_t = 40$ MPa. A small increase in $\sigma_2$ (e.g., from hygrothermal or Poisson effects) could move the index closer to 1.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**The Hull-Girder Analogy:**

A boat floating in waves is subjected to **longitudinal bending** in the vertical plane, just like a beam. The hull is treated as a **box girder** spanning between the two wave crests. The vertical bending moment $M$ at any hull section is the algebraic sum of the weight distribution and the buoyancy distribution. Under a **crest at midship** (hogging condition), the bow and stern are supported by wave crests while the middle is in a trough — the hull tends to bend **upward** in the middle, putting the **deck in compression and the keel in tension**. Under a **trough at midship** (sagging condition), the reverse occurs.

**Derivation of the Bending Stress Formula:**

The classical Euler-Bernoulli beam theory, when applied to a hull cross-section, gives the longitudinal stress at vertical position $y$ from the neutral axis as:

$$\sigma_x(y) = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the entire hull cross-section about the horizontal neutral axis, and $M$ is the bending moment. This is a direct consequence of:
- The plane-sections-remain-plane assumption (cross-sections that are flat before bending remain flat and normal to the deflected neutral axis after bending).
- Linear elasticity ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$).
- The strain varying linearly across the section ($\varepsilon = y/\rho$ where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature).

**Effective Bending Stiffness $EI_{eff}$ of a Composite Hull:**

Unlike a steel or aluminum hull where $E$ is constant, a composite hull has **different effective stiffnesses in different regions** (the hull bottom may have more 0° plies, the side shells may have more ±45° plies, the deck may have a sandwich construction, etc.). The effective bending stiffness of the hull girder is:

$$EI_{eff} = \int_{\text{section}} E_x(z) \cdot z^2 \, dA = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{panels}}} E_i A_i \left[ \frac{t_i^2}{12} + (y_i - y_{NA})^2 \right]$$

The first term inside the brackets is the **own second moment of area** of each plate panel about its own centroidal axis, and the second term is the **parallel-axis shift** from the panel's centroid to the **neutral axis of the entire hull section** $y_{NA}$.

**Why the Parallel-Axis Theorem is Essential:**

When the boat hull has panels of **different materials** (e.g., the deck is a sandwich with PVC core but the bottom is solid FRP), each panel has its **own** centroid and its **own** local second moment of area. To find the **moment of inertia of the entire hull cross-section** about the hull's neutral axis, we must use the **parallel-axis theorem** (also called the **Steiner's theorem**):

$$I_{\text{total}} = \sum_i \left[ I_{i,\text{local}} + A_i d_i^2 \right]$$

where $d_i = y_i - y_{NA}$ is the vertical distance from the panel's centroid to the hull's neutral axis. The neutral axis location $y_{NA}$ is itself found from the **force balance**:

$$y_{NA} = \frac{\sum_i E_i A_i y_i}{\sum_i E_i A_i}$$

The term $A_i d_i^2$ in the parallel-axis theorem is often **dominant** for a boat hull, because the deck and keel are far from the neutral axis. Hence the **stiffness of the deck and bottom panels** (where $y$ is large) matters far more than the stiffness of the side shells (where $y \approx 0$).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PENST742 Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in ABD Matrix:** Students often mix units (e.g., $z$ in mm, $E$ in GPa, $N$ in N). The examiner will deduct **1 full mark** if the unit analysis is inconsistent. **Always state "with all quantities in N, mm, MPa" at the start** of every calculation.
>
> 2. **Confusing Ply and Laminate Coordinate Systems:** The $\bar{Q}$ matrix is in the **laminate** $x$-$y$ axes. The stress limits $X_t, Y_t, S$ are in the **ply material** 1-2 axes. **You MUST rotate** the stresses back to the material coordinates before applying the failure criterion. Many students skip this rotation and get indices that are wrong by orders of magnitude.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ Cross-Term in Tsai-Wu:** The Tsai-Hill criterion does NOT have this term; the Tsai-Wu criterion DOES. Students confuse the two and apply the wrong formula, losing **2-3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Sign of the Tsai-Wu Linear Terms:** $F_1$ and $F_2$ can be **positive, negative, or zero** depending on the relative magnitudes of $X_t$ vs $X_c$ and $Y_t$ vs $Y_c$. Do not blindly assume they are positive.
>
> 5. **B-matrix for "Quasi-Isotropic" Laminates:** $[0°/±45°/90°]_s$ is symmetric, so $B = 0$. But $[0°/±45°/90°]$ (no "s") is **NOT** symmetric and will have a non-zero B-matrix with $B_{16}, B_{26} \neq 0$ if ±45° plies are on one side. **Always check the stacking symmetry.**
>
> 6. **Hull-Girder Bending Stress is Longitudinal:** The stress $\sigma_x = My/I$ is along the boat's length. It is NOT the through-thickness stress from water pressure. These are two **completely separate** failure checks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **The 10-Commandment Rapid Revision Checklist:**

1. **Definition:** The ABD matrix relates forces and moments to mid-plane strains and curvatures in a composite laminate.

2. **Structure:** $[A]$ = extensional (sum of $\bar{Q} \cdot t$), $[B]$ = coupling (sum of $\bar{Q} \cdot z^2 / 2$), $[D]$ = bending (sum of $\bar{Q} \cdot z^3 / 3$).

3. **Symmetric Laminates ⇒ $[B] = 0$.** This decouples stretching from bending and is the **standard choice for boat hulls.**

4. **On-axis $[Q]$ matrix** is computed from $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}$ using the standard reduced-stiffness formulas.

5. **Off-axis $[\bar{Q}]$ matrix** is obtained by transforming $[Q]$ using the $\theta$-dependent trigonometric combinations (6 unique $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ values).

6. **Ply strains:** $\varepsilon_k(z) = \varepsilon^0 + z \kappa$ — linearly varying through the thickness.

7. **Ply stresses in laminate axes:** $\sigma_k = [\bar{Q}^{(k)}] \varepsilon_k$ — must be **rotated** to material 1-2 axes before applying failure criteria.

8. **Failure criteria hierarchy:** Maximum Stress (simple) → Maximum Strain (better for brittle) → Tsai-Hill (quadratic, no linear terms) → **Tsai-Wu (most general, KTU-preferred)**.

9. **Tsai-Wu condition:** $I_{TW} \leq 1.0$. **Margin of Safety:** $MoS = 1/I_{TW} - 1 \geq 0$.

10. **Hull-girder bending:** Treat the whole boat as a beam; $\sigma_x = M y / I$. The **parallel-axis theorem** is essential for composite sections with multiple materials and panel locations.

### **Symbol Flashcards (Memorize These):**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $A_{ij}$ | Extensional stiffness (N/m) |
| $B_{ij}$ | Coupling stiffness (N) |
| $D_{ij}$ | Bending stiffness (N·m) |
| $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ | Transformed reduced stiffness (Pa) |
| $\theta$ | Ply fiber angle (degrees) |
| $X_t, X_c$ | Fiber-direction tensile/compressive strength |
| $Y_t, Y_c$ | Transverse tensile/compressive strength |
| $S$ | In-plane shear strength |
| $N_x, N_y, N_{xy}$ | Force resultants per unit length |
| $M_x, M_y, M_{xy}$ | Moment resultants per unit length |
| $\varepsilon^0, \kappa$ | Mid-plane strain and curvature vectors |
| $F_1, F_2, F_{11}, F_{22}, F_{66}, F_{12}$ | Tsai-Wu strength coefficients |
| $Z = I/y$ | Section modulus for hull-girder bending |

### **Final One-Line Takeaway:**

> **The ABD matrix is the central computational object of composite boat structural analysis. Master its derivation, learn to invert it, recover the ply stresses, and apply the Tsai-Wu failure criterion — these four steps solve 90% of the KTU problems you will encounter in PENST742 Module 3.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Properties of Unidirectional Lamina
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elastic Properties of Unidirectional Lamina

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Unidirectional (UD) Lamina** is the fundamental building block of a fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite used in marine and boat-building applications. It is a single, flat ply in which all the reinforcing fibers (typically **E-glass, Carbon, or Aramid/Kevlar**) are aligned in a single, straight direction within a polymer matrix (commonly **polyester, vinylester, or epoxy** resin). The fibers carry the principal load, while the matrix transfers stress and protects the fibers.

The **Elastic Properties of a Unidirectional Lamina** describe how this orthotropic (or transversely isotropic) ply responds elastically to in-plane mechanical loads. They are expressed through **five independent Engineering Constants** that form the bridge between applied stresses and resulting strains in the principal material coordinate system (axes 1, 2, 3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> "The elastic behavior of a single unidirectional lamina is characterized by five independent engineering constants: longitudinal Young's modulus $E_1$, transverse Young's modulus $E_2$, in-plane shear modulus $G_{12}$, and the two Poisson's ratios $\nu_{12}$ and $\nu_{21}$, related by the Maxwell-Betti reciprocal relation $\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}$."

### 1.2 The Five Independent Engineering Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Range (E-Glass/Epoxy) |
|----------|--------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Longitudinal Modulus | $E_1$ | Stiffness along fiber direction | 38 – 45 GPa |
| Transverse Modulus | $E_2$ | Stiffness perpendicular to fibers | 8 – 12 GPa |
| In-plane Shear Modulus | $G_{12}$ | Resistance to in-plane shearing | 4 – 6 GPa |
| Major Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{12}$ | Transverse strain due to longitudinal strain | 0.25 – 0.30 |
| Minor Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{21}$ | Longitudinal strain due to transverse strain | 0.05 – 0.10 |

> [!NOTE]
> The five constants are **not all independent**. The reciprocal relation $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ leaves only **four truly independent** constants ($E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}$).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **broomstick dipped in resin and laid flat on a table** — that is essentially a unidirectional lamina.

- The **broomstick bristles (fibers) point in one direction** (axis 1). Pulling the broom end-to-end is **very stiff** because the wood sticks resist stretching together → this is $E_1$.
- Now push sideways across the bristles (axis 2). The fibers easily separate because the resin between them is weak → this is $E_2$, which is much smaller.
- Try to **slide one end of the broom sideways** relative to the other — the bristles bend and the resin shears → this is $G_{12}$.
- When you stretch the broom lengthwise, the bristles squeeze together sideways slightly (Poisson's effect) → $\nu_{12}$.
- Because fibers are far stiffer than the resin, the **anisotropy ratio** $E_1/E_2$ for glass/epoxy is typically 3–5, and for carbon/epoxy it can reach **15–30**.

> [!TIP]
> **Boat-Building Relevance:** A boat hull made entirely of a single unidirectional lamina would be extremely stiff along the keel line but weak sideways. This is why real marine laminates are built as **multi-ply laminates** (Module 4), stacking UD plies at different fiber angles to balance stiffness in all directions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the UD Lamina is Orthotropic

A UD lamina possesses **three mutually perpendicular planes of material symmetry**:
1. The plane of the lamina (1–2 plane).
2. The plane containing the fibers and the lamina normal (1–3 plane).
3. The plane transverse to the fibers and containing the lamina normal (2–3 plane).

This makes it a **transversely isotropic** material with a single plane of isotropy (the 2–3 plane) and a unique axis (axis 1, the fiber direction). The 3-direction is mechanically identical to the 2-direction for in-plane analysis.

### 2.2 Constitutive Equations in the Principal Material Coordinate System

Under **plane stress** (the standard assumption for thin boat hull laminates where $\sigma_3 = \tau_{23} = \tau_{13} = 0$), the stress-strain relationship is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Or in compact tensor notation: $\{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}$.

Equivalently in compliance form: $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$.

### 2.3 The Compliance Matrix [S]

The compliance matrix relates strains to stresses and is constructed directly from the engineering constants:

$$
[S] = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} & 0 \\ S_{12} & S_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_{66} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The individual terms are:

$$
S_{11} = \frac{1}{E_1}, \quad S_{22} = \frac{1}{E_2}, \quad S_{12} = -\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}, \quad S_{66} = \frac{1}{G_{12}}
$$

### 2.4 The Reduced Stiffness Matrix [Q]

The reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ is the inverse of the compliance matrix, $[Q] = [S]^{-1}$. The terms are:

$$
Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{\nu_{21} E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{66} = G_{12}
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete KTU 2024 reference sheet** for the elastic analysis of a unidirectional lamina. Every row is a high-yield formula for Module 3 and Module 4 (laminate analysis).

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description |
|---|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ | Reciprocal Poisson's relation (Maxwell-Betti) |
| 2 | $[S] = \begin{bmatrix} 1/E_1 & -\nu_{12}/E_1 & 0 \\ -\nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/G_{12} \end{bmatrix}$ | Compliance matrix in principal coordinates |
| 3 | $[Q] = [S]^{-1}$ | Reduced stiffness = inverse of compliance |
| 4 | $Q_{11} = \dfrac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Longitudinal normal stiffness |
| 5 | $Q_{22} = \dfrac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Transverse normal stiffness |
| 6 | $Q_{12} = \dfrac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Coupling (Poisson) stiffness |
| 7 | $Q_{66} = G_{12}$ | In-plane shear stiffness |
| 8 | $\det[S] = \dfrac{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}{E_1 E_2}$ | Determinant of compliance matrix |
| 9 | $\{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}$ | Forward constitutive law |
| 10 | $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$ | Compliance (inverse) law |
| 11 | $\bar{Q}_{ij} = Q_{ij}$ at $\theta = 0$ | Reduced stiffness is invariant in principal coordinates |

> **Convention used in formulas above:** $\nu_{12}\nu_{21}$ means the *product* of the two Poisson's ratios (NOT absolute value).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **composite boat design**, the $[Q]$ matrix is the foundation for predicting hull deflections under wave slamming loads, panel buckling of bulkheads, and skin-stiffener interaction. Naval architects use these constants to:
- Compute **first-ply failure loads** (Tsai-Wu, Tsai-Hill criteria in Module 4).
- Determine **panel stiffness** $A_{ij}$ matrices of multi-ply laminates.
- Run **finite element analysis (FEA)** of GRP hulls, where shell elements require $[Q]$ for each ply as input.
- Verify classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register) that mandate minimum $E_1$ for structural scantlings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Engineering Constants to Compliance Matrix [S]

**Starting point:** For an orthotropic material under plane stress, Hooke's law is written as three independent strain equations.

**Step 1 — Longitudinal strain equation.** The strain in the fiber direction $\varepsilon_1$ depends on $\sigma_1$ (direct effect) and $\sigma_2$ (Poisson contraction effect). The shear stress $\tau_{12}$ does not produce normal strain:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\sigma_2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the reciprocal relation.** The off-diagonal compliance coefficient must be symmetric ($S_{12} = S_{21}$). Using the Maxwell-Betti relation $\nu_{21}/E_2 = \nu_{12}/E_1$:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E_1}\sigma_1 - \frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_2
$$

**Step 3 — Transverse strain equation.** By symmetry of the constitutive law:

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_1 + \frac{1}{E_2}\sigma_2
$$

**Step 4 — Shear strain equation.** Shear is uncoupled from normal stresses for an orthotropic UD lamina in principal coordinates:

$$
\gamma_{12} = \frac{1}{G_{12}}\tau_{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble into matrix form.** Group the three equations into $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{E_1} & -\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} & 0 \\[6pt] -\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_2} & 0 \\[6pt] 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{12}} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

This proves the compliance matrix entries in Row 2 of the KTU Formula Sheet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Inversion of [S] to Obtain the Reduced Stiffness Matrix [Q]

We need to compute $[Q] = [S]^{-1}$. The standard 2×2 block in the upper left is inverted first.

**Step 1 — Determinant of [S].** Using the block structure (the $S_{66}$ term is decoupled):

$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} 1/E_1 & -\nu_{12}/E_1 \\ -\nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{E_1 E_2} - \frac{\nu_{12}^{2}}{E_1^{2}} = \frac{E_1 - \nu_{12}^{2} E_2}{E_1^{2} E_2}
$$

Now use the reciprocal relation $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} E_2 / E_1$, which gives $\nu_{12}^{2} E_2 = \nu_{12} \nu_{21} E_1$. Substituting:

$$
\det[S]_{2\times 2} = \frac{E_1(1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})}{E_1^{2} E_2} = \frac{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}{E_1 E_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Inverse of the 2×2 block.** For a 2×2 matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & c \end{bmatrix}$, the inverse is $\dfrac{1}{ac-b^2}\begin{bmatrix} c & -b \\ -b & a \end{bmatrix}$. With $a = 1/E_1$, $b = -\nu_{12}/E_1$, $c = 1/E_2$:

$$
[S]_{2\times 2}^{-1} = \frac{E_1 E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} 1/E_2 & \nu_{12}/E_1 \\ \nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand into the four stiffness terms.**

$$
Q_{11} = \frac{E_1 E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \cdot \frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}
$$

$$
Q_{22} = \frac{E_1 E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \cdot \frac{1}{E_1} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}
$$

$$
Q_{12} = \frac{E_1 E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \cdot \frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}
$$

**Step 4 — Shear stiffness is trivially decoupled.**

$$
Q_{66} = \left( \frac{1}{G_{12}} \right)^{-1} = G_{12}
$$

**Step 5 — Final assembled [Q] matrix:**

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & \dfrac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & 0 \\[8pt] \dfrac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & \dfrac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & 0 \\[8pt] 0 & 0 & G_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A unidirectional glass/epoxy lamina used in a yacht hull has the following measured properties: $E_1 = 38$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.5$ GPa, $G_{12} = 4.5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.28$. Compute (a) $\nu_{21}$, (b) the full $[Q]$ matrix, and (c) the strains under in-plane stresses $\sigma_1 = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 20$ MPa, $\tau_{12} = 5$ MPa.

**Part (a):** Reciprocal Poisson's ratio.

$$
\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.28 \times \frac{8.5}{38} = 0.0626
$$

**Part (b):** Compute the denominator $1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}$:

$$
\nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 0.28 \times 0.0626 = 0.01753
$$

$$
1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - 0.01753 = 0.98247
$$

Now the four stiffness terms:

$$
Q_{11} = \frac{38}{0.98247} = 38.679 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{22} = \frac{8.5}{0.98247} = 8.652 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{12} = \frac{0.28 \times 8.5}{0.98247} = \frac{2.38}{0.98247} = 2.422 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{66} = 4.5 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Part (c):** Apply $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$. We can either invert $[Q]$ back to $[S]$ or compute $[S]$ directly:

$$
S_{11} = \frac{1}{38} = 0.02632 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

$$
S_{22} = \frac{1}{8.5} = 0.11765 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

$$
S_{12} = -\frac{0.28}{38} = -0.007368 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

$$
S_{66} = \frac{1}{4.5} = 0.22222 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

Now the strains:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = S_{11}\sigma_1 + S_{12}\sigma_2 = (0.02632)(100) + (-0.007368)(20) = 2.632 - 0.1474 = 2.485 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2 = S_{12}\sigma_1 + S_{22}\sigma_2 = (-0.007368)(100) + (0.11765)(20) = -0.7368 + 2.353 = 1.616 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\gamma_{12} = S_{66}\tau_{12} = (0.22222)(5) = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A 100 MPa load along the fibers produces only $\sim 0.25\%$ strain (very stiff), while a 20 MPa transverse load produces $\sim 0.16\%$ strain across the resin-dominated direction. The $\nu_{12}$ effect causes the longitudinal stress to *reduce* the transverse strain (negative sign in $S_{12}$).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Off-Axis Lamina — Need for Transformed Stiffness $[\bar{Q}]$

**The Problem:** The fiber direction (axis 1) is rarely aligned with the loading direction (axis $x$) of a boat hull. The load comes from a wave, a slamming pressure, or a bending moment, and the plies are oriented at some arbitrary angle $\theta$ (the **fiber orientation angle**).

**The Solution:** Transform $[Q]$ from the principal material axes (1,2) to the global loading axes ($x, y$) using the rotation matrix $[T]$:

$$
[T] = \begin{bmatrix} \cos^{2}\theta & \sin^{2}\theta & 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ \sin^{2}\theta & \cos^{2}\theta & -2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ -\sin\theta\cos\theta & \sin\theta\cos\theta & \cos^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

The transformed stress-strain law is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \bar{Q}_{11} & \bar{Q}_{12} & \bar{Q}_{16} \\ \bar{Q}_{12} & \bar{Q}_{22} & \bar{Q}_{26} \\ \bar{Q}_{16} & \bar{Q}_{26} & \bar{Q}_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The transformed reduced stiffnesses are computed as:

$$
[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1} [Q] [R] [T]^{-T} [R]^{-1}
$$

In simplified closed form (using $m = \cos\theta$, $n = \sin\theta$):

$$
\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11}m^{4} + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{22}n^{4}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11}n^{4} + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{22}m^{4}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{12} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{12}(m^{4} + n^{4})
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{66} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{66}(m^{4} + n^{4})
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^{3}n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})mn^{3}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{26} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})mn^{3} + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})m^{3}n
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ are **non-zero** for an off-axis lamina. This means normal stress $\sigma_x$ can produce shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ — a phenomenon called **shear-extension coupling**. In boat hull design, this coupling is exploited to tailor laminate stiffness and avoid unwanted twist modes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of UD Lamina Elastic Analysis

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete logical pipeline** for analyzing the elastic response of a unidirectional lamina in a boat hull context. The diagram maps how raw material constants propagate through the compliance/stiffness formulations and into the design outcome.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material System Selection] --> B[Glass or Carbon or Aramid Fibers]
    B --> C[Resin System: Epoxy or Vinylester or Polyester]
    A --> D[Laboratory Coupon Testing]
    D --> E[Engineering Constants]
    E --> E1["E1 longitudinal modulus"]
    E --> E2["E2 transverse modulus"]
    E --> G12["G12 shear modulus"]
    E --> NU12["nu12 major Poisson ratio"]
    E --> F[Reciprocal Poisson Relation]
    F --> G[Compute nu21]
    E1 --> H[Build Compliance Matrix S]
    E2 --> H
    G12 --> H
    NU12 --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Matrix Inversion]
    I --> J[Reduced Stiffness Matrix Q]
    J --> K{Is the lamina loaded off-axis?}
    K --> L[No: theta equals 0]
    K --> M[Yes: theta not equal 0]
    L --> N[Direct Use of Q matrix]
    M --> O[Apply Transformation Matrix T of theta]
    O --> P[Compute Transformed Qbar matrix]
    N --> Q[Compute Strains from S times sigma]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Predict Hull Panel Deformation]
    R --> S[Feasibility Check for Boat Design]
    S --> T[Pass: Proceed to Laminate Stacking]
    S --> U[Fail: Re-orient Ply or Change Material]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the UD Lamina Geometry

The following block diagram illustrates the **local vs. global coordinate systems** for a unidirectional ply in a boat hull, the central concept behind the $[\bar{Q}]$ transformation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boat Hull Surface: x along keel, y across hull] --> B[Fiber Orientation Angle theta measured from x axis]
    B --> C[Principal Material Axis 1: along fibers]
    B --> D[Principal Material Axis 2: transverse to fibers]
    C --> E[Stiffness Q11, Q12, Q66 in 1-2 frame]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Transformation by T matrix of theta]
    F --> G[Transformed Stiffness Qbar in x-y frame]
    G --> H[Apply Loading sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy]
    H --> I[Compute Resulting Strain epsilon_x, epsilon_y, gamma_xy]
```

### 4.3 Taxonomy of the Five Engineering Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Elastic Properties of UD Lamina] --> B[Four Independent Constants]
    A --> C[One Derived Constant]
    B --> B1[E1: Longitudinal Modulus]
    B --> B2[E2: Transverse Modulus]
    B --> B3[G12: In-plane Shear Modulus]
    B --> B4[nu12: Major Poisson Ratio]
    C --> C1[nu21 from reciprocal relation]
    C1 --> D[nu21 equals nu12 times E2 divided by E1]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the engineering constants $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{21}$ of a unidirectional composite lamina. State the reciprocal relation between the two Poisson's ratios.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **$E_1$ (1 mark):** Young's modulus of the lamina in the direction of the fibers (longitudinal direction), equal to $\sigma_1/\varepsilon_1$ under uniaxial stress along axis 1.
- **$E_2$ (1 mark):** Young's modulus transverse to the fibers, equal to $\sigma_2/\varepsilon_2$ under uniaxial stress along axis 2.
- **$G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{21}$ and reciprocal relation (1 mark):** $G_{12}$ is the in-plane shear modulus; $\nu_{12}$ is the transverse strain produced by unit longitudinal strain; $\nu_{21}$ is the longitudinal strain produced by unit transverse strain. The Maxwell-Betti reciprocal relation is:
$$
\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} \quad \text{or} \quad \nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\cdot\frac{E_2}{E_1}
$$

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the compliance matrix $[S]$ and the reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ of a unidirectional lamina. Why is $[Q]$ called "reduced"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Difference (2 marks):** The compliance matrix $[S]$ maps stresses to strains ($\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$) and its entries are direct combinations of the engineering constants. The reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ maps strains to stresses ($\{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}$) and is the matrix inverse of $[S]$.
- **Why "reduced" (1 mark):** It is called "reduced" because it applies only under the **plane-stress assumption** (i.e., $\sigma_3 = \tau_{13} = \tau_{23} = 0$), which reduces the full 6×6 3-D stiffness matrix to the 3×3 in-plane form used for thin composite plies in boat hulls.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 3 Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** is the #1 mark-loser. Always express $E$ in GPa, then convert to MPa ($1$ GPa $= 10^{3}$ MPa) before plugging into stress–strain equations when stress is in MPa.
> 2. **Sign error in Poisson's ratio:** $S_{12}$ is **negative** ($-\nu_{12}/E_1$). Students often write $S_{12} = +\nu_{12}/E_1$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the reciprocal relation** ($\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} E_2/E_1$) when computing $\nu_{21}$ from given $E_1, E_2, \nu_{12}$ — full 1-mark cut if omitted.
> 4. **Off-axis question trap:** If asked to compute $\bar{Q}_{16}$, do not write $\bar{Q}_{16} = 0$. It is **non-zero** for $\theta \neq 0$ unless the lamina is transversely isotropic. Setting it to zero loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Determinant of $[S]$:** Always expand the $2 \times 2$ block separately; do not write the determinant as $1/(E_1 E_2 G_{12})$ — the shear block is decoupled, but the normal block carries the Poisson coupling.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

For a unidirectional glass/epoxy lamina used in a small fishing boat hull, the following engineering constants are obtained from laboratory testing:
$E_1 = 40$ GPa, $E_2 = 10$ GPa, $G_{12} = 5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$.

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:**
1. Compute the minor Poisson's ratio $\nu_{21}$.
2. Determine the full compliance matrix $[S]$.
3. Determine the full reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:**
The same lamina is now loaded along a global axis $x$ which is rotated by an angle $\theta = 30°$ from the fiber direction. Compute the transformed reduced stiffness matrix $[\bar{Q}]$.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(a.1) Minor Poisson's ratio $\nu_{21}$ [2 marks]:**

$$
\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\cdot\frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.30 \times \frac{10}{40} = 0.30 \times 0.25 = 0.075
$$

**(a.2) Compliance matrix $[S]$ [2 marks]:**

$$
S_{11} = \frac{1}{E_1} = \frac{1}{40} = 0.025 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

$$
S_{22} = \frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.100 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

$$
S_{12} = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = -\frac{0.30}{40} = -0.0075 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

$$
S_{66} = \frac{1}{G_{12}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.200 \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

$$
[S] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.025 & -0.0075 & 0 \\ -0.0075 & 0.100 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.200 \end{bmatrix} \text{ (GPa)}^{-1}
$$

**(a.3) Reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ [3 marks]:**

First compute the coupling denominator:
$$
1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - (0.30)(0.075) = 1 - 0.0225 = 0.9775
$$

Then:
$$
Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{0.9775} = \frac{40}{0.9775} = 40.921 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{0.9775} = \frac{10}{0.9775} = 10.230 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{0.9775} = \frac{0.30 \times 10}{0.9775} = \frac{3.0}{0.9775} = 3.069 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{66} = G_{12} = 5 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} 40.921 & 3.069 & 0 \\ 3.069 & 10.230 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

For $\theta = 30°$: $m = \cos 30° = 0.8660$, $n = \sin 30° = 0.5000$.

Useful products/powers:
- $m^{2} = 0.7500$, $n^{2} = 0.2500$, $m^{2} n^{2} = 0.1875$
- $m^{4} = 0.5625$, $n^{4} = 0.0625$
- $m^{4} + n^{4} = 0.6250$
- $m^{3} n = (0.8660)^{3}(0.5000) = 0.6495 \times 0.5 = 0.3248$
- $m n^{3} = (0.8660)(0.5000)^{3} = 0.8660 \times 0.125 = 0.1083$

Compute each transformed term:

**$\bar{Q}_{11}$ [1.5 marks]:**
$$
\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11}m^{4} + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{22}n^{4}
$$

$$
= (40.921)(0.5625) + 2(3.069 + 2 \times 5)(0.1875) + (10.230)(0.0625)
$$

$$
= 23.018 + 2(13.069)(0.1875) + 0.6394
$$

$$
= 23.018 + 4.901 + 0.639 = 28.558 \text{ GPa}
$$

**$\bar{Q}_{22}$ [1.5 marks]:**
$$
\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11}n^{4} + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{22}m^{4}
$$

$$
= (40.921)(0.0625) + 2(13.069)(0.1875) + (10.230)(0.5625)
$$

$$
= 2.558 + 4.901 + 5.754 = 13.213 \text{ GPa}
$$

**$\bar{Q}_{12}$ [1.5 marks]:**
$$
\bar{Q}_{12} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{12}(m^{4} + n^{4})
$$

$$
= (40.921 + 10.230 - 4 \times 5)(0.1875) + (3.069)(0.625)
$$

$$
= (31.151)(0.1875) + 1.918 = 5.841 + 1.918 = 7.759 \text{ GPa}
$$

**$\bar{Q}_{66}$ [1 mark]:**
$$
\bar{Q}_{66} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^{2}n^{2} + Q_{66}(m^{4} + n^{4})
$$

$$
= (40.921 + 10.230 - 2 \times 3.069 - 2 \times 5)(0.1875) + (5)(0.625)
$$

$$
= (34.944)(0.1875) + 3.125 = 6.552 + 3.125 = 9.677 \text{ GPa}
$$

**$\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ [1.5 marks]:**
$$
\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^{3}n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})mn^{3}
$$

$$
= (40.921 - 3.069 - 10)(0.3248) + (3.069 - 10.230 + 10)(0.1083)
$$

$$
= (27.852)(0.3248) + (2.839)(0.1083) = 9.046 + 0.308 = 9.354 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{26} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})mn^{3} + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})m^{3}n
$$

$$
= (27.852)(0.1083) + (2.839)(0.3248) = 3.016 + 0.922 = 3.938 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Final transformed matrix:**
$$
[\bar{Q}] = \begin{bmatrix} 28.558 & 7.759 & 9.354 \\ 7.759 & 13.213 & 3.938 \\ 9.354 & 3.938 & 9.677 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ are **non-zero** at $\theta = 30°$. This is the *shear-extension coupling* that does not exist in the principal material frame. A boat designer must account for this when laying up off-axis plies in the hull.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

A unidirectional carbon/epoxy lamina proposed for a high-performance racing yacht hull has the following properties: $E_1 = 140$ GPa, $E_2 = 10$ GPa, $G_{12} = 5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$.

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:**
1. Construct the full compliance matrix $[S]$ and reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$.
2. The lamina is subjected to plane stresses $\sigma_1 = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 30$ MPa, $\tau_{12} = 10$ MPa. Determine all three strain components.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:**
Determine the transformed reduced stiffness matrix $[\bar{Q}]$ when the laminate is oriented at $\theta = 45°$ to the loading direction. Comment on the relative magnitudes of $\bar{Q}_{11}$ and $\bar{Q}_{22}$ and why this matters for racing yacht design.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(a.1) [3 marks]** Following the same procedure as Question A:

$\nu_{21} = 0.30 \times 10/140 = 0.02143$

$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - 0.30 \times 0.02143 = 0.99357$

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} 140.91 & 3.019 & 0 \\ 3.019 & 10.065 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

**(a.2) [4 marks]** With stresses in MPa, it is convenient to use compliance in $(\text{MPa})^{-1}$:

$S_{11} = 1/140000 = 7.143 \times 10^{-6}$ $(\text{MPa})^{-1}$
$S_{22} = 1/10000 = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ $(\text{MPa})^{-1}$
$S_{12} = -0.30/140000 = -2.143 \times 10^{-6}$ $(\text{MPa})^{-1}$
$S_{66} = 1/5000 = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ $(\text{MPa})^{-1}$

Strains:
$$
\varepsilon_1 = (7.143 \times 10^{-6})(200) + (-2.143 \times 10^{-6})(30) = 1.4286 \times 10^{-3} - 0.0643 \times 10^{-3} = 1.364 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2 = (-2.143 \times 10^{-6})(200) + (100 \times 10^{-6})(30) = -0.4286 \times 10^{-3} + 3.000 \times 10^{-3} = 2.571 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\gamma_{12} = (200 \times 10^{-6})(10) = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

For $\theta = 45°$: $m = n = 1/\sqrt{2} = 0.7071$.
- $m^{2} = n^{2} = 0.5000$, $m^{2}n^{2} = 0.2500$
- $m^{4} = n^{4} = 0.2500$, $m^{4} + n^{4} = 0.5000$
- $m^{3}n = mn^{3} = 0.7071 \times 0.7071 \times 0.7071 \times 0.7071 = 0.2500$

**$\bar{Q}_{11}$ [1.5 marks]:**
$$
\bar{Q}_{11} = (140.91)(0.25) + 2(3.019 + 10)(0.25) + (10.065)(0.25)
$$

$$
= 35.228 + 6.510 + 2.516 = 44.254 \text{ GPa}
$$

**$\bar{Q}_{22}$ [1.5 marks]:**
$$
\bar{Q}_{22} = (140.91)(0.25) + 2(3.019 + 10)(0.25) + (10.065)(0.25) = 44.254 \text{ GPa}
$$

(At $45°$ for a transversely isotropic material, $\bar{Q}_{11} = \bar{Q}_{22}$ — a key symmetry property.)

**$\bar{Q}_{12}$, $\bar{Q}_{66}$, $\bar{Q}_{16}$, $\bar{Q}_{26}$ [4 marks]:**
$$
\bar{Q}_{12} = (140.91 + 10.065 - 20)(0.25) + (3.019)(0.5) = 32.744 + 1.510 = 34.254 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{66} = (140.91 + 10.065 - 6.038 - 10)(0.25) + (5)(0.5) = 33.734 + 2.500 = 36.234 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{16} = (140.91 - 3.019 - 10)(0.25) + (3.019 - 10.065 + 10)(0.25) = 31.973 + 0.739 = 32.712 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{26} = (140.91 - 3.019 - 10)(0.25) + (3.019 - 10.065 + 10)(0.25) = 32.712 \text{ GPa}
$$

(Again, $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26}$ at $45°$ for this symmetry.)

**Engineering Comment [Remaining marks]:** At $\theta = 45°$, the carbon/epoxy lamina becomes effectively **isotropic in its in-plane response** ($\bar{Q}_{11} = \bar{Q}_{22}$), with $\bar{Q}_{11}$ dropping from 140.9 GPa (along the fiber) to about 44 GPa — a 68% reduction. For a racing yacht, this means a $45°$ ply is no longer fiber-dominant; the designer must stack multiple plies at different angles to retain stiffness in the dominant loading direction while gaining transverse and shear stiffness from the $45°$ plies.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Elastic Properties of UD Lamina**

- **Five engineering constants** define the elastic response: $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{21}$. Only **four are independent**; the fifth is obtained from the **Maxwell-Betti reciprocal relation** $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot E_2/E_1$.
- **Compliance matrix $[S]$** maps $\sigma \to \varepsilon$; entries are direct combinations of engineering constants. The off-diagonal $S_{12} = -\nu_{12}/E_1$ is **always negative**.
- **Reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$** maps $\varepsilon \to \sigma$; it is the **inverse of $[S]$**, restricted to plane-stress (hence "reduced" from the 3-D 6×6 form).
- The four $[Q]$ terms have a common denominator $1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}$ — a number very close to 1 (typically 0.97–0.99) because the Poisson's product is small.
- $Q_{66} = G_{12}$ exactly — no denominator correction for the shear term.
- **Anisotropy ratio** $E_1/E_2$ is the first indicator of how "fiber-dominant" the lamina is: ~4 for glass/epoxy, ~15 for carbon/epoxy.
- **Off-axis loading** requires the transformed stiffness $[\bar{Q}]$ using the rotation matrix $[T]$. Key property: $[\bar{Q}]$ is a **full 3×3 symmetric matrix** with non-zero $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ — the **shear-extension coupling** that does not exist in the principal frame.
- **Boat-design rule of thumb:** Plies near $0°$ carry axial hull-girder loads; plies near $\pm 45°$ carry shear and torsional loads; plies near $90°$ resist transverse bending and impact (important for slamming resistance in the hull bottom).
- Always **watch units**: $E$ in GPa with $\sigma$ in GPa, OR $E$ in MPa with $\sigma$ in MPa. Mixing causes 1-mark deductions.
- The term $1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}$ is **positive** for engineering materials; if it were negative or zero, the material would be mechanically unstable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Strain Relationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Stress-Strain Relationships # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Stress" in a Composite Boat? In solid mechanics applied to **fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite boat hulls**, **stress** ($\sigma$) is defined as the internal force per unit area that develops within the laminate when an external load is applied. The formal KTU-aligned definition is: $$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} \quad \text{[N/m}^2\text{ or Pa]}$$ In a **composite boat hull**, the stress is shared between the **fibre** (which carries the load primarily along its axis) and the **matrix** (which transfers the load between fibres and resists shear). This **load-sharing** is fundamentally different from metals, where the microstructure is uniform. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition Box:** > **Stress** = Force Intensity = $\sigma_{ij}$ where $i$ denotes the *face* on which it acts and $j$ denotes the *direction* of the force. > - When $i = j$ → **Normal Stress** ($\sigma_{11}, \sigma_{22}, \sigma_{33}$) > - When $i \neq j$ → **Shear Stress** ($\tau_{12}, \tau_{13}, \tau_{23}$) ## 1.2 What is "Strain" in a Composite Boat? **Strain** ($\varepsilon$) is the dimensionless measure of deformation — the change in length divided by the original length, or the change in angle for shear. For a hull plank that stretches when the boat flexes over a wave: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad \text{[dimensionless, often in mm/m or \%]}$$ ## 1.3 Why Are Stress-Strain Relationships Special for Composites? A **monolithic aluminium hull** has a single Young's Modulus (≈ 70 GPa) and a single Poisson's ratio (0.33). It is **isotropic** — properties are the same in every direction. A **composite lamina** (a single ply of glass fibre + polyester resin) has *different* properties along the fibre direction versus transverse to the fibre. This makes it **anisotropic** (more specifically, **orthotropic** with two principal material axes). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — Why this matters for Boat Design:** > A boat hull is loaded in **multiple directions simultaneously** (bending from waves, twisting from torque, hydrostatic pressure). For a composite hull, the **stiffness depends on the fibre orientation** of each ply. Predicting stress & strain correctly is the foundation for: > - Hull deflection calculations > - Ply-by-ply failure prediction > - Optimising the **stacking sequence** of the laminate ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bundle of Bamboo vs. The Rope" Imagine a **bundle of bamboo sticks held together by rubber bands** (this is your composite): - Pull the bundle **along the sticks** → very stiff, little stretch (high $E_1$) - Pull the bundle **sideways across the sticks** → the rubber bands stretch easily (low $E_2$) - The same material behaves **completely differently** in two perpendicular directions. A **rope** (isotropic) would behave identically in every direction. The bamboo bundle behaves like a **composite lamina**. ## 1.5 Hooke's Law — The Foundation For linear elastic materials, stress and strain are linearly related. The general 3D Hooke's Law is: $$\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \cdot \varepsilon_{kl}$$ where $C_{ijkl}$ is the **4th-order stiffness tensor** (81 components, but symmetry reduces it to **21 independent constants** for the most general anisotropic material). For a **composite lamina** under **plane stress** (the standard assumption for thin laminates), this reduces dramatically — and this is what we will derive in detail below. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Levels of Material Symmetry The complexity of the stress-strain relationship depends on the **material symmetry**: | Symmetry Level | Independent Constants | Example | Use in Boat Design | |---|---|---|---| | **General Anisotropic** | 21 | Carbon/Aramid weave in 3D | Rarely used directly | | **Monoclinic** | 13 | Off-axis single ply | Intermediate | | **Orthotropic** | 9 (3D) / 4 (Plane Stress) | Unidirectional glass/epoxy ply | **Most common in KTU problems** | | **Transversely Isotropic** | 5 (3D) / 4 (Plane Stress) | Unidirectional fibre lamina | Common simplification | | **Isotropic** | 2 ($E, \nu$) | Aluminium, steel | Used for metallic boat fittings | ## 2.2 The Reduced Stiffness Matrix for an Orthotropic Lamina (Plane Stress) The cornerstone result for Module 3 is the **plane-stress reduced stiffness matrix** $[Q]$ of an orthotropic lamina. The relationship is: $$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$ This is the **constitutive equation** of a single ply in its **principal material coordinates** (1 = fibre direction, 2 = transverse). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Cheat Sheet" every KTU student must memorise for this module.** | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{11} = \dfrac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Normal stiffness along fibre | Pa | | 2 | $Q_{22} = \dfrac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Normal stiffness transverse to fibre | Pa | | 3 | $Q_{12} = \dfrac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \dfrac{\nu_{21} E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Coupling stiffness (Poisson coupling) | Pa | | 4 | $Q_{66} = G_{12}$ | In-plane shear stiffness | Pa | | 5 | $\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}$ | **Reciprocal relation** (Maxwell-Betti) | — | | 6 | $S_{11} = \dfrac{1}{E_1}, \ S_{22} = \dfrac{1}{E_2}$ | Normal compliances | 1/Pa | | 7 | $S_{12} = \dfrac{-\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \dfrac{-\nu_{21}}{E_2}$ | Coupling compliance | 1/Pa | | 8 | $S_{66} = \dfrac{1}{G_{12}}$ | Shear compliance | 1/Pa | | 9 | $[S] = [Q]^{-1}$ | Compliance is inverse of Stiffness | — | | 10 | $\bar{Q}_{ij} = T^{-1} \cdot Q_{ij} \cdot (T^{-1})^T$ | Transformed reduced stiffness (off-axis) | Pa | | 11 | $\varepsilon_x = \bar{S}_{11}\sigma_x + \bar{S}_{12}\sigma_y + \bar{S}_{16}\tau_{xy}$ | Off-axis normal strain | — | | 12 | $\gamma_{xy} = \bar{S}_{16}\sigma_x + \bar{S}_{26}\sigma_y + \bar{S}_{66}\tau_{xy}$ | Off-axis shear strain | — | > [!NOTE] > The notation $\nu_{12}$ means: "strain in direction 2 caused by stress in direction 1." It is **not** a ratio of strains — it is the **negative of the lateral strain ratio** in classical mechanics, with index order as shown. ## 2.4 Engineering Constants of Typical Marine Composites | Material | $E_1$ (GPa) | $E_2$ (GPa) | $G_{12}$ (GPa) | $\nu_{12}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **E-glass / Epoxy** | 38 | 8.5 | 4.5 | 0.30 | | **E-glass / Polyester** | 35 | 8.0 | 3.5 | 0.30 | | **Carbon / Epoxy (T700)** | 135 | 10 | 5.0 | 0.30 | | **Kevlar / Epoxy** | 76 | 5.5 | 2.0 | 0.34 | These values appear in **nearly every KTU numerical problem**. Memorise the order of magnitude: $E_1 \gg E_2$ for unidirectional composites. ## 2.5 The Off-Axis Problem — Why It Matters In a real boat hull, the **fibres of a particular ply are rarely aligned with the hull's global x-axis**. They are placed at some angle $\theta$ (e.g., $\pm 45^\circ$ for torsional stiffness, $0^\circ$ for axial stiffness, $90^\circ$ for transverse stiffness). The stress-strain relationship in the **global (x, y) axes** becomes: $$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \bar{Q}_{11} & \bar{Q}_{12} & \bar{Q}_{16} \\ \bar{Q}_{12} & \bar{Q}_{22} & \bar{Q}_{26} \\ \bar{Q}_{16} & \bar{Q}_{26} & \bar{Q}_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Notice the new terms $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$!** These are **shear-extension coupling** terms. They mean that a **pure normal stress $\sigma_x$ produces a shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$** in an off-axis ply. This has **no counterpart in isotropic metals** and is a critical KTU exam concept. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility for Boat Design 1. **Hull stiffness optimization** — Designers select ply angles $\theta$ to maximise hull panel stiffness against wave slamming. 2. **Failure prediction** — Knowing $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12}$ in *material coordinates* allows application of **Tsai-Wu** or **Max Stress** failure criteria. 3. **Resin cracking prediction** — Transverse stress $\sigma_2$ governs matrix microcracking, which initiates hull leakage. 4. **Delamination analysis** — Interlaminar shear $\tau_{13}$ depends on through-thickness stress gradients, predicted from $[Q]$ and $[A, B, D]$ matrices. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From 3D Hooke's Law to Plane-Stress $[Q]$ Matrix ### Step 1 — Start with the 3D Generalised Hooke's Law In index notation, for the most general linear elastic material: $$\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$ The stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$ has $3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 81$ components. Symmetry of stress and strain tensors plus strain energy considerations reduce this to **21 independent constants** for a fully anisotropic material. ### Step 2 — Impose Orthotropic Symmetry An **orthotropic material** has three mutually perpendicular planes of symmetry. In its principal material coordinates (1, 2, 3), all shear-extension couplings vanish. The result is a **diagonal-block** stiffness matrix: $$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \sigma_3 \\ \tau_{23} \\ \tau_{13} \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{13} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{23} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{13} & C_{23} & C_{33} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{44} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{55} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \varepsilon_3 \\ \gamma_{23} \\ \gamma_{13} \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$ This has **9 independent constants** for the orthotropic 3D case. ### Step 3 — Apply the Plane-Stress Assumption A single ply in a thin laminate is thin in the 3-direction (thickness $t \ll$ length, width). The out-of-plane stresses vanish: $$\sigma_3 = 0, \quad \tau_{13} = 0, \quad \tau_{23} = 0$$ From the third row of the matrix: $$\sigma_3 = C_{13}\varepsilon_1 + C_{23}\varepsilon_2 + C_{33}\varepsilon_3 = 0$$ Solving for $\varepsilon_3$: $$\varepsilon_3 = -\frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}}\varepsilon_1 - \frac{C_{23}}{C_{33}}\varepsilon_2$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute Back and Eliminate $\varepsilon_3$ Substituting this into the expressions for $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ and simplifying (a long but straightforward algebraic manipulation), and then **re-expressing the result in terms of engineering constants** $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{21}$: $$\sigma_1 = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}\varepsilon_1 + \frac{\nu_{21}E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}\varepsilon_2 = Q_{11}\varepsilon_1 + Q_{12}\varepsilon_2$$ $$\sigma_2 = \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}\varepsilon_1 + \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}\varepsilon_2 = Q_{12}\varepsilon_1 + Q_{22}\varepsilon_2$$ $$\tau_{12} = G_{12}\,\gamma_{12} = Q_{66}\,\gamma_{12}$$ ### Step 5 — Final Compact Form (The Famous Result) $$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}}$$ This is the **on-axis plane-stress stiffness matrix** — the foundation of Classical Lamination Theory (CLT). ## 3.2 Derivation: Compliance Form (Inverse of $[Q]$) To find the strains in terms of stresses, invert $[Q]$: $$[S] = [Q]^{-1}$$ Computing the inverse of the 2×2 block $\{Q_{11}, Q_{12}, Q_{22}\}$: $$[S] = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{E_1} & \dfrac{-\nu_{21}}{E_2} & 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{-\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_2} & 0 \\[6pt] 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{12}} \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification using reciprocal relation:** > The off-diagonal terms $S_{12} = \dfrac{-\nu_{21}}{E_2}$ and $S_{21} = \dfrac{-\nu_{12}}{E_1}$ are equal. Equating them: $\dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = \dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}$, which is **Maxwell-Betti's reciprocal theorem** — a fundamental result from energy conservation. ## 3.3 Derivation: Transformed Reduced Stiffness $[\bar{Q}]$ When the fibre angle $\theta \neq 0$, we must transform $[Q]$ from material axes (1, 2) to global axes (x, y). ### Step 1 — Strain Transformation $$\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12}/2 \end{bmatrix} = [T_{\varepsilon}] \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy}/2 \end{bmatrix}$$ The strain transformation matrix is: $$[T_{\varepsilon}] = \begin{bmatrix} m^2 & n^2 & -2mn \\ n^2 & m^2 & 2mn \\ mn & -mn & m^2 - n^2 \end{bmatrix}$$ where $m = \cos\theta$ and $n = \sin\theta$. ### Step 2 — Stress Transformation $$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = [T_{\sigma}] \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$ $$[T_{\sigma}] = \begin{bmatrix} m^2 & n^2 & 2mn \\ n^2 & m^2 & -2mn \\ -mn & mn & m^2 - n^2 \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key identity:** $[T_{\sigma}]^{-1} = [T_{\varepsilon}]^T$ (this is not a coincidence — it follows from tensor transformation rules under orthogonal coordinate rotation). ### Step 3 — Combine to Get $[\bar{Q}]$ Using $\sigma_1 = Q_{11}\varepsilon_1 + Q_{12}\varepsilon_2$ etc., substitute the transformed strains and stresses, and rearrange: $$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = [T_{\sigma}]^{-1} [Q] [T_{\varepsilon}] \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = [\bar{Q}] \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{[\bar{Q}] = [T_{\sigma}]^{-1} [Q] [T_{\varepsilon}]}$$ The expanded explicit form of each component is: $$\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11}m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{22}n^4$$ $$\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11}n^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{22}m^4$$ $$\bar{Q}_{12} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{12}(m^4 + n^4)$$ $$\bar{Q}_{66} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^2n^2 + Q_{66}(m^4 + n^4)$$ $$\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m^3 n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})mn^3$$ $$\bar{Q}_{26} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})m n^3 + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})m^3 n$$ > [!WARNING] > **$\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ are NON-ZERO for $\theta \neq 0^\circ, 90^\circ$.** This is the most common KTU exam trap. Even though the material has *no* shear-extension coupling in its own axes, a rotated ply **does** exhibit coupling in the global axes. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Engineering Constants to $[Q]$ **Given:** A unidirectional glass/polyester ply with $E_1 = 35$ GPa, $E_2 = 8$ GPa, $G_{12} = 3.5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$. **Find:** The reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$. ### Step 1 — Compute $\nu_{21}$ from reciprocity $$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.30 \times \frac{8}{35} = 0.0686$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the denominator $$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - (0.30)(0.0686) = 1 - 0.0206 = 0.9794$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the $[Q]$ components $$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{35 \times 10^9}{0.9794} = 35.74 \text{ GPa}$$ $$Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{8 \times 10^9}{0.9794} = 8.17 \text{ GPa}$$ $$Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{0.30 \times 8 \times 10^9}{0.9794} = 2.45 \text{ GPa}$$ $$Q_{66} = G_{12} = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Matrix $$[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} 35.74 & 2.45 & 0 \\ 2.45 & 8.17 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}$$ ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Off-Axis Stiffness at $\theta = 45^\circ$ Using $m = \cos 45^\circ = n = \sin 45^\circ = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, so $m^2 = n^2 = mn = 0.5$. **Step 1 — Compute $m^4 = n^4 = 0.25$, $m^2 n^2 = 0.25$** **Step 2 — $\bar{Q}_{11}$** $$\bar{Q}_{11} = (35.74)(0.25) + 2(2.45 + 7.0)(0.25) + (8.17)(0.25)$$ $$= 8.935 + 4.725 + 2.0425 = 15.70 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 3 — $\bar{Q}_{22}$** $$\bar{Q}_{22} = (35.74)(0.25) + 2(2.45 + 7.0)(0.25) + (8.17)(0.25) = 15.70 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 4 — $\bar{Q}_{12}$** $$\bar{Q}_{12} = (35.74 + 8.17 - 14.0)(0.25) + (2.45)(0.5) = 7.4775 + 1.225 = 8.70 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 5 — $\bar{Q}_{66}$** $$\bar{Q}_{66} = (35.74 + 8.17 - 4.90 - 7.0)(0.25) + (3.5)(0.5) = 8.00 + 1.75 = 9.75 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 6 — $\bar{Q}_{16}$** $$\bar{Q}_{16} = (35.74 - 2.45 - 7.0)(0.125) + (2.45 - 8.17 + 7.0)(0.125) = 3.286 + 0.16 = 3.45 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 7 — $\bar{Q}_{26}$** $$\bar{Q}_{26} = (35.74 - 2.45 - 7.0)(0.125) + (2.45 - 8.17 + 7.0)(0.125) = 3.45 \text{ GPa}$$ ### Final Off-Axis Stiffness Matrix $$[\bar{Q}]_{45^\circ} = \begin{bmatrix} 15.70 & 8.70 & 3.45 \\ 8.70 & 15.70 & 3.45 \\ 3.45 & 3.45 & 9.75 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Compare the diagonal terms with the on-axis matrix. The normal stiffness has dropped from 35.74 GPa (on-axis) to 15.70 GPa (at 45°). A 45° ply is **far more compliant** in the global x-direction than a 0° ply. This is exactly why boat designers use **0° plies for axial strength** and **45° plies for shear/torsional stiffness**. # 4.
